"You don't look them in the eye too long," Chen told me during a break, when I asked how you could tell the difference. "You don't give them a reason to remember your face. And you definitely don't relax just because you see women and kids. They've used kids to drop grenades. They've used pregnant women to get close. Charlie doesn't fight fair, and that's actually the smartest thing about him."

The village sweep was different. More structured but more unsettling. Lieutenant Cramer led a full platoon—about thirty of us—into Phuoc An at dawn. We spread out in a line and moved through systematically, checking hooches and bunkers while the villagers watched with that same blank expression I was learning to recognize. Not hostile. Not friendly. Resigned.

We were looking for weapons caches, tunnel entrances, anything that indicated VC presence. What we found was poverty. Families living in one-room structures with dirt floors and corrugated tin roofs. Elderly men too old to have run off to either side. Women cooking rice over small fires. Children with distended bellies and skin diseases.

Sergeant Kowalski, who led first squad, spoke some Vietnamese. He tried talking to the village chief, an ancient man who squatted in the shade and smoked a pipe that smelled like burnt grass. The conversation went in circles. No, no VC here. Yes, sometimes men come at night. No, we don't know which direction. Yes, we want peace. No, we don't know anything useful.

"He's lying," Kowalski said flatly after. "But what are we going to do? Beat it out of him? These people hedge their bets. Help whoever's got the gun pointed at them today, survive until tomorrow, repeat."

We found a trapdoor in one hooch leading to a small bunker. My heart raced as Garrett and Sully pulled it open, weapons ready, but it was just a family shelter—cramped space with some stored rice and a few possessions wrapped in plastic. The woman whose home it was started crying, not from fear exactly, but from exhaustion. Like this was the hundredth time soldiers had torn through her life and she was just so tired of it.

Lieutenant Cramer gave her some C-rations and said something in broken Vietnamese that might have been an apology. She took the cans without thanking him.

"Hearts and minds," Sully muttered as we moved on. "We're winning so many fucking hearts and minds."

That night around the fire—you could have small fires inside the wire if you kept them low—the talk turned philosophical in the way it seemed to when everyone was exhausted. Alvarez wondered aloud if any of this mattered, if we were making a difference or just going through motions until someone decided the war was over.

"Doesn't matter if it matters," Garrett said. "We're here. Orders are orders."

"That's sheep talk," Hendricks said, lighting a cigarette from a heating tab. "You ever think about what we're actually doing? We're propping up a government nobody wants, fighting people who live here, making enemies faster than we can kill them. It's fucking insane."

"Then why'd you come back?" I asked before I could stop myself.

Hendricks looked at me through the smoke. "Because I'm good at insane. And because back in the World, I'm nobody. Here, at least I matter. Even if what I matter for is completely fucked."

"You matter back home," Chen said quietly. "You just have to find different ways to matter."

"Yeah? Tell me what matters about working the line in a factory, going home to an empty apartment, drinking until you can sleep, repeat forever. At least here, every day you survive means something."

Wash had been quiet, which usually meant he was thinking something through. "You all talk too much," he said finally. "This kind of talk is poison. You start questioning the mission, you hesitate. You hesitate, people die. So shut up, do your jobs, and save the philosophy for when you're back in the World with a beer in your hand."

But his voice lacked conviction. I think we all heard it.

---

This morning a helicopter brought in a new lieutenant—fresh from the States, younger than Cramer, with that same eager intensity I probably had three days ago. Feels like three years. He's replacing Lieutenant Morrison from Alpha Company, who stepped on a mine two weeks ago and lost both legs below the knee.

Nobody mentioned this until the new lieutenant—Ellis, his name is—asked where Morrison was.

"Hospital in Japan," Cramer said. "Then probably stateside."

"Will he recover?"

"From being legless? Sure." Cramer's voice was flat. "He'll recover great. Have a wonderful life in a wheelchair."

Ellis looked stricken, like he hadn't quite believed that could happen to officers. I recognized the look because it had been on my face before the mirror at Benning—that conviction that training and intelligence and good intentions would somehow protect you from the randomness of violence.

"First rule," Wash told me during a guard shift. "The universe doesn't give a shit about fair. Good guys die. Bad guys live. Sometimes it's backwards. Mostly it's just chaos wearing a uniform."

I'm trying to see the men around me as individuals but it's hard. They blur together into types: the veteran who's seen too much, the comedian hiding fear behind jokes, the quiet one who might snap someday, the big dumb guy who isn't actually dumb. These are archetypes, not people, and I know that's wrong but my brain is doing it anyway. Self-protection maybe. Easier to lose an archetype than a friend.

But small things break through. Riggs showing me a photo of his girl—not girlfriend, just "a girl he knows"—and the tenderness in his voice when he described her. Chen teaching me to brew better coffee using C-ration instant and a trick with salt that somehow makes it taste less like battery acid. Alvarez praying quietly before we go outside the wire, crossing himself in a gesture so automatic he probably doesn't know he's doing it.

These are people. They have mothers and histories and futures they're trying to reach. And I'm one of them now, whether I'm ready or not.

Tomorrow we're going out on a three-day patrol. Deeper into the jungle, following intelligence reports about enemy movement near the river. This isn't routine. Wash mentioned it casually but his jaw was tight when he said it.

"Pay attention to everything I tell you," he said. "All that cherry patience you've been practicing? Use it. And if shit goes sideways, don't be a hero. Do exactly what I say when I say it."

"What if you're—"

"If I'm down, do what Chen says. If Chen's down, do what Garrett says. Chain of command exists for a reason."

I nodded but inside my stomach was churning.

Sully caught me checking my rifle for the tenth time. "First time is always the worst," he said. "You spend all this energy imagining things, working up fear. Then it happens and you're too busy to be scared."

"Does it get easier?"

He considered that. "No. But you get better at living with it."

The three-day patrol begins before dawn. We move out single file through the wire gate, Wash on point, then Chen with the radio, then me. The jungle swallows us within fifty meters of the perimeter. One moment I can see the firebase behind me, the next there's only green—layers of it, thick and dripping with morning moisture.

The jungle is different than I imagined. Louder. Training films made it seem quiet, ominous, but it's actually screaming with life. Birds I can't see making calls that sound like human voices. Insects buzzing in clouds around our heads. Something crashing through the underbrush that Garrett says is probably a monkey but could be anything. The ground is spongy, thick with decomposing leaves that release a smell like rot and wet earth with every step.

We're following a trail—not an official trail, just a slight depression where feet have passed before. Wash reads it like a book, pausing every fifty meters to examine a broken branch or disturbed leaves. Once he stops completely, raising his fist, and we all freeze. He studies something I can't see for maybe two minutes before moving forward again.

During our first break Chen whispers, "Footprints. Maybe two days old. Small feet, probably locals, but headed the same direction we're going."

"VC?" I ask.

"Could be. Could be villagers. Could be villagers who are also VC at night." He adjusts his glasses, a gesture I'm learning means he's processing something. "That's the thing nobody tells you. It's the same people. The farmer you see during the day might be the one shooting at you after dark. They don't wear uniforms. They just fade back into being farmers when we show up."

By midday the heat is suffocating. Not just hot—aggressive, like it's trying to kill you through simple exposure. My canteen is already half empty and Wash told us to ration water until we reach the river. My uniform is soaked through, chafing in places I didn't know could chafe. Salt tablets make me nauseous but Alvarez says heatstroke is worse.

We pass through an area where the jungle opens up, Agent Orange defoliation from months ago. Dead trees like skeletal fingers pointing at white sky. No birds here. No insects. Just silence and dying vegetation. It feels wrong in a way I can't articulate. Like walking through a graveyard where the graves are still being dug.

"This was supposed to help us see them," Hendricks says, lighting a cigarette even though Wash told him not to smoke on patrol. "All it does is let them see us easier. And it's killing the fucking land. You know what grows back first in this shit? Bamboo. Dense bamboo worse than the original jungle. Real smart."

Wash doesn't tell him to put the cigarette out. I think he needs one himself.

That evening we make camp near a stream, setting up in a circular defensive position with Claymore mines placed along likely approaches. No fires. Cold C-rations eaten in near darkness. Wash assigns guard shifts in two-hour rotations. I get midnight to 0200 with Riggs.

Before my shift I try to sleep but can't. Every sound becomes threat. The stream babbling sounds like voices. Wind through the dead trees sounds like movement. I lie there with my rifle across my chest, eyes open, watching shadows shift in the canopy above.

When Riggs shakes me awake I'm somehow surprised I'd dozed off.

"Your turn, cherry. Stay awake. I'm serious."

Our position overlooks the stream and a small clearing. The moon is nearly full, providing just enough light to make everything look alien. Riggs settles in beside me, the M60 resting on a fallen log, ammunition belt draped over his shoulder.

"You okay?" he asks after maybe twenty minutes of silence.

"Yeah. Why?"

"You're breathing like you're running a marathon."

I force myself to slow down. "Just nervous."

"That's good. Nervous keeps you sharp." He adjusts the machine gun, checking its position for the hundredth time. "My daddy fought in World War Two. Pacific theater. He told me once that being scared doesn't make you a coward. Being scared and doing it anyway—that's courage. Being too stupid to be scared, that's just gambling."

"Were you scared? Your first time out?"

"Shit, yeah. Terrified. Thought I was gonna piss myself every time a branch snapped." He glances at me, face barely visible in the moonlight. "Still get scared. Just better at functioning through it now."

Around 0130 something moves across the clearing. My hand tightens on my rifle but Riggs touches my arm gently.

"Deer," he whispers. "See the way it moves? Four legs, head down. Just a deer."

But I can't shake the feeling we're being watched. That somewhere in the darkness, eyes are calculating distances, counting our positions, reporting back to someone who's planning what comes next.

When Alvarez relieves us at 0200 I'm exhausted and wired simultaneously. Sleep comes in fragments—twenty minutes at a time before some sound jerks me awake. When Wash kicks my boot at 0530 I feel worse than when I laid down.

Day two is harder. The initial adrenaline has worn off and now there's just the grinding reality of moving through hostile terrain on too little sleep and bad rations. My feet are developing blisters inside my boots. The straps of my rucksack have rubbed the skin on my shoulders raw. A cut on my hand from a wait-a-minute vine—thorny vines that hook your clothes and skin—is starting to look infected.

Nobody complains. That's the unspoken rule. You can hurt, you can be miserable, but you don't bitch about it because everyone else is just as miserable and complaining doesn't help.

We reach the river around noon. It's wider than I expected, maybe thirty meters across, flowing brown and lazy between muddy banks. Wash sends Garrett and Hendricks to scout upstream while the rest of us take positions and refill canteens. I drop purification tablets into my canteen and wait the required time, though I see Riggs just drink straight from the river.

"You'll get dysentery," Chen warns him.

"Rather shit myself than die of thirst." But he grins when he says it.

Lieutenant Cramer comes up on the radio. Chen listens, acknowledges, then turns to Wash. "We're to hold here until Alpha Company swings around from the east. Then we move north together, sweep the suspected base camp area."

"How long?"

"Two hours, maybe three."

Wash curses under his breath. "So we just sit here visible from both banks, waiting to get our asses shot off."

But orders are orders.

We dig in as best we can, using entrenching tools to create shallow fighting positions along the bank. The jungle across the river looks identical to the jungle behind us—just green walls with hidden depth. Could be nothing there. Could be a hundred VC watching us work.

An hour passes. Then another. The sun beats down on the river, turning it into liquid light that hurts to look at. Insects swarm. I drink more water, refill my canteen, drink again. My lips are cracked. My tongue feels swollen.

Sully starts telling a story about a bar fight in Boston, something involving his cousin and a misunderstanding about a Red Sox game, but he trails off mid-sentence because we all hear it at the same time.

Voices. Vietnamese. Upstream, maybe a hundred meters.

Wash makes a hand signal: freeze.

We crouch in our positions, weapons ready, barely breathing. The voices get closer. I can see movement now through the vegetation—shapes that resolve into three men in black pajamas and conical hats, moving along the riverbank toward us. They're carrying something. Fishing nets maybe, or—

"Weapons," Garrett breathes. "AKs."

My heart hammers so hard I'm sure they'll hear it. They're forty meters away now. Thirty. They're talking casually, unaware of us. One of them laughs at something another said.

Twenty meters.

Wash doesn't give the order to fire. We just watch as they pass within fifteen meters of our position, so close I can see the pattern on one man's shirt, the rope sandals on another's feet. They walk upstream and disappear around a bend.

When they're gone I realize I've been holding my breath. I let it out slowly, silently.

"Why didn't we—" I start to whisper, but Wash cuts me off with a look.

Later, when we're waiting again, he explains. "Three guys with rifles doesn't mean anything. Could be local security. Could be hunters. We start shooting, we give away our position and maybe kick off a shitstorm we're not ready for. Rules of engagement say we don't fire unless fired upon or unless we're certain of enemy activity."

"They had AKs."

"Lots of people have AKs around here. French left thousands. Chinese send more. Doesn't automatically make them VC." He pauses. "Might have been VC. Probably were. But we let them go because that's the smart play right now."

It doesn't feel smart. It feels like we're fighting with one hand tied behind our backs while the enemy fights however they want.

Alpha Company arrives around 1600, materializing from the jungle in squads. Lieutenant Cramer confers with their CO, maps spread on a poncho. Then we're moving again, a full platoon now, sweeping north in a long line through heavy undergrowth.

The suspected base camp is supposed to be two klicks ahead. Intelligence says it's a way station for troops moving down from Cambodia—bunkers, supply caches, maybe a hospital. If we find it, we'll call in artillery or air strikes. If it's occupied, things will get interesting fast.

We move slowly, carefully. Every twenty meters someone checks for tripwires, booby traps, pressure plates. The jungle is quiet now. Too quiet. Even the insects seem to have shut up.

I'm thinking about those three men. Wondering if they've circled back, if they're watching us now, if they've warned whoever might be ahead. Wondering if I'm about to experience combat or just another day of walking through green nothing and finding abandoned camps and rusted equipment.

Wondering which I'm actually more afraid of.

The base camp, when we find it, is empty.

Not abandoned—empty. There's a difference Wash explains as we move through the network of bunkers and trenches. Abandoned means they left in a hurry, left things behind. Empty means they knew we were coming and took everything that mattered.

The bunkers are sophisticated. Not the crude holes I expected, but engineered positions with overhead cover, firing ports, interconnecting tunnels. Someone spent weeks building this. The trenches are deep enough to stand in, reinforced with logs that show axe marks from careful construction. There are fighting positions dug into the hillside with overlapping fields of fire. A latrine area downslope from the main position. Even a small structure that might have been a field hospital or command post.

But no weapons. No ammunition. No rice stores or medical supplies. No people.

"They've been gone maybe six hours," Sergeant Kowalski says, examining a fire pit that's still faintly warm. "Cleaned out everything useful. Left us the real estate."

Lieutenant Cramer radios it in. We can hear the frustration in his voice as he describes the find. Command will want to know why intelligence was wrong, why we didn't arrive sooner, whose fault it is that we're standing in an empty camp instead of engaging the enemy.

"We'll destroy it," Cramer says after he's off the radio. "Plant charges in the bunkers, collapse the trenches. Deny them the position."

But everyone knows they'll just build another one. And another. The jungle is infinite and our reach extends maybe three hundred meters from whatever fire base we're operating from. Charlie can fade back, wait, return when we leave. He's got time. We're the ones on the clock, counting days until DEROS—Date of Expected Return from Overseas—the magic number that means going home.

We spend the rest of daylight rigging demolitions. I watch Sully and Chen place C-4 charges in the main bunkers, running detonation cord between them like some deadly Christmas decoration. Garrett and Alvarez fill in trenches, destroying months of labor in minutes. Hendricks finds a tunnel entrance and tosses a grenade down it before anyone can stop him.

"The fuck, Hendricks?" Wash shouts over the muffled explosion. "Could've been rigged."

"Wasn't."

"You didn't know that."

Hendricks shrugs, already lighting another cigarette. The gesture says everything about how much he values his own life, which is to say not very much at all.

We pull back two hundred meters before blowing the charges. The explosions are sharp, precise cracks followed by rumbling as bunker roofs collapse inward. Smoke rises through the canopy. Birds that had returned to the area after we arrived flee again, screaming protests.

"Another successful operation," Sully mutters. "We found some empty holes and filled them in. Progress."

That night we set up in a defensive perimeter half a klick from the destroyed camp. Wash is tense in a way I haven't seen before. He triple-checks the Claymore positions, makes everyone confirm fields of fire, assigns extra guards.

"They knew we were coming," he says quietly to Cramer during the officers' conference I'm not supposed to hear. "Which means either someone told them or they've got observation posts we didn't spot. Either way, they know where we are right now."

"You think they'll hit us?"

"I think we just destroyed something they put real work into. How would you feel about that?"

Cramer doesn't answer.

My guard shift is 0200 to 0400 with Chen. We position ourselves on the western edge of the perimeter, where the jungle presses closest. Chen keeps his radio volume low, the handset near his ear, monitoring the battalion net.

"You did good today," he says after maybe thirty minutes of silence.

"We didn't do anything."

"Exactly. You didn't freeze up, didn't do anything stupid, didn't get anyone killed. That's good." He adjusts his glasses, a nervous gesture. "First few patrols, that's all anyone expects. Don't be the guy who fucks up."

"What about being the guy who does something right?"

"Being the guy who does something right means someone else is doing something wrong. You don't want to be in that situation." He pauses, listening to something on the radio. "Besides, right and wrong are flexible here. You follow orders, you watch your sector, you don't waste ammo on shadows. That's the job."

"Hendricks said—"

"Hendricks is on his second tour because he's got a death wish and doesn't know what else to do with it. Don't listen to Hendricks about philosophy. Listen to him about where not to step and how to tell fresh digging from old digging. Everything else is bullshit he tells himself to justify being here."

Around 0330 we hear movement. Not close—maybe seventy-five meters out, maybe more. Something large moving through the undergrowth with irregular rhythm. Start, stop, start again.

Chen keys his handset twice—the signal for possible contact. Across the perimeter I can sense rather than see people coming alert. Safeties clicking off. Weapons being positioned.

The movement continues. Closer now. Fifty meters maybe. I can feel sweat running down my spine despite the cool night air. My finger rests on the trigger guard, not the trigger—Wash's constant instruction—but ready to move.

Then a smell. Distinct. Wrong.

"Water buffalo," Chen whispers, so quietly I almost miss it. "Smell that? That's shit. If it was people they wouldn't bring a buffalo on a night approach."

The creature—buffalo or cow or whatever it is—ambles past our position maybe thirty meters out, completely unconcerned with our existence. After it's gone the jungle settles back into its baseline of insect noise and rustling that could be anything.

"How'd you know?" I ask.

"Six months in-country. You learn the smells. Animals smell different than people. People smell like fish sauce and cigarettes and fear. Animals just smell like animals." He listens to the radio again. "Also, if it was VC, they'd have hit us by now. They don't waste time on theater."

When Riggs and Garrett relieve us at 0400 I'm exhausted but wired. Sleep comes in fragments again. Dreams mixing with reality—I'm back in Indianapolis but the streets are jungle, or I'm in the jungle but my mother is there asking me to explain what I'm doing, why I'm pointing a gun at farmers, what happened to the boy she raised.

I wake with a start to someone shaking my shoulder. It's still dark.

"Gear up," Wash says. "We're moving."

"What time is it?"

"0500. Change of plans. Command wants us at a ville three klicks east. Intelligence says there was VC activity there last night."

"Intelligence," Hendricks says from somewhere in the darkness, "can suck my entire ass."

We move out before dawn, following a different route back. Wash keeps us off trails entirely now, cutting through thick jungle that fights every step. Progress is slow. My uniform catches on wait-a-minute vines. My face collects spider webs that might or might not contain spiders. Something bites my neck and when I reach up my hand comes away bloody.

The village appears gradually through the trees. Small—maybe a dozen hooches arranged in a rough circle around a central area. Cooking fires sending up thin lines of smoke. The smell of fish and rice. Children's voices.

We approach carefully, weapons ready but not pointed. Cramer and Kowalski enter first, calling out in Vietnamese. The villagers emerge slowly, faces arranged in that neutral expression I'm learning to recognize. Not surprised to see us. Not happy either. Just resigned.

An old woman, bent nearly double with age, shuffles forward and begins speaking rapidly. Kowalski translates, though I can only catch pieces. Something about soldiers. Something about last night. Something about fear.

Cramer questions her patiently. She gestures toward the jungle, toward the village, toward the sky. Her hands move with the fluid grace of someone telling a story she's told before.

"She says VC came last night," Kowalski relays. "Six or seven men. Took rice, took information about our patrols. Threatened the village chief—if anyone tells us, they'll come back and people will die."

"Did she see weapons?" Cramer asks.

More rapid conversation. The old woman's voice rises, frustrated or frightened, hard to tell which.

"Yes. Rifles. At least one RPG. They headed north after leaving here."

"North toward the river."

"Yes sir."

Cramer studies his map, calculating. "That puts them between us and Firebase Python. If they're moving during daylight, they're either very confident or very desperate."

Wash spits into the dirt. "Or they're baiting us. Intelligence says they're at the village, we go to the village, they're long gone but now they know our position and strength."

"You got a better idea, Corporal?"

"Yeah. We hole up here, set up in defensive positions, wait for them to either show or not show. Chasing ghosts through the jungle is how people get ambushed."

But Cramer shakes his head. "Orders are to pursue any confirmed enemy activity. We've got confirmation from a civilian source. We move north, link up with Bravo Company coming south, squeeze them between us."

"That's the theory," Wash says, but he's already gesturing for the squad to form up.

We leave the village behind. The children watch us go with huge dark eyes that reveal nothing. The old woman has already turned away, back to whatever tasks occupy her days between visits from men with guns who all want different things.

The jungle north of the village is different—older trees, less undergrowth, almost park-like in places. It should feel safer but somehow feels more exposed. Long sight lines mean we can see farther but so can anyone watching us.

Around 0900 we hear it.

Distant but unmistakable. The sharp crack of small arms fire, the deeper thump of grenades. A lot of it. North and east, maybe two klicks away.

"Bravo Company," Chen says, listening to his radio. "They've made contact. Heavy contact. They're requesting—"

The rest is lost as Cramer starts shouting orders. We're moving double-time now, running through the jungle toward the sound of guns. My rucksack pounds against my spine. My breath comes in ragged gasps. Branches whip my face.

This is it, I think. This is really it.

We're not walking toward some maybe-threat anymore. We're running toward confirmed enemy contact. People are shooting. People are dying. And in maybe fifteen minutes we're going to be part of it.

My mind goes strangely quiet. All the fear and anticipation and questions drain away, replaced by a kind of focused clarity. Left foot, right foot. Watch Wash's back. Keep interval. Don't trip. Don't fall. Keep moving.

The sound of gunfire grows louder.

The firing intensifies as we close the distance—no longer scattered shots but a sustained roar that speaks of disciplined defense meeting determined assault. Chen's radio erupts with overlapping transmissions, voices tight with stress cutting through static. Bravo Company is pinned down in a horseshoe-shaped clearing, taking fire from three sides.

"They walked right into it," Wash says, reading the tactical situation from sound alone. "Classic L-shaped ambush. VC let them get deep into the kill zone before opening up."

Cramer halts us fifty meters from the contact point, behind a low ridgeline that provides defilade from the firing. Through gaps in the vegetation I can see muzzle flashes—bright strobing in the shadows under the canopy. Tracers arc across the clearing like angry fireflies. The crack-thump of AK fire is punctuated by the deeper bark of M16s and the chattering bursts of M60s.

"First and second squad, we're moving up the left flank," Cramer shouts over the noise. "Get in behind their position, suppress that treeline. Third squad holds here, establishes base of fire. Chen, tell Bravo we're coming in from the southwest—don't want them shooting us by mistake."

We move in fire-team rushes—one element up while another provides covering fire, leapfrogging forward in bounds that eat up the distance in controlled bursts. My training takes over. I'm not thinking anymore, just executing: pick a position, sprint to it, drop behind cover, scan for targets, signal Wash I'm set, watch the next man move.

The vegetation opens up and suddenly we can see them—Bravo Company scattered across the clearing in whatever cover they could find when the ambush was sprung. Some are behind fallen logs, others in shallow depressions, a few have managed to get behind a small berm that might have been a rice paddy dike once. Bodies lie in the open—at least three I can see, not moving.

And beyond them, in the treeline maybe eighty meters across the clearing, I see my first confirmed enemy. Not farmers. Not shadows. Actual VC fighters in fighting positions, muzzle flashes marking their locations like constellation points.

"There!" Garrett shouts, pointing. "Bunker, eleven o'clock, fifty meters!"

I see it—a darker shadow in the shadows, a firing port with smoke wisping from it. As I watch, an RPG streaks out of it with a whoosh and a tail of flame, impacting near a cluster of Bravo Company soldiers. The explosion throws dirt and shrapnel in a lethal radius.

"Light them up!" Wash orders.

We open fire as a unit. My first shots go high—I'm jerking the trigger, rushing, all the bad habits basic training tried to break. I force myself to slow down. Breathe. Aim. Squeeze. My next burst walks into the bunker aperture. I can't tell if I hit anything but the fire from that position slackens.

Riggs sets up the M60, tripod deployed, and opens up with long disciplined bursts that saw through vegetation and hammer at identified positions. The sound is enormous—a physical force that seems to compress the air. Brass cascades from the gun in a bright waterfall.

"Grenadier up!" Cramer yells.

Alvarez moves forward with his M79, the stubby grenade launcher that looks almost toy-like. He breaks it open, loads a 40mm round, snaps it shut. The weapon coughs and the round arcs across the clearing in a lazy parabola before impacting in the treeline with a crump that's felt as much as heard.

The VC fire shifts, some of it coming toward our position now. Rounds snap through the air overhead with that distinctive crack that means they're close. Something impacts the tree beside me, spraying bark fragments across my face. I taste blood and realize I've bitten my lip without noticing.

"Keep firing!" Wash yells. "Don't let them pin us!"

I empty a magazine at shapes I can barely distinguish from shadows. Reload. Fire again. The rifle stock kicks against my shoulder with each burst. The smell of cordite fills my nose, chemical and sharp. My ears are ringing despite the sounds of battle.

Chen is on the radio, calling in coordinates for artillery support. His voice is remarkably calm, like he's ordering pizza instead of directing high explosives onto human targets.

"Shot out," the radio crackles. "Splash in thirty seconds."

"Incoming!" Wash screams. "Get down!"

The artillery rounds arrive with a sound like freight trains passing overhead. The impacts are beyond the treeline—correction rounds, walking in toward the target. Geysers of earth erupt skyward. The concussion rolls across the clearing in waves.

"Add fifty, fire for effect," Chen says into the handset.

The next salvo is closer. Trees disintegrate. The VC fire from that sector stops entirely—either they're dead, displaced, or smart enough to know you don't fight artillery with rifles.

Bravo Company uses the suppression to move. I watch them bound back from their exposed positions toward better cover on the far side of the clearing. Some carry wounded. Others lay down covering fire. It's chaos but somehow coordinated—small unit tactics executed under pressure.

Then everything changes.

A whistle—high and shrill—cuts through the gunfire. The VC fire intensifies dramatically, shifting from disciplined to desperate. I see figures moving in the treeline, not retreating but advancing. Breaking cover and running toward the clearing.

"Human wave!" someone screams.

But it's not a wave—it's a fighting withdrawal. The VC are pulling back under fire, using assault as cover for retreat. It's a tactic that should get them slaughtered but somehow it's working. They're laying down suppressing fire, then moving in small groups while others cover them, leapfrogging backward into deeper jungle.

"They're breaking contact!" Cramer yells. "Second squad, pursue! First squad, support by fire!"

Wash looks at him like he's insane. "Sir, that's exactly what they—"

"That's an order, Corporal!"

We advance into the clearing. It feels wrong. Every instinct screams this is a mistake but orders are orders and fear of disobeying is stronger than fear of dying. We move in rushes again, but now we're the ones exposed, crossing open ground while the enemy falls back into prepared positions.

I'm halfway across when I see the wire.

It's almost invisible—thin strand stretched ankle-high between two stakes, partially obscured by grass. I'm about to step over it when Wash's hand clamps on my shoulder and yanks me sideways so hard I fall.

"Booby trap," he says, pointing. "See the wire? Probably a claymore or cluster of grenades. You trip that, you kill everyone within ten meters."

My legs feel weak. I was one step from death and didn't even see it coming.

"Mark it and move around," Wash orders.

Alvarez ties engineer's tape to a nearby branch, fluorescent orange screaming danger. We detour wide, scanning the ground for other tripwires, other hidden killers.

The VC have melted away by the time we reach the treeline. Their positions are empty except for blood trails and scattered equipment—a sandal, an empty magazine, a conical hat with a bullet hole through the crown. We find two bodies, both young men who look barely old enough to shave. Their weapons are gone, taken by their comrades.

"Count," Cramer says.

The butcher's bill: Bravo Company lost four dead, seven wounded—two critical. We took no casualties, which seems impossible but Chen confirms it. The VC left two confirmed dead and unknown wounded, dragged away during their withdrawal.

"We won," Sully says, but there's no triumph in his voice.

"Did we?" Hendricks lights a cigarette with shaking hands. "They picked the ground, they picked the time, they broke contact when they wanted to. We just made noise and threw expensive ordnance at jungle."

We spend an hour securing the area and setting up a landing zone for medevac. The helicopters come in low and fast, flaring hard to settle onto the improvised LZ. Combat medics load the worst wounded first—a kid with stomach wounds who keeps calling for his mother, another with a leg mostly severed above the knee, held on by straps of muscle and determination.

I help carry a body to one of the choppers. He's heavy in that specific way only dead weight can be, like gravity wants him more than the living. His face is intact—he could be sleeping except for the hole in his chest you could put three fingers through. I don't know his name. Never will.

The helicopters lift off, rotors beating the air into submission, and we're alone again with the jungle and the bodies we couldn't save and the enemy we couldn't catch.

That night we stay in the clearing, forming a defensive perimeter among the abandoned enemy positions. Nobody sleeps. We're too wired, too aware of how close it came, too conscious that the VC know exactly where we are and might return.

Around midnight, Wash finds me on guard duty.

"You did good today, Mercer. Didn't freeze. Didn't do anything stupid."

"I almost stepped on a booby trap."

"Almost doesn't count. I was watching. You would've seen it." He pauses. "Maybe."

"Those bodies. They were so young."

"Yeah. So are you. So were the guys we medevaced." He squats beside me, scanning the darkness beyond our perimeter. "You thinking about quitting?"

"Can I quit?"

"Not until your DEROS. But you can quit inside. Stop caring. Stop trying. Just exist until it's time to leave. Some guys do that. Walk around like ghosts. Usually get themselves or someone else killed."

"What should I do?"

"What you're doing. Stay scared. Stay sharp. Trust your squad." He stands, knees cracking. "And write it down. Whatever you're feeling. You're gonna need those words later, when you're trying to figure out what the fuck any of this meant."

He walks away, checking the other positions, leaving me alone with my rifle and my thoughts and the darkness where men with guns wait for reasons I'm no longer sure I understand.

prompt: Imagine a story where your gaze involuntarily broadcasts your private thoughts into anyone you look at and you have no way to switch it off. Set it during a hectic weekend in a bustling city and include at least three vivid episodes where stray thoughts spark unexpected laughter, tears, or chaos in strangers. Focus on themes of boundaries, empathy, and the strange intimacy of forced mind sharing. The story should be 8,000-10,000 words.
generated_story:
The subway car lurched, and Maya gripped the pole harder, her knuckles whitening. She kept her eyes fixed on the scuffed floor, on the gum-wrapper someone had dropped near her sneakers, on anything but the faces surrounding her in the Friday evening crush. A businessman's polished oxfords. A teenager's neon laces. The yellow safety line by the doors.

Don't look up. Don't look up. Don't look up.

The mantra had become her heartbeat over the past forty-eight hours.

It had started Wednesday night. She'd been sitting across from her girlfriend, Priya, at their favorite Thai place on Bleecker Street, the one with the wonky tables and the best pad see ew in the Village. Priya had been mid-sentence, something about her mother's upcoming visit, when Maya's eyes met hers and—

*God, I hope she doesn't want to stay with us again. Last time she rearranged our entire kitchen and I found her reading my journal. Two weeks of that and I might actually lose it.*

Priya had frozen, chopsticks halfway to her mouth. "You... Maya, did you just...?"

Maya had blinked, confused. "Did I what?"

"I heard you. In my head. Like you were speaking directly into my brain." Priya set down her chopsticks carefully. "About my mother. About the journal."

Maya's stomach had dropped. "I didn't say anything."

"No. But I *heard* you."

They'd tested it. Maya had looked at Priya and thought, deliberately: *Can you hear this?*

Priya had gasped. "Yes. Jesus. Yes, I can hear you."

Then Maya had looked away, fixed her gaze on the wall, and thought the same words. Nothing. Priya heard nothing. 

Only when Maya looked at someone did they receive her thoughts, crystal clear, as if she were speaking directly into their mind.

They'd left the restaurant without finishing their meal. Priya had been quiet on the walk home, processing. Maya had kept her eyes on the sidewalk, terrified of accidentally broadcasting her spiraling panic to every stranger they passed. By the time they reached their apartment, Priya's hand was no longer in hers.

"I need to think," Priya had said, and she'd gone to stay with a friend in Brooklyn.

That was two days ago. Maya had called in sick to work—she was a social media manager for a small nonprofit, a job that required attending events and meetings, looking people in the eye, projecting confidence and warmth. Impossible now. She'd spent Thursday in her apartment, lights off, ordering delivery and answering the door with her gaze firmly fixed on the wall beside the delivery person's head.

But Friday morning, her neighbor Mrs. Chen had knocked, asking to borrow sugar. Maya had opened the door and made the mistake—the split-second, devastating mistake—of meeting the old woman's eyes.

*Please just take the sugar and leave I haven't showered in two days and if I think about what's happening to me I'm going to start screaming and never stop—*

Mrs. Chen's face had crumpled. She'd reached out, her weathered hand gripping Maya's wrist. "Oh, honey," she'd whispered, and her eyes had filled with tears. "Oh, sweetheart. Whatever it is, it's going to be okay."

Maya had thrust the sugar container at her and closed the door, then slid down to sit against it, her breath coming in short gasps.

She couldn't stay locked in her apartment forever. She'd needed air, space, the anonymity of the city. She'd needed to move through the world even if she couldn't look at it.

So here she was, on the F train heading uptown, eyes down, practicing the art of navigating New York City blind.

The train squealed into West 4th Street. Bodies pressed closer as more passengers shoved aboard. Maya felt someone's backpack dig into her shoulder blade. Sweat prickled at her hairline despite the October chill. The doors chimed closed.

"Excuse me."

The voice was male, young, close to her left ear. Maya kept her eyes locked on her sneakers.

"Excuse me, miss? You dropped this."

She sensed rather than saw him holding something out to her. Her phone. It must have slipped from her jacket pocket in the crush.

"Thank you," she mumbled, reaching out blindly.

"Here, just—" His hand found hers, pressing the phone into her palm, and the touch made her glance up reflexively.

Their eyes met.

He was probably twenty-three, twenty-four. Shaggy dark hair, a Strand Books tote bag over one shoulder. Kind eyes. 

And into those kind eyes, Maya's thoughts poured like water through a broken dam:

*Thank you thank you god what if I'd lost my phone on top of everything else Priya still hasn't texted back what if she never texts back what if this thing never stops what if I'm like this forever what if I can never look at anyone again what if I die alone because I can't even make eye contact I'm so tired I'm so fucking tired I just want this to stop I want everything to stop I want—*

His expression shifted from friendly helpfulness to shock to something like horror. He took a step back, nearly tripping over the woman behind him.

"I'm sorry," Maya gasped, wrenching her gaze away. "I'm sorry, I didn't mean—"

But he was already pushing through the crowd, muttering apologies, desperate to put distance between himself and whatever broken thing he'd just touched. Maya caught a glimpse of his face before he turned away: pale, shaken, the way you'd look after accidentally seeing something you weren't meant to see. Something intimate and terrible.

The train pulled into 14th Street. Maya squeezed out onto the platform and stood there, breathing hard, letting the crowd flow around her like water around a stone.

The platform emptied. Another train came and went. Maya stood frozen, staring at the grimy tiles beneath her feet, at the ancient gum spots and the faded yellow warning stripe. A rat skittered along the far wall, disappearing into the shadows between the tracks.

She couldn't go home. Mrs. Chen might come back, might want to talk, might look at her with those concerned eyes and receive another unwanted download of Maya's unraveling psyche. She couldn't go to Priya—Priya who hadn't called, hadn't texted, who was probably sitting in her friend's apartment in Park Slope trying to figure out how to break up with someone whose thoughts you could hear.

Maya pulled out her phone. Three missed calls from her mother in New Jersey. A text from her boss asking if she'd be back Monday. Nothing from Priya.

Her thumb hovered over Priya's name in her contacts. What would she even say? *Hey, sorry about the telepathy thing, want to get dinner?*

The absurdity of it almost made her laugh. Almost. Instead, she felt the familiar pressure building behind her eyes, the tightness in her throat.

Not here. Not on a subway platform with security cameras and whoever might wander by.

Maya forced herself to move. She walked toward the exit, keeping her head down, navigating by peripheral vision and memory. Up the stairs, through the turnstile, onto 14th Street where the October evening was settling into that bruised-sky dusk that always made the city feel both endless and claustrophobic.

She started walking west, no destination in mind. Just movement. Just the anonymous rhythm of footsteps on pavement, of being one body among millions, invisible as long as she didn't look up.

At Eighth Avenue she nearly collided with someone.

"Watch it," the man snapped, and Maya jerked her head down harder, mumbling an apology. Her heart hammered. Had she looked at him? Had their eyes met for even a fraction of a second? She didn't think so, but she couldn't be sure, couldn't—

"Maya?"

The voice stopped her cold. She knew that voice.

"Maya, is that you?"

Slowly, keeping her gaze fixed on the sidewalk, Maya turned. She could see jeans, well-worn Converse, the hem of a green jacket she recognized.

"David," she said.

"Holy shit, I haven't seen you since—what, graduation? Four years?" David Chen. Not related to her neighbor Mrs. Chen, just one of those coincidences. They'd been in the same photography program at NYU, had even dated briefly in sophomore year before realizing they were better as friends. He'd moved to LA after graduation, chasing documentary work. "You look... are you okay? You look—"

"I'm fine," Maya said quickly, taking a step back. "Sorry, I'm actually in a hurry, I have to—"

"Wait, Maya, seriously." His voice softened with concern, and she could see his hand reaching toward her in her peripheral vision, the gesture arrested mid-air. "You're scaring me a little. Is something wrong? Are you in trouble?"

The genuine worry in his voice cracked something in her chest. David. Sweet, goofy David who used to make her laugh during their terrible 8 AM film theory class, who'd held her when her grandmother died junior year, who'd sent her a postcard from every state he'd driven through on his way to California.

"I can't explain," she said, her voice barely above a whisper. "I just... I can't."

"Can we get coffee? Just coffee. You don't have to explain anything." A pause. "Please? I'm only in town until Sunday and I'd really like to catch up."

Maya stood there on the corner of 14th and Eighth, people streaming past on both sides, the city roaring around her, and realized she had two choices. She could say no, could continue walking alone through the streets with her eyes on the ground until she wore a path into the concrete or went completely insane. 

Or she could try. She could sit across from an old friend in a coffee shop and see if maybe, somehow, she could learn to manage this. To control it. To exist in the world again.

"Okay," she heard herself say. "Coffee."

"Yeah?" David's voice brightened. "Great! There's that place on 9th Avenue, you know, the one we used to—"

"David." Maya interrupted. "I need to tell you something first. It's going to sound insane."

"Okay..."

"I can't look at you. I can't look at anyone right now. And I need you to not ask me why, to just... accept that. Can you do that?"

The silence stretched for three heartbeats. Then: "Is this some kind of anxiety thing? Because that's okay, I get that. My sister has—"

"It's not anxiety," Maya said, though that wasn't entirely true. "It's complicated. But if we're going to get coffee, you have to promise me you won't try to make me look at you. You won't lean into my sight line or wave your hand in front of my face or any of that. Okay?"

"I promise," David said, and something in his tone made her believe him.

They walked side by side to the coffee shop, not touching, Maya's eyes fixed on the sidewalk while David talked. He filled the space with easy chatter about his work, about the documentary he'd just finished shooting about urban gardens in South Central, about his terrible studio apartment where you could hear every word the neighbors said and they said a *lot*.

The normalcy of it was almost painful.

At the coffee shop, Maya chose a table in the back corner and sat with her back to the wall, facing the exposed brick. David took the chair opposite her without comment, positioning himself so she could see his hands on the table but nothing more.

"What do you want? I'll order," he said.

"Oat milk latte. Large."

She heard him walk away, heard the murmur of his voice at the counter, the hiss of the espresso machine. Heard him return and set two cups on the table.

"They were out of oat milk. I got you almond. Hope that's okay."

"It's fine. Thank you."

Maya wrapped her hands around the warm cup, grateful for something to anchor her. The coffee shop hummed with the usual Friday evening energy—laughter from a group of college students near the window, the clatter of dishes being bussed, indie folk playing just loud enough to provide cover for private conversations.

"So," David said carefully. "You don't have to talk about it if you don't want to. We can just sit here and drink overpriced coffee like civilized people."

Maya almost smiled. "I want to tell you. I just don't know if I should."

"Try me. I once filmed a guy in Highland Park who was convinced he was receiving messages from pigeons. After that, my bar for 'too weird' is pretty high."

She took a sip of her latte, buying time. The almond milk was too sweet, slightly wrong, but she drank it anyway. 

"What if I told you something changed in me three days ago? Something impossible."

"I'd ask what kind of impossible."

"The kind that makes you sound completely insane when you say it out loud."

David shifted in his chair. She could see his fingers drumming lightly on the table, a nervous habit he'd always had. "Maya, you're one of the most rational people I know. If you think something impossible happened, I'm inclined to believe something happened."

She set down her cup. Stared at the brick wall with its vintage concert posters and string lights. A faded Velvet Underground poster caught her eye—the banana, bright yellow against black. She'd had the same one in her dorm room.

"When I look at people," she began slowly, "they can hear my thoughts. Not when I glance at them accidentally, but when we make real eye contact. Everything I'm thinking just... transfers. Directly into their head."

Silence. She waited for the laugh, the kind dismissal, the suggestion that maybe she should talk to someone professional.

Instead: "Jesus."

"Yeah."

"And you're sure? You've tested it?"

"Priya heard everything I was thinking about her mother. A guy on the subway this evening got a full dose of my breakdown. My neighbor Mrs. Chen..." Maya's throat tightened. "I'm sure."

More silence. Then David said, very quietly, "That sounds fucking terrifying."

Something in her chest loosened. Not much, but enough. "It is. I can't go to work. I can't see my family. Priya's basically ghosting me. I can't even buy groceries without risking accidentally making eye contact with the cashier and dumping all my existential dread on them."

"Is it all your thoughts, or just some?"

"I don't know. Maybe just the surface stuff, the loudest thoughts. But that's bad enough. You have no idea what goes through your head at any given moment until someone else might hear it."

David was quiet for a moment, and she could feel him processing. "Can I ask you something? And you can tell me to fuck off if I'm out of line."

"Okay."

"What are you thinking right now?"

Maya blinked at the brick wall. "What?"

"Right now. This second. What's going through your head?"

She catalogued it honestly. "That this almond milk is too sweet. That I miss Priya. That you're being surprisingly cool about this and I'm grateful. That I'm terrified this is permanent. That I haven't showered since Wednesday and I probably smell. That I'm relieved to be sitting down because my feet hurt. That—" She stopped, heat rising to her face.

"That what?"

"That I'm scared of what you're thinking. Whether you believe me or think I've lost it."

"I believe you," David said. "And I don't think you've lost it. But Maya, that's not that bad."

"What?"

"What you were just thinking. It's normal. It's human. It's not—I don't know—dark or shameful or whatever you're afraid of."

"You don't understand. It's not always like that. On the train, that guy, I was—" She pressed her palms against her eyes. "I was having intrusive thoughts. About wanting everything to stop. About dying. About—"

"Hey." David's voice was gentle. "Do you actually want to die? Like, are you in danger right now?"

"No. No, I don't actually want that. It's just... when you're anxious, or depressed, or overwhelmed, your brain goes to these extreme places. You don't mean it. But you think it, just for a second, and then it's gone. Except now it's not gone. Now it gets beamed directly into someone else's consciousness."

"Okay. Okay, I get that." A pause. "Have you tried to control it? Like, to filter what gets through?"

Maya lowered her hands. "How?"

"I don't know. Meditation? Thinking really hard about one specific thing when you look at someone? It's your ability, right? There has to be some way to manage it."

"It's not an ability, David. It's a curse."

"Maybe. But even curses have rules." She heard him lean back in his chair. "Think about it. You said it only happens with eye contact. So there's already a condition. It's not like you're broadcasting to everyone all the time. That's something."

She hadn't thought of it that way. It was still horrifying, but he had a point. There was a boundary, however thin.

"I'm just saying," David continued, "maybe you could practice. Find someone you trust and experiment with it. See if you can control the volume, or the content, or the duration. Scientists don't understand new phenomena by hiding from them."

"I'm not a scientist. I'm a social media manager who can't look at people."

"You're a photographer," David said. "Or you were. And good photographers understand framing, focus, selection. You already know how to control what you see and what you show. This might not be that different."

Maya picked at the cardboard sleeve around her cup. "That's a nice theory."

"It's a starting point." He paused. "Look, I'm in town until Sunday. If you want to practice, I'm volunteering. We can try it somewhere private, controlled. See what happens."

"You'd do that?"

"Maya, we dated for six months. I've seen you with stomach flu. I've heard your thoughts on your freshman roommate. I've read your angry drunk texts about your advisor. There's not much you could think that would shock me at this point."

Despite everything, she almost laughed. "That's different. That was consensual information sharing."

"True. But I'm consenting now. I'm saying I'm willing to be your guinea pig for telepathy experiments." His tone turned more serious. "You can't live like this forever. Eyes down, avoiding everyone. That's not sustainable. So either you figure out how to manage it, or..."

"Or what?"

"Or you find other people like you. This can't be the first time something like this has happened. Telepathy, ESP, whatever you want to call it—people have been claiming this stuff exists for centuries. Maybe they weren't all lying."

Maya's mind spun. Other people like her. The idea hadn't even occurred to her. She'd been so focused on the immediate horror of her situation, she hadn't thought beyond it.

"How would I even find them?"

"Internet, probably. There are forums for everything. Though you'd have to wade through a lot of conspiracy theorists and people who think they're telepathic because they and their twin both like vanilla ice cream."

"This is insane," Maya said. But she was considering it. Both options: practicing with David, searching for others. They were threads to pull, paths forward. Better than hiding in her apartment until she ran out of money or lost her mind completely.

"Probably," David agreed. "But it's also your reality now. So you can either let it destroy you, or you can figure it out." He shifted, and she heard the scrape of his chair. "I should probably mention—I'm meeting some film school friends at a bar in Williamsburg at nine. You could come. Sit in a corner, keep your eyes down, just be around people. Or not. No pressure."

The thought of a crowded bar made her stomach clench. "I don't think I'm ready for that."

"Fair enough. But the offer stands." He stood. "I'm going to get another coffee. You want anything?"

"I'm good."

She listened to him walk away again, back to the counter. Pulled out her phone and stared at the blank screen. Still nothing from Priya.

Her thumb moved almost of its own accord, opening a browser, typing: *telepathy real experiences forum*.

The results were exactly as David predicted—a mix of New Age websites, skeptic debunking pages, and forums with names like "Psychic Awakening" and "Beyond the Five Senses." She clicked on one at random, skimmed the posts. Someone claiming to read their dog's thoughts. Someone asking if telepathy could work long-distance. Someone describing experiences that sounded like schizophrenia.

Then, buried several pages down, a post that made her pause:

*"Three days ago I woke up and could hear colors. Not synesthesia—I've researched that. Actual sounds coming from colors. Red sounds like brass. Blue like wind chimes. It's driving me insane. Has anyone else experienced sudden sensory changes that seem impossible?"*

Posted two weeks ago. Fourteen replies.

Maya scrolled through them. Most were useless—suggestions to see a doctor, claims it was a spiritual awakening, offers to sell crystals. But one response, from a user named SilentWitness, stood out:

*"Yes. Four months ago for me. Different ability, same sudden onset. I've found seven others so far. We're trying to understand what's happening. If you're real and not trolling, DM me."*

Maya's heart kicked against her ribs. Seven others. Different abilities. Same sudden onset.

David returned with his coffee. "You okay? You look pale."

"I think..." Maya swallowed. "I think maybe I'm not alone."

Maya's fingers trembled as she clicked on SilentWitness's profile. Created four months ago. Sparse post history—mostly replies in obscure corners of forums about unexplained phenomena, always measured, never sensational. The last post was from three days ago, in a thread about spontaneous human combustion: *"Not everything that seems impossible is metaphor."*

"What did you find?" David set his coffee down carefully, away from her phone.

"Someone who says there are others. People who developed abilities suddenly, like mine." Maya read the post aloud, her voice barely above a whisper. The coffee shop seemed to press in around her—too bright, too loud. She was aware of every person in her peripheral vision, careful not to let her gaze drift.

"Are you going to message them?"

"I don't know. What if it's fake? Or what if it's real and they're—I don't know—dangerous somehow?"

David was quiet for a moment. "What if they're just scared, like you?"

Maya stared at the screen. SilentWitness. Seven others. Different abilities. The words felt both impossible and inevitable, like looking at an optical illusion and suddenly seeing the second image hidden in the first.

Before she could second-guess herself, she tapped the message button. Her thumbs hovered over the keyboard. What did you even say? *Hi, I'm telepathic, are you legit?*

She typed: *I think I might be one of you. Started Wednesday. Eye contact = thought transfer. Is this real?*

She hit send before she could delete it, then immediately wanted to throw her phone across the room.

"Done?" David asked.

"Done." Maya set the phone face-down on the table like it might bite her. "They probably won't even respond. Or they'll think I'm crazy. Or I'm wrong about all of this and I really am just having some kind of psychotic break."

"You're not having a psychotic break."

"How would you know?"

"Because you're too self-aware about it. People in psychotic breaks don't usually question whether they're in psychotic breaks." David paused. "My sister went through something like that sophomore year. Before she got diagnosed and found the right meds. You're not—this isn't like that."

Maya wrapped her hands around her now-cold latte. "I'm sorry about your sister. I didn't know."

"She's good now. Better. But it was scary for a while." His fingers drummed that familiar pattern on the table. "Look, I know this is overwhelming. But you found a lead. That's good. That's forward motion."

Her phone buzzed.

Maya's stomach dropped. She flipped it over.

SilentWitness: *Wednesday three days ago?*

Maya: *Yes. October 18th. Late evening. You?*

SilentWitness: *June 16th. Evening. Where are you located?*

Maya hesitated. Every true crime podcast she'd ever listened to screamed not to give her location to an internet stranger. But what choice did she have?

Maya: *NYC.*

SilentWitness: *I'm in Boston. The others are scattered—California, Texas, Oregon, North Carolina, Colorado, Arizona, Vermont. None in NYC yet.*

Maya: *Yet?*

SilentWitness: *There's a pattern. Major cities first, then spreading outward. Population density seems to matter. If you're in NYC and you just manifested, you're probably not the only one there.*

Maya's hands had gone numb. She read the message again, then a third time. "David."

"Yeah?"

"They're saying there are probably others in New York. That there's a pattern. That it started in major cities." She looked up at the brick wall, at the Velvet Underground poster. "What the fuck is happening?"

"What else are they saying?"

Maya: *What kind of pattern? What's causing this?*

SilentWitness: *We don't know yet. But it's not random. Every case we've documented has similar parameters: sudden onset, usually evening hours, no warning signs, no prior history of anything unusual. And it's accelerating. I was one of the first in June. By July there were three of us. By September, eight. If you manifested Wednesday, you might be number twelve or fifteen or twenty. We can't track them all fast enough.*

Maya: *This is insane.*

SilentWitness: *Yes. Are you safe right now? Do you have support?*

Maya glanced at David, who was watching her face with concern. "They're asking if I'm safe. If I have support."

"Tell them yes. Tell them you're not alone."

Maya: *I'm with a friend. I'm managing. But I can't live like this. I can't look at anyone.*

SilentWitness: *Understood. Thought transfer is one of the harder ones to manage initially. But it can be controlled with practice. I can help, or connect you with someone who has similar abilities.*

Maya: *What's your ability?*

A longer pause this time. Maya watched the three dots appear and disappear twice before the response came.

SilentWitness: *I can see the moment someone will die. Not the date—just the final moment. One clear image. It happens the first time I touch them.*

Maya's breath caught. She read the message to David.

"Jesus Christ," he said softly.

Maya: *That's horrible. I'm so sorry.*

SilentWitness: *It was. For a while. But I've learned to manage it. To help people, sometimes, when I can. The knowing is terrible, but the helplessness was worse. Once I accepted it and learned to use it carefully, it became more bearable.*

Maya: *How long did that take?*

SilentWitness: *Months. But thought transfer is different. More controllable, potentially. You're not receiving information, you're broadcasting it. That means you have more agency over the content. The woman in Oregon has something similar—she projects emotions through touch. She learned to modulate it in a few weeks.*

"They're saying I can learn to control it," Maya told David. "That someone else with a similar ability learned in a few weeks."

"See? I told you. Rules. Boundaries. You just have to find them." David leaned forward slightly. "Are you going to ask to meet them? Or talk to the other person, the one in Oregon?"

Maya: *Can you connect me with the woman in Oregon?*

SilentWitness: *I'll reach out to her, but I can't guarantee she'll respond quickly. She's... dealing with her own situation. In the meantime, I can talk you through some exercises. Basic mental discipline techniques that might help. And Maya—don't tell anyone else about this. Not yet. Not until you understand what you're dealing with.*

Maya: *Why not?*

SilentWitness: *Because we don't know what this is yet. Because there are people who would see these abilities as threats, or resources to exploit. Because the fewer people who know right now, the safer we all are. Do you understand?*

A chill ran down Maya's spine. She thought about David sitting across from her, about Priya in Brooklyn, about her mother calling from New Jersey.

Maya: *I've already told two people. My girlfriend and my friend.*

The pause felt ominous.

SilentWitness: *How did they react?*

Maya: *My girlfriend is avoiding me. My friend is here with me now. He's being supportive.*

SilentWitness: *The friend—does he believe you?*

Maya looked at David. At his hands on the table, his familiar slouch, the way he was giving her space while still being present. "Do you believe me?" she asked him. "Really?"

"I believe something is happening to you," David said carefully. "I believe you're not making it up. Whether it's exactly what you think it is or something else, I don't know yet. But I believe you."

Maya: *He believes something is happening to me. He's offered to help me practice controlling it.*

SilentWitness: *Be careful. Document everything. If you practice with him, keep it controlled—short exposures, safe environment, clear boundaries. And Maya? If you start experiencing any other symptoms—physical changes, temporal distortions, reality fluctuations—contact me immediately. This is important.*

Maya: *What kind of symptoms?*

SilentWitness: *Time skips. Seeing things that aren't there or not seeing things that are. Feeling like you're in two places at once. Physical manifestations—unusual marks, temperature changes, electrical interference. It might mean your ability is evolving, or that something else is happening. Two of the others have reported secondary manifestations. We're still trying to understand why.*

Maya's mouth had gone dry. "They're saying some people develop secondary abilities. Or that there are other symptoms—time skips, hallucinations, physical changes."

"Have you had anything like that?"

"No. Just the telepathy thing." But even as she said it, Maya wasn't entirely sure. The past three days had been such a blur of panic and isolation. Had there been moments that felt off? Times when the world seemed to stutter or shift? She'd attributed it to lack of sleep, to stress. But now...

Maya: *Nothing like that so far. Just the thought transfer.*

SilentWitness: *Good. Let's keep it that way. I'm sending you my contact info—a more secure channel than this forum. Signal app. End-to-end encrypted. Download it and message me there. And Maya? Welcome to the impossible. I'm sorry you're part of this, but you're not alone anymore.*

The message included a phone number and a username. Maya screenshot it, then closed the forum.

"They want me to contact them on a secure messaging app," she told David. "They're worried about people finding out."

"Makes sense, if this is real. If there are multiple people developing abilities." David ran a hand through his shaggy hair. "This is some X-Men shit."

"This isn't a comic book, David."

"I know. I know it's not. But maybe..." He trailed off.

"Maybe what?"

"Maybe it's not all bad? I mean, it's terrifying and weird and you didn't ask for it. But if there are others, if you can learn to control it—maybe there's something useful in it too. Maybe there's a reason this is happening."

Maya thought about SilentWitness seeing death with a touch. About the woman in Oregon projecting emotions. About her own thoughts spilling into other people's minds whether she wanted them to or not.

"I can't imagine what the reason would be," she said quietly.

Her phone buzzed again. Not SilentWitness this time. Priya.

*Can we talk? Tomorrow? I've been thinking.*

Maya stared at the message. Three days of silence, and now this. She didn't know if she was relieved or terrified.

Maya: *Yes. Where?*

Priya: *Our apartment? I think we need privacy for this conversation.*

Our apartment. Not your apartment. That had to mean something, didn't it?

Maya: *What time?*

Priya: *Noon?*

Maya: *Okay.*

She set the phone down. Her hands were shaking.

"Priya?" David guessed.

"She wants to talk tomorrow. At the apartment."

"That's good, right? That she wants to talk?"

"I don't know. Maybe she's coming to collect her stuff. Maybe she's going to tell me she can't do this." Maya pressed her palms against her eyes. "God, I can't blame her. Who wants to date someone whose every thought might end up in your head?"

"You don't know that's what she's going to say."

"It's what I would say. It's what any sane person would say."

"Maya." David's voice was gentle. "Priya loved you three days ago. That doesn't just disappear. She's probably terrified too, trying to process something completely impossible. Give her a chance."

"And if she leaves?"

"Then you deal with that when it happens. But you can't preemptively grieve something that hasn't happened yet."

Maya lowered her hands. The coffee shop had emptied out while they talked. It was just them and a barista wiping down tables, casting pointed glances at the clock. Nearly nine.

"I should go," Maya said. "You have that thing in Williamsburg."

"Come with me. Just for an hour. We can sit in the back, you can keep your eyes down. Might be good to be around people, even if you're not interacting."

The thought still made her anxious, but less so than it had an hour ago. She had a lead now. A path forward. Other people who understood.

"Okay," she said. "But if it gets to be too much—"

"We leave. No questions asked." David stood, gathered his jacket. "Come on. Let's try this whole 'existing in the world' thing. Baby steps."

The bar in Williamsburg was called The Wayfarer, a name that promised authenticity and delivered exposed brick, Edison bulbs, and a chalkboard menu of craft cocktails with names like "The Brooklyn Bridge" and "Smoke & Mirrors." Maya kept her head down as they entered, following the scuffed floorboards, letting David guide her with a hand hovering near her elbow—not touching, just present.

"Back corner," he said. "There's a booth."

She navigated by peripheral vision and trust, sliding into cracked leather that exhaled decades of spilled beer and desperate conversations. David sat across from her, angling himself so his face stayed out of her direct line of sight.

"I'm going to grab drinks. What do you want?"

"Whatever. Something strong."

"You got it."

He disappeared into the Friday night crowd. Maya sat with her back to the wall, eyes fixed on the table's surface—carved initials, water rings, a phone number scratched into the wood with something sharp. The noise pressed in from all sides: laughter, arguments, the clink of glasses, indie rock she didn't recognize. Every sound felt like a hand reaching for her, trying to pull her gaze up.

Don't look. Don't look. Don't look.

Her phone buzzed. She pulled it out, grateful for the excuse to focus on something.

Priya: *I'm not breaking up with you. I just need to talk. I miss you.*

Maya read the message three times, each time expecting the words to rearrange into something else. Something worse. But they stayed the same.

She typed: *I miss you too. I'm scared.*

Priya: *Me too. But we'll figure it out. We always do.*

Maya: *This isn't like the time we accidentally adopted that feral cat.*

Priya: *No. But that was also impossible and we managed.*

Despite everything—the telepathy, the fear, the exhaustion—Maya smiled. They had managed. The cat, whose name was Gertrude and who hated everyone except Priya, had eventually been rehomed to Priya's mother of all people. Who'd said that if her daughter could love something that hostile, she'd have to try as well.

Maybe this could be managed too.

David returned with two whiskeys, neat. He set one in front of her carefully, avoiding any chance of their hands touching. "My friends are here. They're cool, but I didn't tell them anything about your situation. Just said you were dealing with some stuff and needed space."

"Thanks."

"They'll probably come say hi at some point. You can keep your eyes down, they won't push."

Maya took a sip of whiskey. It burned beautifully. "What if I accidentally look up?"

"Then you'll project your thoughts at film school graduates who are all definitely thinking about their own problems anyway. These are people who chose documentary work—they're professionally empathetic but also professionally detached. You could probably beam your entire therapy session into their heads and they'd just nod and take notes."

"That's oddly comforting."

"I do what I can." David raised his glass. "To impossible things and old friends."

Maya lifted hers, still not looking at him. "To figuring it out."

They drank. The whiskey settled warm in her stomach, taking the edge off her anxiety. Around them, the bar hummed with the particular energy of a Friday night in Brooklyn—young professionals pretending they weren't tired, artists pretending they weren't broke, everyone pretending the city wasn't slowly crushing them into diamonds or dust.

"So," David said, "while we're here and you're marginally calm—want to try an experiment?"

Maya's grip tightened on her glass. "What kind of experiment?"

"Nothing major. Just... look at me for one second. One count. See if you can control what comes through."

"David—"

"Hear me out. You said SilentWitness mentioned mental discipline techniques, right? That the woman in Oregon learned to modulate her ability. You have to start somewhere. Might as well be with someone who knows what they're signing up for."

Maya's heart was already hammering. "What if I can't control it? What if you get everything?"

"Then I get everything. And I'll tell you what I heard, and we'll know that's the baseline. That's useful information." He paused. "But maybe try thinking about something specific. Something simple. Like... I don't know, a color. Or a word. Focus all your attention on that one thing."

"You make it sound easy."

"I make it sound like practice. Which is what it is." His voice softened. "Look, you're going to see Priya tomorrow. Don't you want to have tried at least once to control this? So you can tell her you're working on it?"

He had a point. If she was going to convince Priya—convince anyone—that this was manageable, she needed to prove she could manage it.

"Okay," Maya said. "But just one second. And if it goes badly—"

"If it goes badly, we stop immediately and get very drunk. Deal?"

"Deal."

Maya set down her glass. Wiped her palms on her jeans. She could feel David settling himself, preparing. The bar noise seemed to recede, or maybe her focus was narrowing to this one moment, this one attempt.

She thought about what to project. A color felt too abstract. A word too loaded with meaning. What was simple? What was harmless?

The Velvet Underground poster from the coffee shop floated into her mind. The banana. Bright yellow against black. Iconic, meaningless, safe.

"Ready?" David asked.

"No. But let's do it anyway."

Maya took a breath and lifted her gaze. For the first time in three days, she looked someone directly in the eyes.

David's face came into focus—the familiar slope of his nose, the small scar on his chin from a skateboarding accident sophomore year, his dark eyes warm with concern and trust. For one heartbeat, two, she held his gaze and thought with every ounce of concentration she possessed: *banana banana banana banana banana—*

David blinked. His expression shifted—confusion, then something like surprised amusement.

"Banana," David said. Then he started laughing. "Just... banana. Over and over. Like a dozen bananas marching through my brain."

"That's what I was trying to project!"

"You controlled it! It was just the one word, clear as day. Nothing else leaked through." David leaned back in the booth. "That's huge. That means you can filter. You can choose."

"It was only for a second. And I was concentrating as hard as I've ever concentrated on anything. I can't walk around thinking 'banana' at everyone all the time."

"No, but you can practice. Build up the mental muscle. Learn to maintain that focus for longer periods, or to switch between different thoughts quickly. It's like meditation, or like learning to play an instrument. The first time is the hardest."

"Yeah? You're sure?"

"Okay. What are you going to think this time?"

"Simple. Good. Ready when you are."

"Three," David confirmed. "Just three. On repeat like a meditation mantra."

"How did it feel? The thoughts coming in?"

"Weird. Like hearing someone whisper directly into my ear, but inside my head instead of outside. Not unpleasant, exactly, just... intimate. Immediate." He tilted his head. "Did it hurt you? To focus like that?"

"That's good though. That's a starting point." David flagged down a passing server, ordered them another round. "Okay, so we know you can control it for a few seconds at a time. Next question: can you maintain eye contact without projecting at all?"

"What do you mean?"

"I mean, can you look at someone and think nothing? Just... empty your mind completely?"
