Mara thought about Dolly standing in the rain, about the three weeks of patience it had taken to earn that single moment of trust. "I understand when they're scared," she said finally. "I know what it looks like when something's been hurt and doesn't want to show it."

Jenny studied her for a long moment, then nodded. "That'll do. Come on."

They walked through the barn, Jenny pointing out each horse—a bay gelding named Murphy who liked to test new handlers, a chestnut mare recovering from a tendon injury, a gray who'd come from a rescue situation and still spooked at sudden movements. Mara filed away each detail, watching how the horses responded to Jenny's presence. Calm. Trusting. The kind of relationship that only came from consistency.

"Your work-study covers fifteen hours a week," Jenny said as they reached the end of the barn. "Morning feeds are at six, evening at five. Stalls need mucking daily. Basic grooming, tack maintenance, whatever else needs doing. The equestrian team practices Tuesday and Thursday afternoons—you'll help tack up, cool down after. Questions so far?"

"Six AM?" Mara's first class wasn't until nine-thirty on Tuesdays and Thursdays, but Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays started at eight.

"Problem with that?"

"No, ma'am." Early mornings she could do. Had been doing since she was old enough to hold a job. It was the other hours—the ones spent in classrooms and dining halls and dorm rooms—that scared her.

"Good." Jenny pulled a key from her pocket and handed it over. "This opens the main barn and the feed room. There's a schedule posted in the office—your shifts are marked in blue. You miss one, you better have a damn good reason and you better call ahead."

"I won't miss."

"Hank said you wouldn't." Jenny's expression softened slightly. "He also said you might need some space sometimes. When it gets overwhelming. There's a tack room in the back barn that nobody uses much—lock's broken but the door closes. You need somewhere quiet, that's yours."

Mara's throat tightened. She wondered how much Hank had told Jenny. Whether he'd mentioned the PTSD, the panic attacks, the history that followed her like a shadow. Whether Jenny was offering refuge out of pity or understanding.

"Thank you," she managed.

"Don't thank me yet. Let's see if you can handle Murphy first." Jenny unlatched a stall door, and a massive bay head appeared, ears pinned back. "He's supposed to be turned out in paddock three. Good luck."

The gelding tried every trick. Pulling back on the lead rope. Shoulder-checking her as they walked. Planting his feet and refusing to move. But Mara had learned from Hank how to stay calm, how to wait out a horse's resistance without fighting it. She stood quiet when Murphy pulled, walked forward when he settled, and thirty minutes later he was grazing peacefully in his paddock.

Jenny was leaning against the fence when Mara returned. "Not bad. Most new students would've lost their temper or their nerve by now."

"I don't have much temper left to lose," Mara said without thinking, then froze. Too honest. Too revealing.

But Jenny just nodded. "The ones who've been broken before, they make the best horse people. They understand that healing isn't linear." She pushed off the fence. "You'll do fine here. Your shifts start Monday—morning feed, six AM. Don't be late."

"I won't."

Walking back across campus, Mara felt steadier than she had since arriving. The stables were her anchor. A place where she didn't have to pretend to be anything other than someone who knew how to work.

Her phone buzzed as she reached the quad. A text from Tasha: *Where'd you go? There's a campus tour at 10 if you want to come!*

Mara checked the time. Nine-fifteen. She should go back to the room, should probably join this tour, should start learning to navigate this place. But the thought of spending two hours in a group of chattering strangers made her skin crawl.

She texted back: *Can't. Have to get my class schedule sorted.*

It wasn't entirely a lie. She did need to figure out her classes, make sure the disability accommodations Sarah had arranged were actually in place. But mostly she needed to not be around people for a while.

The student services building was quieter. A few students clustered around computers, a harried-looking advisor rushing past with an armload of folders, but nothing like the chaos of the dorms. Mara found the disability services office on the second floor and knocked on the half-open door.

"Come in!" A woman looked up from her desk, smiling. "What can I do for you?"

"I'm Mara Chen. I think Dr. Sarah Chen contacted you about accommodations?"

"Oh yes!" The woman—her nameplate read Linda Morrison—pulled a file from her drawer. "We have everything set up. Let's see... you're approved for extended test time, a quiet testing room, and the option to record lectures. Your professors have all been notified." She looked up. "Is there anything else you think you might need?"

Mara shifted her weight, her knee clicking. "What about absences? If I need to... if I can't make it to class sometimes?"

Linda's expression grew more serious. "We can work with you on that, but you'll need to communicate with your professors. Let them know ahead of time when possible, follow up after. Most of them are understanding, but you have to meet them halfway."

"Okay."

"Mara." Linda leaned forward slightly. "I know this can be overwhelming. Dr. Chen mentioned you're dealing with PTSD. Have you connected with the campus counseling center yet?"

"I have a therapist. In Tulsa."

"That's great, but the counseling center here can see you for drop-in sessions if you need them. Might be helpful to have someone local." She scribbled something on a card and handed it over. "Just in case."

Mara took the card, tucked it in her pocket next to Jenny's barn key. She was collecting lifelines like talismans. A key. A card. A baseball cap. Sarah's letters back in her room. Physical reminders that she wasn't completely alone in this.

"Thank you," she said.

"You're going to be okay," Linda said, and Mara wanted to believe her.

By the time she made it to the registrar's office to pick up her official schedule, it was almost noon. The campus had filled with students—walking in groups, sitting under trees with textbooks, tossing footballs across the lawns. Everywhere Mara looked, people seemed to know what they were doing, where they belonged.

She found a bench away from the main pathways and pulled out her schedule. Introduction to Psychology. Composition I. College Algebra. Introduction to Sociology. Freshman Seminar. Eighteen credit hours that suddenly felt like eighteen mountains.

Her phone rang. Sarah.

"How's day two?" Sarah asked without preamble.

"I met the stable manager. She gave me a key and a place to hide when I need to."

"Good. That's exactly what you need—structured work and a refuge. What about classes?"

"They start Monday." Mara watched a squirrel dart up a nearby tree, its movements quick and purposeful. "I picked up my schedule. Disability services is all set up."

"And your roommate?"

"She's nice. Talks a lot. Pre-med."

"That bothering you?"

Mara considered. "Not as much as I thought it would. She's... she doesn't push. When I don't want to talk, she fills the silence herself."

"Sounds like a good match." A pause. "You doing okay? Really?"

Was she? Mara tried to inventory her body the way Sarah had taught her. Heart rate elevated but not panicking. Hands steady. Knee aching from all the walking but manageable. A low-level anxiety humming through her system like background noise, but that was normal now. Maybe always would be.

"I'm scared," she admitted. "But I haven't run yet."

"That's the bar we're setting? Not running?"

"For now, yeah."

Sarah laughed softly. "Fair enough. Small goals. What's next on your agenda today?"

"I don't know." Mara looked around the quad, at all these students who seemed to move through their days with such certainty. "I should probably eat something. Figure out where my classes are so I'm not lost on Monday."

"Both solid plans. And Mara? Call me Monday night. After your first day. I want to hear how it goes."

"Okay."

After they hung up, Mara sat on the bench for another few minutes, gathering her courage. Then she stood and started walking, following the campus map on her phone to each of her classroom buildings. Mapping escape routes without consciously meaning to. Noting which rooms had windows that opened, which had back exits, which were on ground floors where she wouldn't feel trapped.

The psychology building was old brick with high ceilings and wooden floors that creaked. Her classroom was on the second floor, small and intimate—maybe thirty desks arranged in a semicircle. Mara stood in the doorway, imagining it full of students, imagining herself trying to participate in discussions about human behavior when her own mind felt like foreign territory.

Her composition class was in a newer building, the room sterile and fluorescent-lit. College algebra in the math tower, sociology in the same building as psychology. The freshman seminar rotated locations—she'd have to check each week.

By the time she'd mapped everything, her knee was screaming and her anxiety had ratcheted up to a dull roar. She needed to eat, knew she should go to the dining hall, but the thought of navigating that crowd, of eating in public while trying to look normal, was too much.

Instead, she found a convenience store on the edge of campus and bought a protein bar and a bottle of water. Ate standing outside, watching cars pass on Lindsey Street. Old habits. She'd eaten standing up more times than she could count—on street corners, in parking lots, outside shelters when she couldn't make herself go in.

Her phone buzzed. Tasha again: *Pizza in the common room tonight! 6pm! Please come? Dev's bringing his Switch and we're doing Mario Kart tournament!*

Mara stared at the message. She could go back to her room, could hide until Monday when she'd have no choice but to face people. Or she could try. Could show up for one hour of pizza and forced socializing and see if she survived it.

*Maybe*, she typed back, which was at least not a no.

The walk back to Walker Tower felt longer than it should have. Her duffel bag was still mostly packed, her side of the room still bare except for her books lined up on the desk. Tasha's side had exploded with color—posters and photos and string lights that made the space feel lived-in, warm.

Mara lay on her bed and closed her eyes, listening to the sounds of the dorm. Doors opening and closing. Music bleeding through walls. Laughter from down the hall. All of it foreign and familiar at once.

She must have dozed off because when she opened her eyes, the light had changed and her phone showed five forty-five. Fifteen minutes until the pizza thing.

Her first instinct was to text Tasha an excuse. Headache. Tired. Needed to study even though classes hadn't started. But something Sarah had said echoed in her mind: *Connection isn't dangerous—it's what keeps us alive.*

Mara sat up, pulled on Hank's baseball cap, and forced herself to stand before she could talk herself out of it.

The common room was already crowded, the same chaos from the night before. Tasha waved from the couch where she sat next to Dev and two other students Mara didn't recognize.

"You came!" Tasha's smile was genuine, bright. "Grab pizza and come sit! We're about to start the tournament!"

Mara took a slice—pepperoni this time—and perched on the arm of the couch, leaving herself space to escape if needed. Dev handed her a controller.

"You play?" he asked.

"Not really."

"Perfect. Neither does Tasha. You two can team up against me and Jordan."

And somehow, impossibly, Mara found herself playing Mario Kart with three people who didn't know her history, who didn't look at her like she was broken, who just handed her a controller and assumed she belonged.

She lost every race. Didn't care.

prompt: Imagine a story where two loners mistreated at school come up with a shocking scheme to win fame by thwarting a staged crisis—yet each secretly plans to double-cross the other to claim the glory. Alternate chapters through their perspectives to highlight how blame, guilt, and rivalry twist their motivations. Tie in a spring setting with realistic high school details, and pack in a final twist that forces each to confront what heroism really means. Aim for 8,000-10,000 words.

# Part 1: Nate

The cherry blossoms along Ridgemont High's front walk were shedding again, pink petals turning to paste under a thousand footfalls. Nate Holcomb watched them from his usual spot on the concrete bench near the flagpole—close enough to look like he belonged, far enough that no one had to acknowledge him. He'd perfected the art of occupying negative space.

"Move," someone said. Not asked. Commanded.

Nate looked up at Derek Paulson, lacrosse captain, jaw like a marble statue and personality to match. Behind Derek stood the usual constellation: Chase, Marcus, that sophomore girl whose name Nate didn't know but whose laugh could strip paint.

"There's room," Nate said, gesturing to the other end of the bench.

Derek's foot came down on Nate's backpack. The canvas crumpled. Inside, Nate heard the crack of something plastic—probably the case of his scientific calculator, the fancy one his mom had saved up for. The TI-84 Plus CE. Silver edition.

"Was there?" Derek said.

The others laughed. Not because it was funny. Because Derek had granted permission.

Nate stood, shouldered the backpack—now with an interesting new geometry—and walked away. He counted his steps. Twenty-three to the double doors. Twelve more to his locker. Numbers were reliable. Numbers didn't suddenly decide you weren't worth basic human decency.

He was spinning his combination when he felt it: the prickle of observation. Nate turned.

Across the hallway, Julia Vance was staring at him.

Not through him, like most people did. Not past him. At him. Her eyes were this startling gray-green, the color of January ice starting to thaw. She stood with her back against the trophy case, earbuds in, but Nate could tell she wasn't listening to anything. The white cord wasn't even connected to her phone.

They had AP English together. She sat in the back corner. He sat in the front, three seats from the window. In four months of class, he'd heard her speak exactly twice: once to say "here" during attendance, once to ask if she could use the restroom. Mrs. Kowalski had looked surprised both times, like a piece of furniture had developed sentience.

Julia held his gaze for three seconds—Nate counted—then looked away.

The warning bell rang. Bodies surged around him, a river of North Face jackets and iPhone screens and inside jokes he'd never understand. Nate grabbed his AP Calc textbook and headed for first period.

---

Calculus was tolerable because Mr. Reeves actually cared about the subject matter more than the social ecosystem. Today they were reviewing optimization problems. Nate finished the worksheet in eleven minutes and spent the remaining thirty-four reading ahead in the textbook. Related rates. The mathematical relationship between changing quantities. 

How fast is the distance between two people increasing if one walks north at 3 mph and the other walks east at 4 mph?

Answer: 5 mph, by the Pythagorean theorem.

How fast is your social status declining if you exist?

Answer: Nate didn't have the formula for that one yet.

Second period was worse. AP English with Mrs. Kowalski, who thought making them sit in a circle would foster "dynamic discussion." It didn't. It just meant Nate had nowhere to hide when Derek decided to perform.

Today they were discussing *The Great Gatsby*. Mrs. Kowalski had written a prompt on the board: *Is Gatsby a hero or a fool?*

"He's pathetic," Derek announced, leaning back in his chair with the confidence of someone who'd never wanted for anything. "The guy reinvents his whole life for some girl who doesn't even want him. That's stalker behavior."

Several people nodded. Derek had that effect. When he spoke, reality rearranged itself to accommodate him.

"Interesting perspective, Derek," Mrs. Kowalski said in that careful teacher voice that meant *you clearly didn't do the reading*. "Anyone want to offer a counterpoint?"

Silence.

Nate looked down at his annotated copy. He'd filled the margins with notes about the American Dream, self-invention, the corrosive nature of obsession. He'd written a whole paragraph about the green light as a symbol of—

"Nate? Thoughts?"

He felt twenty-three pairs of eyes. No—twenty-two. Julia wasn't looking at him. She was looking at her desk, pen moving across her notebook. Writing something. Her handwriting was small, precise, angry-looking.

"I think," Nate started, then stopped. In the third row, Marcus was grinning. Waiting.

"I think Derek's right," Nate said.

Mrs. Kowalski's face fell slightly. Derek smirked.

The period crawled forward. When the bell finally rang, Nate was first out the door. He made it three steps into the hallway before someone grabbed his shoulder.

"Hey."

Julia. Up close, she was taller than he'd thought. Five-nine, maybe five-ten. She wore an oversized gray hoodie with paint stains on the cuffs and jeans torn at the knees—not fashionably, but genuinely worn through. No makeup. Hair pulled back in a ponytail that looked like she'd done it without a mirror.

"Hi," Nate said, because his brain had apparently resigned.

"You didn't mean that. About Gatsby."

Not a question. A statement.

"I—what?"

"In class. You have that copy marked up like a crime scene. I've seen it. You don't think Gatsby's pathetic."

Nate shifted his backpack. The broken calculator shifted with it. "Does it matter what I think?"

"Maybe not to them," Julia said. "But yeah. To me it does."

She was still looking at him. Really looking. Like he was a puzzle she'd decided to solve.

"Why?" Nate asked.

Julia glanced down the hallway. Derek and his friends were at the water fountain, shoving each other, their laughter echoing off the lockers like artillery fire.

"Because," Julia said slowly, "I've been thinking about something. Something big. And I need someone smart enough to pull it off. Someone invisible enough that no one would suspect." She paused. "Someone with nothing left to lose."

"I don't—"

"Meet me," she interrupted. "After school. The field behind the gym. Four-fifteen."

"Why would I—"

"Because you're tired of being treated like you don't exist," Julia said. "And so am I."

She walked away before Nate could respond.

He stood there, students flowing around him like water around a stone, and wondered what exactly he'd just agreed to.

Nate spent third period Spanish conjugating verbs he'd never use in conversations he'd never have. Señora Martinez droned through subjunctive mood while his mind looped back to the hallway. *Someone invisible enough that no one would suspect.* Suspect of what? Julia Vance had spoken more words to him in two minutes than she'd spoken aloud all semester, and every one of them felt like a fishhook.

Fourth period was AP Chemistry. Nate liked chemistry—the clarity of it, the way substances transformed according to immutable laws. You couldn't negotiate with a chemical reaction. Acids and bases didn't care about your social standing.

"Partner up," Mr. Chen announced. "Titration lab. The procedure's on page ninety-four."

The room erupted into strategic shuffling. Within seconds, everyone had paired off. Everyone except Nate and—he looked around—some kid he didn't recognize. New, probably. Dark skin, wire-frame glasses, wearing a Radiohead shirt that looked like it had survived several wash cycles too many.

"Guess we're together," the kid said, sliding onto the stool next to Nate's. "I'm Marcus. Wait, no—different Marcus. This school apparently has a Marcus quota. I'm Andre."

"Nate."

"Cool." Andre flipped open the textbook. "You any good at this?"

"It's just chemistry."

"That's not an answer, but I'm choosing to interpret it positively." Andre grabbed a buret from the equipment rack. "I transferred three weeks ago. Still figuring out the ecosystem. You've got that look though."

"What look?"

"The look of someone who's mapped every exit and calculated exactly how much of yourself you need to show to get through the day." Andre measured out sodium hydroxide solution with surprising precision. "Takes one to know one."

Nate glanced at him. Andre was setting up the stand, his movements economical and practiced. Not someone who'd floundered through labs before.

"Where'd you transfer from?"

"Lincoln Academy. Got kicked out." Andre said it matter-of-factly, the way someone might mention they'd switched phone carriers. "Long story. Well, short story, actually. I punched the dean's son. Turns out that's frowned upon."

"Why'd you punch him?"

"Because he earned it." Andre picked up the phenolphthalein indicator. "One drop or two for this?"

"Two."

They worked in efficient silence. Andre was good—he understood the math behind the procedure, didn't just follow steps blindly. When they finished early, Mr. Chen looked surprised, then approving.

"You should sit with us at lunch," Andre said as they were cleaning up. "Me and a couple other people. We occupy the table by the emergency exit. Strategically positioned. Good sight lines."

"I usually—" Nate stopped. He usually what? Ate in the library? Hid in the bathroom? "Yeah, okay."

Lunch was a social minefield Nate typically avoided through simple non-participation. But he found himself following Andre to a round table in the corner where two other people sat: a girl with purple streaks in her black hair, hunched over a sketchbook, and a tall Asian kid picking at a sad-looking turkey sandwich.

"Nate, meet the island of misfit toys," Andre announced. "That's Violet. She draws things that make art teachers uncomfortable. And that's James. He's our tragic figure—used to be on the soccer team until he came out and suddenly everyone developed amnesia about his existence."

"Andre tells everyone's trauma within thirty seconds of meeting them," Violet said without looking up from her drawing. It appeared to be a very detailed rendering of an eye crying flowers. "It's his thing."

"Efficiency," Andre corrected, sitting down. "Why waste time?"

James smiled slightly. "You're in my gym class. You're the one who's really good at dodgeball but pretends not to be."

Nate blinked. "You noticed that?"

"I notice things." James took a bite of his sandwich, made a face. "My mom thinks turkey and mayo is a complete food group. Anyway, yeah. You've got good reflexes. You just... make yourself small."

"Survival strategy," Violet said, finally looking up. Her eyes were ringed with smudged eyeliner that might have been intentional or might have been there for three days. "We all have them. Mine's looking weird enough that people don't want to engage. Andre's is oversharing until people get uncomfortable. James's is being so aggressively pleasant that people feel guilty about being assholes."

"Does it work?" Nate asked.

"Not even a little bit," all three said in unison.

Nate felt something unfamiliar in his chest. Not quite belonging—that seemed like too much to hope for—but something adjacent to it. Recognition, maybe.

"So what's your story?" Andre asked, stealing a chip from Violet's bag. She slapped his hand reflexively but didn't stop him. "You're smart, quiet, obviously trying to achieve furniture status. What's the damage?"

Nate thought about Derek's foot on his backpack. The cracked calculator. The thousand small erasures that added up to this: sitting in the negative spaces, counting his way through each day.

"Nothing dramatic," he said. "Just the usual."

"The usual is plenty," James said quietly.

They ate. Talked about nothing important: the chemistry test next week, the absolutely terrible cafeteria pizza, some anime Violet was watching that apparently featured existential robots. Normal things. The kind of conversation Nate had forgotten was possible.

He checked his phone. 12:47. 

Four-fifteen was hours away. The field behind the gym. Julia Vance with her gray-green eyes and her proposal that felt like standing at the edge of something vast and uncharted.

"You okay?" Violet asked. "You just went somewhere else."

"Yeah." Nate forced himself back to the table. "Just thinking."

"Dangerous pastime," Andre said.

"I know."

But he kept thinking about it anyway. Through fifth period American History, where they discussed the Civil War and Nate mentally catalogued all the ways society fractures along invisible lines. Through sixth period Physics, where they calculated trajectories and he wondered about the trajectory of his own life if he showed up at four-fifteen versus if he didn't.

The final bell rang at 3:02.

Nate had one hour and thirteen minutes to decide whether curiosity or self-preservation would win.

He found himself walking toward the field anyway, his feet making the decision his brain couldn't. The gym was at the far end of campus, past the parking lot where Derek's Audi sat gleaming like a statement of intent. The field beyond it was mostly unused—too uneven for proper sports, too visible from the road for anyone wanting real privacy.

Julia was already there, sitting in the bleachers, three rows up. She'd changed clothes since English, now wearing black jeans and a dark blue windbreaker. She looked like she was dressed for something that required mobility.

Nate climbed the bleachers. Metal steps, hollow sound. Fourteen of them.

"You came," Julia said.

"You knew I would."

"No. I hoped." She pulled her knees up, wrapped her arms around them. "But hope and knowledge are different things."

Nate sat down, leaving one seat between them. Safety buffer. "So what is this? What's the big thing you're planning?"

Julia was quiet for a long moment, watching the empty field, the afternoon sun turning everything gold and temporary.

Then she said: "I want to destroy Derek Paulson."

Nate waited for the punchline. It didn't come.

"Destroy," he repeated.

"Socially. Publicly. Completely." Julia's voice was flat, clinical. "I want him to know what it feels like when everyone looks through you like you're glass. When the people who worshipped you yesterday won't make eye contact today."

"That's..." Nate searched for the right word. "Ambitious."

"It's necessary."

A breeze kicked up, carrying the smell of cut grass and the distant sound of the marching band practicing something that might have been Beethoven but sounded like a traffic accident. Nate studied Julia's profile. Her jaw was set in a way that suggested this wasn't a passing fantasy. This was a blueprint.

"Why Derek specifically?" Nate asked. "I mean, there's a whole ecosystem of assholes at this school."

Julia turned to look at him. Her eyes had gone cold—that gray-green shifting toward something glacial. "You know what he did last week? Probably not. It wasn't to you, so why would you?"

Nate didn't answer.

"There's this freshman girl. Emma Chen. She's fourteen. Plays violin in orchestra, gets straight A's, smiles at everyone." Julia's hands tightened on her knees. "She made the mistake of having a crush on Derek. Posted something on her private Instagram about thinking he was cute. One of her friends screenshotted it, sent it to him as a joke."

"Christ."

"He printed it out. Put copies in her locker, taped them to her orchestra folder, posted it in the boys' locker room with the caption 'when the nerds get thirsty.' She found out when half the lacrosse team started making slurping noises whenever she walked past." Julia's voice hadn't risen, but something underneath it had sharpened. "She hasn't been back to school in four days. Her parents think she has the flu."

Nate felt something twist in his stomach. He'd seen versions of this before—smaller cruelties, different victims. The ecosystem of Ridgemont High ran on humiliation the way engines ran on gasoline.

"That's horrible," he said, knowing the words were insufficient.

"Horrible is common. Derek's special. He's got this gift for finding exactly where someone's vulnerable and just—" Julia made a pressing motion with her thumb, like crushing an insect. "He does it because he can. Because there are no consequences. Because people like him don't face consequences."

"And you want to change that."

"I want to create consequences so spectacular that everyone remembers them." Julia pulled out her phone, opened the notes app, turned it so Nate could see. "I've been planning this for six weeks. Every detail. Every contingency."

Nate leaned closer. The screen was filled with text—dense paragraphs, numbered lists, what looked like a timeline. He caught fragments: *assembly schedule*, *principal's office access*, *social media coordination*, *emergency protocols*.

"What exactly are you planning?"

Julia pulled the phone back. "A crisis. Fake, but believable. Something that requires a hero. And Derek's going to be that hero—except he won't know it's coming, won't know what he's supposed to do, won't be able to pull it off."

"I don't understand."

"You will." Julia stood, paced to the edge of the bleachers. "Here's the premise: we stage an event. Something scary enough that everyone's watching, that the whole school's talking about it. In that moment, Derek gets the perfect opportunity to be the big man, save the day, prove he's everything he pretends to be."

"But he won't be able to."

"Exactly. Because we'll have engineered it so that what looks like a heroic moment is actually impossible for him to navigate. He'll freeze, or fail, or make the wrong choice. And everyone will see it. The whole school. Maybe more than that, if we do it right." Julia turned back to face him. "People like Derek survive on image. On everyone believing they're special. You crack that image publicly enough, completely enough, and it doesn't repair."

Nate's mind was already working through the logic, finding the holes. "Okay, but staging a crisis at a school—that's going to bring in authorities. Real ones. Police, probably. How do you control that?"

"Carefully," Julia said. "That's where you come in."

"Me."

"You're smart. Like, actually smart, not just good-at-memorizing-things smart. I've watched you in calc, in chemistry. You see systems. You understand how things work." She sat back down, closer this time, close enough that Nate could see the faint scar on her left eyebrow. "I can plan the broad strokes. But I need someone who can handle the details. The timing. The technical aspects. Someone who can think three moves ahead."

"You're describing a chess game where the pieces are real people."

"Yes."

The simplicity of her answer was somehow more unsettling than if she'd tried to justify it.

Nate looked out at the field. A crow landed on the far goalpost, cocked its head like it was listening. He thought about Derek's foot on his backpack. The casual cruelty. The way people like Derek moved through the world as if other people were just NPCs in their personal video game.

He thought about Emma Chen, fourteen years old, hiding at home because someone had weaponized her vulnerability.

"What kind of crisis?" he asked.

Julia smiled. It wasn't a happy expression. "I'm thinking fire alarm. Maybe something with the auditorium—we could rig it to look like there's smoke, sparks from the lighting booth. Nothing that would actually hurt anyone, but enough that it looks real. Enough that evacuation procedures kick in."

"And Derek?"

"Derek will be positioned perfectly. Maybe he's near the auditorium when it starts. Maybe we arrange for him to be the first one to see the 'emergency.' He'll have the chance to alert people, to help, to be the hero everyone already thinks he is." Julia's eyes glinted. "Except we'll have built in complications. Things that require actual quick thinking, actual courage, actual care for other people. Things Derek doesn't have."

"You're assuming he'll fail."

"I'm guaranteeing it. Because I know him. I've studied him. Derek performs when there's a script, when there's a clear way to win. Chaos? Real pressure? He falls apart."

Nate wanted to ask how she knew that. But there was something in the way Julia said *I've studied him* that suggested layers he wasn't ready to excavate yet.

"This is insane," he said.

"Yes."

"We could get expelled. Arrested, maybe."

"Only if we get caught."

"The odds of not getting caught—"

"Are better than you think. Because we're invisible, remember? Nobody's watching us. Nobody suspects the furniture of planning anything." Julia leaned forward. "Look, I get it. You're calculating risk versus reward, running probability scenarios in your head. That's good. I need that. But here's what you should factor in: what's your life look like if nothing changes? If you just keep counting steps and hiding in negative space until graduation?"

Nate didn't answer. He didn't have to.

"Exactly," Julia said. "So maybe this is insane. Maybe it's risky. But at least it's *something*. At least we'd be the ones writing the story for once instead of just being extras in theirs."

The crow took off from the goalpost, wings beating against the golden air. Nate watched it disappear over the tree line.

"When?" he asked.

Julia's expression shifted—not quite a smile, but something like recognition. Like he'd just passed a test he hadn't known he was taking.

"Spring assembly. Three weeks from Friday. That's when we make Derek Paulson's world fall apart."

Nate walked home with Julia's words looping through his mind like a corrupted file. *Three weeks from Friday.* The spring assembly was a Ridgemont tradition—the kind of manufactured school spirit event where the principal gave a speech about excellence, the lacrosse team got paraded across the stage for their regional wins, and everyone pretended to care about announcements for prom committee signups.

Perfect theater for a catastrophe.

His house was a forty-minute walk from school, longer if he took the route through Riverside Park to avoid the main roads where Derek's friends sometimes congregated. Today he went the long way, needing the time to think.

The calculator in his backpack rattled with each step. He still hadn't looked at the damage. Something about Schrodinger's calculator—as long as he didn't check, it was both broken and fine, and he could postpone the conversation with his mom about how he'd need a new one, and the look on her face when she'd have to figure out where the money would come from.

*We'd be the ones writing the story for once.*

Nate turned onto Maple Street, where the houses got smaller and the lawns less manicured. His neighborhood didn't have a name, wasn't part of any planned development. Just a collection of rental properties and aging ranches that predated the McMansions spreading through the rest of the district.

His mom's Toyota was in the driveway. She wasn't supposed to be home until six—she worked reception at a dental office across town—but there it was, slightly crooked like she'd parked in a hurry.

Nate found her at the kitchen table, laptop open, calculator out. Not his broken one. The cheap solar-powered one she used for household budgets.

"Hey," he said.

She looked up, and he saw the tension in her shoulders ease slightly. "Hey yourself. How was school?"

"Fine." The universal answer. The one that required no follow-up.

"You eat lunch?"

"Yeah."

She nodded, turned back to the laptop screen. Excel spreadsheet, rows of numbers in red and black. Nate recognized the layout—he'd helped her set it up last year when she was trying to organize their finances. Income in one column, expenses in another, the third column showing the gap between them.

The gap was always there. Sometimes smaller, never gone.

"Everything okay?" Nate asked.

"Just juggling." She clicked through to another tab. "Water heater's making that noise again. Guy says it needs replacing, not just a repair. I'm trying to figure out if we can make it through summer or if it's going to die spectacularly and flood the basement."

Nate set his backpack down carefully, the broken calculator shifting inside. "How much?"

"For you to worry about? Zero dollars." She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "That's my department."

He knew better than to push. His mom had pride in strange places—she'd accept help with homework, with changing the oil in her car, with fixing the garbage disposal. But money conversations were fortified territory.

"I'm gonna do homework," Nate said.

"There's leftover pasta in the fridge if you want a snack."

Upstairs, his room was exactly as he'd left it: bed made with hospital corners, desk clear except for his laptop and a pencil cup, bookshelf organized by subject and then alphabetically by author. His mom called it neat. His old therapist—back when they could afford therapy—had called it controlled.

Nate sat at his desk and pulled out the calculator. The screen was cracked, a spiderweb pattern radiating from the lower right corner. But when he pressed the power button, it flickered to life. Numbers still displayed. Functions still worked.

Broken but functional. He understood the feeling.

His phone buzzed. Unknown number.

**It's Julia. Got your number from the school directory. Don't freak out.**

Too late. He was already cataloguing how she'd accessed the directory, whether that was password-protected, what other information was in there.

**Just wanted to send you something. Check your email.**

Nate switched to his laptop, refreshed his inbox. New message from an address he didn't recognize: tempaccount4728@protonmail.com.

The email had no subject line. No body text. Just a PDF attachment: RIDGEMONT_PLANS_v3.pdf.

He hesitated, cursor hovering over the file. Opening it felt like crossing a threshold. Before, Julia's plan had been abstract, something that existed only in her notes app and in the theoretical space of conversation. This would make it concrete.

Nate clicked.

The document opened to reveal twenty-seven pages of detailed planning. Julia hadn't been exaggerating when she said she'd been working on this for six weeks. This was architectural in its precision.

**Page 1: OVERVIEW**

*Objective: Stage a controlled crisis during spring assembly that positions Derek Paulson as potential hero, structured to ensure public failure, resulting in permanent reputational damage.*

*Key principles:*
- *No actual danger to students or staff*
- *Plausible deniability for planners*
- *Maximum visibility for target's failure*
- *Minimal exposure window for detection*

**Page 2: VENUE ANALYSIS**

A floor plan of the auditorium, marked with exits, sight lines, camera positions. Julia had noted which cameras were functional (six) and which were decorative only (three). She'd mapped the fire alarm pull stations, the emergency lighting, the locations of fire extinguishers.

**Page 3-5: PERSONNEL**

Lists of who would be where during the assembly. Principal Chen on stage. Vice Principal Rodriguez in the tech booth. Security guard (singular—budget cuts last year) stationed at main entrance. Teachers distributed throughout the seating area in their usual spots, which Julia had observed and documented across multiple events.

And Derek. Third row center, where the lacrosse team always sat. Where everyone would see him when the moment came.

**Page 6-8: THE EVENT**

This was where it got specific. Julia had outlined a scenario involving the lighting booth—old equipment, history of minor malfunctions, plausible that something could spark. Smoke machines from the theater department's storage, carefully positioned. A small pyrotechnic device, the kind used in theater productions, rigged to produce flash and sound but no actual flame.

Someone would need to access the booth beforehand. Set everything up. Ensure the timing device was accurate. Then be far away when it activated.

**Page 9: THE TEST**

*This is the critical moment. When the smoke appears, when the alarm sounds, when everyone's looking around trying to understand what's happening—that's when Derek has his opportunity.*

*If he's the person everyone thinks he is, he'll:*
- *Stay calm*
- *Help coordinate evacuation*
- *Check for people who need assistance*
- *Be the leader, the hero, the guy who deserves every accolade he's ever received*

*But we ensure he can't succeed by:*

The list went on. Complications built into the scenario. A jammed door that would require problem-solving, not just strength. A student (planted) who would need actual emotional intelligence to calm down. Situations that required Derek to be something other than what he was.

Nate kept reading. Page after page of contingencies, backups, exit strategies. Julia had thought about what happened if someone noticed them acting suspicious. What happened if the device failed to trigger. What happened if Derek somehow succeeded anyway.

For that last scenario, she'd written: *Unlikely, but if target succeeds despite parameters, we adjust narrative post-event. Plant questions about whether he knew in advance. Seed doubt. Failure of event doesn't mean failure of objective.*

His phone buzzed again.

**Well?**

Nate stared at the message. The cursor blinked in the PDF, waiting on page fifteen: ROLE ASSIGNMENTS.

Under "Technical Specialist" she'd written: *Nate Holcomb—device setup, timing calibration, sight line verification, emergency abort protocols.*

Under "Coordinator" she'd listed herself: *Scene management, crowd psychology, narrative control, documentation.*

Documentation. He flipped ahead to page twenty-three.

**EVIDENCE CAPTURE**

*Multiple recording devices, positioned to capture:*
- *Target's initial reaction*
- *Target's failure to manage situation effectively*
- *Bystander reactions to target's failure*
- *Overall chaos and target's inability to navigate it*

*Video to be edited and distributed via:*
- *Anonymous social media accounts (pre-established)*
- *Student group chats (via intermediaries)*
- *Local news tip line (if event warrants coverage)*

She wanted to film it. To create a permanent record of Derek's humiliation. To make sure it spread everywhere, unstoppable, viral.

Nate's hands had gone cold.

His phone: **I know it's a lot. But look at page 26.**

He jumped ahead.

**JUSTIFICATION**

*Derek Paulson has tormented dozens of students over four years. School administration knows and does nothing because he's valuable—good grades, sports success, parents donate to the booster club. The usual power structures protect him.*

*This plan bypasses those structures. It creates a moment of objective reality that can't be spin-doctored or covered up. Either he rises to the occasion or he doesn't. Either he's the person everyone thinks he is or he's not.*

*We're not hurting him. We're revealing him.*

Below that, in smaller text:

*And maybe we're proving that invisible people can change the story. That we have power too, if we're willing to use it.*

Nate closed the laptop.

Downstairs, he could hear his mom on the phone, her voice with that particular brightness she used when talking to creditors. "I understand the due date, but if I could just explain the situation..."

He looked at his broken calculator. At his neat room with its organized shelves and controlled surfaces. At the window overlooking Maple Street, where nothing ever changed and nothing ever would unless someone made it change.

His phone buzzed one more time.

**You don't have to decide now. Think about it. But Nate—we could do this. We could actually do this.**

**Three weeks.**

He set the phone face-down on his desk and pulled out his calculus homework. Related rates. The speed at which things changed when other things changed. How one variable's transformation affected another.

He worked through the problems mechanically, his handwriting precise and controlled. But his mind was elsewhere, running calculations of a different kind.

Probability of success. Probability of getting caught. Probability of changing anything at all.

Probability that invisible people stayed invisible unless they set something on fire.

Nate didn't sleep that night. He lay in bed watching headlights sweep across his ceiling as cars passed on Maple Street, each one a small calculation: sedan going 28 mph, SUV at 35, that one guy who always sped through at 50.

At 2:47 AM, he got up and opened his laptop again.

The PDF was still there, waiting. He read it start to finish this time, not skimming, actually processing every detail. Julia's planning was meticulous, almost obsessive. She'd accounted for variables he wouldn't have considered—the way crowd psychology worked, how authority figures responded to chaos, the exact phrasing needed to make an anonymous tip sound credible without sounding coached.

But there were gaps. Technical gaps that made him itch.

The pyrotechnic device she'd described—a standard theatrical flash pot—required specific timing. Too early and people would have time to think, to question. Too late and the moment would pass, Derek would already be positioned somewhere else. The window was maybe thirty seconds wide.

And the smoke machines. Theater department had two: an older 400-watt unit that tended to overheat, and a newer 1000-watt model that produced dense fog but went through fluid fast. Julia's plan called for sustained smoke, enough to create genuine alarm. That meant the 1000-watt. Which meant someone would need to refill it halfway through, or set up both machines in sequence, or—

Nate caught himself. He was problem-solving. He was already designing solutions.

He closed the laptop.

At school the next day, Julia didn't approach him. Didn't look at him in English when Mrs. Kowalski tried to generate enthusiasm for *The Sun Also Rises*. Didn't exist in his peripheral vision at all.

It was deliberate. Nate recognized the strategy: don't create patterns, don't let anyone notice an association forming. Smart.

But at lunch, when he sat down at the corner table with Andre, Violet, and James, his phone buzzed.

**North stairwell. 3rd floor. 1:15.**

He checked the time. Twelve minutes.

"You okay?" James asked. "You look like you're calculating something stressful."

"Final exam probabilities," Nate lied.

"Dude, it's April. Finals are weeks away." Andre stole one of Violet's carrot sticks. She didn't even look up from her sketchbook this time, just moved the bag closer to him in implicit permission. "You need to recalibrate your anxiety timeline."

"Can't. It's pre-loaded at factory settings."

Violet snorted. "That's actually pretty good. You're getting better at this."

"At what?"

"Talking to humans. First day you barely made eye contact. Now you're doing bits." She finally looked at him, one eyebrow raised. "Character development."

Nate felt something warm in his chest, immediately followed by something cold. These people were becoming friends. Real ones, maybe. And he was sitting here planning something that, if it went wrong, could blow up not just his life but his ability to have this—this small, imperfect corner of belonging.

"I need to go to the library," he said abruptly. "Calc homework."

"We have fifteen minutes," Andre pointed out.

"Yeah. I'm slow." Nate grabbed his backpack, nearly knocked over James's water bottle, caught it, set it down too carefully. Smooth.

He made it to the north stairwell at 1:14. The third floor was mostly empty—just admin offices and the guidance counselors, who were probably at lunch. Julia was already there, sitting on the steps between floors, laptop balanced on her knees.

"You read it," she said. Not a question.

"Yeah."

"And?"

Nate sat down two steps below her, angled so he could see if anyone approached from either direction. "The pyrotechnics are wrong."

Julia's expression didn't change. "Wrong how?"

"Flash pots are too unpredictable. You're thinking about them like they're just bigger sparklers, but they're not. Temperature variance, humidity, even air pressure affects burn rate. Your thirty-second window? More like fifteen to forty-five seconds. Maybe."

"So we calibrate—"

"Can't. Not without testing. And testing means someone notices you checking out theatrical explosives and wonders why." Nate pulled out his phone, opened the notes app. "You need something electronic. Programmable. Something where the timing is precise to the second."

Julia leaned forward. "Like what?"

"Arduino board. Simple circuit. You can control output digitally, trigger multiple effects in sequence. Wire it to LED strips for the spark effect—way safer than actual pyrotechnics, looks the same from a distance. Run the smoke machines on the same circuit so everything syncs." His thumbs moved across the screen, sketching out a basic schematic. "Total cost maybe sixty dollars. RadioShack closed but there's a hobby shop on Fletcher Street."

"You've thought about this."

"I thought about the problems with your plan. This is just solving them."

"Right." Julia's lips twitched. Almost a smile. "Just solving them."

They sat in silence for a moment. Below them, a door opened and closed. Footsteps, then nothing.

"I have questions," Nate said.

"Okay."

"Why Derek? I mean, specifically. You gave me the Emma Chen story, and that's—yeah, that's awful. But you started planning six weeks ago. That happened last week."

Julia closed her laptop. "You're wondering if it's personal."

"Is it?"

"Does it matter?"

"Yeah. It does." Nate turned to face her fully. "Because if this is about revenge for something he did to you, that's different than what you said. That's not revealing him. That's just—I don't know. Settling a score."

Julia's jaw tightened. For a moment Nate thought she'd shut down, walk away, leave him sitting on the stairs with his questions unanswered.

Then she said, "Sophomore year. There was this guy. Tyler. He was—" She stopped, started again. "We were friends. Good friends. He was gay and not out yet, and I was basically invisible, so we made sense together. We'd have lunch in the art room, talk about colleges we wanted to go to, normal stuff."

"What happened?"

"Derek found out. I don't know how—someone saw us, someone said something. Doesn't matter. What matters is Derek decided it would be funny to start a rumor that Tyler and I were dating. That Tyler was secretly straight and just pretending to like guys for attention." Julia's voice stayed level, but her hands had curled into fists. "Tyler tried to deny it. That made it worse. Derek kept pushing, kept making jokes. Started calling him 'confused' in front of everyone."

Nate waited.

"Tyler transferred out second semester. Told his parents he wanted to go to private school, better college prep. Really he just wanted to be anywhere else." Julia looked at her fists like she was surprised to find them there, deliberately unclenched her fingers. "He texts me sometimes. He's doing okay now. But he shouldn't have had to leave. Derek should have faced something. Anything."

"But he didn't."

"No one ever does. That's how it works. People like Derek operate with complete immunity because everyone's too afraid to challenge them, or too invested in keeping things stable, or they just don't care enough about the people getting hurt." She met Nate's eyes. "So yeah. It's personal. It's also bigger than that. Both things can be true."

A bell rang somewhere. 1:25. Five minutes until next period.

"I need to know you're really in this," Julia said. "Not just intellectually interested. Not just solving the puzzle because it's there. Actually committed."

"And if I'm not?"

"Then I'll find another way. It won't be as good. But I'll do it anyway." She stood, slinging her laptop bag over her shoulder. "You've got until Friday to decide. That gives us two and a half weeks if you're in. Any longer and the timeline gets too compressed."

She started down the stairs.

"Julia."

She stopped, turned back.

"The Arduino solution. You'd need someone who knows basic circuit design. Who can program the trigger sequence. Who can test it without anyone noticing." Nate stood slowly. "That's specialized knowledge. You can't just YouTube that."

"I know."

"So you already knew you needed me. Before you read my notes on Gatsby. Before you knew anything about me." He watched her face. "You've been watching me longer than four months, haven't you?"

Julia held his gaze for three seconds. Four. Five.

"Yeah," she said finally. "Since freshman year. Since you gave that presentation in biology about neural pathways and I realized you see things the way I do. Systems. Patterns. Cause and effect." She shrugged. "I've been planning this for a long time, Nate. Finding the right person to help—that was part of the plan too."

She left.

Nate stood in the stairwell, listening to her footsteps fade. Somewhere above him, a door opened. The voice of Mrs. Chen, the principal: "—need those numbers by Thursday, not Friday, that's when the superintendent—"

He headed down instead of up, taking the stairs two at a time, not entirely sure if he was running toward something or away from it.

Friday arrived faster than Nate expected. He spent the intervening days in a state of split consciousness—half of him going through normal school routines, the other half running constant simulations of Julia's plan, finding flaws, designing fixes, creating backups for the backups.

He didn't seek her out. Didn't send any messages. But he researched.

The hobby shop on Fletcher Street had Arduino starter kits for forty-eight dollars. LED strips rated for theatrical use were another fifteen. The smoke machines were already in the theater department's inventory—he'd casually asked Mr. Reeves during Physics if the school ever did equipment audits, and learned that nobody tracked that stuff between shows.

He wasn't committing. He was just gathering information. There was a difference.

Thursday night, his mom knocked on his door around nine.

"Yeah?"

She opened it halfway. "You doing okay? You've been quiet this week. I mean, more than usual."

"Just homework. Big calculus test Monday."

"Right." She didn't look convinced. "Listen, if something's going on—if someone's bothering you at school or—"

"Mom. I'm fine."

"Okay." She lingered in the doorway. "The water heater guy came today. Turns out we can patch it for now. Bought us maybe six months."

"That's good."

"Yeah." She smiled, and this time it almost reached her eyes. "Small victories. Anyway. Don't stay up too late."

After she left, Nate pulled up the PDF again. Page fourteen: RISK ASSESSMENT.

Julia had listed the potential consequences in order of severity. At the top: *Expulsion, possible criminal charges for creating false emergency.*

Below that: *Permanent record damage, college application impact.*

Below that: *Social consequences, loss of current relationships.*

At the bottom, in italics: *Probability of meaningful change if we do nothing: 0%.*

Nate closed his laptop and lay back on his bed. His room was dark except for the blue glow of his alarm clock. 9:47 PM.

In thirteen hours, he had to give Julia an answer.

His phone buzzed. Andre, in the group chat that had somehow formed over the week:

**violet found a video of a cat playing piano and i think it's better than mozart. discuss.**

James: **That's not even controversial. Obviously better.**

Violet: **you people have no culture**

**the cat is named Mittens btw**

**MITTENS**

Nate smiled despite himself, typed: **Does Mittens take requests?**

Andre: **HE EMERGES**

**THE SILENT ONE SPEAKS**

Violet: **shut up andre he's been talking all week**

**nate you coming to james's thing tomorrow?**

Nate stared at the message. **What thing?**

James: **oh i forgot to mention. my parents are out of town this weekend. was gonna order pizza, watch bad movies. very low-key. you should come.**

**if you want**

**no pressure**

Andre: **PRESSURE**

**SIGNIFICANT PRESSURE**

**ATMOSPHERIC LEVELS OF PRESSURE**

Violet: **andre i swear to god**

Normal. This was normal. Friends making plans, including him in those plans, assuming he'd want to be there. The kind of normal Nate had stopped expecting freshman year.

**yeah okay. what time?**

James: **7ish? i'll text you the address**

He set his phone down. Looked at the closed laptop. Thought about Friday at 7 PM, eating pizza and watching bad movies with people who'd somehow decided he was worth including.

Thought about Friday at 3:30, meeting Julia with his answer.

Two different versions of who he could be. Two different trajectories.

The math should have been simple.

It wasn't.

Friday's calculus test was a disaster. Not because the material was hard—Nate could do related rates in his sleep—but because his mind kept sliding off the numbers on the page and into other calculations entirely.

Probability of successful Arduino deployment: high, maybe 85%, assuming he could test the circuit twice before the assembly.

Probability of tracing the setup back to him: low, maybe 15%, if he was careful about purchase locations and timing.

Probability of living with himself if he did nothing: declining asymptotically toward zero.

He turned in the test with three problems blank. Mr. Reeves looked surprised.

By lunch, Nate's stomach was twisted into configurations that defied geometry. He sat at the corner table with Andre, Violet, and James, moving french fries around his tray in patterns.

"You're doing the thing again," Violet said, not looking up from her sketchbook. Today she was drawing what appeared to be a mechanical heart with gears for ventricles.

"What thing?"

"The thing where you're physically present but mentally doing advanced mathematics about something stressful." She added shading to one of the gears. "You've got a tell. Your left eye twitches."

Nate forced himself to eat a fry. It tasted like cardboard and regret.

"Big plans this weekend?" Andre asked. "Besides the James pizza extravaganza, obviously."

"Not really."

"Liar," Violet said mildly. "But okay. We all have secrets."

Nate looked at her sharply, but she was still focused on her drawing, expression neutral. Just an observation. Nothing loaded underneath it.

Except maybe there was. Maybe Violet saw more than she let on. Maybe they all did.

"I might have a thing," Nate said carefully. "Before tonight. Something I need to decide about."

James set down his sandwich. "Good decision or bad decision?"

"Don't know yet."

"Here's a thought experiment," Andre said, leaning back in his chair. "Future you, six months from now, looks back at today. Which version of you is he more disappointed in—the one who did the thing or the one who didn't?"

"That's not how decisions work," Nate said.

"Sure it is. Every choice is just future regret or future relief. You're just picking which one you can live with better." Andre stole another carrot stick from Violet's bag. She'd started packing extra. "Like when I punched the dean's son. Future me knew I'd either regret the punch or regret the not-punching. The math said punching was less regrettable."

"You got expelled."

"Yeah, and I sleep fine. Would've hated myself more if I'd just stood there." Andre shrugged. "I'm not saying it was smart. I'm saying it was necessary."

Violet finally looked up. "Sometimes the smart choice and the necessary choice aren't the same thing. Sometimes you have to pick."

Her eyes met Nate's. Gray, steady, knowing something she wasn't saying.

The bell rang. Everyone stood, gathered bags, merged into the hallway traffic. Nate felt James's hand on his shoulder, brief but deliberate.

"Whatever it is," James said quietly, "you're allowed to choose yourself. That's not selfish. That's just survival."

Then he was gone, swept into the crowd, and Nate was left standing by the emergency exit with equations he couldn't solve.

Three-thirty came too fast and too slow simultaneously.

Nate found Julia in the same place as before: the bleachers behind the gym, three rows up. But today she wasn't alone. There was a laptop open beside her and a small cardboard box at her feet.

He climbed the metal steps. Fourteen of them. Same hollow sound.

"You're early," Julia said.

"You're prepared." Nate nodded at the box. "What is that?"

"Materials. Just in case." She didn't ask the question. Didn't say *well?* or *what did you decide?* Just waited.

Nate sat down, leaving the usual gap between them. The afternoon sun was sharp and clinical, turning everything into high contrast. No shadows to hide in.

"I have conditions," he said.

Julia's expression shifted. Not quite relief, but something close. "Okay."

"First: no one gets hurt. And I mean actually hurt, not just theoretically safe. I want redundancies. I want failsafes. I want an abort switch that either of us can hit if something goes wrong."

"Agreed. What else?"

"Second: we document everything. Not just the event—the planning, the setup, all of it. If this goes wrong and we get caught, I want evidence that we thought it through, that we tried to minimize harm. It won't keep us out of trouble, but it might keep us out of jail."

Julia pulled out her phone, started typing notes. "Makes sense. What else?"

"Third: you tell me the real reason. Not Derek and Tyler. Not Emma Chen. The actual, personal, specific thing that made you start planning this six weeks ago."

Julia's fingers stopped moving. "I gave you—"

"You gave me context. Background. Justification." Nate turned to face her fully. "But I've been watching you too, this week. The way you look at your phone sometimes, like you're expecting a message that won't come. The way you flinch when someone walks too close in the hallway. Something happened. Something recent."

Silence. A bird called from somewhere across the field. The distant sound of lacrosse practice—whistles, shouting, the hollow thwack of balls hitting sticks.

"Six weeks ago," Julia said finally, "my dad left. Just—packed a bag and left. Turns out he'd been having an affair with someone from his work. Someone younger, obviously, because that's how the cliché goes."

Nate waited.

"My mom fell apart. Like, completely. Couldn't get out of bed for a week. I had to call her boss, make excuses, forge her signature on my permission slips, figure out which bills got paid first." Julia's voice stayed flat, clinical. "And at school, nobody noticed. Or maybe they noticed and didn't care. Either way, I was just—I was handling a catastrophe alone while everyone around me worried about prom dresses and AP scores."

"That's not about Derek."

"It's about systems that don't work. Adults who are supposed to protect you but don't. Rules that only apply to some people. The way everything just continues functioning on the surface while underneath, everything's falling apart." She looked at Nate. "Derek's part of that system. He's the visible manifestation of how broken it all is. And if I can't fix my parents' marriage or make my mom okay or make any of the big things better, at least I can fix this. This one thing. I can make one person face consequences in a world that's decided consequences are optional."

"That's not fixing anything. That's just—"

"Revenge. I know." Julia's smile was bitter. "But revenge with a purpose. Revenge that means something. Revenge that maybe makes fourteen-year-old Emma Chen feel less alone, or makes Tyler feel like someone finally fought back, or just proves that invisible people can become visible when they choose to be."

The sun was lower now, painting the field in amber. Nate could see Derek across the grass, lacrosse stick in hand, demonstrating something to the sophomore players. Perfect form. Natural leadership. Everyone watching, everyone learning, everyone believing.

"Fourth condition," Nate said.

"There's a fourth?"

"We're honest with each other. Completely. Because if we do this, we're trusting each other with everything. Our futures, basically. And I need to know that whatever else happens, you're not lying to me."

Julia extended her hand. "Deal."

Nate shook it. Her grip was firm, steady, cool against his palm.

"So," Julia said, pulling the cardboard box closer. "Let's talk technical specifications."

They spent the next ninety minutes in the bleachers, heads bent over Julia's laptop, sketching circuits and timing sequences. She'd done more research than Nate expected—found schematics for theatrical effect controllers, read up on smoke machine fluid types, even calculated the acoustic properties of the auditorium to figure out optimal placement for maximum psychological impact.

"You could've done this without me," Nate said at one point.

"No. I could've done a version of this. Probably a worse one." Julia pulled up a new document. "But I needed someone who thinks in systems. Who sees the gaps I miss. You made this better in five minutes than I could've made it in five weeks."

"That's not—"

"Take the compliment, Nate."

He didn't know how to respond to that, so he went back to the schematic. Added a secondary circuit with a manual cutoff. Redundancy on redundancy.

By five o'clock, they had a plan. A real one. Not Julia's ambitious outline but a detailed technical blueprint with contingencies and abort protocols and carefully calculated risk factors.

It felt more real than anything Nate had ever built.

"I need to go," he said, checking his phone. "Thing tonight."

"The pizza thing with your new friends?"

Nate froze. "How did you—"

"I pay attention." Julia closed her laptop. "That's good, by the way. That you found people. Makes you less suspicious if anyone starts wondering why you're suddenly less invisible."

"Everything's tactical with you."

"Not everything. But yeah, most things." She stood, stretched. "Go. Eat pizza. Watch bad movies. Be normal for a night. We start building Monday."

Nate grabbed his backpack, started down the bleachers, then stopped on the fifth step.

"Julia? The thing with your dad. I'm sorry."

She didn't respond immediately. When she did, her voice was quieter than he'd heard it before. "Thanks. Now go. You'll be late."

James's house was in the nice part of town, where the lawns had professional landscaping and the mailboxes matched the shutters. Nate felt acutely aware of his worn sneakers and discount-store jacket as he climbed the porch steps.

Andre opened the door before he could knock.

"THE PROPHECY IS FULFILLED," he announced. "Nate has emerged from his fortress of solitude."

"It's been four hours since lunch," Nate said.

"TIME IS RELATIVE. Come in. Violet's already claimed the good spot on the couch and James is having an existential crisis about pizza toppings."

Inside, the house was exactly what Nate expected: clean, modern, family photos arranged artfully on the walls. Through the doorway to the kitchen, he could see James holding his phone, frowning at what was probably a menu.

"Nate! Settle this." James looked up. "Is pineapple on pizza a crime against nature or a misunderstood delicacy?"

"Loaded question. I refuse to answer."

"COWARD," Andre called from the living room.

Violet appeared in the kitchen doorway. She'd changed from her usual black hoodie into a slightly less black t-shirt. Progress. "Ignore them. We're getting half pepperoni, half mushroom. This is decided."

"When did we decide that?" James asked.

"Just now. I decided for all of us. You're welcome."

They ordered. Ate pizza on James's very nice couch while watching a horror movie from the eighties that was more funny than scary. Argued about whether the special effects were intentionally bad or just dated. Made jokes. Existed in the kind of easy, purposeless way that Nate had forgotten was possible.

And the whole time, part of his mind was running calculations.

Three weeks until assembly. Two weeks to build and test. One week buffer for problems. He'd need to access the theater storage Tuesday, when Mr. Chen had drama club. Wednesday to program the Arduino. Thursday to run a test sequence in his garage while his mom was at work.

"You're doing the thing again," Violet said.

Nate blinked. On screen, someone was being menaced by what appeared to be a killer lamp. "Sorry."

"Don't apologize. Just pull yourself back. You're here. Be here."

So he tried. He watched the terrible movie. Laughed at Andre's running commentary. Helped James clean up when they were done. Existed in this moment, this small pocket of normal, while knowing that in three weeks, he was going to help Julia Vance tear someone's world apart.

And the thing that scared him most wasn't that he might get caught.

It was how much of him was looking forward to it.

prompt: Imagine a 23-year-old graphic design intern who agrees to help her 30-something neighbor move into a quaint Midwestern home. During two playful mishaps—a spilled pitcher of water and an accidental, borderline-revealing moment—both characters find themselves wrestling with desire, propriety, and the spark between friendship and romance. Write an 8,000- to 10,000-word story exploring temptation, miscommunication, and the moment when everything changes.
generated_story:
The moving truck idled in front of 412 Maple Street, its diesel engine rumbling against the Saturday morning quiet of Cedarville, Iowa. Mara Hoffman stood on the sidewalk in cutoff denim shorts and an oversized t-shirt from her college portfolio show, squinting up at the pale yellow Victorian that would apparently be her neighbor's new home. The house had good bones—wraparound porch, gabled roof, the kind of ornate woodwork that real estate agents called "charming" and contractors called "expensive to maintain." It looked like someone's grandmother should be inside baking pies.

Instead, it would house Ryan Chen, the software developer who'd lived in the apartment across the hall from her for the past eight months and had somehow become her default coffee-drinking, bad-movie-watching, two-in-the-morning-can't-sleep confidant. She still wasn't entirely sure how that had happened.

"You came." Ryan appeared from behind the truck, his dark hair falling into his eyes, wearing a faded Northwestern t-shirt and athletic shorts. He looked genuinely surprised, even though she'd confirmed twice via text that she'd be here.

"I said I would." Mara adjusted the ponytail that kept her honey-blonde hair off her neck. Late August in the Midwest meant the air felt like breathing through a wet towel. "Did you think I'd bail?"

"Honestly? Little bit." He grinned, and there it was—that lopsided smile that made something flip in her chest, the same stupid flutter she'd been determinedly ignoring since approximately March. "You're the only person I know in this town who volunteered. Everyone else I had to bribe with pizza."

"Where are the bribed masses?"

"Coming at noon. I wanted to get the heavy stuff positioned before they arrived and ate everything." Ryan pulled down the truck's ramp with a metallic clatter. "Strategic planning."

Mara peered into the truck's dim interior. She'd helped Ryan pack his apartment over the past week, so she recognized most of the furniture, but seeing it all stacked and wrapped in moving blankets made the reality hit differently. He was actually leaving. No more impromptu Wednesday night discussions about whether the MCU had peaked or still had creative juice. No more borrowing his nice camera for client shoots. No more knocking on his door when her anxiety spiraled into insomnia.

Their apartment building was full of people in their twenties doing short-term stints in Kansas City—people who treated it as a waystation before moving to a coast, any coast. Mara had assumed Ryan was one of them. Instead, he'd bought a house an hour north, in the small town where she'd grown up and returned to after her college money ran out and her Kansas City design internship had offered no path to a real job.

"Earth to Mara?"

She blinked. "Sorry. Where do you want to start?"

"Couch first. It's awkward but not that heavy. Then we'll work up to the real nightmare stuff."

The couch was a mid-century modern piece Ryan had found on Facebook Marketplace, all clean lines and walnut legs. Mara took the front end, walking backward up the porch steps while Ryan guided from behind. Her Converse slipped slightly on the third step.

"Careful," Ryan said, his voice tighter.

"I've got it." She did, barely. They maneuvered through the front door into a living room with built-in bookshelves and a fireplace that had been painted over in about six different eras of white. Hardwood floors stretched toward a dining room on one side and a staircase on the other. The whole place smelled like old wood and the particular mustiness that came with houses that had sat empty through a humid summer.

"By the window?" Ryan asked.

They positioned the couch, and Mara straightened, pressing her hand to her lower back. She worked out semi-regularly, but moving furniture used muscles that Pilates videos apparently didn't target.

"You okay?" Ryan was watching her with that concerned expression he got, eyebrows drawn together.

"Fine. What's next?"

They fell into a rhythm. Bookshelf, kitchen table, dresser, boxes upon boxes upon boxes. Ryan had warned her he owned a lot of books, but she hadn't quite believed him until they were hauling the fifth box labeled "BOOKS - LIVING ROOM" up the porch steps. The man had a problem.

"I'm staging an intervention," Mara announced, dropping the box in the living room with the others. "This is hoarding."

"It's curated collecting."

"It's pathological."

"Says the woman with seventeen different notebooks for sketching."

"Those are tools of my trade," Mara protested, but she was smiling. This was familiar territory, the easy banter that had made Ryan's friendship feel less like something she'd pursued and more like something she'd fallen into, as natural as breathing.

By eleven-thirty, they'd cleared about two-thirds of the truck. Mara's t-shirt clung to her back with sweat, and strands of hair had escaped her ponytail to stick to her neck and forehead. Ryan had disappeared into the kitchen, and she heard him swearing softly at something.

"You good in there?"

"Yeah, just—the previous owners left some stuff in the cupboards. Random dishes. A very dusty blender."

Mara wiped her forehead with the back of her hand and surveyed the living room. In the chaos of boxes and furniture, she could almost see what the place would become. Ryan had good taste—not the desperate-to-look-adult matching sets from HomeGoods, but actual pieces with character. It would be a nice house. A house where someone built a life.

She was happy for him. She was. The weird ache in her chest was just nostalgia for their apartment building arrangement, nothing more complicated than that.

Ryan emerged from the kitchen holding a glass pitcher filled with water, condensation already beading on its sides.

"Found glasses too. Figured we could use—"

He didn't finish the sentence.

The pitcher slipped from his hand.

Mara watched it fall in that strange slow-motion way the brain processed disaster—the glass tumbling, water arcing out in a crystal spray, Ryan lunging forward too late. It hit the hardwood with a crack that seemed to echo through the empty house, shards skittering across the floor like dropped stars.

"Shit." Ryan froze, staring at the wreckage. "Sorry, I just—my hand cramped."

"It's fine." Mara was already moving toward the kitchen. "Paper towels?"

"I don't know. Maybe? I haven't unpacked anything."

She found a roll under the sink, still in its plastic wrapper from whoever had lived here before. The kitchen was smaller than she'd expected—outdated cabinets, laminate countertops that were trying very hard to look like granite and failing. But it had a window over the sink that looked out onto a backyard with an actual tree, a massive oak that had probably been there since before the house was built.

When she returned to the living room, Ryan was crouched by the broken glass, carefully picking up the larger pieces.

"Don't," Mara said. "You'll cut yourself."

"I've got it."

"Ryan." She dropped to her knees beside him, close enough that their shoulders almost touched. "Stop. Let me help."

He sat back on his heels, a shard of glass in his palm. His expression was odd—somewhere between embarrassed and something else she couldn't name. The light through the front windows caught the dark brown of his eyes and turned them almost amber.

"You're always helping," he said quietly.

"What?"

"Nothing. Never mind." He placed the glass shard carefully on the paper towel she'd spread out. "I'm just tired."

They cleaned up the mess in silence. Mara swept while Ryan held the dustpan, their movements practiced from eight months of existing in each other's orbit. When they were done, she threw the glass-filled paper towels in the truck's cab to deal with later, and they both stood on the porch, grateful for the slight breeze that had picked up.

"The pizza people will be here soon," Ryan said, checking his phone. "Tyler and Jess are fifteen minutes out. Apparently they're bringing Jess's brother too."

"The one who works at the hardware store?"

"That's the one. I promised him first pick of beer."

Mara leaned against the porch railing, testing its sturdiness. It held. The street was quiet—a few kids on bikes in the distance, someone mowing their lawn two houses down. This was the Cedarville she remembered, the town she'd spent her teenage years desperately wanting to escape. It looked different from this porch, less suffocating than she remembered.

"Why here?" The question came out before she'd fully decided to ask it.

Ryan turned to look at her, one hand shading his eyes from the sun. "What do you mean?"

"I mean—you could've bought a place in KC. Somewhere closer to your office, closer to actual restaurants and things to do. Why drive an hour north to live in the most boring town in Iowa?"

"It's not boring."

"Ryan, the big news last month was that the grocery store started carrying oat milk."

He laughed, and some of the tension in his shoulders eased. "Okay, fair. But I don't know. The housing market in KC is insane, and I kept seeing these places online up here—actual houses with yards and porches and rooms that aren't just boxes. Plus the commute's not that bad. Podcasts exist."

"But you don't know anyone here."

"I know you."

The words hung in the air between them. Mara felt her pulse kick up in a way that was absolutely not helpful. She kept her eyes on the street, on the kids on bikes who were circling back toward them.

"I mean, you're in and out for client meetings anyway," Ryan continued, like he hadn't just said something that made her entire chest feel tight. "And your parents are here. I figured it might be nice, having a friend in town. For both of us."

"Right. Yeah." Mara pushed off from the railing. "That makes sense."

One of the kids—a boy maybe ten years old with a gap-toothed grin—slowed his bike as he passed the house. "You the new people?"

"I'm the new people," Ryan called back. "Just me."

"Cool house. It's haunted though."

"What?"

But the kid was already pedaling away, laughing with his friends, and Mara couldn't tell if he was serious or just messing with the new neighbor.

"Haunted," Ryan repeated. "Great. That definitely wasn't in the listing."

"Every old house in Cedarville is supposedly haunted. Mrs. Patterson down the street swears she sees her dead husband in the garden. It's fine."

"Comforting."

A white sedan pulled up to the curb, and a woman with a blonde pixie cut waved from the driver's seat.
