The figure raised its arms. Stretching? Greeting the changed sky? The gesture looked human, but the proportions were wrong. Too many joints in the elbows, or maybe his eyes were lying about where the elbows should be.

"My daughter lives in California," Dmitri said suddenly. "Sonoma. She makes wine now. Can you imagine? A Russian girl making California wine." He laughed, but it caught in his throat. "I called her yesterday. Told her about my doctor's appointment—routine thing, just a checkup. She said she'd visit in March, for my birthday. I told her not to bother, that March in Chicago is miserable, all dead snow and false springs. She said she'd come anyway."

Marcus understood what he was being told. "You can't call her now."

"No."

"But she'll know something's happening. She'll see the news."

"If there still is news. If California still exists in a form that has news." Dmitri reached for his tea, found it cold, drank it anyway. "I'm not worried about her, Marcus. She's strong. She survived me and her mother's death and a divorce that nearly destroyed her. She'll survive this too, whatever this is. What worries me is that I won't know. That's the real apocalypse, isn't it? Not the end of the world, but the end of knowing."

A crack ran across one of the windows—not breaking, just appearing, a fracture in the glass that hadn't been there a moment before. Marcus watched it branch and spread like lightning captured mid-strike, then stop. The window held. The crack remained, visible but static, as if reality had paused mid-collapse.

"The building's old," Dmitri said, following his gaze. "Built in 1920, converted in 2005. They kept the original windows where they could. Character, the developers said. Now I'm wondering if old glass responds differently to whatever this is."

Marcus stood and approached the window, careful not to touch it. Up close, he could see that the crack wasn't quite in the glass—it was in the space the glass occupied, a flaw in the medium between inside and outside. He moved his head slightly, and the crack shifted perspective, revealing depth it shouldn't have.

"It's spreading into dimensions we can't see," he murmured.

"Then we're fish in a drying pond," Dmitri said. "Watching the water vanish into an up we can't comprehend."

The thunder sound came again, louder this time, sustained. It rattled Marcus's ribs and made his vision blur. When it passed, he realized he could hear something else underneath it—voices, maybe, or something like voices. Harmonics that suggested language without forming words he recognized. The sound didn't come from outside. It came from inside, from the changed building three blocks south, transmitted through the air or through reality itself.

"They're singing," Dmitri said.

"That's not singing."

"Then what would you call it?"

Marcus had no answer. The sound continued, rising and falling in patterns that felt structured, meaningful. It reminded him of recordings he'd heard of whale song, that eerie beauty that came from intelligence operating on different principles than human thought. This was like that but more so—consciousness announcing itself in a register that bypassed understanding and went straight to the hindbrain.

His legs felt weak. He returned to the sofa and sat heavily.

"I was supposed to present a design today," he said, apropos of nothing. "Mixed-use development in Pilsen. Affordable housing on the lower floors, market-rate condos above, retail at street level. Standard stuff. I spent three weeks on the renderings."

"Will you miss it?"

The question surprised him. "Miss what?"

"That world. The one where mixed-use developments matter. Where you ride the same bus every morning and worry about being late. Where the worst thing that can happen is a bad presentation or a failed marriage or growing old alone." Dmitri's eyes were distant. "I miss the world where my wife was alive. But that world ended seven years ago. I built a new life in the world that came after. Smaller. Quieter. But still mine."

"This isn't the same."

"Isn't it? Loss is loss. Change is change. The scale doesn't matter to the person experiencing it."

Marcus wanted to argue, wanted to insist that personal grief and the fundamental restructuring of reality were categorically different, but his certainty wavered. Maybe Dmitri was right. Maybe apocalypse was always intimate, always experienced one consciousness at a time, regardless of how many consciousnesses were experiencing it simultaneously.

The shimmer was close enough now that Marcus could see individual elements within it. Structures, definitely—buildings or machines or organisms, the categories bleeding together. They rose from the transformed ground like crystals growing in time-lapse, their surfaces covered in patterns that might have been windows or might have been sensory organs or might have been purely decorative. Some moved, flowing like mercury up and down their own surfaces. Others stood still, anchored to geometries Marcus's eyes couldn't quite process.

And between the structures, moving with purpose, were things he had to call people for lack of a better word. They had the right number of limbs, more or less. They moved upright. But their proportions shifted as they walked, adapting to the space they moved through, as if the relationship between body and environment was negotiable rather than fixed.

"Are they us?" Marcus asked. "The people who were there before?"

"Does it matter?"

"Yes. Christ, yes, it matters."

"Why?"

Because. Because identity mattered. Because continuity of self was the foundation of everything that made life meaningful. Because if those transformed figures weren't the people they'd been before the wave passed through them, then when the wave reached this building, Marcus would cease. And if they were still themselves, just changed, then cessation might be preferable to whatever alteration was coming.

He couldn't articulate any of that, so he said nothing.

Dmitri rose with a soft grunt and moved to a bookshelf. He ran his fingers along the spines—a gesture of familiarity, of long acquaintance with these particular objects. He pulled down a slim volume bound in blue cloth, its spine creased from multiple readings.

"Akhmatova," he said, returning to his chair. "You know her work?"

"No."

"Russian poet. Lived through revolution, Stalin, siege, terror. Watched her world end multiple times and kept writing." He opened the book, flipped through pages with the confidence of someone who knew exactly what he was looking for. "Here. Listen:

'Everything is plundered, betrayed, sold,
Death's great black wing scrapes the air,
Misery gnaws to the bone.
Why then do we not despair?'"

He closed the book gently. "She wrote that in 1921. Petrograd was starving. The revolution had devoured its children. Everything she'd known had been destroyed. And still she asked: why don't we despair?"

"Did she answer the question?"

"Of course not. The question is the answer." Dmitri set the book on the arm of his chair. "Despair is a choice, Marcus. Even now. Especially now."

The shimmer touched another building, this one closer—perhaps two blocks south. An old brownstone, the kind that housed three or four units, families stacked vertically, sharing walls and water pressure and the sounds of each other's lives. The wave passed through it in seconds, and the transformation was immediate and total.

The brownstone became fluid, its walls rippling like cloth in wind. The windows elongated, stretched into shapes that weren't quite oval and weren't quite rectangular. The door—Marcus could see the door clearly from this height—opened and closed repeatedly, rhythmically, like breathing. And from that door emerged figures.

They looked almost human. Almost.

They moved into the street, which had itself begun to change. The asphalt softened, became something between solid and liquid, responding to the pressure of their feet. Where they stepped, colors bloomed—deep purples and golds and colors Marcus had no names for, hues that seemed to vibrate at frequencies his optic nerves weren't designed to process.

"They're not running," Dmitri observed. "Look. They're not afraid."

It was true. The figures moved with what looked like curiosity rather than panic. One bent to touch the transformed ground, trailing its fingers through the substance that had been asphalt. Another looked up at the sky—at the portion of sky that was still recognizably sky—with what Marcus interpreted as wonder. A third approached the edge of the transformed zone, where changed reality met unchanged, and placed its palm against the boundary. The shimmer rippled outward from the point of contact, spreading faster.

"They're helping it," Marcus whispered. "They're accelerating it."

"Or they're exploring it. Participating in it." Dmitri leaned forward. "What if it's not destroying them, Marcus? What if it's offering them something?"

"Offering what?"

"I don't know. More. Different. A floor we've never had access to before."

Marcus felt something crack inside his chest—not breaking, just opening. A space he hadn't known was sealed. "You want this."

"I'm curious about it. Which is different from wanting but not entirely unrelated." Dmitri smiled, and for the first time since they'd met, Marcus saw peace in the old man's face. "I'm eighty-three. I've lived a full life by most measures. But full of what? Routine. Repetition. The same thoughts in different configurations. I learned long ago that newness is the rarest commodity. And here comes something genuinely new. Why shouldn't I be curious?"

"Because it might kill us."

"Might. Or might not. We don't actually know." He gestured at the transformed figures in the street. "They seem fine. Different, yes. But fine."

A helicopter passed overhead—or something that had been a helicopter before the shimmer touched the sky it was flying through. Its rotors were wrong, spinning in directions that shouldn't be possible, but it stayed aloft. Marcus could see figures in the cockpit, or where the cockpit should be. They seemed to be piloting it with competence despite the fundamental alteration of the aircraft's physics.

"Everything's adapting," he said.

"Everything always adapts. That's what everything does."

The wind chime sound changed pitch, dropping several octaves into a range Marcus felt more than heard. It reminded him of the times he'd stood near the speakers at concerts, when the bass was turned up so loud it became tactile, a pressure wave rather than a sound. This was like that but deeper, resonating in his bones and teeth and the fluid of his inner ear.

Dmitri winced. "That's new."

"What does it mean?"

"How should I know? I'm just an old man watching the world change. Again." He stood, moved to the window despite the crack, despite the proximity of the shimmer. "Come here. Look at this."

Marcus joined him. Together they stood at the glass, looking down at the transformed street, the altered buildings, the figures that had been human or still were human or were something between or beyond.

"Tell me what you see," Dmitri said.

Marcus stared down at the street, trying to find language for what his eyes reported. "I see... order," he said finally. "Not chaos. The changes have structure. Pattern."

"Good. What else?"

"The boundary." Marcus pointed at the shimmer's edge, now perhaps a block and a half away. "It's not moving at a constant speed anymore. It's pulsing. Advancing in waves."

"Like a heartbeat," Dmitri said.

"Like breathing."

They were both right, Marcus realized. The shimmer expanded and contracted rhythmically, each pulse pushing the boundary a few feet farther north. At this rate, it would reach the building in less than an hour. Maybe half that.

His architect's training kicked in automatically—calculating volume, measuring rate of expansion, estimating time to arrival. The numbers scrolled through his mind with the same detached precision he'd bring to determining load-bearing requirements or thermal efficiency. This would kill him in approximately thirty-seven minutes, plus or minus ten, depending on whether the pulse rate remained constant.

"I should run," he said, but didn't move.

"Should you?"

"Survival instinct. Basic biology."

"And yet you're still standing here." Dmitri's reflection in the cracked glass smiled at him. "Why is that, do you think?"

Marcus didn't answer because he didn't know. Or because he knew and couldn't articulate it. Something in him had shifted when he watched those figures emerge from the transformed brownstone. They'd moved with intention, curiosity, even something that looked like joy. Whatever had happened to them, they hadn't been annihilated. They'd been changed, and they'd accepted the change, and they'd continued.

Maybe continuation was enough.

"Tell me about your wife," Marcus said abruptly. "Nina. What was she like?"

Dmitri was quiet for a long moment, his eyes distant. When he spoke, his voice was softer. "Stubborn. Brilliant. She taught mathematics at the university—topology, differential geometry, things I couldn't begin to understand. She used to say that most people think math is about numbers, but really it's about relationships. How things connect. How spaces curve. How you can transform one shape into another through continuous deformation." He paused. "She would have loved this. The geometry of it. She'd have pulled out paper and started sketching equations while the world ended."

"What would she have calculated?"

"The shape of the boundary, probably. Whether it's expanding in Euclidean space or something stranger. She was obsessed with manifolds—surfaces that look flat locally but curve in higher dimensions. She used to say we're all living on a manifold without realizing it, that the universe is curved in ways we can't perceive because we're embedded in it."

Marcus thought about that. "So this could be... what? The manifold becoming visible?"

"Or folding. Or intersecting with another manifold. Or unfolding into dimensions we couldn't access before." Dmitri shrugged. "I'm speculating wildly. Nina would have corrected me by now. Told me I'm using the terms wrong. But she's not here, so I'll remain incorrect and unmourned."

Below them, one of the transformed figures had stopped in the middle of the street. It stood perfectly still, head tilted back, arms slightly raised. Marcus couldn't tell if it was looking at something or listening or simply experiencing whatever the change had brought. Other figures moved around it without collision, as if they could sense its position without seeing it, or seeing it in ways that didn't require direct line of sight.

"They're navigating differently," Marcus said. "Watch how they move. They're not using vision the way we do."

"Maybe they're using everything. Vision, sound, touch, senses we don't have names for." Dmitri pressed his palm against the window, next to but not touching the crack. "Nina used to do this exercise with her students. She'd have them close their eyes and navigate a room using only sound and air pressure. Most of them crashed into furniture immediately. A few learned. One girl—I remember Nina telling me this—one girl said she could feel the walls even without touching them. Sense them through the way they displaced air, created zones of stillness and movement. Nina called her a natural topologist."

"What happened to her?"

"The girl? No idea. This was years ago." He paused. "Or maybe Nina never told me. Maybe I'm combining stories, building a narrative from fragments. Memory is unreliable, especially at the end."

"This isn't the end."

"Isn't it? We're thirty minutes from transformation or obliteration or whatever this is. That feels like an ending to me."

"But those people down there," Marcus gestured at the figures in the street. "They continued. They're still going. That's not ending, it's... transition."

"Ah." Dmitri smiled. "You're convincing yourself. Good. Better to go into the unknown curious than terrified."

Was that what he was doing? Marcus examined his emotions carefully, like a doctor palpating for tumors. Fear was present—a cold weight in his stomach, a tightness in his throat. But alongside it was something else. Anticipation, maybe. The feeling he used to get as a child on Christmas Eve, that electric sense that something extraordinary was about to happen.

He'd lost that feeling somewhere in adulthood. When had wonder been replaced by obligation? When had potential become burden? He couldn't pinpoint a moment. It had been gradual, erosional, the way a river wore down stone—not through dramatic force but through persistent, patient repetition.

The shimmer pulsed again, and the boundary jumped forward. Now it was perhaps three-quarters of a block away. Marcus could see the moment of transformation clearly: the air seemed to thicken, become visible, and then reality on the far side of that threshold was simply different. The change was instantaneous but not violent. More like a phase transition—water becoming ice, ice becoming water—matter reorganizing into a new configuration without losing its essential substance.

"I'm going to the roof," Dmitri announced suddenly.

"What?"

"The roof. If I'm going to watch this happen, I want to see it from the highest point available." He was already moving toward the door. "Coming?"

Marcus hesitated. The logical choice was to leave the building entirely, to run north as fast as his legs could carry him, to put as much distance between himself and the advancing boundary as possible. But logic felt irrelevant now. Logic belonged to the world that had existed this morning, when his biggest concern was being on time for a presentation he'd never give.

"Yes," he said. "I'm coming."

They took the stairs—the elevator was useless without power—and climbed. Dmitri moved slowly but steadily, one hand on the railing, breathing carefully through his nose. Marcus stayed close, ready to catch him if he stumbled, but the old man navigated the climb with the determination of someone who'd made a decision and wouldn't be deterred by something as trivial as physical limitation.

The door to the roof was locked. Dmitri produced another key—he seemed to have keys for everything—and pushed it open. Cold air hit them like a wall, carrying that inverted-earth smell, ozone and copper and wrongness. The wind was stronger up here, whipping around the rooftop equipment, making the ventilation ducts moan.

And the view.

From six stories up, Marcus could see the full scope of what was happening. The shimmer extended across the entire southern half of the city, a hemisphere of altered reality that rose from the ground to disappear into the clouds. Or through the clouds—he couldn't tell which. The boundary was perhaps half a block away now, advancing in those rhythmic pulses, eating building after building, street after street.

To the north, Chicago remained itself. The skyline was recognizable—Willis Tower, John Hancock Center, the jagged collection of downtown high-rises Marcus had known his entire life. But to the south, everything had been remade. The architecture was still there, but transformed into something between building and organism, structure and process. Towers that rippled and swayed. Streets that flowed like rivers. Bridges that seemed to exist in multiple states simultaneously—solid and liquid, present and absent, connecting shores that occupied different dimensional spaces.

"My God," Marcus whispered.

"Not God," Dmitri said. "Or maybe God, depending on your definition. But not the God of churches. Something else. Something that was always there, maybe, and we just couldn't see it until now."

Figures moved through the transformed cityscape by the thousands. They walked, ran, stood still, gathered in groups, dispersed again. From this height, Marcus couldn't make out individual features, but he could see the overall pattern of their movement. It wasn't random. There was organization to it, purpose. They were going about their lives—or beginning new lives—in the world that had been made available to them.

"Do you think they remember?" Marcus asked. "Who they were before?"

"I think memory is just another thing that's changing." Dmitri moved to the roof's edge, leaning against the low parapet. "Nina used to say that memory isn't storage, it's reconstruction. Every time you remember something, you're rebuilding it from fragments, and each rebuilding changes it slightly. Given enough iterations, the memory becomes fiction. A story you tell yourself about who you were." He looked at Marcus. "Maybe they remember everything. Maybe they remember nothing. Maybe the question stops making sense after transformation."

The wind chime sound was louder up here, unimpeded by walls or glass. Marcus felt it in his entire body now, a vibration that seemed to originate from inside his cells. It wasn't unpleasant anymore. It felt almost musical, a frequency his body was learning to resonate with.

"I'm scared," he said, surprised by his own honesty.

"Good. Fear means you're paying attention." Dmitri pulled his coat tighter against the wind. "I'm scared too. But I'm also—this will sound strange—grateful. How many people get to witness something genuinely unprecedented? How many lives end not with revelation but with repetition, the same thoughts in the same configurations until consciousness just... stops?"

"So this is better?"

"This is different. Whether different is better, we'll find out." He smiled. "Or we won't. Either way, the question will be answered."

Marcus looked north again, toward the part of the city that hadn't changed yet. He could see movement there too—people fleeing, cars gridlocked on every street, the chaotic scramble of a population trying to outrun the inevitable. How far could they get? Milwaukee? Madison? The Canadian border? And what would they find when they arrived? More of the same? Or would the shimmer be there too, advancing from every direction, a global transformation that made geography irrelevant?

"There's nowhere to run, is there?" Marcus said.

"Probably not. But that's always been true. We just pretended otherwise."

The boundary was at the edge of the block now. Marcus could see the exact line where changed reality met unchanged, a threshold sharp enough to cut. On one side, a parking lot with three cars and a dumpster. On the other side, those same elements transformed—the cars becoming something between vehicle and sculpture, the dumpster rippling like liquid metal, the asphalt breathing in slow waves.

And then the boundary touched the building.

prompt: Imagine a story about the launch of Satan’s first soul bank branch on Earth in a busy modern city. Follow an ambitious branch manager, a skeptical teller, and a rival angelic regulator as they navigate themes of capitalism, bureaucracy, and moral compromise. The story should feature at least two comedic mishaps and a twist that makes you question the value of the human soul. 8,000-10,000 words.
generated_story:
The ribbon-cutting ceremony for Hell's first terrestrial branch office was scheduled for nine a.m. sharp, which gave Marcus Hellstrom exactly forty-five minutes to figure out why the soul deposit system kept converting eternal damnation into frequent flyer miles.

"It's the API integration," said Cheryl, crouched beneath the marble counter with her tablet glowing green in the dimness. "Someone in IT used the wrong deprecation protocol."

Marcus pressed his palms against his eyes. At thirty-four, he was the youngest branch manager in the history of the Infernal Banking Corporation, and he'd spent the last six months convincing the Board of Archdemons that Earth's consumer culture was finally ready for direct soul liquidity services. Now, three minutes before the regional press arrived, his flagship branch was turning millennia of torment into Delta SkyMiles.

"Can you fix it?"

"Can I fix it?" Cheryl's voice echoed from beneath the counter. "Marcus, I've been a teller for six hundred years. I've processed soul forfeitures during the Crusades, during the Industrial Revolution, during the entire run of *American Idol*. But I can't rewrite reality code in forty-two minutes."

The branch occupied what had once been a Chase bank in downtown Seattle, sandwiched between a bubble tea franchise and a store that sold either crystals or drugs—Marcus had never been entirely sure which. The marble floor gleamed like black ice. The walls were that specific shade of corporate gray that made humans feel both important and insignificant. Seventeen-foot ceilings created the appropriate sense of cosmic awe.

The only unusual feature was the interest rate display behind the counter, where instead of percentages, scrolling demonic script announced: "ETERNAL DAMNATION: NOW WITH COMPETITIVE APY!"

Marcus had fought for three months to get that display approved.

"Thirty-eight minutes," he said, watching Cheryl's legs kick as she worked. She'd chosen to manifest today in full human form: practical flats, sensible slacks, a blouse that said *I respect your financial privacy while quietly judging your moral choices*. Her name tag read CHERYL ABADDON, which was technically against human branding guidelines, but she'd refused to use anything else.

"How bad would it be," Marcus asked, "if we just... postponed?"

Cheryl's head emerged from beneath the counter. She had sharp features, dark eyes that had witnessed the fall of empires, and an expression that suggested she'd heard worse ideas but couldn't immediately remember when.

"The Board sent us here because you convinced them humans were ready to commodify their own souls without all the traditional folderol—the contracts in blood, the midnight crossroads, the dramatic violin music. You said modern capitalism had done our work for us. You said humans already understood that everything has a price, including themselves."

"I know what I said."

"So if we postpone, they'll assume you were wrong. They'll recall you to Processing. Do you want to go back to Processing, Marcus?"

He did not want to go back to Processing. Processing was an endless gray maze where the damned sorted themselves by category: sins of pride, sins of greed, sins of replying-all to company emails. Marcus had done eight decades in Processing after his own death in 1987, and he'd emerged with a singular ambition: to never, ever return.

"Fixed it," Cheryl announced, standing. "We're back to standard soul-to-damnation conversion. Though I should mention—"

The door chimed.

Not the electronic chime Marcus had installed, but something older. Something that resonated in the jaw and the joints. The temperature dropped six degrees.

A woman entered wearing a white pantsuit so pristine it hurt to look at directly. Her hair was blonde and severe. Her briefcase appeared to be made of solidified light. She walked with the kind of measured precision that suggested she'd once led armies and was still deciding whether that had been a good idea.

"Mr. Hellstrom," she said, and her voice had harmonics that made Marcus's teeth ache. "I'm Celeste Lumière. Heaven's Bureau of Soul Commerce sent me to observe your opening. I believe you received the notification?"

Marcus had received seventeen notifications, each one more passive-aggressive than the last. Heaven's bureaucracy had expanded considerably since the Renaissance, when they'd realized that simply opposing evil was less effective than regulating it into submission.

"Ms. Lumière. We weren't expecting Heaven's delegation until—"

"I prefer to arrive early," she said, her smile sharp enough to cut theology. "It allows me to observe... irregularities."

She glanced around the branch with an expression that suggested she was already drafting violation reports in her head. Her gaze lingered on the interest rate display, then moved to the framed motivational poster on the wall: "YOUR SOUL: THE ASSET THAT KEEPS ON LOSING VALUE."

"That violates the Soul Commerce Transparency Act of 2019," Celeste said, pulling out a tablet that glowed with pale golden light. "Section four, subsection twelve: all promotional materials must clearly state the irreversible nature of soul forfeiture."

"It's ironic," Marcus said.

"Irony," Celeste replied, "is not a recognized defense under celestial compliance law."

Cheryl made a sound that might have been a cough or might have been suppressed laughter. Marcus shot her a look that promised a very long performance review.

"Perhaps," Marcus said, reaching for the diplomatic training he'd received in Hell's management program, "we could discuss compliance issues after the opening ceremony?"

"Oh no," Celeste said, settling into one of the customer chairs with the confidence of someone who'd negotiated the Council of Nicaea. "I think we should discuss them now."

The door chimed again. This time it was the press: three reporters, two cameras, and a blogger who'd somehow convinced himself that the opening of a literal demonic bank was actually a viral marketing stunt for a new streaming series.

Marcus smoothed his tie, checked his reflection in the black marble wall, and prepared to explain to the world why their souls were now eligible for direct deposit.

The reporter from the *Seattle Times* was a nervous woman in her mid-fifties who kept glancing at Celeste as if trying to determine whether the white pantsuit was a fashion statement or a declaration of war. Her photographer, a bored-looking man with impressive facial hair, was already snapping pictures of the interest rate display.

"Mr. Hellstrom," the reporter said, her voice tight with the kind of professional anxiety that suggested her editor had sent her here as punishment, "can you explain exactly what services the Infernal Banking Corporation will be offering?"

Marcus activated what Cheryl privately called his "mortal charm protocol"—the slight softening of features, the reduction of the sulfurous undertone in his voice, the careful modulation that made humans forget they were talking to someone who'd been dead for thirty-seven years.

"We're offering what every modern financial institution should offer: liquidity, flexibility, and competitive rates on the most underutilized asset most people own—their immortal soul."

The blogger, who wore a t-shirt that said HELL YES in glittery letters, practically vibrated with excitement. "So this is like crypto, but for souls?"

"It's nothing like crypto," Celeste interjected, her voice carrying the weight of someone who'd watched humanity invent and destroy a thousand currencies. "Cryptocurrency is speculative vapor. Souls are eternal constructs with intrinsic metaphysical value."

"Exactly," Marcus said, though agreeing with Celeste made his teeth ache. "What we're doing is bringing an ancient system into the modern age. For millennia, soul transactions required elaborate rituals, blood contracts, specialized knowledge. We're democratizing damnation."

"That's your slogan?" the *Times* reporter asked, scribbling. "Democratizing damnation?"

"One of them."

Cheryl appeared at his elbow with a tablet. "Marcus, we need to discuss the—"

"Not now."

"It's about the system conversion I mentioned—"

"I said not now."

Cheryl's expression suggested that when this was over, she would make him experience certain very specific circles of hell, probably the ones involving customer service training videos that never ended.

The second reporter, a young man from a tech blog who'd clearly dressed up for the occasion in a blazer that didn't quite hide his *Hellboy* t-shirt, raised his hand. "How does the actual transaction work? Do people sign in blood or..."

"Standard electronic signature," Marcus said. "We accept DocuSign, Adobe Sign, even a good old-fashioned stylus on a tablet. The medium doesn't matter. What matters is intent and consideration."

"Consideration?"

"The exchange of value. In traditional banking, you deposit money and receive interest. With us, you deposit your soul and receive immediate liquidity in the form of wish fulfillment, luck augmentation, or targeted success enhancement."

"You're describing magic," Celeste said.

"We're describing services," Marcus countered. "Heaven has been in the miracle business for two thousand years. Are we really going to debate semantics?"

Celeste's smile could have frozen the sun. "Heaven doesn't charge interest on miracles, Mr. Hellstrom."

"Heaven also has a waiting list that averages seven years for routine grace disbursements. We guarantee same-day wish fulfillment."

The photographer had stopped taking pictures and was now openly staring at the exchange. The *Times* reporter's pen hovered over her notebook, apparently stuck between journalism and existential crisis.

"Let me get this straight," the tech blogger said slowly. "You're saying people can trade their eternal souls for... what, exactly? A better job? A nicer apartment?"

"Among other things," Marcus said. "We also offer relationship enhancement packages, creative inspiration bundles, and our premium service—comprehensive enemy elimination."

"You kill people?" The *Times* reporter's voice shot up an octave.

"We don't kill anyone," Marcus said smoothly. "We simply ensure that certain obstacles to our clients' success are... removed. Legally. Sometimes those obstacles happen to be people who then experience catastrophic, completely natural-seeming accidents."

"That's conspiracy to commit murder," Celeste said, tapping her tablet. "Federal violation. I'm adding it to the report."

"It's not conspiracy if we don't actually do anything ourselves. We simply loan our clients temporary access to localized probability manipulation."

"That's the most evil parsing of causality I've heard since the Reformation."

"Thank you."

The door chimed again. This time it was Maria Santos, the City Council member who'd fought for two months to block the branch's business license before ultimately losing to a very expensive legal team and what Marcus suspected was a small amount of actual corruption.

She wore a power suit in deep navy and an expression that suggested she'd come here to declare war.

"Mr. Hellstrom," she said, not bothering with pleasantries. "I'm here to inform you that the City Council is filing an immediate injunction against your business operations on the grounds of public safety, moral hazard, and violation of city ordinance 47-B regarding the sale of intangible personal assets within commercial zones."

Marcus felt something cold settle in his chest. This wasn't part of the plan. The legal team had assured him all municipal issues were resolved.

"Councilwoman Santos, I believe if you check your records, you'll find we've satisfied all regulatory requirements—"

"You've satisfied the *current* requirements. The Council voted last night to create new ones. Effective immediately, any business trafficking in immortal soul commerce must undergo a six-month environmental and ethical impact review."

Cheryl made a sound like steam escaping a kettle.

"That's targeted regulatory overreach," Marcus said, feeling his carefully maintained human appearance beginning to slip. The temperature in the room dropped another three degrees. "You can't create ex post facto restrictions—"

"Watch me," Maria said.

The reporters were frantically taking notes. The blogger had his phone out, apparently live-streaming. The photographer was getting what would undoubtedly be excellent shots of Marcus's face as it cycled through several shades of rage.

Celeste, Marcus noticed, was smiling.

"I believe," she said, standing with the grace of someone who'd practiced the gesture for several centuries, "that the City Council is well within its rights to protect its citizens from predatory supernatural commerce. I'll be filing Heaven's full support for this injunction."

The ribbon outside remained uncut. The champagne remained unopened. And Marcus Hellstrom's carefully constructed career was beginning to crumble like ash.

Marcus felt the heat building in his core—not metaphorical heat, but the actual thermodynamic reality of contained hellfire trying to express itself through a mortal-seeming vessel. He'd spent six months in anger management seminars specifically to avoid this moment.

"Councilwoman," he said, and his voice carried harmonics that made the marble counter vibrate, "perhaps we should discuss this in private."

"I think public is perfect," Maria said, and Marcus realized with sinking certainty that she'd timed this specifically for the press. This wasn't about souls or safety. This was about her upcoming reelection campaign and the seventeen evangelical churches in her district.

The blogger's phone was definitely recording. The *Times* reporter looked like she'd stumbled into a much better story than expected. Even the bored photographer seemed engaged now, his camera clicking rapid-fire.

Cheryl touched Marcus's elbow. "Remember the training," she murmured. "Remember what they said about mortal bureaucrats. You can't threaten them. It only makes them stronger."

She was right. Heaven had learned this centuries ago: the best way to neutralize Hell wasn't holy water or exorcisms, it was paperwork. Regulations. Committees. The slow grinding machinery of bureaucratic process that could delay anything indefinitely while maintaining perfect moral superiority.

"Fine," Marcus said, forcing his temperature down, willing his human appearance back into stability. "Six months. We'll undergo your review."

"During which time," Maria said, clearly having prepared for this response, "your business operations will be suspended pending review completion."

"That's not—" Marcus stopped himself. His hands were clenched so tight his knuckles had gone white. Or whiter. He was never entirely sure what color his manifested bones were supposed to be. "You're intentionally stalling a legitimate business to score political points."

"I'm protecting my constituents from predatory soul harvesting," Maria said, and her smile was the kind of pure political calculation that Hell itself could admire. "If your business is as legitimate as you claim, six months won't matter."

"Six months is half a fiscal year. We have quarterly targets. Performance metrics. The Board—" He stopped again, realizing he was explaining Hell's corporate structure to a city councilwoman while reporters documented every word.

Celeste was still smiling that serene, infinitely patient smile that angels wore when watching demons fail. "Perhaps," she said, "this is an opportunity for reflection. To consider whether commodifying human souls really serves anyone's interests except Hell's quarterly earnings reports."

"Heaven has quarterly earnings reports," Marcus snapped.

"We call them 'grace distribution metrics,'" Celeste said. "It's different."

"It's literally not different. You have targets. You have performance reviews. You have middle management angels who haven't had an original thought since the Bronze Age pushing paperwork while actual suffering goes unaddressed because it's not in the current fiscal quarter's priorities."

The room went very quiet.

Celeste's smile didn't waver, but something cold flickered behind her eyes. "Mr. Hellstrom, I understand you're upset. But perhaps personal attacks aren't the most professional response to legitimate regulatory concerns."

The tech blogger raised his hand like he was in school. "So wait, Heaven has performance reviews?"

"Everyone has performance reviews," Cheryl muttered. "Existence is one long performance review with occasional breaks for suffering."

"Ms. Abaddon," Celeste said, turning her attention to Cheryl for the first time, "I notice your employment records show you've been in soul acquisition services since 1423. That's nearly six centuries of extracting human souls for infernal purposes. How do you reconcile that with any sense of morality?"

Cheryl looked at Celeste with an expression Marcus had seen exactly once before, when a minor demon in Processing had suggested reorganizing the filing system. "How do I reconcile it? I don't. I'm a demon. We're not big on reconciliation."

"But you were human once."

"Everyone was human once, even you. Being human doesn't make you good. It makes you complicated and mostly confused until you die and realize the universe is run by the same petty bureaucrats you dealt with at the DMV."

The *Times* reporter was writing so fast her pen was smoking.

"I think," Maria said, clearly trying to regain control of her own ambush, "we're getting off topic. The point is that this business poses serious ethical questions, and the City Council has a responsibility—"

The door chimed again.

This time the temperature didn't drop. It inverted. For a brief moment, Marcus felt like he was standing inside a star—not the heat, but the pressure, the sense of massive forces held in precarious equilibrium.

The man who entered wore a gray suit that cost more than most cars. His shoes were Italian leather. His tie was silk that seemed to shift colors depending on the angle of observation. He was in his sixties, distinguished, with silver hair and eyes that had the peculiar quality of seeming both very young and impossibly old.

Marcus felt his stomach drop through several theological dimensions.

"Mr. Hellstrom," the man said, and his voice was pleasant, cultured, and absolutely devoid of the harmonics that marked angelic or demonic speech. It was purely, perfectly human. "I'm Thomas Covenant. I represent the Infernal Banking Corporation's legal team. I understand there's been some regulatory confusion."

Maria Santos's expression flickered—just for a moment—with something that might have been fear or recognition.

Thomas turned to her with a smile that was friendly and utterly terrifying. "Councilwoman Santos. How lovely to see you again. I believe we met at that fundraiser last spring? The one at the Maritime Museum? You were very passionate about waterfront development."

"I don't—" Maria started.

"You don't remember," Thomas finished. "That's understandable. It was a crowded event. Though I do recall you mentioning some concerns about your son's college applications. Harvard, wasn't it? Very competitive these days."

The room had gone completely silent.

"Is that a threat?" Maria's voice was steady, but Marcus could see her hands trembling.

"A threat?" Thomas looked genuinely confused. "Councilwoman, I'm simply making conversation. Though since you mention it, I should inform you that attempting to impose ex post facto regulatory restrictions on a business that has satisfied all existing legal requirements is, itself, a violation of several federal commerce statutes. The Corporation would be forced to file suit. Federal court. Very public. Very expensive for the city."

"The city has excellent legal counsel."

"I'm sure you do. Though I notice the city's insurance policy specifically excludes coverage for lawsuits involving 'divine, infernal, or metaphysically ambiguous entities.' Paragraph forty-seven, subsection C. It's a common exclusion. Underwriters added it after the Vatican's lawsuit against Purgatory, Inc. in 2015."

Maria's face had gone pale.

"Of course," Thomas continued, "litigation is so unpleasant. Perhaps we could find a compromise? The Corporation undergoes your six-month review, but continues operating during that period? We could even agree to certain... restrictions. No soul acquisitions over, say, $500,000 in equivalent value. No transactions with city employees. Full quarterly transparency reports filed with your office."

He was offering her a face-saving exit, Marcus realized. A way to claim victory while backing down.

Maria looked at the reporters, at the cameras, at Celeste's carefully neutral expression. "I'll need to consult with the full Council."

"Of course. Take your time. We're not going anywhere." Thomas's smile widened fractionally. "We have a ten-year lease."

Maria left without another word, her heels clicking on the marble floor like a countdown timer.

Thomas turned to the reporters. "I apologize for the disruption, ladies and gentlemen. Municipal politics can be so complicated. Perhaps Mr. Hellstrom could continue his presentation?"

The *Times* reporter raised her hand. "Mr. Covenant, can you comment on the legal basis for soul transactions under federal law?"

"Gladly," Thomas said. "Under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act of 1993, spiritual transactions between consenting adults are protected provided they don't violate existing criminal statutes. Souls, being intangible personal assets, fall under property law as interpreted in the landmark case *Heaven v. Mephistopheles*, decided by the Ninth Circuit in 2003."

He was lying, Marcus realized. Or not lying exactly—Thomas never lied—but creating legal precedents that sounded real enough that no one would check until it was too late.

Celeste stood. "I think," she said quietly, "we need to discuss this somewhere more private."

Thomas turned to Celeste with the expression of a man who'd been expecting her objection for several centuries. "Ms. Lumière. How delightful that Heaven sent someone with actual authority instead of the usual compliance drones. Shall we use the conference room?"

"You have a conference room?" Marcus asked.

"Second floor. I had it installed yesterday." Thomas gestured toward the staircase Marcus had assumed led only to storage. "After you."

The conference room turned out to be significantly larger than the floor plan suggested, which meant Thomas had done something either architecturally impossible or metaphysically questionable. Probably both. The table was black walnut, the chairs were leather that had never touched a cow, and the windows looked out onto a Seattle skyline that was subtly wrong—the Space Needle was three degrees further north, and there were buildings that didn't quite exist.

"Pocket dimension?" Cheryl asked, running her hand along the wall.

"Sublease from a minor reality vendor," Thomas said. "Completely legal under the Extradimensional Property Act of 2018."

"There is no Extradimensional Property Act of 2018," Celeste said.

"Not yet. But there will be, retroactively, once our legal filing is processed. Causality is so flexible these days."

Marcus sat down heavily. His morning had officially exceeded his capacity for crisis management. "Thomas, what are you doing here? The Board said they were sending legal support if needed, but—"

"But you assumed they meant a junior demon with a law degree from Dartmouth." Thomas smiled. "Marcus, the Board doesn't invest in terrestrial branches lightly. When we commit resources, we commit properly. Which brings me to why I'm really here."

He pulled out a file folder—actual paper, which was somehow more ominous than any tablet—and slid it across the table.

"Your soul acquisition targets for Q1 are... ambitious."

Marcus felt his stomach tighten. "I know they're high, but I built the projections based on Seattle's demographics. Tech workers, venture capitalists, middle management types who are already selling pieces of themselves for marginal advancement—"

"You're projected to acquire fifteen hundred souls in the first quarter."

"Yes."

"That's more than our Singapore branch does in a year."

"Singapore has different market conditions. Seattle is prime territory. These people are desperate for meaning, validation, success. They're already one bad performance review away from existential crisis. We're just offering a clear transaction instead of the slow grind of corporate self-destruction."

Thomas tapped the folder. "And if you fail to meet these targets?"

Marcus didn't answer. He didn't need to. Everyone in Hell knew what happened to failed experiments.

Celeste leaned forward. "This is exactly why Heaven opposes terrestrial soul banking. You're not offering people salvation or damnation based on their moral choices. You're creating arbitrary quotas that incentivize aggressive acquisition regardless of actual sin or virtue."

"Heaven has quotas," Thomas said mildly.

"We have *goals*. For redemption. For grace distribution. It's not the same."

"Isn't it? You have performance metrics for angel assignments. Targets for prayer response times. I've seen your internal memos, Celeste. Heaven is as bureaucratic as anywhere else."

"How did you see our internal memos?"

Thomas smiled.

Cheryl, who'd been silent through this exchange, suddenly stood. "Marcus, we need to talk. Privately."

"Cheryl, whatever it is—"

"Now."

There was something in her voice that made Marcus follow her out into the hallway, leaving Thomas and Celeste in what would undoubtedly become either a theological debate or a legal cage match.

The hallway was narrow and smelled faintly of sulfur, which Marcus found oddly comforting after the sanitized horror of the conference room.

"What is it?"

Cheryl pulled out her tablet. "Remember that system conversion issue I kept trying to tell you about?"

"The one that's fixed?"

"It's not fixed. I *told* you it wasn't fixed. I said 'I should mention—' and then the angel arrived and you stopped listening."

Marcus pressed his palms to his eyes. "How bad?"

"The API integration is working now, but the data migration from Hell's central database didn't complete. We have no historical records. No precedents. No case files for comparison. Which means when we process our first soul acquisition, we'll have no way to properly value it."

"We use the standard assessment algorithm—"

"Which requires historical data to calibrate. Without it, we're guessing. And if we guess wrong, if we over-value or under-value a soul, the discrepancy goes straight into the quarterly reports. Which means—"

"Which means the Board will know immediately that we're operating blind."

"Exactly."

Marcus leaned against the wall, feeling the weight of six months of planning, persuasion, and political maneuvering threatening to collapse. "Can you pull the data manually?"

"From Hell's central database? Marcus, that thing runs on infrastructure that predates the Roman Empire. It's held together with prayer, spite, and the tears of damned IT professionals. A manual data pull would take weeks, maybe months."

"We don't have months. We have—" He checked his watch. "—about twenty minutes until the official opening ceremony, at which point we're expected to be fully operational."

Cheryl's expression softened slightly. "I can implement a workaround. Use industry averages, mortality tables, census data. It won't be precise, but it'll be close enough that the Board might not notice immediately."

"How close is close enough?"

"Seventy, maybe seventy-five percent accuracy."

"And the other twenty-five percent?"

"Could end up accidentally damning people who don't deserve it, or letting actual monsters walk away with bargain-basement soul prices."

Marcus laughed, and it came out slightly unhinged. "So basically we're creating a system that might randomly distribute damnation based on bad data and hopeful estimates."

"I mean, that's not that different from how mortality worked before modern medicine."

"Cheryl."

"I'm just saying, humans have been getting randomly screwed by cosmic systems for millennia. At least we're being efficient about it."

The door to the conference room opened. Thomas emerged looking pleased, which was never a good sign. Celeste followed, her expression carved from ice.

"We've reached an understanding," Thomas announced. "Heaven will allow the branch to operate for the full six-month review period, with certain conditions."

"What conditions?" Marcus asked, though he suspected he didn't want to know.

"Full transparency on all soul acquisitions over $100,000 in equivalent value. Monthly compliance audits. And—" Thomas paused for effect. "—Heaven gets to embed an observer in the branch. Full time. Starting today."

"Absolutely not," Marcus said.

"Already agreed to it," Thomas replied cheerfully. "Celeste will be your observer. She'll have full access to all systems, all transactions, all customer interactions."

Marcus looked at Celeste, who was smiling the way saints smile in paintings right before they're martyred. "You're joking."

"Mr. Hellstrom, I assure you I take soul commerce very seriously. I'll be here every day, nine to five, observing and documenting. Think of me as your guardian angel."

"I don't need a guardian angel. I need a functional branch with reasonable autonomy and minimal celestial interference."

"Then you should have thought of that before opening a soul bank in a city with active Heaven regulatory oversight." Celeste's smile widened. "I start tomorrow. I suggest you prepare appropriate workspace."

She left, her white pantsuit somehow remaining pristine despite the journey through Hell's pocket-dimension conference room.

Marcus turned to Thomas. "You gave Heaven direct access to our operations."

"I gave Heaven the illusion of oversight while securing operational freedom. She can observe all she wants. She can file her reports. But the agreement doesn't give her veto power over individual transactions. She can document, but she can't interfere. It's perfect."

"It's a disaster. She'll report every minor irregularity, every questionable acquisition—"

"Which will give you excellent documentation of compliance efforts when the Board reviews quarterly performance. Heaven's oversight actually protects you, Marcus. It demonstrates good faith engagement with regulatory concerns."

Cheryl made a sound that might have been agreement or might have been the noise a soul makes when it gives up.

Thomas checked his watch. "Fifteen minutes until the ceremony. I suggest you prepare your opening remarks. Make them inspirational. Humans love inspirational speeches about self-actualization and taking control of their destiny. Don't mention the quotas."

He left, taking the impossibly expensive suit and the implied threat of Thomas Covenant Legal Representation with him.

Marcus and Cheryl stood alone in the hallway.

"So," Cheryl said eventually. "We're opening a soul bank with broken systems, Heaven surveillance, and quarterly targets that require us to damn fifteen hundred people in three months."

"That's correct."

"And if we fail, you go back to Processing and I probably get reassigned to Cold Calls, which is literally just an eternity of telemarketing."

"Also correct."

"Want to just quit now? We could walk out. Become freelance demons. I hear there's good money in corporate consulting."

Marcus considered it. The door was right there. Thomas hadn't locked it. They could leave, abandon the branch, let Hell's grand experiment in terrestrial soul banking collapse before it began.

But then he'd spent six months for nothing. Eight decades in Processing for nothing. His entire death for nothing.

"No," he said. "We open. We meet the targets. We make this work."

"Even with seventy-five percent accuracy?"

"Even with seventy-five percent accuracy."

Cheryl nodded slowly. "You know this is probably going to end in some kind of cosmic legal battle that makes the news for weeks."

"Almost definitely."

"And we'll probably accidentally damn at least a few people who don't deserve it."

"Statistics suggest it's inevitable."

"And Heaven is going to document everything and use it against us."

"That's their entire operational strategy, yes."

Cheryl smiled, and it was the kind of smile Marcus imagined she'd worn during the Crusades. "Well then. Let's go damn some souls."

They walked back down to the main floor, where the reporters were still waiting, the champagne was still unopened, and the ceremonial ribbon across the door fluttered in a breeze that came from no earthly source.

The ribbon was red silk, which Marcus had initially thought was too on-the-nose until Thomas had explained that focus groups showed humans responded positively to "bold color choices in financial branding." Now, standing before it with oversized scissors that gleamed like they'd been forged in something other than a factory, Marcus wondered if he should have gone with the navy blue.

The reporters had repositioned themselves for optimal angles. The tech blogger was practically vibrating. Even the bored photographer seemed interested now, probably because the morning had involved legal threats, interdimensional real estate, and at least two beings who definitely weren't human trying to pretend they were.

"Ladies and gentlemen," Marcus began, activating his mortal charm protocol again. His voice smoothed, his features softened, the slight scent of brimstone that sometimes accompanied his emotions faded to something more like expensive cologne. "Thank you for your patience during that... regulatory discussion."

"Is the branch actually opening?" the *Times* reporter asked, pen poised.

"Absolutely. The Infernal Banking Corporation is proud to bring soul liquidity services to Seattle, working in full cooperation with all relevant oversight bodies." He managed not to choke on the words. "What you're witnessing today is the democratization of an ancient transaction. For too long, soul commerce has been restricted to the desperate, the criminal, the truly damned. We're bringing it to everyone."

"Why is that a good thing?" The question came from a woman Marcus hadn't noticed before—mid-forties, wearing a blazer and an expression of profound skepticism. Her press badge identified her as being from *The Stranger*, Seattle's alt-weekly.

"Because," Marcus said carefully, "transparency is always preferable to secrecy. Right now, people sell pieces of themselves every day. They take soul-crushing jobs for marginal pay increases. They compromise their values for career advancement. They sacrifice relationships, health, ethics—all for abstract promises of success or security. We're just making the transaction explicit."

"So you're admitting that modern capitalism already extracts souls, and you're just cutting out the middleman?"

Cheryl coughed. It sounded like laughter filtered through six centuries of cynicism.

"I'm saying," Marcus continued, "that we're offering clarity. A clear exchange: your immortal soul for concrete, measurable benefits. No ambiguity. No false promises. Just a straightforward transaction with clearly defined terms."

"And the downside?" the *Stranger* reporter pressed. "What's the catch?"

"Eternal damnation," Marcus said. "But that's clearly stated in all our promotional materials, as Ms. Lumière helpfully reminded us earlier."

The tech blogger's hand shot up. "How does eternal damnation actually work? Like, is it fire and brimstone, or more psychological torture, or—"

"That's proprietary information," Marcus said quickly. "Each damnation is customized to the individual based on their specific sins, fears, and psychological profile. We take personalization very seriously."

"That sounds worse than fire and brimstone," the *Times* reporter muttered, but she was writing it down.

Marcus gripped the scissors. The metal was warm, almost alive. He positioned the blades around the ribbon and paused, suddenly aware that this moment would define everything that followed. Once he cut this ribbon, the branch was officially open. Souls could be acquired. His quota clock would start ticking.

Seventy-five percent accuracy, he thought. Twenty-five percent margin of error. How many people would that hurt? How many would slip through cracks in the data, damned by bad algorithms and missing files?

But then, how many were already being hurt by the existing system? The informal soul-crushing that happened in corporate offices, the slow erosion of self that modern life demanded? At least his system was honest.

He cut the ribbon.

The silk parted with a sound like a sigh, and for just a moment, Marcus felt the weight of something vast and ancient clicking into place. Somewhere, in Hell's central bureaucracy, a file opened with his name on it. A clock started. Three months. Fifteen hundred souls.

The reporters clapped politely. The blogger whooped. The photographer captured the moment from six different angles.

"We're officially open," Marcus announced. "Banking hours are nine to five, Monday through Friday. We offer free consultations, competitive rates, and—for our first week only—a special promotion: fifteen percent bonus on all soul deposits over $50,000 in equivalent value."

"You're having a sale on eternal damnation?" The *Stranger* reporter looked like she couldn't decide whether to be appalled or impressed.

"We prefer to think of it as an introductory offer."

The door—the actual customer entrance door, not the ceremonial one—chimed. Marcus turned, expecting another reporter or perhaps Thomas returning to deliver more impossible news.

Instead, a young man entered. Early twenties, wearing a hoodie and the kind of carefully maintained stubble that suggested software engineering. He carried a messenger bag covered in laptop stickers and the hunted expression of someone who'd been awake for thirty-six hours straight.

He looked around the branch, taking in the marble floors, the interest rate display, the small crowd of press, and Marcus holding ceremonial scissors like a weapon.

"Is this," he asked, "actually a bank where I can sell my soul?"

The room went completely silent.

Marcus set down the scissors carefully. This was it. His first customer. The first soul acquisition in the history of Hell's terrestrial branch system. Everything depended on how he handled this.

"Yes," he said simply. "We offer soul liquidity services. Are you interested in a consultation?"

The young man nodded slowly. "I'm a game developer. Indie. I've been working on this project for three years. It's brilliant—it's going to revolutionize narrative design in open-world environments. But I'm out of money. Out of time. My girlfriend left me because I spent our rent money on asset licenses. My parents think I'm wasting my life. I just need... I need one break. One chance to finish it and show everyone I'm not crazy."

Marcus felt something twist in his chest. This was exactly the kind of customer he'd predicted: young, desperate, convinced that their dream was worth any price. The kind of person who would trade eternity for validation.

The kind of person he'd been, once, before the car accident that killed him and the decades in Processing that followed.

"How much do you think you need?" Marcus asked.

"Fifty thousand dollars. Maybe sixty, to be safe. That would cover development for six months, marketing, submission to festivals—"

"I mean, what do you think your soul is worth?"

The young man blinked. "I... I don't know. How do you value a soul?"

"That's what we do," Cheryl said, appearing beside Marcus with her tablet. She'd activated her professional teller mode: efficient, slightly cold, the voice of someone who'd processed thousands of these transactions and had learned not to think too hard about any of them. "We assess based on age, potential, accumulated sin, creative output, relationship bonds, and future probability vectors."

"That sounds complicated."

"It is. Please have a seat. This will take a few minutes."

The reporters were frantically documenting everything. The tech blogger was definitely live-streaming. Marcus could feel the weight of their attention, the sense that whatever happened next would define how the world understood soul banking.

The young man sat in one of the customer chairs. Cheryl sat across from him, tablet glowing with data entry fields that definitely didn't come from any mortal software company.

"Name?"

"Jason. Jason Chen."

Cheryl's fingers moved across the tablet. Marcus watched the data populate, pulling from sources he didn't want to think about too carefully—social media profiles, credit reports, things that should have required warrants and probably several violations of privacy law.

"Age twenty-three," Cheryl read. "Software engineer, currently unemployed. Student loan debt: $127,000. Credit score: 584. Relationship status: single. Creative works in progress: one, estimated market value..." She paused, frowning. "This is the problem."

Marcus moved closer. "What problem?"

"The system is trying to assess his game's potential value, but without historical data, it's just guessing. It's showing estimates ranging from $5,000 to $50 million depending on which algorithm it uses."

"Use the conservative estimate," Marcus said quietly.

"That would undervalue his soul significantly. If his game actually succeeds—"

"Use the conservative estimate."

Cheryl looked at him for a long moment, and Marcus saw something flicker in her ancient eyes. Understanding, maybe. Or pity. Then she tapped the tablet. "Estimated soul value: $180,000."

Jason's eyes widened. "That much?"

"Souls appreciate over time," Marcus said, falling back into his script. "You're young. You have creative potential. Strong family bonds, even if strained. No major sins beyond standard moral compromises. That makes your soul relatively valuable."

"So I could borrow the sixty thousand and still have equity?"

The word "equity" in reference to a human soul made Marcus want to laugh or cry or possibly both. "Essentially, yes. We'd retain title to your soul, but the excess value would remain yours until... well, until death."

"And then?"

"Then we collect. You spend eternity in Hell, experiencing personalized torment designed specifically for you."

Jason nodded like Marcus had just explained a mortgage structure. The reporters were writing furiously. The *Times* woman looked physically ill.

"Can I think about it?"

"Of course. We recommend full consideration of any soul transaction. Take as long as you need."

"Like, a week?"

"However long you need."

Jason stood, clutching his messenger bag. "I'll come back. I just need to... I need to think."

He left, and the door chimed behind him with a sound like opportunity departing.

The *Stranger* reporter raised her hand. "You almost had him. Why did you tell him to think about it? Isn't aggressive sales tactics kind of Hell's whole thing?"

"We prefer informed consent," Marcus said, though it physically pained him to use those words. "Coerced souls have diminished value. We want customers who understand exactly what they're trading."

What he didn't say: they needed customers who would hold up in any legal or celestial challenge. Heaven would be reviewing every transaction. One claim of coercion or fraud, and the entire branch could be shut down.

The press conference wound down after another twenty minutes of questions about interest rates, contractual terms, and whether Hell offered any kind of soul insurance (they did not). The reporters left with notebooks full of impossible quotes. The blogger left promising to make them viral. The photographer left with what would undoubtedly be very interesting pictures.

The branch was officially open.

And they had exactly zero souls acquired.

Cheryl locked the door behind the last reporter and leaned against it. "That could have gone worse."

"We let our first customer walk away."

"You let him walk away. I had the paperwork ready."

Marcus sat in one of the customer chairs, suddenly exhausted. Manifestation in the mortal world was always draining, but today felt especially heavy. "If we'd pushed him, if we'd used any of the usual tricks—"

"Celeste would have documented it. Heaven would have called it coercion. The branch would be shut down before lunch." Cheryl sat beside him. "You made the right call. Doesn't mean it felt good."

"We have quarterly targets."

"I know."

"Fifteen hundred souls in three months."

"I can do math, Marcus. I've been doing math since before calculus was invented."

"Then you know that letting customers walk away is not a viable strategy."

Cheryl pulled out her tablet, scrolling through data. "We had forty-seven unique website visits this morning. Eighteen phone calls. Seven email inquiries. The press is going to make us famous, or infamous, which amounts to the same thing for our purposes. People will come."

"Will they sign?"

"Some will. The desperate ones. The ambitious ones. The ones who've already given up on redemption and just want something concrete to show for it." She paused. "The ones like us."

Marcus looked at her. In six hundred years, Cheryl had probably processed tens of thousands of souls. She'd seen empires rise and fall. She'd watched humanity invent new ways to damn themselves with every technological advancement. And she was still here, still processing, still maintaining her precise records and her bitter humor.

"Do you ever regret it?" he asked. "Your choice?"

"Every day," Cheryl said. "But regret doesn't pay the bills. Neither does redemption, for that matter." She stood, smoothing her practical slacks. "We should prepare for tomorrow. Celeste will be here at nine sharp, and she strikes me as someone who arrives ten minutes early just to prove a point."

"What do we do about the data problem?"

"I'll implement the workaround tonight. It won't be perfect, but it'll be functional. We'll refine the algorithms as we process more transactions. Eventually, we'll have enough local data that we won't need Hell's central database."

"Eventually doesn't help us with quarterly targets."

"No," Cheryl agreed. "But it's what we have."

She headed upstairs to what Marcus assumed was probably an impossible office space, leaving him alone in the branch. The marble floors gleamed. The interest rate display scrolled its demonic promises. The ceremonial ribbon lay in two pieces on the floor, severed and somehow still beautiful.

His phone buzzed. A message from Thomas: "Excellent opening. Board is pleased. Remember: targets are non-negotiable. —T"

Marcus deleted it and sat in the silence of his brand-new branch, thinking about Jason Chen and his revolutionary game, about quarterly targets and seventy-five percent accuracy, about the twenty-five percent who would slip through the cracks and be damned by bad data and missing files.

Tomorrow, Celeste would arrive with her tablet and her celestial clipboard and her absolute certainty that she was on the right side of an ancient war.

Tomorrow, customers would come with their desperate dreams and their negotiable ethics.

Tomorrow, the real work would begin.

But tonight, Marcus Hellstrom sat alone in Hell's first terrestrial branch and wondered if democratizing damnation was really any better than the old system, or just more efficient.

The interest rate display flickered, and for just a moment, the scrolling text changed to something new: "WELCOME TO THE FUTURE OF SOUL COMMERCE. PLEASE TAKE A NUMBER."

Marcus laughed, alone in the dark, because what else could you do when you'd just opened a bank for the damned and let your first customer walk away?

The marble floor was cold beneath his feet. The city outside continued its endless rush. And somewhere in Hell's vast bureaucracy, a clock was ticking down his three months.

He had fifteen hundred souls to acquire.

He had seventy-five percent accuracy.

He had a celestial observer arriving in the morning.

Marcus stood, straightened his tie, and locked the door. Tomorrow would come soon enough, with all its impossible demands and moral compromises.

Tonight, he would let himself doubt.

After all, even demons were allowed one moment of uncertainty before the real damnation began.

Marcus woke at 6:47 a.m. in his apartment—a studio in Capitol Hill that cost $2,400 a month and had a view of a brick wall—to the sound of his phone vibrating with the specific rhythm that meant Hell's central server was having problems.

He lay in bed for thirty seconds, staring at the ceiling and considering whether eternal oblivion might be preferable to checking his email. Then professional obligation won over existential despair, and he reached for the phone.

Seventeen messages. Sixteen were automated system alerts about data synchronization failures. The seventeenth was from someone named Malphas with a signature line that read "Vice President of Terrestrial Operations, Third Circle."

Marcus had never heard of Malphas, which meant Malphas was either very new or very old. In Hell's bureaucracy, both options were equally dangerous.

The message was brief: "Branch performance review scheduled for end of Q1. Expect in-person evaluation. Non-negotiable attendance. Achievement of quota targets will be primary assessment metric. —M"

Marcus set the phone down carefully, as if it might explode.

Three months. One evaluation. Fifteen hundred souls, or back to Processing.

He showered in water that never quite got hot enough, dressed in the same gray suit he'd worn yesterday—manifestation meant his clothes never actually got dirty, one of the few perks of being post-mortem—and arrived at the branch at 8:15.

Cheryl was already there, which shouldn't have been possible since he hadn't given her a key. She sat at her teller station wearing a different but equally sensible outfit, surrounded by three laptops, two tablets, and what appeared to be a very old book bound in something that was definitely not leather.

"Don't ask how I got in," she said without looking up.

"I wasn't going to."

"The data workaround is functional. I pulled mortality tables from the CDC, sin probability matrices from a Jesuit database that really should have better security, and economic projections from the Federal Reserve. Fed the whole thing into our assessment algorithm along with some creative assumptions about metaphysical value depreciation."

"Will it work?"

"Define 'work.'" Cheryl finally looked up, and Marcus saw that she'd been awake for considerably longer than was advisable even for someone who technically didn't need sleep. "It'll produce numbers. Whether those numbers accurately represent the eternal value of human souls is a theological question I'm not qualified to answer."

"You've been processing souls for six hundred years."

"Exactly. Which is how I know I'm not qualified. The more you do this, the less sense any of it makes." She gestured at her screens. "I ran test scenarios. Based on our algorithm, a pediatric oncologist with two kids and a gambling problem is worth $340,000. A venture capitalist who donates to effective altruism charities but emotionally abuses his staff is worth $890,000. A grad student studying medieval poetry who volunteers at a homeless shelter is worth $23,000."

Marcus felt something cold settle in his stomach. "The algorithm prioritizes economic impact over moral virtue."

"The algorithm prioritizes whatever generates the most value for Hell's quarterly reports. Moral virtue is a depreciating asset in modern capitalism." Cheryl's expression was unreadable. "We could adjust the weighting. Make it prioritize different variables."

"Like what?"

"Kindness. Compassion. The amount of good someone might do if they weren't damned." She paused. "But that would lower our acquisition values across the board. Which means we'd need more souls to hit quota. Which means more people damned overall."

Marcus sat down at the desk that was apparently his—solid oak, expensive chair, nameplate that read MARCUS HELLSTROM, BRANCH MANAGER in letters that seemed to shift between Latin and something older. "So we can damn fewer people for more value, or more people for less value, but either way we're damning people."

"Welcome to middle management," Cheryl said. "Where every decision is a moral compromise wrapped in a performance metric."

The door chimed. 8:47 a.m. Celeste was, predictably, thirteen minutes early.

She entered like she was conducting an inspection, white pantsuit somehow even more pristine than yesterday, carrying a briefcase that hummed with barely contained divine energy. Her gaze swept the branch with the precision of someone cataloging violations.

"Mr. Hellstrom. Ms. Abaddon." She set her briefcase on the counter with a sound like a gavel. "I'll need desk space, network access, and copies of all operational procedures. I'll also require a list of every customer interaction, including the gentleman from yesterday—Jason Chen, I believe?"

"He didn't sign anything," Marcus said. "There's nothing to report."

"Consultation attempts are still reportable under the Soul Commerce Transparency Act. Section seven, subsection three: 'All entities engaged in soul acquisition must document initial contact, terms discussed, and outcome.'" Celeste pulled out a tablet. "I'll need Mr. Chen's full name, contact information, and a summary of what you offered him."

Cheryl made a sound like grinding gears. "We offered him a consultation. That's literally the only thing that happened."

"Then writing the report should be very easy." Celeste's smile could have frozen hydrogen. "I'll expect it by noon."

Marcus felt his temperature rising—literally, the ambient heat in the room ticking up three degrees. "Ms. Lumière, if you're going to document every conversation, every consultation, every person who walks through that door—"

"Yes?"

"—then you're going to be filing a lot of reports."

"I'm aware. I brought extra storage." She patted her briefcase.

The door chimed again. 8:53 a.m.

A woman entered, mid-fifties, wearing scrubs and exhaustion like a second skin. She had the look of someone who'd worked a double shift and then decided that selling her soul was more manageable than one more patient interaction.

"Is this the place?" she asked. "The soul bank?"

Marcus stood, activating his mortal charm protocol. "Yes. Welcome to Infernal Banking Corporation. I'm Marcus Hellstrom, branch manager. How can we help you today?"

The woman laughed, and it sounded like something breaking. "I'm a nurse. Fifteen years in the ICU. I'm so tired I can't remember what day it is. My daughter needs college tuition. My ex-husband needs to be launched into the sun. And I just need—" She stopped, swaying slightly. "I need to not be so fucking tired all the time."

Cheryl was already standing, moving around the counter with a smoothness that suggested she'd caught collapsing customers before. "Ma'am, why don't you sit down? Can I get you water?"

"I don't want water. I want my soul to be worth enough to fix this." The woman sat heavily in a customer chair. "Can you do that? Can you make it so I'm not drowning anymore?"

Marcus glanced at Celeste, who was watching with that particular angelic expression that suggested she was already writing her report in her head.

"What's your name?" he asked gently.

"Linda. Linda Morrison."

Cheryl was already pulling up data on her tablet, though she angled it away from Celeste's sight line. Marcus saw numbers populating, algorithms running, the system trying to quantify fifteen years of saving lives against crushing debt and exhausted desperation.

"Linda," Marcus said, sitting across from her, "before we discuss any transaction, I need to make sure you understand what we're offering. We provide immediate financial relief in exchange for your immortal soul. After death, you would be transferred to Hell's custody for eternal processing."

"I know what damnation is," Linda said. "I'm Catholic. Or I was, before I spent enough time in the ICU to realize that if God's up there, he's not paying attention to the people I watch die."

Celeste made a small sound of disapproval.

"Our assessment process takes about twenty minutes," Marcus continued. "We'll evaluate your soul's current value based on age, accumulated life choices, potential future impact, and other factors. Then we'll make you an offer. You can accept, decline, or negotiate terms."

"How much do souls usually go for?"
