She was peeling her fifth potato when Sister Rebecca came into the kitchen, moving quickly, her face pale. She was one of the older women, nearly forty, and she taught the younger children in the schoolhouse.

"Maya," she said quietly, glancing over her shoulder. "Have you seen Iris this morning?"

"She's cleaning Brother Nathaniel's house. Why?"

Sister Rebecca moved closer, her voice dropping to barely a whisper. "I saw him looking at her yesterday. During the singing. You know that look."

Maya's hands stilled on the potato. "I know."

"You need to be careful," Sister Rebecca said, and there was something in her voice—a kind of desperate urgency—that made Maya look up from the cutting board. The older woman's eyes were red-rimmed, her mouth tight. "I know you think about leaving. Don't pretend you don't. I've seen it in your face."

Maya's breath caught. To speak of leaving was forbidden. To even think it was considered a betrayal of the community, of God's plan. But Sister Rebecca was still talking, her words coming faster now.

"There's a way," she whispered. "Through the eastern fence, where it runs along the creek bed. The wire is loose there—has been for months. Brother Nathaniel doesn't know, or maybe he just hasn't bothered to fix it. You could squeeze through. Both of you."

"Why are you telling me this?" Maya kept her voice low, her hands resuming their work in case anyone else came into the kitchen.

Sister Rebecca's laugh was bitter and small. "Because I was nineteen once. Because Brother Nathaniel chose me for sacred union when I was twenty, and I've spent twenty years paying for it." She touched Maya's wrist, just briefly. "Because your sister reminds me of my daughter."

Maya had known Sister Rebecca had children—three of them, somewhere in the compound—but she'd never heard her speak of a daughter. "Where—"

"She died," Sister Rebecca said flatly. "Ten years ago. Childbirth. She was sixteen." Her fingers tightened on Maya's wrist. "Don't let him take Iris. Whatever you have to do."

Before Maya could respond, they heard footsteps on the porch. Sister Rebecca stepped back, her face smoothing into the placid mask they all learned to wear. Sister Anne came through the door with an armload of firewood, and the moment was gone.

But Maya's heart was hammering. A loose section of fence. An escape route. Hope, sharp and painful, bloomed in her chest.

She worked through the morning in a daze of planning. They would need supplies—food, water, warm clothes. Money, if she could find any. A map would be ideal, but she hadn't seen one in years. The compound kept no such things. Brother Nathaniel said maps were tools of the corrupt world, designed to pull people away from their true home.

When Iris returned from cleaning duty just before lunch, Maya pulled her aside near the women's washroom.

"Did he..." Maya couldn't finish the sentence.

Iris shook her head, but her hands were trembling. "He just talked. About purity and purpose and sacred duty. He said I was reaching the age of flowering, that God had special plans for me." She swallowed hard. "Maya, I'm scared."

"I know." Maya looked around to make sure they were alone, then lowered her voice to barely a breath. "We're leaving. Soon. Maybe tonight."

Iris's eyes went wide. "How?"

"I'll tell you later. Just—pack anything small you can't live without. Hide it under your mattress. And act normal. Can you do that?"

Her sister nodded, though she looked terrified.

The afternoon passed with agonizing slowness. Maya was assigned to laundry duty, boiling clothes in great steel tubs behind the kitchen house. The work was hot and endless, steam rising into the cool October air. She scrubbed and wrung and hung while her mind turned over the problem of supplies. They couldn't take much—whatever they carried would have to fit in their pockets or a single small bag. Too much would be noticed.

She was hanging Brother Nathaniel's shirts on the line when she found it: a twenty-dollar bill folded small and forgotten in a pocket. Her heart leapt. It wasn't much, but it was something. She palmed it quickly and tucked it into her bra.

At dinner, the community gathered in the Meeting Hall. Long tables filled with people—sixty-three souls in total, Maya knew, because Brother Nathaniel counted them every Sunday and announced the number like a victory. Sixty-three people who had turned their backs on the sinful world and embraced his vision of righteousness.

Sixty-three people who ate thin stew and stale bread while Brother Nathaniel's table groaned with roasted chicken and fresh vegetables.

Maya sat with the other unmarried women, Iris beside her. Across the room, Brother Nathaniel held court at the head table with his inner circle—Brother Thomas, Brother Paul, Brother David, and the married men who'd proven their loyalty over the years. As Maya watched, Brother Nathaniel's gaze drifted toward their table. Toward Iris.

He smiled, and Maya felt ice form in her stomach.

After dinner came evening prayer, then free time until lights out at nine. Maya used the hour to slip into the storage shed while everyone else was gathered for the evening's Bible study. She'd volunteered to mend torn work pants, giving her an excuse to access the sewing supplies. But once inside, she moved quickly to the back shelves.

Candles. Matches. A coil of rope. She found a canvas satchel and began filling it, her hands working fast. Dried apples from last year's harvest. A jar of preserved peaches. Two dented canteens that might go unnoticed. A folding knife with a rusted blade.

"What are you doing?"

Maya spun around, her heart in her throat.

Brother David stood in the doorway, backlit by the setting sun. He was thirty, broad-shouldered, with a scar across his jaw from some accident before he'd come to the compound. He'd always made Maya nervous—there was something watchful about him, something that suggested he saw more than he should.

"Sewing supplies," Maya said, trying to keep her voice steady. "For the mending."

He stepped inside and closed the door behind him. Maya's pulse hammered. The shed suddenly felt very small, very dark.

"You're lying," he said quietly.

Maya's mouth went dry. Brother David moved closer, his boots scuffing against the wooden floor. The satchel sat on the shelf behind her, half-filled with contraband. If he looked past her shoulder, if he saw what she'd gathered, it would all be over.

"I don't know what you mean," she said, but her voice came out thin and unconvincing.

He stopped three feet away, studying her face. In the dim light filtering through the shed's single grimy window, his expression was unreadable. "You've been different lately. Distracted. And I've seen the way you watch the fence line during work detail."

Maya's mind raced. Deny everything? Try to run? She was fast, but he was stronger, and the yard outside would be filled with people finishing their evening chores. She'd never make it to the dormitory, never warn Iris.

"Brother Nathaniel has plans for your sister," David said, and something in his tone made Maya look up sharply. "I heard him talking to Brother Thomas yesterday. He's going to announce the betrothal this Sunday. During service."

Three days. They had three days.

"Why are you telling me this?" Maya asked.

David glanced toward the door, then back at her. When he spoke again, his voice was barely audible. "Because I had a sister once. Before I came here. She was sixteen when our father—" He stopped, his jaw tightening. "I couldn't help her. But maybe I can help you."

Maya stared at him, trying to parse whether this was genuine or some kind of test. Brother Nathaniel was known for using loyal followers to root out dissent, to catch people in moments of weakness.

"I don't need help," she said carefully. "I'm content here. We both are."

David's laugh was quiet and mirthless. "You're a terrible liar, Maya." He reached past her, and she flinched, but he was only pulling down the satchel. He looked inside, then met her eyes again. "You're planning to run."

It wasn't a question. Maya said nothing.

"The east fence," David continued. "Where it crosses the creek. Sister Rebecca told you, didn't she?"

Maya's breath caught. "How did you—"

"She's been trying to get people out for years. Did she tell you that? Most of them get caught. Brother Nathaniel has men watching that section now. Has for months." He set the satchel on the shelf and crossed his arms. "If you try to go that way, you'll be dragged back before you make it a hundred yards."

Maya felt the hope that had been building all day crumble into ash. "Then why are you here? To gloat? To turn me in?"

"To offer you a better way out."

She blinked. "What?"

David moved to the window and peered out, checking the yard. Satisfied they were still alone, he turned back to her. "The main gate. The padlock."

"Brother Nathaniel has the only key. Everyone knows that."

"He has a key," David corrected. "I have the other one."

Maya felt the world tilt slightly. "You're lying."

"I've been planning this for eight months. Watching patterns, gathering supplies, waiting for the right moment." He pulled something from his pocket—a small silver key that caught the fading light. "I was going alone. But if you and Iris come with me, we can help each other. Three people have a better chance than one."

"Why wait?" Maya demanded. "If you have the key, why haven't you left already?"

"Because I'm not stupid. The gate might get me past the compound, but there's still twenty miles of mountain road before we hit civilization. Brother Nathaniel has friends in town—the sheriff, half the gas station attendants, people at the grocery store. They all think he's running some kind of wholesome religious retreat up here. They report anyone suspicious." David's voice hardened. "I need to get far enough away that he can't just make a phone call and have me dragged back. That means timing, supplies, and a solid head start before anyone notices we're gone."

Maya studied him, trying to see past the surface to whatever truth lay beneath. "How do I know this isn't a trap? That you're not just trying to catch me so you can look good in front of Brother Nathaniel?"

"You don't," David admitted. "But ask yourself this—what do I gain from trapping you? Brother Nathaniel already trusts me. I'm on the inner council. I get better food, better housing, more freedom than almost anyone here except the other Brothers." He leaned against the shelf. "I don't need to prove anything to him. And I sure as hell wouldn't waste my time playing games when I could just tell him what I've seen."

It made sense. A sick, dangerous kind of sense. Maya wanted to believe him—God, she wanted to—but fourteen years in the compound had taught her that trust was a luxury that could get you killed.

"When?" she asked finally.

"Tomorrow night. Two AM. Brother Paul has gate watch, and he sleeps through most of his shift. I've tested it twice—thrown rocks near his post, made noise. He doesn't even stir." David pulled a folded piece of paper from his pocket and handed it to her. "I drew this from memory. It's a map to the highway. Once we're there, we can hitchhike or walk to the next town. I've got two hundred dollars hidden. It's not much, but it'll get us bus tickets somewhere far."

Maya unfolded the paper. The map was crude but detailed—the compound, the road, landmarks he'd noted during the furniture delivery runs he sometimes made with Brother Nathaniel. Her hands shook slightly as she studied it.

"Why Iris and me?" she asked. "There are other people here who'd want out."

"Because you're already planning to run, which means you're desperate enough to take the risk. And because—" He hesitated. "Because I know what's going to happen to your sister if she stays. I've seen it happen before. Brother Nathaniel likes them young. He likes them scared. And he likes breaking them."

The words hung in the air between them, ugly and true.

"What about Sister Rebecca?" Maya asked. "She helped me. I can't just leave her."

"She won't come. I've asked before." David's expression softened slightly. "She thinks she deserves this. Thinks she's being punished for some sin she committed in her past life. You can't save someone who doesn't want to be saved."

Maya folded the map and tucked it into her pocket. "If this is a trap—"

"It's not."

"But if it is, I'll kill you myself before I let Brother Nathaniel take my sister."

David nodded slowly. "Fair enough." He moved toward the door, then paused. "Two AM tomorrow. Be ready. And Maya?" He looked back at her. "Don't tell anyone. Not Sister Rebecca, not your sister—not yet. The fewer people who know, the less chance of someone letting it slip."

"Iris needs to know."

"Tell her tomorrow afternoon. Give her just enough time to prepare but not enough time to panic and give us away." He opened the door a crack, checking the yard again. "And for God's sake, act normal. Eat dinner, go to prayers, do everything exactly like you always do."

Then he was gone, slipping out into the twilight like a shadow.

Maya stood alone in the shed, her heart pounding so hard she thought it might burst through her ribs. She looked at the satchel of supplies, at the key David had showed her, proof that sat only in her memory now. Every instinct screamed that this was too good to be true, that she was being set up, that tomorrow would end in the isolation room or worse.

But what choice did she have? Sunday was three days away. If Brother Nathaniel announced Iris's betrothal, there would be no escape. The community would celebrate, would watch Iris like hawks to make sure she didn't run, would guard her until the sacred union was complete.

Tomorrow night. Two AM.

Maya grabbed the satchel and emptied it back onto the shelves, arranging everything to look undisturbed. She found the thread and needles she'd supposedly come for, tucked them under her arm, and stepped out into the evening.

The dormitory was thick with the smell of unwashed bodies and old wool blankets when Maya returned. She went through the motions of preparing for bed—washing her face in the basin, changing into her nightgown, folding her day clothes on the trunk at the foot of her bunk. Iris was already under her covers, facing the wall, but Maya could tell from her breathing that she wasn't asleep.

Maya wanted to tell her. Wanted to whisper everything David had said, to share the terrible weight of hope that pressed against her chest. But his words echoed in her mind: *Don't tell anyone. Not yet.*

She climbed into bed and lay staring at the ceiling beams. Around her, the dormitory settled into sleep. Someone coughed. A bed frame creaked. Outside, the wind picked up, rattling the loose boards on the north wall. Maya listened to these familiar sounds and tried to imagine never hearing them again. Tried to imagine a world beyond the fence.

The problem was, she almost couldn't. Fourteen years was a long time. Long enough that her memories of before—of her parents, of a house with carpets and a television, of school buses and grocery stores—had faded to the texture of dreams. She knew they'd been real once, but they felt distant and uncertain, like stories someone had told her about someone else's life.

What would it be like out there now? She'd been eight when they'd arrived at the compound. The year 2009. Brother Nathaniel had said the world was ending even then, collapsing under the weight of its own sin. He'd shown them newspaper clippings about wars and disasters, about economic collapse and moral decay. He'd said the compound was an ark, a safe harbor while everything else drowned.

But it was 2023 now. Fourteen years later. Had the world ended, like he'd promised? Or was it still out there, still turning, full of people who didn't spend their days waiting for divine judgment?

Maya turned these questions over in her mind until exhaustion finally pulled her under.

She dreamed of the isolation room. Of darkness so complete it felt solid, pressing against her eyes. She dreamed of her mother's face—or what she thought was her mother's face, though the features were blurred and uncertain. She dreamed of a chain-link fence that stretched upward forever, disappearing into clouds.

When the prayer bell rang at five-thirty, Maya woke with a gasp.

One more day. Just one more day to survive without giving anything away.

Morning prayer was agony. Maya knelt on the hard floor and kept her head bowed, but she could feel Brother Nathaniel's attention sweeping across the congregation like a searchlight. When his gaze passed over her, she forced herself to remain perfectly still, to give nothing away.

"The world outside these walls grows darker every day," Brother Nathaniel intoned from his pulpit. "But we remain pure. We remain faithful. We are the chosen, the elect, the ones who will inherit the earth when the corruption has been burned away."

Maya had heard variations of this sermon hundreds of times. The words rolled over her like water.

"And as we prepare for the Reckoning, we must ensure our numbers grow. We must raise up the next generation in righteousness." His voice took on a warmer tone, almost fatherly. "This Sunday, I will announce a joyful union. A sacred joining that will strengthen our community and please the Lord."

Maya felt Iris stiffen beside her. Around them, the other women and girls shifted, some with excitement, some with dread. Everyone knew what such announcements meant. Someone's daughter, someone's sister, would be chosen.

"The young woman in question has been blessed with beauty and virtue," Brother Nathaniel continued. "She will be a vessel for the pure souls waiting to be born into our fold."

Don't look at her, Maya thought desperately. Don't look at Iris. Don't give him the satisfaction.

But Brother Nathaniel's gaze found them anyway, settling on Iris with unmistakable intent. He smiled—a smile Maya had seen him direct at other girls before their names were called. A smile that said: You belong to me now.

Iris's hand found Maya's beneath the folds of their skirts. Her sister's fingers were ice-cold and trembling.

After prayer, Maya managed to catch Iris in the narrow hallway between the Meeting Hall and the dining area. She pulled her into the corner by the coat hooks, where they had a few seconds of relative privacy.

"Don't panic," Maya whispered. "I have a plan."

"He's going to announce it Sunday. Everyone knows it's me. Did you see the way he looked—"

"I know. But we won't be here Sunday." Maya glanced over her shoulder to make sure they were alone. "We're leaving. Tomorrow night."

Iris's eyes went wide. "How?"

"I'll tell you everything later. Right now, just focus on getting through today. Act normal. Don't do anything to draw attention."

"Maya, if we get caught—"

"We won't." Maya tried to sound more certain than she felt. "Just trust me. Can you do that?"

Iris nodded, though she looked terrified. "What do I need to do?"

"Pack light. Only what you can fit in your pockets—nothing that will look suspicious if someone sees you. I'll have everything else ready."

They separated as footsteps approached. Sister Anne rounded the corner with an armload of hymnals, and Maya busied herself adjusting her coat on its hook. Her heart hammered, but she kept her expression neutral.

The day crawled by. Maya was assigned to the gardens, harvesting the last of the squash before the first hard freeze. She worked alongside three other women, her hands moving automatically while her mind spun through everything that could go wrong. What if David had been lying? What if Brother Paul didn't sleep through his watch? What if someone saw them crossing the yard toward the gate?

What if they made it out but got caught on the road?

What if Brother Nathaniel sent people after them?

What if they escaped into a world that was nothing like Maya remembered, a world that had no place for two women who'd spent half their lives locked behind a fence?

She was so lost in thought that she didn't notice Sister Rebecca approaching until the older woman was right beside her, kneeling in the dirt.

"You're going to try it," Sister Rebecca murmured, her hands busy pulling weeds. To anyone watching, they would look like two women working in companionable silence. "I can see it in your face."

Maya said nothing, but her hands stilled on the squash plant.

"Don't use the eastern fence," Sister Rebecca continued. "It's a trap. He knows about it."

"I know," Maya said quietly. "Someone already told me."

Sister Rebecca nodded slowly. "Good. That's good." She pulled another weed, her movements deliberate. "When you get out—if you get out—don't trust the sheriff. Don't trust anyone in town who might recognize you from the compound. They all think Brother Nathaniel's some kind of saint."

"How far do we need to go?"

"At least two towns over. Maybe three." Sister Rebecca's voice was barely audible now. "There's a women's shelter in Cascade Springs. About sixty miles west. If you can get there, they'll help you. They don't ask questions."

Maya committed the name to memory. Cascade Springs. Sixty miles.

"Why are you helping me?" Maya asked. "If we get caught, he'll know you knew something."

Sister Rebecca was quiet for a long moment. When she finally spoke, her voice was thick with emotion. "Because my daughter didn't get a chance to run. Because I didn't help her when I should have. Because I've spent ten years watching other girls disappear into the same trap that killed her." She turned to look at Maya directly, her eyes fierce. "Get your sister out of here. Whatever it takes."

That afternoon, Maya found a moment to pull Iris aside near the laundry lines. The sheets hanging around them provided a curtain of privacy, the white fabric snapping in the wind.

"Tonight," Maya said. "Two AM. Meet me by the supply shed. Wear your warmest clothes to bed—layers, but nothing that looks unusual. Bring nothing you can't hide."

"Who's helping us?" Iris asked. "How are we getting past the gate?"

Maya hesitated. She still wasn't entirely sure she could trust David, but they were past the point of no return now. "Brother David. He has a key to the padlock."

Iris's face paled. "Brother David? Maya, he's one of them. He's on the inner council."

"I know. But he says he's been planning to leave for months. He has money, supplies, a map." Maya gripped her sister's shoulders. "I don't know if we can trust him. But I know what happens if we stay."

Iris swallowed hard. "Okay. Okay. Two AM."

They separated quickly as Sister Anne emerged from the kitchen house, carrying an empty laundry basket. Maya forced herself to go back to work, to fold sheets and hang shirts and act like her world wasn't about to change forever.

Dinner that evening was worse than usual. Brother Nathaniel held forth from the head table about purity and obedience, his voice booming through the Meeting Hall. Maya could barely taste the food—some kind of bean stew that sat heavy in her stomach. Across the table, Iris picked at her bread, her face bloodless.

After dinner came evening prayer, then the hour of personal reflection before lights out. Maya volunteered for dish duty, which gave her an excuse to be in the kitchen house after most people had gone to their dormitories. When she was certain she was alone, she slipped into the pantry and grabbed what she could—a tin of crackers, some dried meat, two apples that were starting to go soft. She wrapped everything in a dish towel and hid it behind a loose board near the back door.

Her hands were shaking. This was real. This was actually happening.

She returned to the dormitory just before lights out. Iris was already in bed, and Maya climbed into her own bunk without speaking. The dormitory fell into darkness. Around her, women and girls settled into sleep, their breathing gradually evening out into familiar rhythms.

Maya lay awake, fully dressed beneath her nightgown. She'd put on two pairs of socks, her thickest shirt, and her long underwear. If anyone noticed, she'd say she was cold. That the night winds were getting harsher. That she couldn't seem to get warm.

The hours crawled by. Midnight came and went. Maya watched the shadows on the ceiling and counted her heartbeats, trying to keep them slow and steady.

One-thirty. Outside, an owl called. Someone in the dormitory turned over in their sleep, their bed frame creaking.

One-forty-five. Maya's muscles were tight with tension, her jaw clenched so hard it ached.

Two AM.

Maya sat up slowly, every movement careful and controlled. She pulled her boots from under the bed and laced them in the darkness, her fingers fumbling with the leather straps. Around her, the dormitory breathed the deep, even breath of sleep.

She stood, testing each floorboard before putting her full weight down. One step. Two. Three. The door was ten feet away, but it felt like miles.

She was halfway there when someone stirred.

Maya froze, her heart in her throat.

Iris sat up in her bunk, already dressed. She'd been waiting, her boots in her hands. Their eyes met in the darkness, and Maya saw her own fear reflected back.

Together, they moved toward the door.

The dormitory door opened with a sound that seemed deafening in the silence—a soft creak of hinges that Maya had heard a thousand times before but never quite like this. She and Iris slipped through, easing it closed behind them with agonizing slowness.

Outside, the October night was clear and brutally cold. Stars scattered across the sky like broken glass, and the moon hung fat and bright above the pines. Maya's breath plumed white in the air. Beside her, Iris was barely more than a shadow, her dark coat blending into the darkness.

They moved along the side of the dormitory, keeping to the deepest shadows. The compound lay quiet around them—the Meeting Hall a dark bulk against the sky, the other buildings silent and still. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked once and fell silent.

The supply shed was fifty yards away, across open ground. Maya's heart hammered as they broke from cover, half-running, half-walking, trying to move quickly without making noise. The yard seemed vast and exposed under the moonlight. Every step felt like a betrayal, footprints in the frost that would point back to them like accusations.

They reached the shed and pressed themselves against its wooden wall, breathing hard. Maya counted to thirty, listening. Nothing stirred. No shouts of alarm, no running footsteps.

"Where is he?" Iris whispered, her voice barely audible.

Maya shook her head. She didn't know. Two AM, David had said. They were here. But the shed's dark doorway remained empty.

Minutes passed. Maya felt sweat forming under her layers despite the cold. What if he'd changed his mind? What if this had been a test after all, and right now he was reporting to Brother Nathaniel that the Coleman sisters had tried to run?

Then a figure detached itself from the shadows near the workshop, moving toward them with quick, purposeful strides.

David.

He carried a large canvas pack over one shoulder and had a wool cap pulled low over his ears. When he reached them, his face was grim.

"Thought you might not come," he said quietly.

"Thought you might not either," Maya shot back.

David's mouth twitched—not quite a smile. "Fair." He glanced at Iris, who was staring at him with undisguised suspicion. "You brought her up to speed?"

"She knows enough," Maya said. "Do you have the key?"

He patted his pocket. "Gate's about two hundred yards that way. Brother Paul's in the guard shack—I checked twenty minutes ago. Dead asleep, like I said." He shrugged the pack higher on his shoulder. "We stay quiet, stay low, we should make it without him ever knowing we were there."

"And if he wakes up?" Iris asked.

David pulled something from his belt—a heavy wrench, the kind used in the workshop. "Then I handle it."

Maya felt her stomach turn. "You'd hurt him?"

"If it means the difference between getting out or spending the rest of my life here?" David met her eyes. "Yeah. I would. Wouldn't you?"

Maya didn't answer. She didn't know if she would or not. Brother Paul was in his sixties, a quiet man who mostly kept to himself. But she also remembered the way he'd stood guard while they'd dragged Rebecca's daughter to the isolation room years ago, the way he'd turned his back on her screaming.

"Let's just hope it doesn't come to that," she said finally.

They moved together across the compound, three shadows flowing from building to building. Maya led them past the chicken coops, where the birds stirred and clucked softly in their sleep. Past the woodpile, stacked ten feet high and smelling of pine sap. Past the old truck that hadn't run in years, its tires flat and its windows starred with cracks.

The fence loomed ahead, twelve feet of chain-link topped with barbed wire. The gate was set into the eastern section, a double-wide entrance just large enough for vehicles. The guard shack sat beside it—a small plywood structure with a single window and a space heater that glowed orange through the glass.

David held up a hand, signaling them to stop. They crouched behind a cluster of bare lilac bushes twenty yards from the gate. Through the shack's window, Maya could see Brother Paul slumped in a chair, his head tilted back, his mouth open. Even from here, she could hear the faint rasp of his snoring.

"Stay here," David whispered. "If anything goes wrong, run for the creek. Don't wait for me."

He moved before Maya could argue, crossing the open ground in a low run. She held her breath as he reached the gate, as his hand went to his pocket and emerged with the key.

The padlock hung from a heavy chain wrapped through the gate's handles. David worked quickly, his movements precise. The lock clicked open with a sound that seemed impossibly loud. He unwound the chain carefully, catching it before it could rattle against the metal.

Inside the shack, Brother Paul stirred. His head lolled to one side, and he mumbled something unintelligible.

David froze, the chain still in his hands.

Brother Paul's eyes opened. For one endless moment, he stared straight ahead at nothing, his face slack with sleep. Then his gaze drifted toward the window. Toward the gate. Toward David standing there like a statue with the chain in his hands.

Recognition flickered across Brother Paul's face.

He surged to his feet with a shout, his chair clattering backward. "Stop! Stop right there!"

David dropped the chain and yanked the gate open. "Run!" he bellowed. "Run now!"

Maya grabbed Iris's hand and they sprinted from the bushes. Behind them, the shack's door flew open and Brother Paul stumbled out, still shouting. "Brothers! Wake up! Someone's at the gate!"

Lights flared to life in the men's dormitory. Maya heard doors slamming, voices rising in confusion and alarm. She and Iris reached the gate just as David threw it wide, the metal screeching on its hinges.

"Go!" David shoved them through. "Don't stop, don't look back!"

They burst through onto the gravel road beyond. The forest pressed in on either side, dark and infinite. Maya's lungs burned as she ran, pulling Iris along. Behind them, the compound was erupting into chaos—more lights, more shouting, the sound of boots on gravel.

"They're running!" someone yelled. "Get the truck! Get Brother Nathaniel!"

The road stretched ahead, pale in the moonlight. Maya's boots pounded the gravel, each impact jarring up through her legs. Beside her, Iris was gasping for breath but keeping pace. Behind them, David's heavier footfalls echoed.

"The trees!" he shouted. "Get into the trees!"

Maya veered left, crashing through the underbrush at the road's edge. Branches whipped at her face, thorns caught at her clothes. Iris stumbled and Maya hauled her upright, dragging her deeper into the forest. Pine needles crunched under their feet. The darkness was absolute here, the canopy blocking the moonlight.

They ran blind, arms outstretched to ward off tree trunks. Maya's shin cracked against a fallen log and she went down hard, taking Iris with her. They hit the ground in a tangle of limbs, the impact driving the air from Maya's lungs.

David crashed to a halt beside them. "Up! Get up! They'll have flashlights in a minute."

Maya pushed to her feet, tasting blood where she'd bitten her lip. Her hands were scraped raw and her shin throbbed, but she was mobile. Iris was already standing, her breathing ragged but her eyes fierce.

From the direction of the compound, beams of light began sweeping through the trees. Maya heard Brother Nathaniel's voice, commanding and furious: "Find them! They can't have gone far!"

David grabbed Maya's arm and pulled her forward. "This way. There's a ravine about a quarter mile in. If we can reach it, we can follow it down to the creek and lose them."

They moved as quietly as they could, which wasn't quiet at all. Every footfall seemed to crack like a gunshot. Every branch that snapped under their weight might as well have been a flare announcing their position. Behind them, the lights were spreading out, forming a net.

"There!" a voice shouted. "I see something moving!"

A flashlight beam swung toward them, and Maya caught a glimpse of Brother Thomas's face, hard and determined. He was less than thirty yards behind them, crashing through the underbrush with two other men.

David changed direction abruptly, pulling them east. The ground sloped downward, and Maya's feet slipped on pine needles and loose rocks. She could hear water now—the creek, rushing somewhere below them.

The ravine appeared without warning, a sudden drop-off that yawned in the darkness. David skidded to a halt at the edge, and for a horrible moment Maya thought they were trapped. But then he was lowering himself over the side, finding handholds in the rocky face.

"Climb down! Quick!"

Iris went next, her hands scrabbling for purchase. Maya followed, her fingers finding cracks in the stone. The ravine was perhaps fifteen feet deep, its sides steep but climbable. She descended in a controlled fall, half-climbing, half-sliding.

She hit the bottom and found herself on a narrow strip of rocky ground beside the creek. The water was running fast and high, fed by the previous week's rains. David was already moving downstream, and Maya and Iris hurried after him.

Above them, flashlight beams played across the ravine's edge. "They went down!" Brother Thomas called. "Get the ropes!"

But the ravine twisted and turned, and within minutes they'd moved beyond the lights' reach. The creek led them deeper into the forest, away from the compound, away from the only home Maya had known for fourteen years.

They walked for what felt like hours, following the water's edge. The creek gradually widened, becoming a proper stream. The forest changed around them—the pines giving way to mixed hardwoods, the undergrowth becoming denser. Maya's clothes were soaked from splashing through shallow sections. Her teeth chattered with cold.

Finally, when the sounds of pursuit had long since faded, David called a halt. They collapsed on a mossy bank, gasping for breath. Maya's legs trembled with exhaustion. Beside her, Iris had wrapped her arms around herself, shivering violently.

"We need to keep moving," David said, but even he sounded winded. "Put distance between us and them before sunrise."

"How far to the road?" Maya asked.

David pulled out his crude map, though it was too dark to read. "Maybe five miles? Hard to say—we went off course when they spotted us."

Five miles through dense forest in the dark. Maya wanted to weep, but she forced herself to stand. They'd come this far. They couldn't stop now.

"Here." David shrugged off his pack and pulled out two wool blankets. "Wrap up. We'll move slower, but at least you won't freeze."

Maya took one and draped it over Iris's shoulders, then wrapped the other around herself. The wool was scratchy and smelled of mothballs, but it was warm.

They walked on. The forest was alive with night sounds—owls calling, small animals rustling in the underbrush, the creek's constant murmur. Maya's world narrowed to the next step, and the next. Don't think about how far they still had to go. Don't think about Brother Nathaniel organizing search parties. Don't think about what would happen if they were caught.

Just walk. Just keep walking.

Dawn was still hours away.

The creek led them through a gauntlet of deadfall and brambles. Maya's legs were scratched bloody beneath her skirt by the time the first gray light began filtering through the canopy. She'd lost track of how long they'd been walking—three hours? Four? Time had become elastic, stretching and contracting with each stumbling step.

David stopped where the creek pooled into a wider basin, the water black and still in the predawn darkness. "We need to rest," he said quietly. "Just for a few minutes."

Maya wanted to argue, wanted to say they couldn't afford to stop, but her body made the decision for her. She sank down on a flat rock near the water's edge, and Iris collapsed beside her. The younger girl was shaking—whether from cold or shock or exhaustion, Maya couldn't tell.

"Drink," David said, kneeling to fill the canteens he'd brought. "The water's clean this far up the mountain."

Maya cupped her hands and drank from the creek. The water was ice-cold and tasted of stone and pine. It cleared some of the fog from her mind, though her exhaustion remained bone-deep.

"How much farther?" Iris asked, her voice hoarse.

David consulted his map again, though there was barely enough light to see it. "If we've been following the creek like I think we have, we should hit the service road in another mile or so. From there it's maybe two miles to the highway."

Three miles. It sounded impossible. Maya looked down at her boots—sturdy work boots that had served her well in the compound gardens but were never meant for this kind of trek. The leather was soaked through, and she could feel blisters forming on both heels.

"What happens when we reach the highway?" Iris asked.

"We hitch a ride," David said. "Flag down the first vehicle that looks safe and get as far from here as we can."

"And if no one stops?"

"Then we walk until someone does." David's voice was matter-of-fact, but Maya heard the uncertainty beneath it. They were gambling on the kindness of strangers, on the possibility that someone would see three bedraggled people on a mountain highway and choose to help rather than drive past.

"Sister Rebecca mentioned a women's shelter," Maya said. "In Cascade Springs. She said they help people without asking questions."

David nodded slowly. "That's about sixty miles west of here. We'd need to catch a bus, or convince someone to drive us that far." He pulled an energy bar from his pack and broke it into three pieces, handing them around. "But yeah. That's probably our best bet."

The energy bar was stale and tasted like cardboard, but Maya forced herself to eat it. Beside her, Iris managed a few bites before wrapping the rest in her pocket.

"I can't believe we're really out," Iris whispered.

Maya reached for her sister's hand. "We're not out yet. Not until we're far enough away that he can't find us."

The light was growing stronger now, the sky shifting from black to deep blue. Birds began their morning songs—a sound Maya had heard every day of her life but never quite like this, never with this much space around it. In the compound, even the birdsong had felt contained, bounded by the fence.

"We should move," David said, standing and shouldering his pack. "Want to hit that service road before full daylight. Less chance someone from town will see us and report back to Brother Nathaniel."

They followed the creek for another half mile before it veered sharply south. David led them away from the water then, striking northwest through dense underbrush. The going was harder here—no clear path, just endless trees and rocks and thorny bushes that caught at their clothes.

Maya's shin, which she'd cracked against the log during their flight, throbbed with every step. She could feel it swelling inside her boot, the pain sharpening from a dull ache to something more urgent. But she didn't say anything. Complaining wouldn't make the distance any shorter.

The sun was fully up when they finally broke through to the service road. It was barely more than two tire tracks through the forest, overgrown with weeds and pocked with potholes. But it was a road. Evidence of the outside world.

"This way," David said, turning left. "Should take us down to the main highway."

They walked in silence now, too exhausted for conversation. Maya watched the forest scroll past and tried to imagine what came next. The shelter in Cascade Springs—what would it be like? Would they let them stay? Would there be people there who could help them start over?

Start over. The phrase felt strange, almost meaningless. Maya was twenty-two years old, but she'd never had a bank account, never owned a phone, never driven a car. She could sew and cook and recite scripture and work a garden, but what use were those skills in a world she barely remembered?

And what about Iris? At nineteen, her sister had even less memory of life before the compound. She'd been five when they'd arrived—kindergarten age. Did she even remember their parents? Their old house? Anything?

Maya glanced at Iris trudging beside her, and her sister caught her eye and managed a small, exhausted smile. Whatever came next, at least they'd face it together. At least Iris wouldn't be forced into Brother Nathaniel's bed, wouldn't be hollowed out and broken like Rebecca's daughter.

That had to be enough. For now, that had to be enough.

The service road wound downhill through a series of switchbacks. Maya's legs burned with the effort of controlling her descent, her knees threatening to buckle with each step. Beside her, Iris stumbled over a root and caught herself against a tree trunk.

"You okay?" Maya asked.

Iris nodded, but her face was gray with exhaustion. "Just tired."

"Not much farther," David said, though Maya suspected he was lying. Everything was farther than it seemed when you were this worn down.

They rounded another bend and the road widened slightly. Fresh tire tracks marked the dirt—someone had driven through here recently, probably within the last few days. Maya felt a spike of anxiety. What if it had been someone from the compound? What if they were walking into a trap?

But David seemed unconcerned, so she pushed the worry aside and kept walking.

The sound of traffic reached them before they saw the highway—a distant hum that gradually resolved into individual vehicles. Maya's heart leapt. They were close. So close.

The service road ended at a locked gate with a "No Trespassing" sign. David climbed over it, then helped Maya and Iris across. On the other side, a gravel parking area gave way to a two-lane highway that cut through the forest like a scar.

Maya stood at the edge of the asphalt and stared. She'd seen roads before, during the rare trips to town when Brother Nathaniel needed extra hands to load furniture. But this felt different. This road didn't lead back to the compound. This road led away, toward possibility, toward a future she couldn't yet imagine.

A truck blew past, going at least sixty miles per hour. The wind from its passage whipped Maya's hair across her face. Iris flinched backward.

"Now what?" Maya asked.

David was scanning the highway in both directions. "Now we wait for someone who looks like they might stop. And we pray they're not the kind to ask too many questions."

They waited. Five minutes. Ten. Cars and trucks passed in both directions, but none of them slowed. Maya watched each one with a mixture of hope and dread. Part of her wanted someone to stop, to offer help. Part of her was terrified of what might happen when they did.

What would they say? Where would they claim to be coming from? David had money, but not enough to explain three people on a remote mountain highway with no car, no luggage, nothing but the clothes on their backs.

"There," David said suddenly, pointing east. "That one."

A battered pickup truck was approaching, its engine loud and rattling. The driver—an older man with a white beard—was slowing down, his eyes on them. He pulled onto the shoulder about twenty yards past them and leaned out his window.

"You folks okay?" he called. "Need some help?"

David stepped forward, his body language carefully nonthreatening. "Car broke down about five miles back. We've been walking since midnight. Could you give us a lift to the next town?"

The man studied them for a long moment. Maya held her breath. She could see him taking in their disheveled appearance, the scratches on their faces, the way Iris was swaying with exhaustion. Could see him weighing whether to get involved.

"Climb in the back," he said finally. "I'm heading to Riverside. That work for you?"

"That's perfect," David said. "Thank you. Really."

They scrambled into the truck bed, Maya and Iris settling against one side while David braced himself against the cab. The truck lurched into motion, gravel pinging against the undercarriage.

As they accelerated onto the highway, Maya looked back toward the service road they'd emerged from. Somewhere beyond those trees, beyond miles of forest, the compound lay like a wound in the landscape. Brother Nathaniel would be organizing search parties, calling in favors, doing everything in his power to bring them back.

But right now, in this moment, they were free.

The wind whipped through Maya's hair as the truck picked up speed. Beside her, Iris had her eyes closed, her face tilted toward the weak October sun. David was watching the road ahead, his expression unreadable.

Maya closed her own eyes and let herself breathe. They weren't safe yet. They wouldn't be safe for a long time, maybe never. But they were moving. They were alive. They were out.

That would have to be enough.

prompt: Imagine a story where a mysterious microbe begins dissolving all plastic, plunging a seaside town into crisis. Center on an oceanographer teamed with a local artisan as they scrap modern conveniences to reinvent tools and rebuild their community using traditional methods. Weave in themes of environmental awakening, the hidden cost of convenience, and the tension between nostalgia for the old world and hope for a greener future. Include at least two pivotal flashback scenes showing the protagonists’ former reliance on plastic and finish with a communal ritual that symbolizes the town’s new values. Aim for 8,000–10,000 words.
generated_story:
The first sign was the handles.

Dr. Maya Chen stood in her laboratory at the Cascade Bay Marine Research Center, staring at what had been, just yesterday, a perfectly functional sampling container. The plastic grip had gone soft and sticky, like taffy left in the sun, except the temperature had never risen above sixty-two degrees. She touched it with her gloved finger, and the material yielded, leaving a dimple that didn't spring back.

"That's the third one this week," said Kenji from across the room, not looking up from his microscope. "Quality control must be sleeping at the factory."

But Maya wasn't so sure. She'd been collecting water samples from the bay every morning for six years, and she'd never seen polymer degradation like this. It wasn't uniform wear—the metal parts were fine, the glass was pristine, but every plastic component had begun to weaken, to lose structural integrity in a way that defied normal aging.

She walked to the window. Beyond the salt-streaked glass, Cascade Bay stretched gray and restless under an October sky, fishing boats bobbing near the harbor, their colored buoys marking crab trap lines. The town of Tidecross climbed the hillside beyond—pastel houses, church steeple, the old cannery converted to artisan shops. Population 3,847. Her home for three years now, though she still thought of herself as a visitor, someone who would eventually return to the university, to real research, to a city where you could get decent sushi.

Her phone buzzed. The screen showed a text from the harbormaster: *Need you at dock 7. Boat problems. All of them.*

Maya grabbed her field kit—the one with the canvas straps she'd added after the original nylon had frayed—and headed out. The October wind cut through her jacket as she hurried down Harbor Street, past the Copper Kettle Café where tourists sipped overpriced coffee, past the storefront that still displayed a faded banner reading "Tidecross: Where Tradition Meets the Sea."

Tradition. That had been the chamber of commerce's attempt at rebranding three years ago when the last fish processing plant closed. Now the town survived on tourism, research grants, and the stubborn determination of people who'd been here for generations and refused to leave.

Dock 7 was chaos.

Fishermen clustered around boats, voices raised. Jim Kowalski, who'd captained the *Mary Catherine* for forty years, held up a length of rope—or what had been rope. The nylon had separated into fibrous mush, like cotton candy dissolving in water.

"Happened overnight," Jim was saying. "Every synthetic line on every boat. Look at this!"

Maya pushed through the crowd. Fishing line, navigation equipment housings, buoy markers, waterproof casings—anything made of plastic showed the same degradation. Some items were merely sticky or soft. Others had begun to dissolve entirely, leaving behind a thin, clear residue that smelled faintly of acetone.

"You're the scientist," Jim said, spotting her. "What the hell is this?"

Before Maya could answer, Sharon Alvarez from the Tidecross Gazette appeared, phone already recording. "Dr. Chen, is this related to the equipment failures at the research center? We're getting reports from all over town—plastic containers, pipes, even car parts."

Maya's mind raced. Widespread simultaneous polymer degradation across multiple synthetic types wasn't possible. UV exposure affected plastics over years, not overnight. Chemical contamination would need a massive spill. Unless—

"I need samples," she said. "Everything that's degrading. And I need to test the water."

"The water?" Jim frowned. "You think something in the bay is doing this?"

Maya was already moving, pulling collection vials from her kit. "I don't know what I think yet. But this isn't normal weathering, and it's not manufacturing defects. Something is actively breaking down these polymers."

She spent the next hour collecting samples—the gummy residue from boat equipment, water from around the docks, even scrapings from the plastic siding of the harbormaster's office, which had begun to look like it was weeping. By the time she returned to the lab, her mind was constructing and discarding hypotheses. 

Bacterial? Possible. There were plastic-eating bacteria, but they worked slowly, selectively, certainly not across all polymer types simultaneously. Fungal? Enzymatic? Some kind of chemical catalyst in the water?

Under the microscope, the water samples revealed nothing unusual at first. The normal plankton. The expected bacteria colonies. But then, at 400x magnification, she found them.

They didn't look like much—tiny spherical structures, translucent, almost gelatinous. They moved with flagella-like appendages, clustering around fragments of degraded plastic in the sample. As she watched, one of the spheres attached itself to a microplastic particle. Within seconds, the plastic began to break down, literally dissolving into the surrounding water.

"Kenji," Maya called, her voice tight. "You need to see this."

She'd made the call to Professor Adelaide Okonkwo at 3 PM. By 6 PM, her former mentor at the University of Washington had reviewed the samples Maya had photographed and sent digitally, and the response came back in a two-word text: *Don't panic.*

Followed immediately by: *And don't tell anyone yet.*

But it was too late for either.

By sunset, the hardware store had sold out of duct tape, metal tools, and anything that didn't contain plastic. The pharmacy reported that twenty-three prescription bottles had failed, their contents spilling together into an unsalvageable mess. The elementary school closed because the plumbing—PVC throughout—had begun to leak from invisible cracks.

Maya stood in front of the emergency town council meeting at 8 PM, facing two hundred frightened residents crammed into the community center. She explained what she'd found, or at least the outline of it: an unknown microorganism, waterborne, consuming plastic at an unprecedented rate.

"How long until it stops?" asked Mayor Hendricks, a woman who'd run Tidecross like a pragmatic ship captain for fifteen years.

"I don't know if it will," Maya admitted.

"Can you kill it?"

"I don't even know what it is yet. We're sequencing its DNA now, but that takes time. And it's in the bay, it's in the harbor, it's probably in the groundwater by now—"

"So what do we do?" This from a man in the back, voice cracking. "Everything we have is plastic. Everything."

The room erupted in overlapping voices, fear transforming into anger, into desperate questions no one could answer.

That was when someone else spoke up.

"We adapt."

The voice was calm, almost quiet, but it cut through the chaos. Maya turned to see a woman standing in the side aisle, someone she'd seen around town but never really noticed.

The woman was in her sixties perhaps, with steel-gray hair pulled back in a practical bun and hands that showed decades of work. She wore a flannel shirt and jeans, and her weathered face held an expression of weary patience.

"I'm Ruth Larsen," she said. "Some of you know me. I make furniture—real furniture, the kind that lasts. And I'm here to tell you that humans got along just fine before plastic existed. We've only been drowning in the stuff for seventy years. We can remember how to live without it."

"That's easy for you to say," someone shouted. "You work with wood. What about the rest of us?"

"I'm saying we have skills in this town that we've forgotten about," Ruth continued. "Glassblowing. Pottery. Metalwork. Textile weaving. My grandmother raised six kids without a single piece of plastic in her house. We've gotten soft, and now we're paying for it."

Mayor Hendricks raised her hand for quiet. "Mrs. Larsen makes a point, but we're talking about infrastructure. Water pipes. Electrical insulation. Medical equipment. These aren't things we can just replace with wood and pottery."

"Then we need help," Ruth said, turning to Maya. "Doctor, you're calling the government, right? FEMA? EPA? Somebody?"

Maya had tried. She'd called everyone she could think of, sent emails, uploaded data. But it was Saturday night, and the responses had been automated acknowledgments, promises that someone would review her submissions on Monday.

"They don't believe it's urgent," she said quietly. "Or they think it's localized. Just Tidecross."

"Is it?" the mayor asked.

Maya pulled up her laptop, projecting onto the community center's screen. "I've been monitoring reports. Six hours ago, a marina in Port Angeles reported equipment degradation. Four hours ago, a research vessel in the Juan de Fuca Strait lost all its plastic components. An hour ago, Victoria, BC posted about similar problems."

The map she'd created showed red dots spreading like a rash along the coast.

"It's moving," Maya said. "North and south along the coastline. Following the water."

The room went silent. On the screen, the red dots seemed to pulse, malevolent, unstoppable.

"How fast?" asked the mayor.

"Based on the pattern? The entire Pacific Northwest coast will be affected within a week. Maybe less."

"Jesus Christ," someone whispered.

"And then what?" another voice called out. "It keeps going? Down to California? Across the ocean?"

Maya didn't answer. She didn't have to. The implications hung in the air like smoke.

"We need to evacuate," someone said. "Get out while we still can."

"And go where?" Ruth Larsen stood again. "You think this stops at the county line? You think Seattle's going to be any better? At least here we have space, we have resources, we know our neighbors."

"She's right," Mayor Hendricks said, though her face was ashen. "If this is spreading as fast as Dr. Chen suggests, running won't help. We need to prepare. We need to figure out how to survive this."

The meeting lasted another two hours, degenerating into a mix of planning and panic. Committees were formed—water supply, food storage, communication, medical needs. Someone suggested gathering all the unaffected plastic in town into a central location, keeping it dry, rationing it for critical uses. Someone else pointed out that if the microorganisms were already in the groundwater, that wouldn't matter.

Maya slipped out near midnight, her head pounding. She walked back to the lab through streets that felt different now, ominous. Every car she passed was a collection of degrading components. Every building held pipes that might fail. The infrastructure of modern life was eating itself, and she had no idea how to stop it.

Back in the lab, Kenji had finally isolated a sample of the microorganism and begun the preliminary sequencing. He looked up when she entered, his face tight with exhaustion.

"It's not a bacterium," he said without preamble. "Not a fungus either. The genetic structure is... I don't even know what to call it. There are sequences that don't match anything in the database. Not even close."

Maya pulled up a chair, studied the screen. He was right—the DNA sequencing showed patterns she'd never seen before, long chains of base pairs that seemed to fold in on themselves in impossible ways.

"Could it be synthetic?" she asked. "Engineered?"

"Maybe. But by who? And why would they release it here?"

"Or it could be natural," Maya said slowly, "just something we've never encountered before. Deep ocean, maybe, brought up by currents—"

"And perfectly designed to eat plastic?" Kenji shook his head. "That's one hell of a coincidence."

Maya's phone buzzed. A text from Professor Okonkwo: *Got your data. Coming tomorrow with a team. Don't drink the water.*

Don't drink the water. Maya looked at the coffee maker on the counter—plastic housing, plastic reservoir. The water cooler in the corner—plastic bottle. The pipes running through the building's walls, carrying water from the municipal supply, which drew from the bay after treatment, which meant—

"Kenji," she said carefully. "When did you last drink water? Here in the lab, I mean."

He frowned. "This morning. From the tap. Why?"

"And your hands. When you were handling the samples, before we knew what they were—"

"I was wearing gloves. Mostly." His expression shifted from confusion to alarm. "Wait. You think—"

"I don't know. But we need to assume these things can survive outside the water. Can maybe even survive inside a host."

"Inside us?"

Maya pulled up her shirt sleeve, examined her forearm under the desk lamp. The skin looked normal. She pressed gently, felt nothing unusual. But the microorganisms were small, microscopic. If they were in her bloodstream, in her tissues, she wouldn't necessarily feel them.

Not yet.

"We need to get tested," she said. "Blood work, full panel. And we need to start documenting everything—symptoms, timeline, exposure levels."

"Symptoms of what? We don't even know if they're harmful to humans."

"They're dissolving every plastic surface they touch. We're full of synthetic materials—medications, implants, even microplastics we've ingested over years. If these things can—"

She didn't finish. Didn't need to.

They sat in silence while the implications settled like sediment. Maya's mind was already racing through the literature—case studies of foreign bodies rejected by immune systems, allergic reactions to medical implants, the documented presence of microplastics in human blood, lungs, even placental tissue. If the organisms could break down environmental plastics, why not the synthetic particles already embedded in living tissue?

"I'm calling the hospital," Kenji said, reaching for his phone.

"And telling them what? That we might be infected with a plastic-eating microbe that doesn't match any known organism?" Maya shook her head. "They'll think we're hysterical. We need data first."

"Then we test ourselves. Now."

They worked through the night, drawing blood samples, preparing slides, running every diagnostic the lab equipment could handle. By 4 AM, they had their answer, though Maya wished they didn't.

The organisms were there. Sparse but present, floating in their blood plasma like tiny ships in a vast sea. Under the microscope, they moved with that same flagellar motion, seeking, searching. As Maya watched, one of them encountered what looked like a microplastic fragment—probably from food packaging, water bottles, any of a thousand sources—and attached itself.

"They're not multiplying," Kenji said, studying his own sample. "At least not rapidly. The concentration is low."

"For now." Maya saved the images, timestamped and catalogued. "But we don't know what triggers reproduction. Presence of plastics? Temperature? pH levels?"

Her phone rang. The caller ID showed a Seattle number she didn't recognize.

"Dr. Chen?" The voice was male, clipped, official. "This is Dr. Marcus Webb from the CDC. We've been reviewing your submissions regarding the polymer degradation in Tidecross."

Finally. "Yes, I've been trying to reach—"

"We need you to stop all testing immediately and secure your samples."

Maya's hand tightened on the phone. "Excuse me?"

"Your samples and any organisms you've isolated are to be considered biosafety level three materials pending classification. We're dispatching a team to take custody within twelve hours. In the meantime, all research must cease."

"That's impossible. We're trying to understand—"

"Dr. Chen, this is not a request. If what you're describing is accurate, you're dealing with a potential biological threat. Continued amateur investigation could contaminate additional—"

"Amateur?" Maya's voice rose despite herself. "I have a PhD in marine biology and six years of field research. These organisms are already in the water supply, they're already spreading up and down the coast, and you want me to stop looking for answers?"

"We have protocols for situations like this."

"You have protocols for unknown organisms that dissolve plastic at an unprecedented rate? Please, share them."

A pause. When Webb spoke again, his tone had shifted slightly, less bureaucratic. "Dr. Chen, I understand your frustration. But there are concerns at the federal level about the origin of these organisms. If they're engineered, if this is some kind of release—intentional or otherwise—then we're looking at potential bioterrorism, and that means different agencies, different jurisdictions."

"So while you're figuring out jurisdiction, what are we supposed to do? Our infrastructure is failing. People are scared."

"We'll have resources there by tomorrow morning. Crisis management, emergency supplies, a full containment team. Can you hold position until then?"

Maya looked at Kenji, at the blood samples still under the microscope, at the data they'd collected. "Do we have a choice?"

"No," Webb said simply. "You don't. Secure your lab, Dr. Chen. And for God's sake, don't let anyone else come into contact with the organisms."

He hung up before she could respond.

"Containment team," Kenji said. "That's not ominous at all."

"We need to warn people." Maya was already pulling up her contact list. "If these things are in the water, if they're in us—"

"The mayor already told everyone not to drink tap water. What else can we do?"

Maya thought of the two hundred people at the town meeting, all of them breathing the same air, touching the same surfaces, drinking water from the same source for days or weeks before they'd known anything was wrong. How many were already carrying the organisms in their bloodstream? How many were sitting in their houses right now, tiny plastic-dissolving factories growing inside them?

She pushed the thought away. They didn't know enough yet. Maybe the organisms couldn't survive long in human tissue. Maybe the immune system would clear them. Maybe—

Her phone buzzed again. Ruth Larsen, the furniture maker from the meeting, had somehow gotten her number.

*Dr. Chen, can you come to my workshop? Found something you should see.*

Maya stared at the text. It was nearly five in the morning. "What's Ruth Larsen doing up at this hour?"

"Same thing we are," Kenji said. "Not sleeping."

They found Ruth in a converted barn workshop on the outskirts of town, surrounded by the smell of sawdust and linseed oil. Workbenches lined the walls, covered with hand tools that gleamed even in the pre-dawn darkness. Everything here was wood, metal, glass—not a piece of plastic in sight.

"Thank you for coming," Ruth said, leading them past a half-finished dresser. "I know you're busy. But after the meeting, I started thinking about something my grandmother told me. About the year the bay died."

"The bay died?" Maya glanced at Kenji. "When?"

"1953." Ruth pulled out a leather-bound journal, its pages yellow with age. "My grandmother kept records. She was the town librarian, documented everything. That summer, something killed all the fish in Cascade Bay. Just for three weeks—then they came back, like nothing happened. But during those three weeks, strange things occurred."

She opened the journal to a marked page. The handwriting was elegant, precise:

*July 18, 1953. The bay is clear as glass today. You can see straight to the bottom, thirty feet down. No fish anywhere. The Kowalski boys found something strange on the beach—black stones, smooth as eggs, that dissolve when you touch them. Father Morrison says it's a sign, but he won't say of what.*

Maya leaned closer. "Dissolve?"

"There's more." Ruth turned pages. "July 22: The dissolution continues. Three boats lost their caulking overnight. The harbor master says it's rot, but that doesn't explain the metal fittings that have gone soft. Mrs. Chen at the general store—no relation to you, I assume—reports that several jars of preserves have gone bad, their rubber seals failing all at once."

"Rubber seals," Kenji breathed. "Natural rubber is a polymer."

"July 29," Ruth continued. "Whatever afflicted the bay has passed. The fish returned this morning in great schools, as if they'd only been hiding. The strange stones are gone. The boats that survived are being repaired. Father Morrison held a service of thanksgiving, though most of the town is too exhausted to attend. We are calling it the Black Summer, though we do not speak of it much. Some things are best forgotten."

Ruth closed the journal. "After that, nothing. My grandmother lived another thirty years and never mentioned it again. But I remember her telling me once, when I was young: 'The sea gives and the sea takes. And sometimes it takes back what we've given it.'"

Maya felt cold despite the workshop's warmth. "This happened before. Seventy years ago."

"Before plastic was everywhere," Ruth said. "Before we'd filled the ocean with it. Maybe this is a natural cycle. Maybe whatever those organisms are, they've always been here, dormant, waiting."

"Waiting for what?"

"For enough plastic to wake them up." Ruth's expression was grave. "My grandmother's generation survived three weeks of this. We have decades' worth of plastic waste to clean up. How long do you think it will take this time?"

Outside, the sky was beginning to lighten. Maya could hear the bay in the distance, waves against the breakwater, eternal and indifferent. She thought of the organisms under her microscope, the map of spreading red dots, the CDC official with his containment protocols.

"We need to find out what happened in 1953," she said. "Why it stopped. Your grandmother's journal—is there anything else?"

"Just one thing." Ruth turned to the final marked page. "September 12: Mrs. Yamoto suggested we perform the old ceremony, the one the fishing families used to do before the season. We gathered at sunset, brought offerings of glass and wood and stone. It felt foolish, pagan almost, but we were desperate. The priest blessed it all, and we cast it into the bay from Founder's Rock. Two days later, the affliction ended. Coincidence, probably. But it gave us comfort to think we'd done something."

Maya stood motionless in Ruth's workshop, the old journal still open before her. A ceremony. Offerings cast into the bay. It sounded like folklore, superstition—the kind of thing desperate people did when science failed them.

Except science was failing them now.

"You think there's a connection," Kenji said, and it wasn't a question.

Ruth closed the journal carefully, her work-worn hands reverent. "I think my grandmother wasn't a superstitious woman. She documented facts. And the fact is, they did something, and then it stopped."

"Correlation isn't causation," Maya said automatically, but her mind was already turning over the implications. What if the ceremony hadn't been magical thinking? What if it had been something else—a change in chemistry, in temperature, in some environmental factor they hadn't understood?

Her phone buzzed. A text from Professor Okonkwo: *Leaving Seattle in 20 minutes. Your samples show proteolytic activity unlike anything I've seen. We need to talk about containment.*

"I have to get back to the lab," Maya said. "But Ruth—can I borrow this journal? Or make copies?"

"Take it," Ruth said. "I have a feeling we're going to need all the information we can get."

They drove back through town as dawn broke over Cascade Bay. The streets were quieter than usual for a Sunday morning. Through windows, Maya could see people moving around inside their homes, probably checking pipes, sorting through belongings, trying to figure out what they could save.

At the research center, Professor Okonkwo's van was already in the parking lot. Maya felt a rush of relief—her mentor was a woman who got things done, who didn't wait for bureaucratic approvals. She'd saved Maya's dissertation when the data looked unsalvageable, had written the recommendation that got her this position in Tidecross.

Adelaide Okonkwo was in her sixties, originally from Lagos, with close-cropped silver hair and an expression that could freeze undergraduates at fifty paces. She stood in the lab now, already masked and gloved, examining Kenji's microscope slides.

"Tell me everything," she said without preamble.

Maya did. The degradation timeline, the organism's structure, the DNA sequencing, the spread pattern up and down the coast. And then, feeling slightly foolish, she added Ruth's story about 1953.

Adelaide listened without interrupting. When Maya finished, her mentor was quiet for a long moment.

"In 1952," Adelaide finally said, "the Pacific Proving Grounds conducted seventeen nuclear tests. Castle Bravo series. The radiation spread further than anyone predicted."

Maya felt her stomach drop. "You think this is related to nuclear testing?"

"I think organisms evolve in response to environmental pressure. Radiation causes mutations. Most are fatal, but some..." Adelaide gestured to the microscope. "Some create something new. Something that can survive in conditions that would kill everything else."

"But why would radiation create something that eats plastic?"

"Maybe it didn't. Maybe it created something that eats hydrocarbons—oil, basically. Plastic is just refined petroleum. To an organism that evolved to break down oil slicks, synthetic polymers might look like an unlimited food source."

Kenji pulled up a new screen on his computer. "There were oil spills in this area in the fifties. Small ones, not well documented, but the shipping records show several tanker incidents."

"So an organism evolved to clean up oil," Maya said slowly, "and we've spent seventy years filling the ocean with plastic—"

"Which is basically concentrated food," Adelaide finished. "And now there's enough of it that the organism can sustain itself, reproduce, spread."

"But it stopped in 1953. Ruth's grandmother said it only lasted three weeks."

Adelaide was studying the journal now, her reading glasses perched on her nose. "September 12, they did their ceremony. What was the weather like that week?"

Maya pulled up historical weather data on her phone. "There was a storm. A big one. Category 2 hurricane passed offshore on September 14."

"Temperature drop?"

"Significant. The bay temperature fell from 62 degrees to 48 in less than two days."

Adelaide nodded slowly. "Temperature-dependent reproduction. At warmer temperatures, the organism thrives. But drop it below a certain threshold, and it goes dormant again."

"That's why it's happening now," Kenji said, excitement entering his voice. "October, but this year has been warmer. The bay temperature is still 59 degrees. Usually it would be in the low fifties by now."

"Climate change," Maya breathed. "We've warmed the water enough that the organism can stay active longer."

"And if the warming continues," Adelaide said grimly, "it might never go dormant again."

They stood in silence, the implications settling over them like a shroud. Maya thought of the ceremony described in Ruth's grandmother's journal. Had the storm been coincidence? Or had the gathered townspeople somehow sensed the weather turning, known on some instinctive level that cold was coming?

"The CDC wants us to stop," Maya said. "They're sending a containment team."

"Of course they are. Because if this gets out—if the public learns that there's an organism in the ocean that can eat all our plastic, and that it's spreading, and that we can't control it..." Adelaide shook her head. "Economic collapse. Infrastructure failure. Panic on a global scale."

"So we do what? Pretend it's not happening?"

"We find a solution before they can classify it as a national security threat and bury it under twenty layers of classified research." Adelaide pulled out her own phone. "I have colleagues. People who owe me favors. We can set up a distributed research network, share data through unofficial channels."

"That's illegal," Kenji pointed out.

"So is letting civilization collapse because we're too afraid of bureaucracy to do our jobs."

Maya thought of the town meeting, of the fear in people's faces. Of Jim Kowalski holding up his dissolved fishing line. Of the elementary school closed because the plumbing had failed. This wasn't an abstract research problem. This was people's lives.

"What do we need?" she asked.

Adelaide smiled—a fierce expression. "Everything you've got. And access to that bay water for round-the-clock monitoring. If we can establish the exact temperature threshold for dormancy, we might be able to trigger it artificially."

"Cool the entire Pacific Ocean?" Kenji's tone was skeptical.

"Cool specific areas. Bays, harbors, places where the infrastructure is most vulnerable. Dry ice, cold water currents, underwater refrigeration units—I don't know yet. But first we need the data."

They worked through the morning, setting up monitoring stations along the bay's edge, taking water samples every two hours, measuring temperature, salinity, organism concentration. Adelaide's colleagues checked in via encrypted messages—a researcher in Vancouver had found the same organism, another in Monterey confirmed it was moving south, a third at Scripps Institution reported that the genetic sequencing suggested the organism could metabolize multiple polymer types.

By noon, Mayor Hendricks arrived with Sharon Alvarez from the Gazette.

"The CDC team will be here in three hours," the mayor said. "They're setting up a command post at the community center. They want to talk to you, Dr. Chen."

"I'm sure they do."

"They also want all your samples. Your research. Everything."

Maya exchanged glances with Adelaide. "We can't give them everything."

"Can't or won't?"

"Does it matter?" Adelaide stepped forward. "Mayor Hendricks, I don't know if Dr. Chen has explained the full situation. This organism appears to be naturally occurring. It's been here before. And if we don't understand it, if we let it become a classified military problem instead of a scientific one, people will die."

"People are already dying." The mayor's voice was tight. "We just got word from the hospital. Mrs. Patterson—she's eighty-three, has a pacemaker. The plastic housing failed this morning. They couldn't replace it in time."

The room went silent.

"She was one of my grandmother's friends," Ruth said quietly from the doorway. Maya hadn't heard her come in. "She taught Sunday school. Made the best blackberry pie in three counties."

"I'm sorry," Maya said, and meant it. This wasn't just data anymore. It had never been just data.

"The CDC team will want quarantine protocols," the mayor continued. "They'll want to control information flow. And frankly, I don't know if I can stop them."

"You can't," Adelaide said. "But you can slow them down. Give us time to understand what we're dealing with. Because I promise you, once they classify this as a containment situation, solutions will take a back seat to control."

Mayor Hendricks studied them each in turn. Finally, she nodded. "How much time do you need?"

"Seventy-two hours."

"I can give you twenty-four. Maybe. After that, this is out of my hands."

When the mayor left, Ruth moved closer to the monitoring equipment. "That ceremony my grandmother wrote about. What if it wasn't magic? What if they were doing something that actually worked?"

"Like what?" Kenji asked.

"They threw offerings into the bay. Glass, wood, stone. All things that don't react with the organism. What if—" Ruth paused, thinking. "What if they were diluting it? Giving it materials it couldn't eat, crowding out the plastic?"

Adelaide looked up sharply. "Competitive inhibition. Introduce non-reactive materials that physically block the organism from accessing plastic surfaces."

"Would that work?" Maya asked.

"In a lab setting, maybe. But the bay is millions of gallons of water. You'd need tons of inert material."

"We have tons," Ruth said. "The old quarry, three miles north. It's been closed for forty years. Full of stone."

"And the old cannery," Sharon Alvarez added, her reporter's mind clearly racing. "They've got thousands of glass jars in storage. Were planning to sell them as vintage décor."

Maya felt something shift in her chest—not quite hope, but maybe its possibility. "We'd need to test it first. Small scale, controlled conditions."

"Then let's test it," Adelaide said.

They set up the experiment in a large water tank outside the lab. Adelaide prepared a sample of bay water dense with organisms. Maya added fragments of degrading plastic. Within minutes, the organisms swarmed them, breaking down the polymer.

Then Ruth emptied a bag of smooth quarry stones into the tank.

At first, nothing changed. The organisms continued their work. But as the stones settled, creating physical barriers, blocking access to the plastic surfaces, the rate of degradation slowed. Not stopped, but noticeably reduced.

"It's working," Kenji breathed.

"It's working a little," Adelaide corrected. "You'd need to cover every plastic surface, create complete barriers. That's not sustainable on a large scale."
