"You could sit with Ashley and them. They always get good seats in the student section."

Before Maya could respond, Mrs. Patterson called time and started going over the answers. But Jake's invitation stayed with her. Everyone was so eager to include her, to fold her into the fabric of Millbrook life. It should have felt welcoming. Instead, it felt suffocating, like being fitted for clothes that weren't quite her size.

By lunch, she was ready for quiet.

She grabbed a sandwich from the cafeteria line and made her way to the art room. Mrs. Chen was at her desk eating a salad and grading what looked like watercolor assignments. She looked up when Maya entered.

"Maya, right? Coming to join Simone?"

"If that's okay."

"Of course. Make yourself at home." Mrs. Chen gestured to the tables scattered around the room. "Just clean up after yourself."

Simone was in the back corner, earbuds in, working on a large charcoal piece. Maya approached carefully, not wanting to startle her. Simone must have sensed her presence because she pulled out one earbud and glanced up.

"Hey. You came."

"Needed a break from the cafeteria social Olympics."

"Felt that." Simone pushed her drawing aside slightly, making room at the table. "Sit. You can work on something if you want, or just eat. No pressure."

Maya unwrapped her sandwich and pulled out her sketchbook. For a while, they worked in silence—Simone adding shadows to what looked like a portrait, Maya attempting to capture the way rain had beaded on the school's windows that morning. The only sounds were charcoal on paper, the occasional rustle of a snack wrapper, and the muffled indie rock coming from Simone's phone.

"Can I ask you something?" Maya said after a while.

"Shoot."

"Why do you eat alone? I mean, you seem cool. You could probably sit with people if you wanted to."

Simone was quiet for a moment, considering. "I used to try that. Freshman year, I sat with different groups, tried to find my people. But everyone here has known each other since kindergarten. They've got their stories, their inside jokes, their history. And I'm—" She paused, choosing her words carefully. "I'm not interested in performing. In being the right kind of Black girl to make white people comfortable. In code-switching through lunch every day."

Maya set down her pencil. "Yeah. I get that."

"Plus," Simone continued, her tone lighter, "lunch is only thirty minutes. I'd rather use it productively. I'm applying to art schools next year—Pratt, RISD, maybe CalArts. Every minute I spend on my portfolio matters more than whether I know who's dating who."

"Where do you want to go?"

"Honestly? Anywhere that's not here." Simone smiled, but there was an edge to it. "Don't get me wrong, Millbrook's not the worst place. People are mostly decent. But it's small. Everyone knows everyone's business. And I need..." She gestured vaguely with her charcoal-stained hand. "More. Different perspectives. Other Black artists. Professors who actually understand what I'm trying to say with my work."

"That makes sense," Maya said. "I was looking at schools in New York before we moved. Now I don't even know if I can do that. My parents are talking about staying here through graduation, then maybe moving back south. Everything's in flux."

"That's rough." Simone studied her for a moment. "You miss home a lot."

It wasn't a question, but Maya answered anyway. "Constantly. It's like—" She struggled to articulate the feeling. "Everything here is fine. The people are nice. The school is decent. But it's not mine, you know? I didn't choose this."

"Nothing about high school is really a choice," Simone said. "We're all just marking time until we can get out and actually live."

Mrs. Chen looked up from her grading. "Such optimism, you two."

"We're artists, Mrs. Chen," Simone said with exaggerated solemnity. "We're supposed to be brooding and existential."

"Well, brood on your own time. You have portfolio reviews coming up, Simone. And Maya, if you're interested, I could use another student in AP Studio Art next semester. You have the skills."

"I'll think about it," Maya said, surprised by the offer.

The bell rang too soon. Maya gathered her things reluctantly, not ready to face the afternoon's crowds and noise.

"Same time tomorrow?" Simone asked as they headed for the door.

"Yeah. Definitely."

"Cool. And hey—" Simone paused. "You're doing okay, Biloxi. For what it's worth."

"Thanks. So are you."

The rest of the day passed in a blur. Spanish was a verb conjugation marathon. AP History involved taking notes on the Constitutional Convention while Coach Bradley's attention kept drifting to his football playbook. By the time Maya's mom picked her up, she was exhausted in a way that went beyond physical tiredness.

"How was day two?" her mother asked as Maya climbed into the Honda.

"Better than day one."

"That's progress. Make any friends?"

Maya thought of Simone, of their quiet lunch in the art room, of the way their conversation had felt real rather than performative. "Maybe. Yeah. I think so."

"Good. Your father and I were worried about—well. It's good you're settling in."

That night, Maya lay in bed scrolling through Instagram again. Keisha had posted pictures from a beach bonfire—their friend group gathered around flames, marsh

mallows on sticks, the Gulf dark and infinite behind them. Maya felt the familiar ache of missing out, of being displaced from the life she was supposed to be living.

Her phone buzzed. Simone: *working on a piece inspired by that driftwood from class. keeps making me think about things that travel far from where they started*

Maya stared at the message, feeling seen in a way that was both uncomfortable and comforting.

*same. everything feels displaced right now*

*yeah but displacement can be interesting. forces you to see things differently. makes better art sometimes*

*maybe*

*definitely. trust me im a professional brooder*

Maya smiled despite herself. *thought you only brooded on your own time*

*mrs chen doesn't know everything*

They texted for another hour—about art, music, the suffocating sameness of small-town Texas. Simone sent pictures of her work, dark moody pieces that felt raw and honest. Maya shared sketches from her journal, studies of water and light that she hadn't shown anyone else.

*these are really good,* Simone wrote. *you have this way of capturing movement. like the water's actually flowing on the page*

*thanks. it's the only thing i know how to draw anymore. everything here is so still*

*come to the lake with me sometime. it's not the gulf but there's water. might help*

*maybe*

*no pressure. just putting it out there*

Maya fell asleep with her phone still in her hand, Simone's messages a small bright spot in the overwhelming newness of everything else.

---

By Friday, Maya had developed a routine. She arrived early enough to catch Simone under the awning if it was raining, or by the courtyard bench if it wasn't. They'd walk to their separate classes via Simone's shortcut routes. Lunch was always in the art room—sometimes just the two of them, occasionally joined by Marcus from her English group, who turned out to be a surprisingly talented sculptor working on a series of abstract pieces about isolation. Afternoons ended with Studio Art, where Maya was beginning to accumulate her own collection of work on the classroom walls.

Ashley still invited her to sit in the cafeteria sometimes, and Maya went occasionally, not wanting to seem rude. The conversations were pleasant but forgettable—homecoming dresses, someone's dramatic breakup, speculation about college acceptance rates. Brooklyn had started dating Connor, which apparently was scandalous because Connor had previously been interested in Madison, though Madison insisted she didn't care.

"You're coming to the game tonight, right?" Ashley asked on Friday morning, cornering Maya at her locker. "Everyone goes. It's basically mandatory school spirit."

"I don't know—"

"Come on, it'll be fun! We're playing our rivals, there's this whole thing where we steal their mascot banner if we win, it's very dramatic." Ashley's enthusiasm was hard to resist. "Plus there's a bonfire after at Jake's farm. His parents are cool about it as long as we don't do anything stupid."

Maya caught Simone's eye across the hallway. Simone made a subtle gesture that might have been encouragement or mockery, Maya couldn't tell which.

"Okay," Maya heard herself say. "Sure. I'll come."

"Yes!" Ashley clapped her hands together. "This is going to be great. You'll see—Friday night lights are a whole thing here. Wear maroon if you have it."

That evening, Maya pulled on a maroon sweater she'd found in her closet and let her mother drive her to the school. The parking lot was already packed, cars overflowing onto the grass, families streaming toward the stadium with blankets and thermoses. The lights were visible from a mile away, blazing against the darkening sky like a beacon.

"Want me to pick you up after?" her mother asked.

"I might go to a bonfire thing. I'll text you."

Her mother's face lit up with barely contained joy. "That sounds wonderful, baby. Have fun. Be safe."

Maya followed the crowd through the gates, past the concession stand selling popcorn and candy, up into the stands where the student section was already a mass of maroon and gold. She spotted Ashley immediately—hard to miss with her group taking up an entire row, all wearing matching spirit wear and face paint.

"Maya!" Ashley waved frantically. "Up here!"

Maya climbed the bleachers, squeezing past families and younger kids. Ashley made space between herself and Brooklyn, who gave Maya a painted-on smile.

"You made it! Okay, so that's our side—" Ashley pointed to the field where the Millbrook Mustangs were warming up, "and that's the enemy. The Liberty Hill Panthers. We hate them. It's tradition."

"Got it," Maya said, trying to match Ashley's energy.

The game started with the national anthem, everyone standing with hands over hearts. Then the teams took the field and the crowd erupted. Maya had been to football games in Biloxi—her school had a decent team—but this was different. Here, it felt like the entire town had turned out. She recognized faces from around Millbrook: the woman from the hardware store, the man who ran The Skillet, even Mrs. Chen from the art department, sitting with what must have been her family.

The game itself was close, both teams trading touchdowns. Jake was the quarterback—Maya realized this when Ashley started screaming his name every time he threw a completion. Around her, everyone knew the cheers, the chants, when to stomp their feet on the metal bleachers. Maya tried to follow along, clapping when others clapped, standing when others stood.

At halftime, the band performed an elaborate routine while the cheerleaders tumbled across the track. Ashley pulled out her phone to take selfies, dragging Maya into several shots before she could protest.

"For the memories!" Ashley insisted. "Your first Millbrook game!"

During the third quarter, Maya spotted Simone on the far end of the student section, sitting alone with a sketchbook balanced on her knees. She wasn't watching the game at all—her attention was focused entirely on her drawing, earbuds blocking out the noise. Maya felt a strange pull toward her, toward that bubble of creative solitude in the middle of manufactured school spirit.

"I'll be right back," she told Ashley, who was too absorbed in the game to really hear.

Maya made her way over, carefully stepping over bags and feet. Simone looked up as Maya's shadow fell across her page.

"Knew you couldn't resist the call of mandatory fun," Simone said, pulling out an earbud.

"Ashley was very persuasive." Maya sat down beside her. "What are you drawing?"

"The lights." Simone tilted her sketchbook so Maya could see. She'd captured the harsh glare of the stadium lights, the way they created pools of brightness and shadow, the moths circling in their glow. "They're interesting. Very theatrical. False day in the middle of night."

"That's good," Maya said. "The moths especially."

"Thanks." Simone returned to her work. "You don't have to sit here if you want to be with your friends."

"They're not really my friends. Not yet. Maybe not ever."

"Fair enough." Simone added a few more lines. "The bonfire's going to be worse, by the way. Just warning you. Lots of drunk seniors, couples making out, someone always ends up crying about something. It's very predictable."

"You've been?"

"Once. Freshman year. Thought I should try the whole high school experience thing." Simone's expression was wry. "Learned my lesson."

"So what do you do on Friday nights?"

"Draw. Listen to music. Sometimes I drive out to the lake and photograph the water at different times of day. I'm working on a series about reflection and distortion." She glanced at Maya. "You could come sometime. If you want. Might help with the homesickness."

"I'd like that."

They sat in comfortable silence for a while, the roar of the crowd washing over them without demanding their participation. Millbrook scored again and the bleachers erupted, metal thundering under hundreds of stomping feet. Simone didn't even look up from her drawing.

"You know what's weird?" Maya said. "Back in Biloxi, I went to games sometimes. It felt normal. But here, watching everyone get so invested in high school football, it feels like—"

"Theater," Simone finished. "Everyone performing their role. The jocks, the cheerleaders, the devoted fans. Like we're all extras in a movie about small-town America."

"Exactly."

"That's because it is theater. This whole town runs on performance. Everyone playing the part they've been assigned since birth." Simone's charcoal moved across the page in confident strokes. "That's why I can't wait to leave. I want to go somewhere people get to choose who they are."

The game ended with Millbrook winning by a field goal. The crowd went wild, storming onto the field, the band playing the fight song. Ashley appeared beside Maya, flushed and breathless.

"We won! Come on, everyone's going to Jake's now. You coming, Simone?"

"Hard pass," Simone said without looking up.

"Your loss." Ashley grabbed Maya's hand. "Come on, it's going to be so fun!"

Maya let herself be pulled into the current of students flowing toward the parking lot. She glanced back once and saw Simone still sitting in the emptying bleachers, alone with her art under the harsh stadium lights, and felt a pang of envy for that kind of certainty, that ability to opt out without guilt.

Jake's family farm was twenty minutes outside town, down a series of increasingly rural roads. Maya rode with Ashley, Madison, and Brooklyn in Madison's SUV, country music blasting from the speakers. The other girls sang along to every word, windows down, the night air rushing in warm and grass-scented.

The bonfire was already blazing when they arrived, a massive pile of wood and brush sending sparks into the dark sky. There must have been fifty people already there, maybe more, clustered in groups around trucks with their beds open, music competing from multiple sources. Someone had strung lights between trees, giving the whole scene a dreamlike quality.

"Isn't this amazing?" Ashley said, leading Maya toward a group near the fire. "This is what Millbrook's all about. Community."

Maya accepted a soda from a cooler, watching the scene unfold around her. Jake and his football teammates were being loud near the fire, reliving plays from the game. A group of girls she recognized from various classes were taking pictures, posing in front of the flames. Couples had claimed the darker spaces between vehicles. Everyone seemed to know exactly where they belonged.

"Maya!" Jake approached, still in his grass-stained uniform, face flushed with victory and probably alcohol. "You came! What'd you think of the game?"

"It was great. Congrats on the win."

"Thanks. You play any sports? We could always use more people for spring teams."

"Not really my thing."

"Oh, you're more artsy, right? I've seen your stuff on the walls in Mrs. Chen's room. That's cool too."

He meant it as a compliment, but Maya heard the categorization happening in real time. Artsy. Not athletic. The new girl from Mississippi who draws things. She was being filed away, given her place in Millbrook's social taxonomy.

The night wore on. Someone started a game of cornhole. Someone else broke out a guitar. Ashley introduced Maya to at least a dozen more people whose names immediately fled her memory. Everyone was friendly, welcoming even, but Maya felt like she was watching from behind glass, present but not quite participating.

Around eleven, she texted her mom for a ride and found a quiet spot at the edge of the property to wait, away from the noise and firelight. The stars were incredible here—brighter than in Biloxi where light pollution from the casinos dulled the sky. She pulled out her phone and took a picture, then opened her messages to Simone.

*you were right about the bonfire. very predictable*

*told you. where are you now?*

*waiting for my mom. sitting in a field looking at stars*

*better than the party?*

*definitely better than the party*

*want to go to the lake tomorrow? i usually go saturday mornings. can pick you up*

Maya hesitated only a moment. *yeah. that sounds good*

*cool. text me your address. ill be there at 9*

Her mother's headlights appeared at the end of the long driveway. Maya walked toward them, leaving the bonfire behind, already thinking about tomorrow, about water and light and the possibility of finding something in Millbrook that actually felt like hers.

Saturday morning arrived cool and clear, the kind of weather that made Maya almost forgive Texas for not being the Gulf Coast. She was waiting on her front porch when Simone's car pulled up—an old Honda Civic with a dented bumper and a collection of stickers on the back window: National Parks, indie bands Maya had never heard of, a rainbow flag, and one that simply said "ART OR DIE."

"Morning, Biloxi," Simone called through the open window. "Ready for some mediocre Texas lake water?"

Maya grabbed her backpack and jogged to the car. "Can't be worse than sitting in my room all day."

The interior smelled like coffee and vanilla air freshener that wasn't quite masking the scent of old fast food. A tangle of charging cables spilled from the center console, and the passenger seat held a collection of sketchbooks that Simone quickly moved to the back.

"Sorry, I basically live in here sometimes," Simone said as Maya buckled in. "My mom works doubles at the hospital on weekends, so I just drive around. It's better than being home alone."

They headed out of the subdivision, past the downtown strip Maya was starting to recognize, and onto a farm road that cut through pastures and clusters of oak trees. Simone had music playing low—something atmospheric with a female vocalist Maya didn't recognize.

"This is Mitski," Simone said, catching Maya's curious look. "You know her?"

"No, but I like it. It's kind of sad?"

"The best music is kind of sad." Simone turned it up slightly. "She's half-Japanese, half-American, writes about displacement and identity and never quite belonging anywhere. Very relatable for anyone who doesn't fit the default setting."

Maya listened to the lyrics about wanting to be wanted, about bodies in motion, about the weight of other people's expectations. "Yeah," she said softly. "I get that."

They drove in comfortable silence for a while, the landscape opening up around them. Maya watched hawks circling over fields, cattle clustered under trees, the occasional farmhouse set back from the road. It was pretty in a way she was learning to appreciate, even if it would never be home.

"So what happened to you?" Simone asked suddenly. "Like, what's your story? Why did your family really move here?"

Maya had gotten used to the abbreviated version she gave most people—dad's job, corporate transfer, nothing dramatic. But something about the car's intimacy, the fact that they were going somewhere together, made her want to tell the truth.

"My dad got a promotion," she said. "Regional manager for the whole central Texas division. More money, better opportunities, all that. And my mom, she's a nurse, she found a position at the hospital here that's actually better than what she had in Biloxi. So on paper, it makes total sense."

"But?"

"But nobody asked me. I mean, I'm seventeen. I had a life. I had plans. I was going to apply to Tulane, stay close to the Gulf, maybe study marine biology or art or something where I could still see the water every day." Maya felt her throat tighten. "And then one day my parents sat me down and said we're moving to Millbrook, Texas in six weeks. Like it was already decided. Like my entire life could just be packed up and relocated because it was convenient for them."

Simone nodded slowly. "That's brutal. My situation's different but I get the powerlessness thing. My mom moved us here when I was twelve after my parents divorced. She's from Texas originally, wanted to be near her family. I had no say in leaving Dallas."

"Do you like it here?"

"No," Simone said bluntly. "I mean, my aunt and uncle are cool, my cousins are okay. But Millbrook is small and white and the art scene is basically Mrs. Chen and that one gallery downtown that sells paintings of bluebonnets to tourists. There's no culture, no museums worth visiting, no diversity. Everyone here thinks different means someone who prefers Whataburger to Dairy Queen."

Maya laughed despite herself. "That's pretty accurate."

"The thing is," Simone continued, taking a turn onto an even smaller road, "I've learned to use it. The isolation, the boredom, the feeling of not belonging—it all goes into my work. My portfolio is basically a love letter to displacement. Gallery schools eat that stuff up."

"Is that cynical or strategic?"

"Both? I don't know. Maybe I'm just good at making art out of pain." Simone glanced at her. "What about you? What do you make art about?"

Maya thought about her sketchbooks full of water studies, her obsessive attempts to capture light on waves, the way she drew the Gulf over and over like a prayer. "Home," she said. "I guess I make art about home. About what I've lost."

"Past tense. That's interesting."

"What do you mean?"

"You said 'what I've lost,' not 'what I miss' or 'where I'm from.' Like it's already gone. Completed." Simone turned down a dirt road marked with a small sign: "Lake Millbrook Access."

Maya sat with that observation. Was it true? Had she already given up on Biloxi, on the idea that she could ever go back to the life she'd had?

The lake appeared through the trees—not the vast expanse of the Gulf but still more water than Maya had seen since moving here. It was irregularly shaped, ringed with grass and rocks and the occasional boat dock. The morning sun turned its surface silver, and a few boats were already out, their wakes creating rippling patterns.

Simone parked in a small gravel lot and killed the engine. "It's not the ocean," she said, "but it has its own thing going on. Come on, I'll show you my spot."

They grabbed their bags and followed a narrow trail through tall grass. Simone moved with the confidence of someone who'd walked this path many times, eventually leading Maya to a flat rock outcropping that jutted into the water. It offered a view across the lake's widest point, with morning light catching the ripples and making them shimmer.

"Okay," Maya admitted, settling onto the warm stone. "This is actually nice."

"Right?" Simone was already pulling out her camera, an older DSLR with a serious-looking lens. "I come here to shoot reference photos mostly. The light changes constantly, the water's never the same twice. It's good for studying movement and reflection."

Maya watched as Simone moved around the outcropping, adjusting angles, waiting for boats to pass through her frame. There was something meditative about her focus, the way she disappeared into her work. Maya pulled out her own sketchbook and began drawing—not the Gulf this time, but this lake, these rocks, this specific quality of Texas morning light.

They worked in silence for nearly an hour, the only sounds the distant hum of boat motors and the occasional call of a bird. Maya found herself relaxing in a way she hadn't since arriving in Millbrook. There was no performance here, no need to explain herself or fit into someone else's expectations. Just water and light and the companionable presence of someone who understood.

"Can I ask you something?" Maya said eventually. "That first day, in the cafeteria—you looked at me. Like really looked at me. What were you thinking?"

Simone lowered her camera and came to sit beside Maya, their legs dangling over the rock's edge. "Honestly? I was thinking, there's another one. Another Black girl who's going to try to fit in with Ashley's group because it's easier than being alone. Who's going to code-switch her way through lunch and act like their questions about her hair aren't microaggressions."

"Wow. Harsh judgment."

"I know. I'm sorry. It's just—" Simone picked at the hem of her shorts. "I've seen it before. There was another Black girl here, Jasmine, she graduated last year. She spent all four years trying so hard to be one of them. Changed the way she talked, straightened her hair, dated this white boy who definitely fetishized her. And they liked her, but they never really saw her, you know? She was acceptable because she made herself small."

"What happened to her?"

"She went to A&M. Last I heard she was rushing a sorority and still pretending." Simone met Maya's eyes. "So yeah, when I saw you with Ashley's group, I figured that's what you'd do too. Make yourself fit. And I get it—it's survival. But I was disappointed."

"And now?"

"Now I think maybe you're different. Maybe you're actually willing to be uncomfortable instead of invisible." Simone smiled slightly. "That lunch in the art room—you chose discomfort. That matters."

Maya absorbed this. "I'm not trying to disappear. But I'm also not trying to be some kind of statement, you know? I just want to exist without it being a whole thing."

"That's the problem though. For us, existing is always a thing. We don't get to be neutral." Simone gestured broadly. "In Millbrook, in Texas, in America—we're always marked. The question is just whether we let other people define what that mark means."

A pontoon boat drifted past, families visible on its deck. Kids were jumping into the water, their shrieks carrying across the surface. Maya watched them and thought about Ashley's easy inclusion, Brooklyn's casual remarks, the way everyone seemed to assume she'd be grateful for their acceptance.

"I don't think I can do what Jasmine did," Maya said quietly. "Make myself smaller. I'm already too tired from moving, from starting over. I don't have energy left to perform."

"Good," Simone said. "Don't. Be tired. Be angry. Be homesick. Make art about it." She bumped Maya's shoulder with her own. "And hang out with the weird art kid who eats lunch alone. We're way more interesting than the homecoming committee."

Maya laughed. "You know what's funny? Back in Biloxi, I wasn't even the artsy loner kid. I had friends, went to parties, did normal high school stuff. I was just... regular."

"Maybe regular is overrated."

"Or maybe displacement changes you. Makes you someone different than who you were."

"Like driftwood," Simone said, referencing their earlier conversation. "Shaped by the journey."

They stayed at the lake until noon, working on their respective projects, occasionally commenting on light or composition or the way a particular boat created interesting wake patterns. When they finally packed up, Maya felt steadier somehow, like she'd found solid ground in this new landscape.

On the drive back, Simone said casually, "So there's this thing next month. San Antonio has this Day of the Dead festival, and the art museum does this whole exhibition. I go every year. Want to come?"

"Seriously?"

"Yeah. It's like a three-hour drive but totally worth it. Actual culture, real Mexican art, food that isn't Tex-Mex garbage. We could make a day of it."

Maya thought about her sketchbook, about the morning at the lake, about the slow work of building something new from pieces of the old. "Yeah," she said. "I'd really like that."

As Simone's car pulled up to Maya's house, her phone buzzed with a text from Ashley: *brunch tomorrow with the girls? the skillet at 11?*

Maya looked at the message, then at Simone, then back at her house where she could see her mother watching through the window, hopeful that her daughter was adjusting, making friends, learning to be happy here.

*maybe,* she typed back. *i'll let you know.*

"The balancing act begins," Simone observed.

"I guess so." Maya grabbed her backpack. "Thanks for this. The lake, I mean. It helped."

"Anytime, Biloxi. See you Monday?"

"See you Monday."

Maya watched the Honda disappear down the street, then turned to face her house, her mother's expectant smile, the weekend stretching ahead with its complicated choices about who to be and where to belong. She didn't have answers yet, but at least now she had questions worth asking.

prompt: Write a story about a mythological character meeting someone from the present. The story should be between 8,000 and 10,000 words and should be funny. The main character should be Poseidon. He and his wife are traveling to a beach resort. There should be a family at the resort, and the father annoys Poseidon. Poseidon uses his powers to get revenge on the man. At the end, Poseidon should create a storm. The ending is happy.
generated_story:
The Toyota Camry sputtered into the parking lot of the Sunset Vista Beach Resort like a dying fish, its engine hacking out one final protest before Poseidon killed the ignition. He sat there for a moment, hands gripping the steering wheel, and stared at the peeling paint on the resort's sign—a faded sunset that had clearly seen better decades.

"It has character," Amphitrite said from the passenger seat, her voice carrying that particular tone she used when she was trying very hard to be supportive.

"It has mold," Poseidon muttered. "I can smell it from here."

"You smell the ocean. That's what we came for, remember? The brochure said 'authentic coastal experience.'"

"The brochure was printed in 1987."

Amphitrite unbuckled her seatbelt with a decisive click. "You promised me a vacation. A real vacation. No divine interventions, no petty vengeance schemes, no turning anyone into sea creatures because they gave you decaf instead of regular."

"That only happened once."

"Three times."

"The barista was extraordinarily rude on two of those occasions."

She turned to face him fully, and even after three thousand years of marriage, Poseidon still found himself captivated by the way light moved across her features. Today she looked entirely human—no hint of her usual otherworldly glow—just a beautiful woman in her forties wearing sensible capris and a sun hat that said "Beach Please" in curly letters. She'd bought it at a gas station in Delaware and seemed unreasonably proud of it.

"Poseidon," she said, and the way she said his name—the full name, not 'darling' or 'husband' or 'you absolute barnacle-brain'—meant business. "We talked about this. Zeus is in Cabo. Hades is doing that weird spiritual retreat in Sedona. Hera actually went to therapy. We're all trying to adapt to the modern age."

"Zeus got kicked out of Cabo for trying to seduce a weather reporter during a hurricane segment."

"Which is why we're setting a better example." She reached over and patted his knee. "Two weeks. No powers. No smiting. Just a god and his wife enjoying an affordable beach vacation like normal people."

Poseidon looked at the resort again. The main building was a three-story affair painted an optimistic shade of turquoise, with plastic palm trees flanking the entrance and a banner advertising "SUMMER RATES - 30% OFF!!!" even though it was September. A concrete dolphin statue near the door had one fin broken off, and someone had drawn a mustache on its face in permanent marker.

"I once lived in palaces made of coral and pearl," he said. "I commanded the seas. Marine life bowed before me. Odysseus spent ten years trying to get home because I was mildly annoyed with him."

"And now you're going to spend two weeks at a two-star resort in Delaware because your wife made a non-refundable reservation, and you love her very much."

He sighed—a sound like waves collapsing on themselves. "I do love you very much."

"I know." She kissed his cheek. "Now come on. I want to see if the pool really does look like a kidney bean, like the online photos suggested."

They gathered their luggage from the trunk: two modest suitcases that definitely didn't contain Poseidon's trident (which Amphitrite had made him leave in a storage unit in Newark) or any of his sacred relics (also in Newark, next to the trident). The resort's glass doors parted with a hydraulic wheeze, releasing a blast of air conditioning that smelled like coconut air freshener and chlorine.

The lobby was doing its very best. There was a front desk made of plastic wood, a waiting area with several floral-print couches that had seen better decades, and a large fish tank in the corner where three depressed-looking goldfish circled endlessly. Elevator music played from speakers somewhere, an instrumental version of "Kokomo" that made Poseidon's left eye twitch.

Behind the desk stood a teenager with spectacular acne and a name tag reading "BRAD - HERE TO HELP!" He looked up from his phone with the enthusiasm of someone being asked to explain quantum physics at gunpoint.

"Welcome to Sunset Vista," Brad droned. "Checking in?"

"Reservation under Poseidon," Amphitrite said brightly.

Brad's fingers moved across his keyboard with the speed of continental drift. "First name?"

There was a pause. Amphitrite's smile became slightly fixed.

"Just Poseidon," Poseidon said.

Brad looked up, his expression suggesting he dealt with weird guests daily and had long since stopped caring. "Like the god?"

"Exactly like the god."

"Cool. Driver's license?"

Poseidon produced his New Jersey driver's license, which listed his full legal name as "Poseidon N. Poseidon." The photo was terrible—he'd blinked during the flash and looked vaguely seasick, which was ironic considering he literally couldn't get seasick.

Brad processed their information with the mechanical efficiency of someone counting down the minutes until their shift ended. "Okay, Mr. Poseidon, you're in room 237. Checkout is at eleven. Continental breakfast from six to nine. Pool closes at ten. Beach access is through the back gate." He slid two key cards across the desk. "There's a family reunion happening this week, so it might be a little loud. The Hendersons. They rented out, like, half the resort."

"How delightful," Amphitrite said.

"Yeah." Brad was already back on his phone. "Elevator's to your right."

They wheeled their suitcases toward the elevator, and Poseidon pressed the button. Nothing happened. He pressed it again.

"Stairs?" Amphitrite suggested.

"Stairs," Poseidon agreed grimly.

As they climbed to the second floor, a sound began to filter down from above—the unmistakable chaos of children shrieking, adults laughing too loudly, and someone's portable speaker blasting "Who Let the Dogs Out" at a volume that probably violated several noise ordinances.

The Henderson family reunion was in full swing.

Room 237 was exactly what Poseidon had expected, which somehow made it worse. Two double beds with comforters featuring a nauseating pattern of seahorses and starfish, a television bolted to a particleboard dresser, a bathroom with tiles that might have been white in 1992, and a view of the parking lot partially obscured by a palm tree that was definitely fake—he could tell because real palm trees had a certain vibrational quality that this plastic impostor lacked entirely.

Amphitrite, demonstrating the supernatural optimism that had sustained her through three millennia of divine drama, immediately opened the curtains. "Look! You can see a sliver of ocean if you lean way out and squint."

"I can sense every ocean on the planet from anywhere in the world," Poseidon said, dropping his suitcase on the luggage rack with more force than necessary. "I don't need to squint."

"But can you sense it like a normal tourist?" She was unpacking now, hanging up sundresses in the closet that smelled faintly of mothballs. "That's the whole point. We're being normal."

Through the wall came a thud, followed by a child's voice screaming, "UNCLE DEREK CANNONBALLED WITH HIS SHORTS ON!"

More laughter. More chaos. Poseidon closed his eyes and counted to ten in ancient Greek, which he'd found more soothing than English. When he opened them again, Amphitrite was watching him with an expression that was part sympathy, part warning.

"You're doing that thing where your aura flickers," she said. "I can see it even though you're suppressing your divine form. You look like a fluorescent light that's about to die."

"I am not accustomed to being surrounded by this many mortals in such close quarters. The last time I was in a building this crowded, it was the Parthenon, and people were worshipping me."

"Well, now people are going to ignore you completely, and you're going to be fine with it." She crossed the room and took both his hands. "Darling. We need this. You need this. When was the last time you relaxed? And I mean actually relaxed, not 'sat on your throne glowering at marine life' relaxed."

He wanted to argue, but she had a point. The last few centuries had been exhausting in ways the previous millennia hadn't been. Fewer believers meant less ambient worship, which meant maintaining his divine form required actual effort now. Plus there was climate change affecting his oceans, plastic in the gyres, acidification, overfishing—the mortals were doing more damage to his domain than any Titan ever had, and he couldn't even smite them for it because apparently that was "an overreaction" and "would violate numerous international laws."

Zeus had sat him down last year—actually sat him down in a Starbucks in Manhattan, of all places—and told him he needed to "find balance" and "maybe try yoga." This from a god who'd recently been banned from four major hotel chains and had an active warrant out for his arrest in Thailand.

"Fine," Poseidon said. "We're normal tourists. We'll do normal tourist things. We'll swim. We'll eat fried food. We'll participate in whatever mind-numbing activities this resort offers."

"That's the spirit!" Amphitrite beamed. "Now put on your swim trunks. I want to see this kidney bean pool."

The pool was, indeed, shaped like a kidney bean, though a kidney bean that had been stepped on and then repaired by someone with only a vague understanding of geometry. It was surrounded by plastic lounge chairs, half of which were occupied by members of the Henderson family reunion. Poseidon counted approximately thirty people ranging from infants to octogenarians, all unified by matching neon green t-shirts that said "HENDERSON FAMILY REUNION 2024: SEAS THE DAY!"

The pun made his teeth hurt.

"Isn't this nice?" Amphitrite said, settling into one of the few empty chairs. She'd changed into a tasteful one-piece and oversized sunglasses that made her look like a movie star trying to go incognito. "Just listen to the sound of people enjoying themselves."

What Poseidon heard was a cacophony that made the Sirens' songs seem melodic by comparison. Three children were having a splashing war in the shallow end. Two teenagers were trying to drown each other while laughing hysterically. An elderly man—presumably the patriarch Henderson—was holding court near the deep end, telling a story that involved a lot of hand gestures and the phrase "and that's when the raccoon bit through the screen door."

Poseidon eased himself into the pool, trying to ignore how the chlorine levels were catastrophically high. The water immediately responded to his presence, becoming slightly calmer, the pH balancing itself automatically, a small current forming around him like a protective barrier.

"No powers," Amphitrite called from her chair without looking up from her romance novel.

He willed the water to return to normal, which felt deeply unnatural, like asking his lungs to stop processing oxygen. The water reluctantly obeyed, going back to its choppy, over-chlorinated state.

One of the splashing children—a girl maybe seven or eight—stopped mid-splash and stared at him. She had the unnerving ability of children everywhere to sense when something was slightly off about the world.

"Are you a good swimmer?" she asked, paddling closer.

"I'm adequate," Poseidon said carefully.

"My dad says I'm the best swimmer in the whole family. I can hold my breath for forty-three seconds."

"Impressive."

"Can you hold your breath longer than that?"

Poseidon, who didn't technically need to breathe when submerged in water, said, "Probably not."

"I bet I could beat you in a race."

"I'm certain you could."

The girl seemed satisfied with this and paddled away to inform everyone that she'd met a man who admitted she was a better swimmer. Amphitrite was definitely laughing behind her sunglasses; he could tell by the way her shoulders were shaking.

A beach ball suddenly smacked him in the face.

"Sorry!" A teenage boy—HENDERSON FAMILY REUNION plastered across his chest—swam over to retrieve it. "Didn't see you there, man."

"It's fine," Poseidon said, his eye twitching again.

"You guys here for the reunion?"

"No, just—"

"Oh, you're regular guests! That's cool. Sorry about all the noise. My family goes pretty hard." The kid grinned, then seemed to remember something. "Hey, there's gonna be a sandcastle competition on the beach tomorrow morning. Ten o'clock. You should totally enter. My cousin Derek thinks he's gonna win, but he sucks at architecture. Could use some actual competition."

Before Poseidon could politely decline, the boy had already swum away, tossing the beach ball back into the fray.

A sandcastle competition.

Poseidon, who had literally raised islands from the ocean floor, who had sculpted underwater palaces that made Atlantis look like a strip mall, was being invited to participate in a sandcastle competition.

He climbed out of the pool and returned to his chair, dripping chlorinated water onto the concrete.

"We're leaving," he announced.

"We just got here."

"The boy invited me to a sandcastle competition."

Amphitrite lowered her sunglasses. "And you're going to politely participate like a normal tourist."

"I will destroy them all."

"You will build a modest, non-supernatural sandcastle."

"It will be the greatest sandcastle in human history."

"It will be an average sandcastle that doesn't make children cry."

They stared at each other. From the pool came a shout of "MARCO!" followed by an enthusiastic chorus of "POLO!"

"Fine," Poseidon said. "Average sandcastle. No powers."

Amphitrite smiled and went back to her book. "That's my husband."

The evening brought what the resort's events calendar—laminated and mounted near the elevator—cheerfully described as "Family Fun Night in the Courtyard." Poseidon had suggested ordering room service and watching whatever mortal entertainment the television offered, but Amphitrite insisted they needed to "engage with the community."

"We're gods," he'd protested. "We don't have communities. We have worshippers, subjects, and occasionally people we curse."

"Had worshippers," she'd corrected gently. "Past tense. Present tense is 'neighbors' and 'fellow guests,' and you're going to be pleasant to them."

So now he stood in the resort's courtyard—a generous term for a concrete patio decorated with more plastic palm trees and strings of lights that flickered arrhythmically—watching the Henderson family prepare for what appeared to be some sort of talent show. A makeshift stage had been constructed from plywood and optimism. Folding chairs were arranged in uneven rows. Someone had dragged out a karaoke machine that looked older than Brad the desk clerk.

"This is hell," Poseidon muttered. "Hades was right. Hell is real, and it's a family reunion at a beach resort in Delaware."

"Hades is currently paying someone to align his chakras with crystals," Amphitrite reminded him. "Let's not take spiritual advice from him right now." She'd changed into a sundress the color of sea foam and had woven small shells into her hair—real shells, from actual oceans, which made Poseidon feel slightly better about everything. At least she hadn't abandoned all connection to their nature.

A middle-aged woman in a HENDERSON FAMILY REUNION shirt approached them, carrying two red Solo cups filled with something that smelled aggressively fruity. Her name tag identified her as "SHARON - AUNT."

"You two looked like you needed drinks!" Sharon thrust the cups at them with the enthusiasm of someone already three drinks deep herself. "It's my famous Henderson Hurricane. Secret ingredient is love. And rum. Mostly rum."

Amphitrite accepted both cups gracefully. "How kind of you."

"Are you folks enjoying your stay? I know we're probably driving everyone crazy with all the noise. There's just so many of us! My brother Carl—that's him over there with the mustache—he's got six kids. Six! Can you imagine? I told him, Carl, you know what causes that, right?" She laughed at her own joke, loud enough to make several people turn around. "You two have kids?"

The question hung in the air like a depth charge. Poseidon felt Amphitrite tense beside him, her fingers tightening almost imperceptibly around her cup. It was a simple question, the kind mortals asked each other constantly without realizing they were stepping into millennia of complicated divine history.

They'd had children, of course. Dozens of them, scattered across centuries and pantheons. Triton, their son, who now ran a sustainable aquaculture consulting firm in Seattle and hadn't returned Poseidon's calls in six months. Rhode, Benthesicyme, the various nymphs and minor deities whose names mortals had forgotten even if Poseidon hadn't. Not to mention the demigods, those brief and tragic experiments in mingling divine and mortal blood.

"No," Amphitrite said, her voice perfectly pleasant. "No children."

"Oh, well, you're not missing much! I love my nephew and nieces, but lord, the noise!" Sharon took a long drink from her own cup. "Anyway, you should stick around for the talent show. It's gonna be a hoot. My nephew Derek does this thing where he plays the ukulele while yodeling. It's absolutely terrible. You'll love it."

She wandered off to inflict Henderson Hurricanes on other unsuspecting guests, leaving Poseidon and Amphitrite standing at the edge of the gathering crowd.

"I'm sorry," Poseidon said quietly. "We could go back to the room."

"No." Amphitrite squared her shoulders. "We're staying. We're going to watch Derek play the ukulele while yodeling, and we're going to applaud politely, and we're going to pretend our family isn't scattered across the world avoiding each other."

"When did divine family dynamics become so complicated?"

"Approximately three thousand years ago when Zeus couldn't keep it in his chiton and set a terrible example for everyone."

The talent show began with the sort of organized chaos that made Poseidon nostalgic for actual chaos—the primordial kind, before the Titans, when at least entropy was honest about its intentions. The Henderson family had many talents, though talent in the traditional sense seemed optional.

A young girl performed a dance routine to a pop song Poseidon didn't recognize, featuring moves that seemed designed to cause joint damage. An elderly man recited a poem he'd written about his first car, which rhymed "Chevrolet" with "back in the day" no fewer than seven times. Twin boys did a magic trick that involved one of them hiding very obviously behind a curtain while the other pretended to make him disappear.

Derek—the infamous Derek—did indeed play ukulele while yodeling. The song was "Ring of Fire" by Johnny Cash, which seemed like an odd choice until Poseidon realized Derek was just playing random songs from memory with no particular theme. The yodeling started roughly halfway through and continued for reasons that defied explanation. Several people in the audience had their phones out, presumably to document this moment for posterity or blackmail purposes.

"That was something," Amphitrite said when Derek finished to enthusiastic applause.

"That was an affront to music," Poseidon replied, then caught her warning look. "I mean, very... authentic."

An older woman took the stage next—someone's grandmother, judging by the way children clustered around her feet. Unlike the previous performers, she didn't launch immediately into her act. Instead, she stood there, surveying the crowd with sharp eyes that seemed to see more than they should.

"I'm Vera Henderson," she announced, her voice carrying despite her small frame. "Some of you know me as Grandma Vera, some as Great-Grandma Vera, and some probably just know me as that woman who emails too many cat videos." Laughter from the Henderson contingent. "I've been coming to this beach for seventy years. Came here on my honeymoon in 1954, when this resort was brand new and actually nice."

"It was nice seventy years ago?" Poseidon whispered to Amphitrite.

"Shh."

"My husband and I used to sit on that beach every summer," Vera continued, pointing toward the dark ocean beyond the resort grounds. "We'd watch the waves and make wishes. Silly, I know. Making wishes to the ocean like it's listening. Like there's something out there that cares about what two old people want." Her gaze swept the crowd and seemed to linger for just a moment on Poseidon. "He passed fifteen years ago. Heart attack. Quick, which was a blessing. And I kept coming back here, every year, because this is where I felt closest to him. To the life we had."

The crowd had gone quiet. Even the children had stopped fidgeting.

"I'm telling you this because tomorrow, I'm going to be eighty-nine years old. And this is probably my last Henderson family reunion. My last time at this beach." Vera's voice didn't waver. "So tonight, I want to sing a song my husband used to sing to me. It's called 'La Mer,' which is French for 'The Sea.' He learned it in the war. Never could pronounce the words quite right, but he tried."

She nodded to someone operating the karaoke machine, and music began to play—a slow, haunting melody that Poseidon recognized immediately. Charles Trenet's famous song, written in 1946. He'd heard it countless times over the decades, sung by mortals who loved the ocean without knowing it could love them back.

Vera began to sing in French that was, indeed, heavily accented and occasionally mispronounced. But there was something in her voice—some quality of genuine emotion, of memory and loss and enduring love—that transformed the performance into something that made Poseidon's chest ache in a way he hadn't experienced in centuries.

*La mer, qu'on voit danser le long des golfes clairs, a des reflets d'argent...*

He felt Amphitrite's hand slip into his. When he glanced at her, he saw tears tracking silently down her face, and he realized he was crying too, which was absurd because gods didn't cry at karaoke performances by octogenarian mortals at cut-rate beach resorts.

Except apparently they did.

*La mer, des reflets changeants sous la pluie...*

Vera sang about the sea's changing reflections under the rain, about love everlasting, about the depths of human connection reflected in the depths of the ocean. She sang like someone saying goodbye. When she finished, the silence lasted three full heartbeats before the applause began—not the polite golf-clap that had followed Derek's yodeling, but genuine appreciation.

Poseidon found himself applauding harder than he'd applauded anything in recent memory, possibly ever. Beside him, Amphitrite was wiping her eyes with the back of her hand, her mascara slightly smudged.

"I need a minute," she said, and slipped away toward the beach access gate.

Poseidon followed her because that's what you did after three thousand years of marriage—you knew when your wife needed you to follow her, and you followed.

The beach was dark except for the pale glow of emergency lights mounted on posts along the boardwalk. The sand was cool under Poseidon's bare feet—he'd kicked off his sandals somewhere between the courtyard and the gate. Amphitrite stood at the water's edge, her sundress rippling in the night breeze, staring out at the Atlantic like she was trying to read something written in the waves.

He came up beside her and said nothing. Sometimes silence was the only appropriate response to grief, even old grief, even grief that wasn't technically yours but that you recognized in someone else's performance of a French song about the sea.

"She reminded me of Ione," Amphitrite said finally.

Poseidon had to think for a moment before the name surfaced from the vast archives of his memory. Ione. One of Amphitrite's Nereid sisters. She'd loved a mortal sailor, against all advice, against all common sense. It had ended the way those stories always ended—with the mortal growing old and dying while the immortal remained unchanged, unable to follow, unable to forget.

"I haven't thought about her in five hundred years," Amphitrite continued. "She stopped coming to family gatherings after he died. Just disappeared into the deep trenches. Father said she wanted to sleep until she forgot his face." She wrapped her arms around herself. "I wonder if she ever did. Forget, I mean."

"I don't think we're built to forget," Poseidon said. "The forgetting is what makes them mortal. The remembering is what makes us divine. And cursed."

"That's cheerful."

"You married me for my optimism."

That got a small laugh, watery but genuine. She leaned against him, and he wrapped his arm around her shoulders, and they stood there watching the waves roll in with a rhythm that had existed since before humans learned to count time.

The ocean called to him the way it always did—a low thrumming in his blood, a sense of vast awareness spreading out in all directions. Even suppressing his powers, even pretending to be mortal, he couldn't stop feeling every current, every creature, every drop of water from here to the Mariana Trench. Three miles out, a pod of dolphins was hunting. Seven miles south, a container ship was struggling against a storm front. Beneath his feet, buried in the sand, were fragments of shells and sea glass that remembered when they were whole.

"Do you ever regret it?" Amphitrite asked quietly. "Marrying me instead of... anyone else. Someone who wouldn't ask you to suppress yourself. Someone who wouldn't drag you to terrible resorts and make you promise not to use your powers."

"No."

"That was very quick."

"Because it's very true." He turned her to face him, his hands on her shoulders. "I've made many mistakes in my extremely long life. I've been petty, vengeful, dramatic—"

"Still are, sometimes."

"—still am, sometimes. But marrying you was the one decision I've never once questioned. Not in three thousand years. Not even now, standing in a dark beach in Delaware while Derek Henderson's yodeling echoes in my soul."

She laughed properly at that, the kind of laugh that started in her chest and spilled out like light on water. "Your soul will recover."

"I'm not certain. The damage may be permanent."

They kissed then, soft and familiar, the kind of kiss that held millennia of practice and still somehow felt new. When they pulled apart, Amphitrite's eyes were glowing slightly—just a hint of her true nature bleeding through, the blue-green luminescence of deep ocean water where strange creatures lived.

"You're glowing," he said.

"So are you."

He looked down at his hands and saw she was right. His skin had taken on a faint shimmer, like moonlight on waves. They were both slipping, their human disguises wearing thin under the weight of emotion and proximity to the sea.

"We should probably get back before someone sees us," Amphitrite said, though she made no move to leave.

"Probably."

They stayed where they were.

A voice called out from the boardwalk: "Hey! You two okay out there?"

Poseidon and Amphitrite immediately dimmed their divine auras back to nothing. A figure was approaching across the sand—young, wearing a HENDERSON FAMILY REUNION shirt. As she got closer, Poseidon recognized the girl from the pool, the one who'd declared herself the best swimmer in the family.

"We're fine," Amphitrite called back. "Just enjoying the ocean."

"My dad says you're not supposed to swim at night. The riptides are dangerous." The girl had reached them now, peering up with that unnerving child-intensity. "Are you guys married?"

"We are," Poseidon said.

"How long?"

"Very long," Amphitrite said. "Longer than we probably should admit."

"My parents have been married twelve years. My dad says marriage is like being on a team, and you have to practice to stay good at it. Do you guys practice?"

"Every day," Poseidon said, surprising himself with the honesty. "What's your name?"

"Maya Henderson. I'm eight and three-quarters. Are you really gonna do the sandcastle contest tomorrow?"

"Your cousin invited me, so yes. I suppose I am."

"Cool. I'm doing it too. I'm gonna build a castle with a moat and towers and everything. My dad's gonna help, but mostly I'm doing it myself because I'm good at building stuff." Maya bent down and scooped up a handful of wet sand, examining it critically. "You gotta use the wet sand, not the dry sand. The dry sand doesn't stick together right. That's the secret."

"Wise advice," Poseidon said solemnly.

"Yeah, I know. That's why I'm gonna win." She looked back toward the resort, where the talent show had apparently ended and the Henderson family was beginning their migration indoors. "I gotta go. My mom's probably looking for me. Bye!"

She ran off across the sand, her small footprints disappearing almost immediately in the dark.

"Eight and three-quarters," Amphitrite murmured. "I remember when mortals counted their ages like that. When every quarter-year felt significant because they had so few of them."

"She's going to build a castle with a moat and towers."

"Are you planning your competition strategy?"

"I'm planning to let her win."

"Liar. You're planning to build something spectacular and then deliberately sabotage it at the last minute so a child feels victorious."

Poseidon said nothing, because she was absolutely right.

They walked back to the resort hand in hand, past the pool where the water had gone still and mirror-like, past the lobby where Brad was scrolling through his phone with the dedication of someone avoiding actual work, up the stairs to room 237 with its seahorse comforters and view of the parking lot.

Poseidon expected to sleep poorly. The bed was too soft, the air conditioning too loud, and the walls too thin—he could hear the Henderson family through every surface, their conversations and laughter and occasional arguments creating a constant hum of mortal existence.

But Amphitrite fell asleep almost immediately, curled against his side with one hand resting on his chest, and the sound of her breathing was older and more familiar than anything the Hendersons could produce. He found himself relaxing despite everything, his consciousness drifting in that strange space between waking and sleeping that gods rarely experienced.

Somewhere around two in the morning, he heard the ocean calling. Not with words—the ocean didn't speak in words, despite what the poets claimed. It spoke in feelings, in urges, in the deep knowing that came from being fundamentally connected to something vast and old and powerful.

*Come swim,* the ocean said. *Remember what you are. Remember what we are together.*

He almost got up. Almost slipped out of bed and walked down to the beach and let himself dissolve into the waves, becoming water and power and divine will instead of this limited, mortal-shaped thing sleeping in a resort with a broken dolphin statue out front.

But Amphitrite murmured something in her sleep and tightened her hand on his chest, and he stayed where he was.

The ocean would still be there tomorrow. It would be there for all his tomorrows, until the sun expanded and boiled the seas away and even gods had to find new domains to protect.

For now, he could be a man on vacation with his wife.

For now, that was enough.

Morning came with the aggressive enthusiasm of seagulls screaming outside the window and the smell of burnt coffee wafting from the continental breakfast station. Poseidon woke to find Amphitrite already dressed, applying sunscreen with the methodical attention of someone preparing for battle.

"The sandcastle competition starts in forty-five minutes," she announced. "I took the liberty of scouting the beach while you were snoring."

"I don't snore."

"You snore like a whale with a deviated septum. I've recorded it. I have evidence." She tossed him his swim trunks. "There are already six families setting up their territories. The Henderson clan has claimed the prime real estate near the water. Maya has a bucket that's at least three gallons. She means business."

Poseidon dragged himself out of bed, every joint protesting in ways that were entirely psychosomatic—gods didn't actually have joint pain—but somehow his body had decided to commit fully to the mortal experience. "I'm too old for this."

"You're literally timeless."

"I'm timeless and too old for this."

The continental breakfast was a monument to barely edible efficiency. Waffle batter dispensed from a machine that wheezed like an asthmatic. Pastries wrapped in plastic that might have been fresh sometime in the previous decade. Coffee in an industrial-sized urn that had clearly given up on flavor and settled for temperature and caffeine content.

Poseidon poured himself a cup while Amphitrite assembled a plate of fruit that had been cut into cubes so uniform they looked computer-generated. Around them, the Henderson family filled every available surface, their neon green shirts creating a visual assault that made his eyes water.

"You're the guy from last night!" Derek appeared beside him, loading his own plate with approximately seventeen pancakes. "You coming to the sandcastle thing?"

"Apparently."

"Awesome! Fair warning, my Uncle Ray is super competitive. Last year he brought, like, actual architectural plans. Laminated and everything. My dad said it was cheating, but the judges allowed it because technically the rules don't say you can't bring blueprints."

"There are judges?"

"Oh yeah. Mrs. Chen who owns the gift shop, Brad from the front desk, and some random lady they grab off the beach. It's very official." Derek drowned his pancake tower in syrup. "The prize is a fifty-dollar gift certificate to the resort restaurant, which sounds good until you eat at the resort restaurant and realize why they have to give away gift certificates."

Sharon materialized from the waffle station, her paper plate sagging under the weight of carbohydrates. "Morning, you two! Did you sleep okay? The walls in this place are paper-thin. I could hear the people next door watching TV until midnight. Who watches infomercials at midnight? What kind of person makes that choice?"

"The desperate and the insomniac," Amphitrite offered.

"Probably both. Hey, you guys should sit with us! We've got three tables pushed together over there. Come on, there's always room for more."

Before either of them could politely decline, Sharon had already herded them toward the Henderson breakfast encampment, where approximately twenty people were engaged in the complicated social choreography of a large family meal. Introductions flew past too quickly to track—Carl with the mustache, his wife Jennifer, their six children whose names all started with J for reasons no one could adequately explain, Aunt Patricia who was apparently not actually an aunt but a family friend who'd been absorbed into the collective years ago, and Great-Uncle Ray, a lean man in his seventies with the intense eyes of someone who took sandcastle architecture very seriously.

"You're entering the competition?" Ray asked, studying Poseidon like a general assessing enemy forces.

"Casually," Poseidon said.

"There's no such thing as casual sandcastle construction. You either commit to structural integrity or you build a pile of mud that collapses at high tide. Which are you?"

"I haven't decided yet."

Ray nodded slowly. "Psychological warfare. I respect that. But know this—I've been perfecting my technique for thirty years. I have custom tools. I understand load distribution. I've read papers on the optimal water-to-sand ratio."

"Ray, it's sandcastles, not the Manhattan Project," Carl said through a mouthful of waffle.

"The Manhattan Project was also about precise ratios and structural integrity, so your point is invalid."

Maya appeared at Poseidon's elbow, dragging her three-gallon bucket. "You ready? We should get down there early. The good sand goes fast."

"The sand is literally everywhere," one of her brothers said. "It's a beach."

"The *good* sand, Jason. The stuff that's not all rocky and gross. You wouldn't understand because you're not an artist."

"You're eight."

"Eight and three-quarters. And more mature than you."

Poseidon found himself genuinely smiling, which surprised him. These mortals with their elaborate family dynamics and their serious approach to fundamentally silly competitions—there was something almost endearing about it. Or maybe he was just sleep-deprived enough that everything seemed endearing. Hard to say.

The beach had transformed into a construction site. Families claimed territories with towels and coolers, staking out their domains like ancient tribes. The judges had set up a folding table with a handwritten sign reading "OFFICIAL JUDGING STATION" and clipboards that looked extremely official despite being decorated with dolphin stickers.

Mrs. Chen from the gift shop wore a visor that said "BEACH GODDESS" in rhinestones. Brad appeared to be actively trying to sleep while standing up. The random beach lady they'd recruited looked confused about why she'd agreed to this but committed to seeing it through.

"Welcome to the 15th Annual Sunset Vista Sandcastle Competition!" Mrs. Chen announced through a megaphone that screeched feedback. "The rules are simple: you have two hours, you can only use materials found on the beach, and absolutely no power tools. We learned that lesson the hard way. Looking at you, Greg Henderson."

"That was five years ago!" someone shouted from the Henderson camp. "Let it go!"

"Your drill left a crater! We had to fill it in!" Mrs. Chen cleared her throat. "Judging will be based on creativity, structural integrity, and overall aesthetic appeal. The winner receives a fifty-dollar gift certificate to our restaurant, plus bragging rights, which are frankly worth more than the certificate. Any questions?"

Maya's hand shot up. "What if someone sabotages your castle?"

"If we catch them, they're disqualified. If we don't catch them, that's good strategy and also life lessons." Mrs. Chen checked her watch. "Alright, builders—on your marks!"

The beach erupted into organized chaos. Uncle Ray immediately began surveying his plot with a level that he'd somehow smuggled onto the beach. Maya attacked the sand with her bucket like she was trying to extract revenge. Derek started digging what appeared to be either a moat or a grave—the intent was unclear.

Poseidon stood at the edge of his claimed territory, staring down at the sand. Amphitrite sat in a beach chair behind him, reading her romance novel with the studied casualness of someone pretending not to watch.

"You're thinking too hard," she called without looking up from her book.

"I'm trying to decide how elaborate I should make this."

"You're trying to decide how elaborate you can make it without obviously using powers."

He couldn't argue with that. The sand beneath his feet hummed with potential. He could feel every grain, understand its composition, know exactly how it would respond to water and pressure and the careful application of divine will. He could build something magnificent. Something that would last centuries. Something that would make Uncle Ray weep with inadequacy.

But Maya was three feet to his left, her tongue poking out in concentration as she packed sand into her bucket, and she'd said she was going to win.

He knelt down and started digging with his hands.

The two hours passed in a strange meditation of sand and water and sun. Poseidon had forgotten what it felt like to work with his hands in such a direct, limited way—no reaching out with his consciousness to reshape matter, no commanding the elements to obey his will. Just scooping, packing, smoothing, the way humans had done since they first discovered that wet sand held its shape.

He built a castle. Nothing spectacular. Four towers connected by walls, a central keep, careful attention to symmetry and proportion but nothing that screamed divine intervention. It was, objectively, a very nice sandcastle. It would not make anyone weep with inadequacy, which was probably for the best.

Next to him, Maya was putting the finishing touches on her creation—a sprawling complex with multiple levels, seashell decorations, and a moat that she'd lined with stones to prevent erosion. For an eight-and-three-quarters-year-old, it was genuinely impressive.

On his other side, Uncle Ray had constructed what could only be described as a sand fortress. It had buttresses. It had a working drawbridge made from driftwood. It had arrow slits in the towers that were architecturally accurate to 13th-century military design. The man had not been exaggerating about his commitment.

"Time!" Mrs. Chen's megaphone screech cut through the beach noise. "Step away from your castles! Judges will now commence judging!"

The three judges made their way down the beach, stopping at each creation to examine it, confer in hushed tones, and make notes on their clipboards. When they reached Maya's castle, she stood at attention like a soldier awaiting inspection.

"Tell us about your design," Mrs. Chen said.

"It's called the Castle of the Seven Seas," Maya announced. "Each tower represents a different ocean. The moat is for protection but also for beauty because water is important to castle ecosystems. I used shells I found to represent marine life because a castle by the ocean should acknowledge the ocean."

Brad nodded, possibly awake. "Nice shells."

They moved to Poseidon's entry. He suddenly felt absurdly nervous, which was ridiculous. He was a god. He'd faced Titans and sea monsters and the wrath of Zeus. He should not be nervous about three mortals judging his sandcastle.

"This is very clean work," Mrs. Chen said, walking around it. "Good symmetry. Solid wall construction. The towers have a classical quality."

"It's boring," the random beach lady said. "No offense, but it looks like every castle in every medieval movie. Where's the personality?"

Poseidon opened his mouth to defend his creative choices, then closed it. She wasn't wrong. He'd built it carefully, competently, but safe. He'd been so focused on not using his powers that he'd forgotten to put any actual inspiration into it.

The judges reached Uncle Ray's fortress and spent a full five minutes examining it from every angle. Ray stood with his arms crossed, trying to look casual but clearly desperate to explain every architectural decision.

Finally, Mrs. Chen turned to face the gathered crowd. "The judges have reached a decision. This year's winner of the 15th Annual Sunset Vista Sandcastle Competition is..." She paused for dramatic effect. "Maya Henderson, for her Castle of the Seven Seas!"

Maya screamed and jumped so high Poseidon was briefly concerned about her achieving escape velocity. Her family erupted in cheers. Uncle Ray nodded with the gracious dignity of a defeated warrior who respected a worthy opponent.

The random beach lady approached Poseidon while everyone was celebrating. "Hey, I hope I wasn't too harsh. Your castle was really well-made. Just... safe, you know? Like you were holding back."

"I was holding back," Poseidon admitted.

"Well, maybe next time don't. Life's too short for safe sandcastles." She wandered off to return to her own beach activities, leaving Poseidon standing beside his very competent, very boring creation.

Amphitrite appeared at his side. "You did good."

"I built a forgettable castle and lost to an eight-year-old."

"Eight and three-quarters. And yes, you did. Graciously. I'm proud of you." She linked her arm through his. "Come on. Maya's family is organizing a celebratory lunch at the restaurant, and apparently we're invited. Sharon already said she wouldn't take no for an answer."

"The restaurant Derek said we should avoid?"

"The very same."

Poseidon looked back at his sandcastle one more time. In a few hours, the tide would come in and erase it, along with all the others. All that work, all that careful construction, returned to undifferentiated beach. Maybe that was the point. Maybe the impermanence was what made it matter.

"Alright," he said. "Let's go experience questionable resort cuisine with our new friends."

"That's my husband," Amphitrite said, squeezing his arm. "Embracing the experience."

"I'm embracing something. I'm not sure it's the experience. Might just be resignation."

"Close enough."

The Sunset Vista Restaurant occupied the ground floor of the resort's east wing, decorated in what Poseidon could only describe as "aggressive nautical theme." Fishing nets draped across the ceiling, plastic lobsters mounted on the walls, and a ship's wheel that served no apparent purpose except to occasionally smack unwary diners in the head. The menu featured dishes with names like "Catch of the Day (Frozen)" and "Shrimp Scampi (May Contain Shrimp)."

"This is spectacular," Amphitrite whispered, studying a decorative anchor that was clearly made of painted cardboard. "It's like someone described the ocean to an alien who then had to decorate based purely on that description."

"I'm going to smite something," Poseidon muttered.

"You're going to order the fish and chips and enjoy them."

The Henderson family had commandeered six tables pushed into an approximate rectangle, with Maya seated at the head like a tiny, victorious general. Someone had fashioned a crown from twisted napkins and placed it on her head. She wore it with the dignified pride of someone who'd just conquered Europe.

Sharon waved them over to two empty seats. "You made it! We were worried you'd skip out. Smart money was on you two sneaking away to do couple-y stuff instead of eating with a bunch of strangers."

"We're embracing new experiences," Amphitrite said, sliding into her chair with grace that seemed wasted on the plastic seat.

"That's the spirit! Fair warning—the food here is aggressively mediocre. But the company makes up for it." Sharon grabbed a bread basket and thrust it at them. "These rolls are from this morning. Probably. Maybe yesterday morning. Hard to say."

Poseidon took a roll that had the density of volcanic rock and tried to look grateful.

Across the table, Uncle Ray was sketching something on a napkin with intense focus. Carl leaned over to peer at it. "You drawing your castle?"

"I'm analyzing where I went wrong," Ray said without looking up. "The buttresses were sound, but I think I underestimated the aesthetic appeal of shell decoration. Next year, I'm incorporating found objects while maintaining structural integrity."

"Or you could just accept that an eight-year-old beat you."

"Eight and three-quarters," Maya called from her throne. "And I didn't just beat him. I dominated."

"That's my girl," Carl said proudly. "Confident bordering on insufferable. Gets it from her mother."

His wife Jennifer threw a roll at him, which he caught without looking.

A waitress appeared—name tag reading "DARLENE," expression reading "I've seen too much"—and began taking orders with the efficiency of someone trying to speedrun their shift. When she reached Poseidon, she didn't even make eye contact. "What'll it be?"

"Fish and chips."

"Tartar sauce?"

"Is it... real tartar sauce?"

"It's tartar-sauce-adjacent. Made with real mayonnaise-like product."

"I'll pass."

Darlene moved on to Amphitrite. "And you?"

"The garden salad, please. Dressing on the side."

"Smart choice. Only thing in the kitchen that can't be overcooked." Darlene disappeared toward the kitchen with everyone's orders, moving with the resigned grace of someone who'd long ago accepted their fate.

Derek leaned across the table toward Poseidon. "So what do you do? Like, for work?"

It was an innocent question. The kind mortals asked each other constantly to establish social context and determine whether someone was worth networking with. For Poseidon, it was complicated.

"I'm in water management," he said, which was technically true.

"Oh, like sewage?"

"More like... oceanic systems. Resource allocation. Environmental monitoring."

"That's cool. Is it, like, because of climate change and stuff? My teacher says the oceans are in trouble."

Poseidon felt his jaw tighten. "Your teacher is correct. The oceans are in significant trouble."

"Can you fix it?"

Such a simple question. Such an impossible answer. Could he fix it? Technically, yes. He could summon tsunamis to destroy coastal industry. He could make the seas unnavigable until humans learned to respect them. He could wake the kraken—all of them, there were seven now, they'd bred—and let them rampage through shipping lanes until mortals remembered why their ancestors made offerings to sea gods.

But Amphitrite's foot pressed against his under the table, a gentle warning.

"It's complicated," he said instead. "It requires cooperation from a lot of people making better choices."

"That sucks. Adults are always saying stuff requires cooperation from a lot of people. Seems like a cop-out." Derek grabbed another roll from the basket. "Like, if you could fix it yourself, would you?"

"Derek," his mother warned. "Don't interrogate the nice man about environmental policy."

"I'm not interrogating. I'm just asking. My debate teacher says we should always ask hard questions."

"Your debate teacher doesn't have to live with you after you ask hard questions at lunch."

But Derek's attention had already drifted to his phone, leaving Poseidon sitting with the weight of that innocent inquiry. *If you could fix it yourself, would you?*

Yes. He would. He'd wanted to for decades now, watching the slow destruction of his domain while tied by complicated agreements with other deities, international laws that somehow applied even to gods, and Amphitrite's gentle insistence that mass destruction wasn't the answer to humanity's problems.

"He asks good questions," Amphitrite said quietly. "Even when they're uncomfortable."

"Especially when they're uncomfortable."
