The figure was too tall, the limbs too long, the joints bending at angles that human anatomy didn't permit. It wore no protective suit, no respirator, despite the toxic atmosphere. Its skin had the same wet sheen as the dog's chitinous plates.

Behind it, two more figures resolved from the darkness. A man Kael didn't recognize, his body even more drastically transformed, ribs visible through translucent skin that pulsed with bioluminescence. And something that might have been human once but was now barely bipedal, its head crowned with branching structures that resembled antlers or perhaps neural tissue turned inside-out.

"What are you?" Kael managed.

The Voss-thing tilted its head, and the movement was eerily familiar. That was how Dr. Voss had looked when considering a difficult question, when parsing through incomplete data.

"We're what comes next," it said. "What the plague makes possible. You know this, Kael. You've always known."

"I don't—" Kael's head throbbed, that familiar pain of reaching for memories that disintegrated under examination. "I don't remember."

"No. You chose not to. Easier that way." The Voss-thing took a step closer, and Kael saw that its feet weren't quite touching the ground. It hovered a few centimeters above the stone, supported by nothing visible. "But it's time to stop running."

The plasma cutter felt slick in her grip. Could she even harm these things? They looked fragile, but the plague rewrote biology at the molecular level. They might be more durable than steel, might regenerate from any wound she could inflict.

"The facility is close," the Voss-thing continued. "Three hundred meters, just like your scanner indicated. We've kept it intact. Waiting."

"For what?"

"For you to come home."

The word hit her like a physical blow. Home. The concept felt simultaneously alien and achingly familiar. She had flashes—a sleeping pod, the hum of air recyclers, the antiseptic smell of a research station. Her hands working at a terminal, inputting data she couldn't quite see. Dr. Voss standing beside her, saying something urgent, something about containment protocols and irreversible cascades.

"I'm not going anywhere with you," Kael said, though her voice lacked conviction.

The dog growled again, deeper this time, and lunged forward. It moved faster than Kael expected, faster than its malformed body should have allowed. It went for the Voss-thing's leg, jaws snapping.

The Voss-thing moved its leg without seeming to move, a spatial displacement that made Kael's eyes hurt to track. The dog's teeth closed on empty air, and momentum carried it forward, tumbling across the stone floor.

"Don't," Kael said sharply, stepping between the dog and the transformed figures. "Don't hurt it."

The Voss-thing regarded her with something that might have been amusement. "We wouldn't. It's already one of us, in its way. Incomplete, yes, but transforming. Given time, it will complete the process."

"And become what? Like you?"

"Like itself. That's what you never understood about the plague, Kael. It's not a disease. It's an optimization. An unlocking of potential. Every organism becomes what it was meant to be, freed from the constraints of evolutionary dead-ends."

The dog limped back to Kael's side, favoring its normal legs over the clawed one. She could hear its labored breathing, see the confusion and pain in its eyes. Whatever the plague was, whatever these things believed it represented, the dog was suffering.

"It's in pain," she said.

"Metamorphosis is uncomfortable. You should know. You're transforming too."

Kael's hand went reflexively to her respirator, as if to check that the seal was intact. "I'm not—I'm protected. The suit—"

"The suit delays. It doesn't prevent. You've been breathing Thera's air for years now. Filters fail. Seals crack. The plague is patient." The Voss-thing gestured toward Kael's head. "Your memory loss. The fever dreams. The sense of disconnection from yourself. Those aren't symptoms of exposure, Kael. They're symptoms of transformation. Your brain is reorganizing. Making room for what you're becoming."

"No." But even as she said it, doubt wormed through her. How many filters had she replaced? How many times had the seal warning flashed on her HUD before she'd gotten around to checking it? And the memories that felt like they belonged to someone else—what if they did? What if she was becoming someone new, personality dissolving like morning fog?

"Come to the facility," the Voss-thing said, its voice gentler now, almost kind. "We have your records there. Your real memories, backed up before the transformation began. You can know who you were. You can choose who you'll become."

Kael looked down at the dog. It was watching her, those liquid brown eyes full of trust she hadn't earned. Whatever these things were offering, it wasn't salvation. The Voss-thing's body was wrong in ways that went beyond physical transformation. There was something hollow in its eyes, something absent that had nothing to do with humanity and everything to do with personhood.

"I choose to keep walking," Kael said.

She turned and started back the way she'd come, toward the environmental seal, toward the toxic surface. The dog followed without hesitation.

"You can't run from this," the Voss-thing called after her. "The transformation is already happening. Every hour you delay is an hour of unnecessary suffering."

Kael didn't answer. She focused on putting one foot in front of the other, on the familiar weight of her pack, on the sound of the dog's uneven gait beside her.

"We'll be here when you're ready," the Voss-thing said, and its voice was already fading, as if it were moving away rather than Kael. "We'll always be here. Waiting for you to come home."

The environmental seal resisted as Kael shoved against it, metal shrieking against stone. Her arms trembled with the effort, muscles burning from exhaustion and fear. Behind her, the bioluminescent glow of the under-gallery faded to nothing, leaving only the pale beam of her helmet light cutting through the darkness.

The dog squeezed through first, its malformed body somehow more flexible than it should be. Kael followed, scraping her shoulder against the corroded edge hard enough that warning lights flashed on her suit's integrity display. A small breach, already sealing with emergency foam. But every breach made the suit weaker, brought her closer to breathing Thera's poisoned air unfiltered.

She emerged into the Gallery of Ascending Light, and the perpetual twilight felt almost welcoming after the claustrophobic tunnel. The purple-gray sky pressed down through gaps in the collapsed ceiling, and the familiar taste of copper and ash penetrated even her respirator's filters.

Kael walked quickly, putting distance between herself and the seal. Her mind raced, trying to process what she'd seen. Dr. Voss. Transformed but recognizable. Speaking in that measured, analytical way, even as her body defied every law of physics and biology Kael understood.

*Your brain is reorganizing.*

She pressed her gloved hand against her temple, as if the pressure could hold her thoughts in place. How much of what she remembered was real? The laboratory with its sterile light—had that been a memory or a dream? The crystalline object in her hands—had she held it, or had someone else? And if her personality was dissolving, how would she even know?

The dog whined, pulling her attention outward. It had stopped walking and was staring back toward the environmental seal. Not with aggression now, but with something else. Longing, maybe. Or recognition.

"Don't," Kael said sharply. "We're not going back there."

The dog looked up at her, and for a moment she could have sworn she saw understanding in its eyes. Then it turned and continued forward, limping on its three normal legs, dragging the claw.

They navigated through the ruins in silence. Kael checked her scanner obsessively, plotting routes that took them away from the facility the Voss-thing had mentioned. Three hundred meters down the tunnel—she could still go there, could access those backed-up memories, could learn who she'd been before. The temptation gnawed at her.

But she'd seen what Dr. Voss had become. Whatever transformation the plague offered, it hollowed you out. Made you into something that wore your face while everything that had made you *you* was stripped away.

Unless it didn't. Unless that was another lie, another distortion. How could she trust her own judgment when her mind was compromised?

The dog stopped suddenly, its head snapping toward a collapsed archway to their right. Kael raised the plasma cutter, but nothing emerged from the shadows. After a moment, the dog relaxed and continued walking.

False alarm. Or the dog's senses picking up something her human perception missed. Either way, they kept moving.

Hours passed. Or maybe minutes. Kael's sense of time had never fully recovered from whatever the plague had done to her circadian rhythms. The bruised sky never changed, the light never varied. She marked progress by the ache in her legs, by the way her water supply ticked downward, by the dog's increasingly labored breathing.

They needed shelter. Rest. Food, though her remaining protein bars wouldn't go far split between two. But every structure they passed felt wrong somehow—too exposed, too close to the under-galleries, too obviously a place someone or something might come looking.

Finally, as her legs threatened to give out entirely, Kael spotted a half-collapsed watchtower at the edge of the Gallery. It leaned at a severe angle, its upper floors completely gone, but the ground level looked intact. More importantly, it had only one entrance, which she could defend if needed.

"Here," she said to the dog. "We stop here."

The interior was bare stone, stripped long ago of any furnishings or equipment. But it was dry, and the walls blocked the worst of the wind that had started to pick up across the ruins. Kael set down her pack and slumped against the wall, every muscle in her body screaming protest.

The dog collapsed beside her, flanks heaving. Up close, Kael could see more details of its transformation. The chitinous plates weren't uniform—some areas looked almost polished, catching the faint light with an opalescent sheen, while others were rough and cracked. The claw that had replaced its front paw opened and closed spasmodically, as if the dog couldn't control the movement.

She opened her pack and retrieved one of the two remaining protein bars. The dog's eyes tracked the movement, but it didn't move, didn't beg. Just watched with patient hunger.

"We need to ration," Kael said, more to herself than the dog. "Make this last."

But even as she said it, she was breaking the bar in half. The dog's need was immediate, visible in every rib pressing against its skin. Her own hunger was chronic, backgrounded by so many other concerns that she barely felt it anymore.

She held out half the bar. The dog didn't lunge this time. It carefully took the food from her hand, its teeth never touching her glove, and swallowed it in two quick gulps.

Kael ate her half slowly, forcing herself to chew each tasteless bite, to extract every possible calorie. Her stomach cramped with the influx of food after so long eating minimally. She drank water from her canteen, calculating how long the supply would last, where the nearest potential water source might be.

The scanner showed several options, but all of them involved going deeper into the ruins, closer to underground systems where the Voss-thing and its companions might be waiting.

"We'll head for the perimeter tomorrow," she said to the dog. "Get out of the Gallery entirely. There's... I think there's a research outpost to the northwest. Twelve, maybe fifteen kilometers. If we can reach it, there might be supplies. Equipment."

The dog tilted its head, that eerily human gesture of attention.

"I know," Kael continued. "You don't understand me. But I need to talk. Need to hear a voice, even if it's just my own."

Except it wasn't just her own anymore, was it? If her brain was reorganizing, if the plague was slowly rewriting her personality, then the voice in her head belonged to someone who was part-Kael and part-something else. The thought made her want to scream.

Instead, she took another small sip of water and sealed the canteen.

"I think I was a scientist," she said quietly. "Before. The flashes I get—they're usually laboratories, research stations. And Dr. Voss knew me. Knew my name. So I was part of whatever team was studying the plague." She paused. "Or maybe I was part of the team that *caused* it."

The dog shifted closer, until its body pressed against her leg. The warmth was startling—she'd expected the chitinous patches to be cold, inorganic, but they radiated heat like fevered skin.

"What if I did this?" The question came out barely above a whisper. "What if I'm responsible for Thera, for all those dead cities, for you?" She looked down at the dog. "What if I deserve to transform? To become like Dr. Voss?"

The dog licked her gloved hand with its forked tongue, a gesture so simple and trusting that Kael felt tears burning behind her respirator again.

"You don't care," she said. "You don't care what I did or didn't do. You're just trying to survive."

She closed her eyes and let her head rest against the stone wall. The fever was building again—she could feel it creeping up from the base of her skull, that familiar heat that preceded the fragmented dreams. She should take stimulants, should force herself to stay alert in case something came looking for them.

But exhaustion dragged at her like gravity, and she was so tired of fighting.

"Just for a few hours," she murmured. "Then we move."

The dog's breathing had already evened out into sleep. Kael listened to it, used it as an anchor, and let herself drift.

The dream came immediately, vivid and sharp-edged.

She was in a laboratory, but not the sterile space from her earlier flashes. This one was older, more primitive. Thera's ruins surrounded it—she could see the Gallery through reinforced windows, still intact, still magnificent. The bioluminescent ceiling pulsed in regular cycles, and people moved beneath it. Hundreds of them. Thousands. A thriving civilization going about its daily business.

Dr. Voss stood beside her, younger but unmistakable. "The precursor civilization lasted at least eight thousand years," she was saying, gesturing at a holographic display. "Then they vanished. No war. No plague. Just... gone. Every city abandoned overnight."

Dream-Kael looked at the display, at glyphs she could suddenly read. *Transformation*. *Ascension*. *Becoming*.

"They did this to themselves," dream-Kael said. "The plague isn't a weapon. It's a tool. Something they engineered deliberately."

"For what purpose?" Dr. Voss asked.

"Evolution. Forced, rapid, comprehensive." Dream-Kael's hands moved across controls she didn't remember learning. "They were trying to transcend biological limitations. To become something beyond physical form."

"Did it work?"

Dream-Kael turned from the display to look at Dr. Voss directly. When she spoke, her voice carried certainty and something else. Something like awe.

"We're about to find out."

The dream shattered like glass, each shard a different memory she couldn't quite grasp. Kael woke gasping, her respirator fogged with condensation, her body slick with sweat inside the suit. The fever had broken, leaving her wrung out and shaking.

The dog was awake too, watching her with those mismatched eyes—one brown and normal, the other beginning to facet like an insect's compound eye. How long had she slept? Her chronometer said six hours, but that felt both too long and not long enough.

She sat up slowly, her head pounding. The dream clung to her consciousness with unusual persistence. Usually the fever dreams dissolved upon waking, leaving only emotional residue—dread, guilt, longing. But this one remained vivid, detailed, almost more real than the crumbling watchtower around her.

*We're about to find out.*

Had she said those words? Had she actually stood in that laboratory, watching holographic displays of a dead civilization's final project? Or was her deteriorating mind simply filling gaps with plausible fiction?

Kael checked her suit's medical readouts. Blood oxygen normal. Heart rate elevated but stable. Temperature slightly high—38.2 Celsius—but nothing critical. The cognitive function test that she could trigger from her wrist display sat there, unused. She'd stopped taking it weeks ago, after the results had started showing progressive decline in pattern recognition and memory retention. Knowing the numbers only made it worse.

The dog stood and stretched, three legs cooperating in the movement while the clawed limb scraped against stone with a sound that set Kael's teeth on edge. It looked at her, then at the entrance to the watchtower, the message clear: time to move.

"Yeah," Kael agreed, her voice hoarse. "Let's get out of here."

She packed quickly, her movements automatic from long practice. Water, scanner, the last protein bar carefully wrapped. The plasma cutter clipped to her belt. Everything else she'd abandoned long ago—unnecessary weight, tools for a survival she'd stopped believing in.

Outside, the bruised sky had darkened slightly, Thera's equivalent of night. The wind had picked up, carrying streamers of toxic dust that made visibility drop to perhaps fifty meters. Good. Harder for anything to track them. Bad. Harder to navigate, easier to get turned around in the labyrinth of ruins.

Kael activated her helmet light and checked the scanner. The device flickered, its cracked screen showing interference patterns that hadn't been there before. She tapped it sharply against her palm—a useless gesture, but one that had occasionally worked in the past. The display cleared partially, showing a route toward the perimeter of the Gallery.

"Northwest," she told the dog. "Fifteen kilometers. We can make that in... six hours? Maybe eight with rest stops."

The dog tilted its head in that disturbingly human way, then started walking in the direction she'd indicated. It was limping worse than yesterday, the clawed leg dragging more than supporting. Kael watched it struggle for a few steps before making a decision she knew was probably stupid.

She caught up and knelt beside it. "Let me look at that."

The dog froze, uncertain. Its haunches tensed, ready to bolt despite its obvious pain.

"I'm not going to hurt you," Kael said softly. "Just want to help."

She extended her hand slowly, letting the dog smell her glove, giving it time to decide. After a long moment, it relaxed slightly. Kael gently lifted the clawed limb.

Up close, the transformation was even more disturbing. The claw looked functional—it had proper articulation, the segments moved smoothly when she carefully manipulated them. But the connection point where paw became claw was inflamed, the tissue red and weeping clear fluid. The plague's work, still ongoing, flesh and chitin warring for dominance.

"This is infected," Kael said. She dug through her pack and found her medical kit—mostly empty now, but she still had antiseptic spray and some self-adhesive bandaging. "Gonna sting."

She sprayed the wound site, and the dog yelped but didn't pull away. Good. It was learning to trust her. Or maybe it was just too tired to fight. Either way, she cleaned the area as best she could and wrapped it loosely with bandaging that would probably fall off within an hour but might help until then.

"There," she said, releasing the limb. "That's the best I can do."

The dog licked her glove again, then straightened and took a few experimental steps. The limp was still pronounced, but it moved with slightly less obvious pain.

They set off together into the ruins.

The Gallery stretched endlessly in the failing light, a necropolis of impossible scale. Kael had been walking through it for days—or weeks?—and still hadn't reached the far edge. The precursor civilization had built on a scale that made human architecture look like children's toys. Even collapsed and eroded, the monuments dwarfed everything.

She tried to imagine this place alive, thriving, the way she'd seen it in her dream. Thousands of beings moving through streets that were now choked with debris, conducting their lives under the bioluminescent ceiling that had since dimmed to the faintest glow. Had they been happy? Had they known what was coming when they'd engineered their plague, their tool of forced evolution?

*To become something beyond physical form.*

But the Voss-thing still had a physical form. Altered, impossible, but material. So whatever the precursors had been attempting, it hadn't worked. Or it had worked differently than intended. Or—and this thought made Kael's skin crawl—it was still working, still unfolding across centuries, and what she'd encountered in the under-gallery was just another intermediate stage.

The scanner beeped, pulling her attention back. She glanced at the display and her stomach dropped.

Movement. Multiple contacts. Behind them, closing fast.

"Run," Kael said.

The dog didn't need to be told twice. It bolted, three-legged gait awkward but faster than Kael expected. She ran after it, her pack bouncing against her spine, breath rasping in the respirator. The contacts on the scanner spread out, moving to flank them.

Not random. Coordinated. Hunting.

Kael risked a glance back and saw shapes flowing through the darkness—too fluid to be human, too purposeful to be debris caught in the wind. She couldn't make out details, but there were at least five of them, possibly more.

The dog veered suddenly toward a collapsed structure, disappearing into a gap between fallen pillars. Kael followed without hesitation, squeezing through an opening barely wide enough for her pack. On the other side was a small courtyard, enclosed on three sides by intact walls. Only one exit besides the gap they'd entered through—a doorway leading deeper into the structure.

Trap. Her tactical training—where had she learned tactical training?—screamed that this was a trap. But the contacts were almost on them, and there was nowhere else to go.

She grabbed the dog and dove through the doorway.

Inside was darkness so complete that her helmet light seemed to stop after only a few meters, absorbed by nothing. The air smelled different here—less copper and ash, more like wet stone and something organic she couldn't identify. Decay, maybe, or growth.

Kael pressed against the wall beside the doorway, plasma cutter drawn, and waited.

The sounds of pursuit stopped. No footsteps in the courtyard. No shapes visible through the doorway. Just silence, heavy and expectant.

"We know you're in there, Kael."

Not the Voss-thing's voice this time. This one was male, deeper, with an odd harmonics that suggested multiple vocal cords vibrating at once.

"We're not trying to frighten you," the voice continued. "We're trying to help. The transformation is accelerating. You felt it in your sleep, didn't you? The fever. The dreams that are more than dreams."

Kael's finger tightened on the plasma cutter's trigger. She could fire blind, fill the doorway with superheated plasma. But she had limited charge, and there were multiple contacts out there. She'd maybe take down one before the others swarmed her.

The dog pressed against her leg, trembling. Not from fear this time. From something else. Its body was hot, feverish, the same heat Kael felt in her own skin.

"Your companion is suffering," the voice said, somehow gentle despite its alien harmonics. "The incomplete transformation is painful. We can finish it. Make it whole."

"By turning it into something like you?" Kael called back, surprised by the steadiness in her voice.

"By turning it into itself. Into what it was always meant to become. The plague doesn't destroy, Kael. It reveals. It strips away evolutionary compromises and lets organisms achieve their full potential."

"If it's so great, why did the precursors disappear?"

Silence. Then: "They didn't disappear. They transcended. Became something beyond physical detection. Beyond matter itself. We're following the same path. We've just... not completed the journey yet."

"How long?" The question came out before Kael could stop it. "How long have you been transforming?"

"Some of us for years. Some for decades. Time becomes... flexible, once the metamorphosis reaches certain thresholds. We experience duration differently than you do."

Kael tried to process that. Decades of transformation, and the Voss-thing could still speak, could still think coherently enough to track her through the ruins. Maybe the hollow quality she'd sensed wasn't absence. Maybe it was something else. Something she couldn't understand because she was still too human.

Or maybe they were just very convincing monsters, wearing human speech like a disguise.

"Leave us alone," Kael said. "We're not interested in your transcendence."

"It's not about interest. It's inevitable. You've been breathing Thera's air for too long. Your blood chemistry has already shifted. Your neural pathways are reorganizing. In six months, maybe less, you'll be like us whether you choose it or not. The only question is whether you suffer through the transition alone, fighting every step, or accept help."

Six months. The number hit her like a physical blow. She'd known intellectually that her exposure was cumulative, that every breath through failing filters brought her closer to transformation. But she'd avoided calculating timelines, avoided thinking in concrete terms about what was happening to her body.

The dog whimpered and pressed harder against her leg. Its body was shaking now, and when Kael looked down, she saw its eyes were unfocused, rolling back. Fever. The transformation was spiking, triggered by stress or proximity to the others or just its own internal timeline.

"You see?" the voice said. "We can feel its distress. We're connected, all of us who carry the plague. Your companion is calling for help, and we want to answer."

"Don't touch it," Kael said, hearing the desperation in her voice.

"We won't. Not without permission." A pause. "But Kael... its pain is unnecessary. A few hours in the facility, and the transformation would complete cleanly. No suffering. No fear. It would emerge whole."

"And hollow. Like you."

"Is that what you think we are? Hollow?" The voice carried something that might have been sadness. "We're more than we were. More thoughts, more sensory input, more *existence* in every moment. The personality you're trying to preserve—that limited, frightened thing—is just a chrysalis. Why mourn its dissolution when something beautiful is waiting to emerge?"

Because I'm terrified, Kael thought but didn't say. Because I don't trust your promises. Because the dog's suffering is awful, but at least it's still *the dog*, and I don't know what it would be if you finished transforming it.

The dog's shaking intensified, and foam appeared at its mouth. Seizure. The transformation was triggering neurological storms, and Kael had nothing in her medical kit that could help. She could watch it convulse and maybe die, or she could...

"If I bring it to the facility," Kael heard herself say, "will you let me leave afterward?"

"Of course. We're not captors, Kael. We're offering a choice."

"Swear it. Whatever you've become, there must be some concept of honoring agreements."

Another pause. Then: "We swear by the Pattern That Unfolds. Your companion will receive treatment, and you'll be free to leave. We'll even provision you—food, water, medical supplies. Everything you need to reach the perimeter."

The dog's convulsions were worsening. Kael had perhaps minutes before the seizure caused permanent damage.

She looked down at the animal that had trusted her, that had followed her away from the under-gallery even though its body was breaking down. She thought about watching it die in this dark structure, alone except for her, its last moments filled with pain and confusion.

"All right," Kael said. "All right. I'll bring it."

The transformed beings waited in the courtyard with an inhuman patience that made Kael's skin crawl. Five of them, she counted as her helmet light swept across their forms. Each radically different from the others—one humanoid enough to pass in shadow, another so extensively modified it took her several seconds to identify which end was the head. They stood motionless, not even the subtle shifts of weight or breathing that living things exhibited. Like statues that happened to be made of flesh.

The Voss-thing stepped forward as Kael emerged, the dog limp in her arms. Its convulsions had subsided into occasional twitches, but its breathing was shallow, irregular.

"This way," the Voss-thing said, already turning back toward the under-gallery entrance.

They moved through the ruins in formation—two of the beings ahead, two behind, with the Voss-thing walking beside Kael. An escort or a guard detail. Probably both. The dog's weight dragged at her arms, heavier than it should be. Or maybe she was just that exhausted, running on reserves she'd depleted days ago.

"How did you find us?" Kael asked, partly to distract herself from the surreal horror of the situation, partly because she needed to know. If they could track her this easily, there was no point in running.

"We didn't find you. We've always known where you were." The Voss-thing's voice carried no triumph, just statement of fact. "The plague creates connections between those it touches. We can sense each other across distances. Feel the shape of transformation in nearby organisms. Your companion's distress called to us like a beacon."

"So I've been leading you to me. Every day I traveled with the dog."

"Yes and no. We weren't tracking you specifically. But when transformation spikes—fever, seizure, the critical thresholds—it becomes difficult to ignore. Like hearing someone scream. You can choose not to respond, but you can't choose not to hear."

Kael looked down at the dog's labored breathing. "And mine? You can sense my transformation too?"

"Of course. You're further along than you realize. Another month, perhaps two, and you'll begin showing external changes. The internal reorganization is nearly complete."

The words should have terrified her. Instead, she felt only a strange numbness. Too many revelations, too much fear—eventually the system overloaded and produced nothing. She kept walking.

They reached the environmental seal and passed through. The under-gallery's bioluminescent ceiling had brightened, responding to the presence of the transformed beings. In the blue-green glow, Kael could see details she'd missed before. The walls weren't smooth stone—they were carved with microscopic glyphs, millions of them, covering every surface. Text or decoration or perhaps instructions for the transformation itself.

"The precursors left records," the Voss-thing said, following Kael's gaze. "Most we still can't read, even with our enhanced cognition. But enough to understand the broad strokes. They spent eight thousand years preparing. Engineering the plague vector by vector, generation by generation. Testing it on themselves, on animals, on the planetary biosphere."

"Why?" The question came out hoarse. "If it was so painful, so destructive—"

"Because they were dying anyway. The native ecology of Thera was failing. Some interaction between their industrial processes and the local biochemistry created cascading collapse. They could evacuate, abandon the planet, or they could adapt. They chose adaptation."

The tunnel branched, and they took the left path, descending deeper. The air grew warmer, heavy with moisture. Kael's respirator filters struggled with the humidity, producing a whistling sound on each exhale.

"How do you know all this?" she asked.

"Dr. Voss knew it. Before transformation, she spent thirty years studying precursor sites. That knowledge didn't disappear when she changed. It integrated, expanded. She understood the glyphs with human cognition—I understand them with something more."

*She.* *I.* The Voss-thing's pronouns kept shifting, as if it couldn't quite decide whether it was still the person whose face it wore. Kael wanted to ask about that, about the continuity of identity across transformation, but the dog whimpered in her arms and the moment passed.

The tunnel opened into the chamber the scanner had detected—much larger than Kael expected. Easily a hundred meters across, the ceiling lost in shadow despite the bioluminescent glow. Equipment filled the space, though "equipment" felt like the wrong word. Organic structures that might have been machines or might have been organisms, arranged in concentric circles around a central platform. Everything pulsed with bioluminescence, creating rhythms that made Kael's eyes hurt to track.

"Lay your companion there," the Voss-thing said, gesturing to the platform.

Kael's instincts screamed at her to run. Whatever this place was, it reeked of wrongness, of biology twisted into purposes it was never meant to serve. But the dog's breathing had become even more labored, and there were flecks of blood in the foam around its mouth.

She carried it to the platform and gently set it down. The surface was warm, slightly yielding, like skin. The dog's eyes opened briefly, found hers, held for a moment that felt both eternal and devastatingly brief.

"I'm sorry," Kael whispered. "I don't know what else to do."

The Voss-thing approached the platform and placed one too-long hand on the dog's flank. Immediately, the bioluminescence in the room shifted, all the rhythms synchronizing, pulsing in time with the dog's failing heartbeat.

"The transformation arrested prematurely," the Voss-thing said, its voice distant, focused. "The original damage to its leg created scar tissue that resisted integration. The plague has been trying to work around it, but the conflict is causing systemic failure."

"Can you fix it?"

"We can complete what was started. Remove the blockage. Allow the transformation to finish."

Kael watched as tendrils of bioluminescent matter rose from the platform, wrapping gently around the dog's body. They looked fragile as spider silk but she could feel the power in them, see the way they pulsed with directed purpose.

"How long?"

"Four hours. Maybe five. You should rest. The medical bay has facilities—"

"I'm staying here." The words came out flat, final.

The Voss-thing regarded her for a long moment, then nodded. "As you prefer."

It moved to one of the organic machines and seemed to merge with it, the boundaries between its body and the structure blurring. The other transformed beings positioned themselves around the chamber, motionless, their attention focused inward or on the dog or on something Kael couldn't perceive.

She found a spot along the wall and slid down to sitting, every muscle in her body protesting. Her pack provided minimal cushioning against the stone, but she barely noticed. All her attention was fixed on the platform, on the dog's small form wrapped in alien light.

The tendrils worked with disturbing precision, probing, adjusting, weaving through flesh that parted without bleeding. Kael could see the dog's chest rising and falling, the rhythm becoming steadier as the minutes passed. The convulsions stopped. The foam disappeared. Whatever the tendrils were doing, it was working.

She should have felt relief. Instead, she felt loss.

This creature, whatever it became after transformation, wouldn't be the dog that had followed her from the alcove. Wouldn't have those liquid brown eyes full of simple trust. It would be optimized, enhanced, unlocked—all the words the Voss-thing had used. But it wouldn't be what it was.

Kael's hand went to her respirator, pressing against the seal. How many more breaths until her own transformation became irreversible? How many more days before she woke up and couldn't remember why preserving her humanity mattered?

"You're thinking about running again."

She looked up. The Voss-thing had detached from the organic machine and stood a few meters away. In the shifting light, its face looked almost normal, almost like the Dr. Voss from Kael's fragmentary memories.

"Thinking about it," Kael admitted. "Won't do it. I gave my word."

"Words matter to you. Agreements. Structure." The Voss-thing tilted its head. "You were always like that. Even before. Dr. Amara Voss was chaos incarnate—brilliant but scattered. You were the organized one. The one who kept the research on track."

Kael felt something click in her mind, a fragment of memory suddenly sharp and complete. An argument in that bright laboratory. Dr. Voss gesturing wildly at holographic data, insisting they had to publish immediately, warn the colonies, evacuate Thera. And Kael—younger, certain, so fucking certain—arguing for more data, more controlled testing.

*We don't know enough yet. If we raise the alarm prematurely, they'll quarantine the planet. Destroy everything. We need six more months, maybe a year.*

"I stopped you," Kael said, the realization hollow in her chest. "You wanted to warn people, and I stopped you."

"You convinced the ethics board to give us more time. Yes." The Voss-thing's voice carried no accusation. "By the time we understood what the plague really was, it had already spread beyond containment. Not just Thera—three colony ships, two research stations, an orbital platform. All infected."

"How many died?"

"Died?" The Voss-thing considered the question. "None, technically. Everyone transformed. But if you're asking how many retained enough cognitive function to be considered continuous with their original personality..." It paused. "Approximately forty percent. The rest fragmented during transformation. Became something new without connection to what they were."

Sixty percent. Thousands of people, their identities dissolved like sugar in water, replaced by something that wore their bodies like ill-fitting clothes.

"And you blame yourself," the Voss-thing continued. "That's why you chose amnesia. Why you used the memory wipe protocols to erase everything except basic survival skills. You couldn't live with what you'd done, so you chose not to remember it."

Kael's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against the stone floor, forcing them still. "The backed-up memories you mentioned. In the facility. If I access them..."

"You'll remember everything. Your real name—it's not Kael, by the way. The full scope of the research. The decisions you made. The people you loved. Dr. Voss. The others on the research team. Your daughter—"

"Stop." The word came out strangled. "I don't... I can't..."

But the Voss-thing's words had already triggered something, a cascade of fragmented images. A child's laughter. Small hands reaching up to be held. A name on the tip of her tongue that she couldn't quite grasp. Brown eyes—the same shade as the dog's—looking up at her with absolute trust.

*Mama, look! I found a beetle!*

The memory shattered, leaving only absence.

"She transformed early," the Voss-thing said quietly. "One of the first exposures. You tried everything to reverse it, to halt the process. But the plague doesn't reverse. It only moves forward."

Kael pressed the heels of her hands against her respirator, as if she could push the grief back inside. "What happened to her?"

"She completed transformation. The cognitive continuity was good—nearly ninety percent. She remembers you. She's here, actually. In one of the deeper chambers. I can take you to her, after your companion finishes treatment."

The offer hung in the air like poison. A daughter she couldn't remember but who remembered her. A connection that could make everything—the guilt, the isolation, the slow dissolution of self—bearable. Or destroy her completely.

On the platform, the dog stirred. The tendrils were retracting, their work apparently complete.

prompt: Write a story about two teenagers exploring first love in the privacy of one’s childhood home only to be humorously blindsided by an unexpected phone call and a sibling’s oblivious return. Play up themes of embarrassment, budding intimacy, and how everyday technology can crash the most romantic moments. Set it in a nostalgic early 2000s suburb and include at least two comedic interludes caused by modern devices gone awry. Make the story roughly 8,000-10,000 words long.
generated_story:
The house smelled like vanilla air freshener and old carpet, the same scent that had permeated Jamie Chen's childhood for the past sixteen years. She stood in the foyer on that humid August afternoon in 2003, hyperaware of everything: the grandfather clock ticking in the living room, the hum of the central air conditioning struggling against the heat, and most acutely, the presence of Marcus Webb standing behind her, close enough that she could feel the warmth radiating from his skin.

"Your parents won't be back until six?" Marcus asked, his voice carrying that slight crack it sometimes did when he was nervous. At seventeen, he was still growing into himself—all lanky limbs and basketball player height, with dark curly hair that he was constantly pushing out of his eyes.

"Six-thirty," Jamie corrected, setting her backpack down by the stairs. "Mom texted me from the outlet mall. They're still shopping with Aunt Linda." She pulled out her Nokia 3310, the one with the scratch on the screen from when she'd dropped it at the movie theater last month, and showed him the message as proof. The blue-lit screen displayed her mother's careful typing: "HAVING FUN. HOME BY 630. LEFTOVERS IN FRIDGE. LOVE MOM."

Her parents had only recently started texting, and her mother still typed like she was composing a formal letter.

Marcus grinned, and Jamie felt that familiar flutter in her stomach—the one that had been appearing with increasing frequency since the end of junior year, when their friendship of three years had begun shifting into something else entirely. Something that made her palms sweat and her thoughts scatter like dandelion seeds.

"And your brother?" Marcus asked.

"Tyler's at band practice until five, then he's going to Craig's house to play videogames. He won't be back until after dinner." Jamie led Marcus into the living room, suddenly conscious of how ordinary everything looked: the floral couch her mother had bought at Sears, the entertainment center with its collection of VHS tapes and a handful of DVDs, the framed school photos chronicling her and Tyler's progression through their awkward phases. "Sorry it's kind of boring here."

"Are you kidding?" Marcus dropped onto the couch, and it creaked under his weight. "This is way better than my house. My dad's been home all week doing remote work stuff, which means he's always walking in on everything. Last night he interrupted my phone call with you three times to ask if I'd seen his reading glasses."

Jamie laughed, settling onto the couch beside him—not quite touching, but close enough that the possibility hummed between them. They'd been dating officially for exactly two weeks, ever since the night he'd kissed her at the Fourth of July fireworks display at the community park. Two weeks of holding hands in the mall, of long phone conversations that stretched past midnight, of carefully choreographed run-ins with friends designed to look casual. But this was different. This was the first time they'd been truly alone together, without the buffer of public spaces or the threat of parental supervision.

"So," Marcus said.

"So," Jamie echoed.

They looked at each other, and then they both started laughing—the nervous, slightly hysterical laughter of teenagers acutely aware of possibility and completely unsure how to navigate it.

"This is weird, right?" Jamie said. "We've hung out a million times, but now it feels—"

"Different," Marcus finished. He reached over and took her hand, lacing their fingers together. His palm was slightly damp, which made Jamie feel better about her own nervousness. "Good different, though."

"Yeah," Jamie agreed softly. "Good different."

The grandfather clock chimed the half-hour. Three-thirty. Three hours of privacy stretched before them like an ocean. Marcus shifted closer, and Jamie could smell his cologne—the Curve for Men he'd probably borrowed from his older brother, mixed with the laundry detergent scent of his t-shirt. Her heart was beating so loudly she was certain he could hear it.

"Can I kiss you?" he asked, and the earnest politeness of it made her heart squeeze.

"You don't have to ask every time," she whispered, but she was already leaning in.

Their lips met, soft and tentative at first, then with growing confidence. This was their seventh kiss—Jamie had been counting—but it still felt new and electric, like something out of the teen movies she and her friends analyzed frame by frame at sleepovers. Marcus's hand came up to cup her cheek, his thumb brushing against her jaw, and Jamie thought that this, right here, was probably what all those love songs were about.

They broke apart, foreheads touching, both breathing a little harder.

"Okay," Marcus said, his voice rough. "Okay, that was—"

"Yeah," Jamie agreed.

They kissed again, longer this time, and Jamie felt herself sinking back against the couch cushions. Marcus followed, his weight comfortable and thrilling as he braced himself above her. His hand found her waist, resting there lightly, asking permission without words. She covered his hand with hers, answering yes.

The grandfather clock ticked on. The air conditioner hummed. Somewhere in the neighborhood, a lawnmower started up, its distant buzz barely registering in Jamie's consciousness. Everything felt perfect and terrifying and exactly right.

Until, from the coffee table, her cell phone erupted into its programmed ringtone—a tinny, digital version of "In Da Club" by 50 Cent that she'd spent an hour programming last week.

They both froze, Marcus hovering above her, their lips still inches apart. The phone buzzed against the glass table surface, the vibration amplifying the tinny melody into an insistent mechanical chorus.

"Don't answer it," Marcus whispered, but his body had already tensed, the spell of the moment fracturing.

Jamie tilted her head to see the screen. "It's my mom."

"You said she wouldn't call."

"She usually doesn't." Jamie sat up, and Marcus shifted back to give her space, running a hand through his curly hair in frustration. She grabbed the phone, thumb hovering over the answer button. Her mother's name blinked on the blue screen: MOM CELL.

"I have to answer. If I don't, she'll worry." Jamie pressed the button and brought the phone to her ear, acutely aware of how her voice might sound—breathless, guilty. She forced herself to sound normal. "Hello?"

"Honey, it's Mom." Her mother's voice carried that particular quality it always had when she was trying to sound casual but actually wasn't. "Is everything okay there?"

"Yeah, everything's fine. Why?"

"No reason. Just checking in. You're home from Kelly's house already?"

Jamie's stomach dropped. Kelly's house. She'd told her parents she was going to Kelly's house after summer school to work on their history project. She'd completely forgotten to account for the timeline. "Yeah, we finished early. Kelly had to go with her mom to her sister's soccer game."

Marcus was watching her, picking up on the tension. He mouthed, "What's wrong?"

Jamie shook her head slightly. Her mother was saying something about the outlet mall, how Aunt Linda had found a great deal on kitchen towels, how they were thinking about stopping at Olive Garden for an early dinner. Jamie made appropriate noises—"uh-huh," "that sounds nice," "yeah, the endless breadsticks"—while her mind raced.

"So you're there by yourself?" her mother asked, and there it was, the real reason for the call. The checking-up disguised as casual conversation.

"Tyler's here," Jamie lied automatically, then immediately regretted it. What if her mother had already talked to Tyler? What if he'd told her he was at band practice?

"Oh, I thought he had band practice until five."

"He came home to grab his drumsticks. He forgot them." The lie came easier the second time, building on itself. "He's leaving in like five minutes."

Marcus's eyes widened. He started to stand up, probably to go hide somewhere, but Jamie grabbed his arm and shook her head firmly. The movement would make noise. Any change would make noise.

"Well, that's good. I don't like you being there alone." Her mother paused, and Jamie could hear the muffled sound of Aunt Linda talking in the background, something about sizes. "Listen, honey, we might be a little later than six-thirty. The restaurant is pretty busy. Probably closer to seven. Will you be okay?"

"Yeah, Mom. I'm fine."

"There are leftovers in the fridge. The pasta from Tuesday. And don't forget to lock the front door."

"I won't. I locked it when I got home." Another lie, smooth as silk.

"Good girl. We'll see you around seven. Love you."

"Love you too." Jamie ended the call and let out a breath she didn't know she'd been holding.

"That was close," Marcus said.

"She knows."

"What? No, she doesn't. You were totally convincing."

Jamie shook her head, setting the phone down on the coffee table like it might bite her. "She never calls to check in. Never. And she definitely never calls just to chat about kitchen towels and Olive Garden." She looked at Marcus, anxiety replacing the warmth that had been building between them. "She knows something's up."

"Maybe she's just being a mom. My mom calls me like five times a day when I'm out."

"Your mom is different. Mine trusts me. Or, she did trust me." Jamie stood up, needing to move, to think. She walked to the window and peered out at the street, half expecting to see her parents' Honda Accord pulling into the driveway, her mother having driven home specifically to catch her in a lie.

But the street was empty except for Mr. Peterson from across the way, pushing his lawnmower in neat diagonal lines across his lawn. A perfectly normal summer afternoon in suburban Illinois.

Marcus came up behind her, not touching but close. "Hey. Even if she suspects something, so what? We're not doing anything wrong. We're just hanging out."

"We're not just hanging out," Jamie said quietly.

"Okay, we're making out. But that's not illegal. We're teenagers. This is literally what teenagers do."

Jamie turned to face him. "My parents are really strict about this stuff. Like, really strict. My mom still thinks I'm twelve. And my dad—" She didn't finish the sentence. Her father was a gentle man, quiet and methodical, a mechanical engineer who approached problems with careful analysis. But when it came to his daughter, there was a protective streak that bordered on obsessive. He'd lectured her for twenty minutes when she'd gotten her belly button pierced last year, and that had been with permission.

"What are you saying?" Marcus asked, and she could hear the hurt creeping into his voice. "Do you want me to leave?"

"No. I don't know. Maybe?" Jamie pressed her palms against her eyes. "This is stupid. I'm being paranoid."

"You're not being paranoid. You're being careful." Marcus stepped back, creating distance between them. "Look, I can go. I'll say I just stopped by for a minute to drop off that CD I burned for you. If your mom asks, that's all it was."

"What CD?"

"I don't know, I'll make one really fast. I'll go home right now and—"

"Marcus." Jamie dropped her hands and looked at him. At his earnest face, his nervous energy, the way he was already planning an alibi to protect her. Something softened in her chest. "Don't go."

Marcus's expression shifted from resignation to hope. "You sure?"

"No," Jamie admitted. "But I don't want you to leave just because I'm freaking out." She glanced at the clock—3:47. Her mother wouldn't be home for over three hours. That phone call had probably been exactly what Marcus said: just a mom being a mom. "I'm being ridiculous."

"You're not ridiculous." Marcus closed the distance between them again, but didn't reach for her. "Your parents sound intense. My parents barely notice when I'm home or not."

Jamie managed a weak laugh. "Want to trade?"

"God, no. Your mom makes amazing pot stickers."

The tension broke a little, and Jamie felt herself relax incrementally. Marcus had this effect on her—he could make her laugh even when she was spiraling. It was one of the things she loved about him. Loved. The word had been hovering at the edges of her thoughts for weeks now, too big and terrifying to examine directly.

"Come on," she said, making a decision. "Let's go upstairs. We can watch a movie or something."

"Upstairs?" Marcus's eyebrows rose.

"My room has a TV. And a door that locks." Jamie saw his expression and quickly added, "Not like that. I just mean—privacy. No windows facing the street. No grandfather clock making me paranoid about time."

"Okay," Marcus said, but his voice had that uncertain quality again. They were venturing into new territory, and they both knew it.

Jamie led him up the carpeted stairs, past the family photos that documented every awkward phase of her childhood. Second-grade Jamie with pigtails and missing front teeth. Fifth-grade Jamie with braces and unfortunate bangs. Eighth-grade Jamie trying too hard to look mature in her first school dance dress. She was suddenly hyperaware of how embarrassing all of it was, this documented evidence of her dorkiness.

"Cute," Marcus said, pausing at the photo of seven-year-old Jamie holding up a trophy nearly as big as she was. "What's this one?"

"Spelling bee. Fifth grade. I misspelled 'privilege' in the final round."

"That's a hard word."

"I spelled it with two L's." Jamie tugged at his hand. "Come on, stop looking at those."

Her room was at the end of the hall, next to the bathroom. She'd spent all morning cleaning it in anticipation of this moment—vacuuming the carpet, making her bed with the good comforter, hiding the embarrassing stuffed animals that normally lived on her pillow. Her walls were covered in magazine cutouts and posters: Justin Timberlake, Avril Lavigne, a giant print of *The Lord of the Rings* movie poster. Her desk was cluttered with textbooks and college prep materials—SAT study guides, a thick folder labeled "COLLEGE APPS," printouts about scholarships.

Marcus stepped inside, and Jamie watched him take it all in. She felt weirdly vulnerable, like he was seeing something private. He'd been in her room before, but always with the door open, always with her parents or brother somewhere in the house. This was different.

"I like it," Marcus said, walking over to examine her CD collection on the bookshelf. "Very you."

"What does that mean?"

"Organized. But also—" He gestured at the chaos of her bulletin board, which was covered in ticket stubs, Polaroids of friends, random quotes she'd cut out from magazines. "Also not."

Jamie closed the door and turned the lock with a quiet click that seemed to echo. Marcus heard it and turned around. They looked at each other across the small space of her bedroom, the air suddenly charged again.

"So," Jamie said. "Movie?"

"Sure. What do you want to watch?"

She had a stack of DVDs on top of her TV—mostly rom-coms and teen movies she'd bought or borrowed. *10 Things I Hate About You*, *She's All That*, *Drive Me Crazy*. "You pick."

Marcus flipped through them, then laughed. "These are all kissing movies."

"They're not all kissing movies."

"This one has a couple kissing on the cover. This one's literally called *Drive Me Crazy*. And Heath Ledger makes out with Julia Stiles on a paintball field in this one."

Jamie felt her cheeks heat. "Okay, fine. But that's just what movies are. People kiss in movies."

"I'm not complaining." Marcus pulled out *10 Things I Hate About You* and held it up. "This one?"

"Perfect."

While Marcus figured out her DVD player, Jamie sat on her bed, then immediately worried that sitting on the bed was too suggestive. Should she sit at her desk chair? But that would be weird, watching a movie from across the room. She stayed on the bed but sat against the headboard, legs crossed, trying to look casual.

Marcus got the movie started and then hesitated, looking between the bed and the floor.

"You can sit up here," Jamie said, patting the space next to her. "I don't bite."

"That's disappointing," Marcus said, but he climbed up next to her, and they settled in side by side, shoulders touching. The opening credits began to roll, but Jamie found it impossible to focus on the screen. Every point of contact between them felt electric—his arm against hers, his knee occasionally bumping her leg, the rise and fall of his breathing.

They made it about fifteen minutes into the movie before Marcus turned to her and said, "I can't concentrate on this."

"Me neither," Jamie admitted.

"Can I kiss you again?"

Jamie answered by closing the distance between them. This kiss was different from the ones downstairs—slower, deeper, like they had all the time in the world. Which they did, Jamie reminded herself. Three hours. The thought was intoxicating and terrifying in equal measure.

Marcus shifted, and they rearranged themselves until Jamie was lying back against her pillows and Marcus was beside her, propped up on one elbow. The movie played on, forgotten. His hand traced lazy patterns on her arm, then her shoulder, then carefully, tentatively, along the curve of her waist. Each touch sent shivers through her.

"Is this okay?" he whispered against her lips.

"Yeah," Jamie breathed. "More than okay."

They kissed until her lips felt swollen, until she lost track of time entirely. Marcus's hand slipped under the hem of her t-shirt, just barely, his palm warm against the skin of her lower back. Jamie's own hands were in his hair, running down his shoulders, exploring the unfamiliar territory of someone else's body.

Somewhere in the haze of sensation, Jamie heard a sound that didn't belong—a mechanical rumble from outside. A car engine. Probably just a neighbor. But then it shut off, and car doors slammed, and voices drifted up from the driveway.

Her mother's voice. Her father's deeper rumble.

Jamie's eyes flew open. She pushed Marcus back, her heart suddenly hammering for an entirely different reason. "Oh my God. They're home."

Marcus sat up fast, running his hands through his hair to flatten it. "What? But it's only—" He looked at her alarm clock. "Four-thirty. They said seven."

"I know what they said!" Jamie was already off the bed, frantically smoothing down her shirt, checking her reflection in the mirror above her dresser. Her lips were red and obviously kissed. Her hair was a mess. She looked exactly like someone who'd been making out for the past forty-five minutes.

Downstairs, the front door opened. Her father's voice called out, "Jamie? You home?"

"What do I do?" Marcus hissed, standing in the middle of her room looking panicked.

"I don't know! Hide?"

"Where?"

"I don't know! The closet?"

"That's so cliché—"

"Jamie?" Her mother's voice now, closer. On the stairs.

There was no time. Jamie made a split-second decision, unlocked her bedroom door, and swung it open just as her mother reached the top of the landing. She stepped out into the hallway, pulling the door mostly closed behind her, trying to look casual and probably failing spectacularly.

Her mother stopped, her hand on the banister, and her eyes narrowed with immediate suspicion.

Jamie's mother stood at the top of the stairs in her outlet mall clothes—khaki capris and a floral blouse that looked suspiciously new—with that particular expression that mothers everywhere have perfected: the one that says they already know the answer to the question they're about to ask.

"Hi, honey," her mother said, her voice carefully neutral. "What are you doing up here?"

"Just—I was changing. My shirt had a stain." Jamie gestured vaguely at her t-shirt, which had no stain whatsoever. "From lunch. Ketchup."

Her mother's eyes did a quick scan—Jamie's face, her hair, the partially closed door behind her. "I thought you were at Kelly's house working on your project."

"I was. We finished early. I told you on the phone, remember? Kelly had to go to her sister's soccer game."

"Right." Her mother took a step closer, and Jamie resisted the urge to back up against the door. "And Tyler?"

"Band practice. Then Craig's house." Jamie's voice sounded too high, too forced. She tried to modulate it. "Why are you guys home so early?"

"Your Aunt Linda wasn't feeling well. Something she ate at lunch." Her mother was now close enough that Jamie could smell her perfume—White Diamonds, the same scent she'd worn for years. "We decided to skip the restaurant and head back."

"Oh. That's too bad. Is she okay?"

"She'll be fine." Her mother's gaze drifted past Jamie to the bedroom door. "Is someone in your room?"

"What? No." The lie felt enormous in Jamie's mouth, impossible to swallow. "Why would someone be in my room?"

"Jamie." Her mother's voice shifted into a tone Jamie knew well—disappointed but not surprised, like she'd been expecting this exact conversation. "I can hear the TV. And I heard voices when we pulled up."

Jamie's mind raced. She could feel sweat forming at her hairline. Behind her, through the crack in the door, she could sense Marcus frozen in place, probably terrified. "I was watching a movie. You're hearing the movie."

"What movie?"

"*10 Things I Hate About You*."

"Uh-huh." Her mother crossed her arms. "And you're watching it alone?"

From downstairs, Jamie's father called out, "Everything okay up there?"

Her mother kept her eyes locked on Jamie. "Fine, honey. Just talking to Jamie." Then, quieter, "Open the door, sweetheart."

"Mom—"

"Open it."

There was no point in continuing the charade. Jamie felt something crumble inside her—whether it was hope or dignity, she wasn't sure. She reached behind her and pushed the door open wider.

Marcus stood in the middle of her room looking like he wanted the floor to swallow him whole. His hair was still disheveled despite his attempts to fix it, and his t-shirt was wrinkled. To his credit, he didn't try to hide or make excuses. He just stood there, shoulders squared, and met her mother's gaze.

"Mrs. Chen," he said quietly. "Hi."

Jamie's mother's expression cycled through several emotions in rapid succession—surprise, anger, disappointment, resignation. She settled on a tight smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Marcus. I didn't realize you were here."

"I should have called first. That was rude of me." Marcus's voice was steady, but Jamie could hear the tremor underneath. "I'm sorry."

"How long have you been here?"

"About an hour, maybe? We were just watching the movie and—"

"With the door locked?"

The question hung in the air. Jamie found her voice. "Mom, we weren't doing anything wrong. We were just—"

"Just what? Just lying to me about where you were and who you were with?" Her mother's voice was rising now, the careful control slipping. "Just having a boy in your bedroom with the door locked while you thought your father and I would be gone for hours?"

"Linda." Jamie's father appeared at the top of the stairs, slightly out of breath. He took in the scene—his wife's rigid posture, Jamie's guilty face, Marcus looking like he wanted to disappear—and his expression hardened. "Marcus. I think you should go home now."

"Dad—" Jamie started.

"Now," her father repeated, his voice quiet but firm. The engineer's voice, the one he used when something was non-negotiable.

Marcus moved toward the door immediately. He paused next to Jamie, and she saw him wanting to say something—an apology, maybe, or a goodbye—but her father's presence made it impossible. "I'm sorry, Mr. and Mrs. Chen. Jamie, I'll—I'll talk to you later."

"Actually," Jamie's mother said, "I don't think that's a good idea. Not for a while."

Marcus looked stricken, but he didn't argue. He edged past Jamie's parents and headed down the stairs. Jamie heard the front door open and close, and then he was gone, leaving her alone to face the consequences.

Her parents stood in the hallway, a united front. Her mother spoke first. "Living room. Now."

The walk downstairs felt like a death march. Jamie's legs were shaky, and her face was burning with shame and anger—though she wasn't entirely sure who she was angry at. Her parents for coming home early? Herself for lying? The universe for terrible timing?

She sat on the floral couch, the same couch where she and Marcus had been kissing less than an hour ago. Her parents took the armchairs across from her, creating a tribunal. The grandfather clock ticked loudly in the silence.

"I want to hear it from you," her mother said. "The truth. All of it."

Jamie took a breath. "Marcus and I are dating. We have been for two weeks. I told you I was going to Kelly's house because I knew you wouldn't let him come over if you weren't home. We were watching a movie. That's it."

"That's it?" Her father's voice was sharp. "You lied to us, snuck a boy into our house, locked yourself in your bedroom with him—that's it?"

"We were just watching a movie!"

"With your hair messed up and your face flushed and—" Her mother stopped, pressing her fingers to her temples. "Jamie, do you think we're stupid? Do you think we don't remember what it's like to be seventeen?"

"Then why are you freaking out? Nothing happened!"

"But it could have," her father said. "And that's the point. You put yourself in a situation where—where things could have gotten out of hand."

Jamie felt tears of frustration burning behind her eyes. "You don't trust me."

"You lied to us," her mother countered. "How can we trust you when you lie to us?"

"I lied because I knew you'd react like this! You treat me like I'm a child!"

"You're sixteen!"

"Almost seventeen! And Marcus is a good guy. He's respectful and smart and—"

"I don't care if he's the second coming of Jesus Christ," her father interrupted, and the profanity—mild as it was—shocked Jamie into silence. Her father almost never invoked religious imagery. "You lied to us. That's what this is about. Not Marcus, not what you were or weren't doing. The lying."

"Your father's right," her mother added, her voice softer now but no less firm. "We've always been honest with each other, haven't we? We've always had an open relationship. If you wanted to have Marcus over, you could have asked."

"And you would have said no."

"Maybe. Maybe not. But you didn't give us the chance to say anything because you didn't ask." Her mother leaned forward. "Do you understand why that's a problem? Why that breaks our trust?"

Jamie did understand, which somehow made it worse. She wiped at her eyes, angry at herself for crying. "I'm sorry. I should have asked."

"Yes, you should have." Her father's voice was gentler now. "Jamie, we know you're growing up. We know you're going to want to date and spend time with boys. But there have to be rules. Boundaries."

"Like what?" Jamie asked, dreading the answer.

Her parents exchanged a glance, having one of those silent conversations that long-married couples could have without words. Her mother spoke first. "No more Marcus for two weeks."

"What? That's not fair!"

"You can text him or talk to him on the phone," her mother continued as if Jamie hadn't spoken. "But no hanging out, no dates. Two weeks."

"Mom—"

"And," her father added, "when those two weeks are over, if you want to see him, you ask us first. Every time. And you follow our rules."

"Which are what, exactly?"

"No being alone in the house together," her mother said. "If he comes over, we're home. If you go somewhere, it's a public place, and you tell us where you're going and when you'll be back. And you keep your phone on and charged at all times."

"So basically prison," Jamie muttered.

"Basically consequences," her father corrected. "You're lucky we're not grounding you for longer. Your mother wanted a month."

Jamie looked at her mother, who had the grace to look slightly sheepish. "I'm being lenient because this is your first major offense. But if it happens again—if you lie to us again—the consequences will be much more severe. Do you understand?"

"Yes," Jamie said, the word bitter on her tongue.

"And I want you to think about why you felt you had to lie," her mother continued. "Really think about it. Because we're not unreasonable people, Jamie. We've always tried to be fair with you. But trust goes both ways. If you can't be honest with us, how can we trust you with more freedom?"

The logic was sound, which made it even more frustrating. Jamie knew her parents were right—she had lied, and that was wrong. But she also knew that if she'd asked permission to have Marcus over, they would have said no, or at least made it so awkward and supervised that it wouldn't have been worth it. There was no winning.

"Can I go to my room now?" Jamie asked, her voice small.

Her parents looked at each other again. "Yes," her father said. "But your door stays open from now on. No more locking it."

"What about when I'm changing?"

"Bathroom," her mother said. "Go on. We'll call you down for dinner in an hour."

Jamie stood and trudged back upstairs, feeling the weight of their disappointment following her. In her room, the TV was still on, the menu screen for *10 Things I Hate About You* playing on a loop. She turned it off and collapsed on her bed, staring at the ceiling.

Her cell phone was still downstairs on the coffee table where she'd left it. She felt cut off, isolated. Marcus was probably home by now, probably mortified. She wondered if his parents would find out, if they'd punish him too, if he'd decide she was too much trouble and end their relationship before it really began.

The thought made fresh tears spring to her eyes. Two weeks felt like an eternity.

Jamie lay on her bed staring at the textured white ceiling, tracing the familiar patterns of stippled paint with her eyes. The house had settled into an uneasy quiet—her parents downstairs probably discussing her moral failings, Tyler still blissfully unaware at band practice, and her own room feeling less like a sanctuary and more like a cell with its door propped open by one of her father's engineering textbooks.

Her alarm clock read 5:12 PM. Marcus had been gone for forty-two minutes.

She rolled onto her side and caught sight of herself in the dresser mirror. Her hair was still messed up, falling out of the ponytail she'd hastily thrown it into that morning. Her lips were still slightly swollen from kissing. Evidence of her transgression, written on her face for anyone to see. She looked away.

The Justin Timberlake poster on her wall suddenly seemed childish, a relic of some younger version of herself who didn't have to worry about consequences and trust and the complicated mathematics of teenage freedom. She remembered putting it up last year, how excited she'd been to get the magazine with the pullout poster, how she and Kelly had spent an entire afternoon deciding on the perfect placement. That had been before everything got so complicated, before boys stopped being distant celebrities and became real people who could make your heart race and your palms sweat.

Before Marcus.

She thought about the first time she'd really noticed him, not just as her brother's friend's older brother, but as someone separate and interesting. It had been at the end-of-year school assembly in May. He'd been sitting three rows ahead of her, and when Principal Morrison had made some terrible dad joke about summer reading, Marcus had turned around to catch his friend's reaction and had accidentally locked eyes with Jamie instead. He'd smiled—not the polite smile you give a friend's little sister, but a real smile, like he was genuinely happy to see her. And something had shifted.

After that, they'd started talking more. First in group settings, then in smaller groups, then just the two of them. Long conversations about music and movies and college plans and whether or not the universe was infinite or just really, really big. He made her laugh. He listened when she talked about her stress over AP classes and her parents' expectations. He didn't treat her like a kid.

And now her parents had taken him away for two weeks. Two weeks might as well be two years in teenage time.

Jamie sat up and looked at her desk, at the college prep materials stacked in neat piles. Princeton, Stanford, MIT—schools her parents had been pointing her toward since middle school. Schools that required perfect grades and perfect test scores and perfect extracurriculars. Schools that left no room for imperfection, for lying to your parents, for getting caught making out with your boyfriend.

The boyfriend she couldn't see for two weeks.

She heard footsteps on the stairs and quickly wiped at her face, trying to compose herself. Her mother appeared in the doorway—not entering, respecting the boundary of Jamie's room even as she'd stripped away most of Jamie's privacy.

"Can I come in?" her mother asked.

Jamie shrugged, which her mother took as permission. She sat on the edge of Jamie's bed, smoothing down her new floral blouse. Up close, Jamie could see that her mother looked tired, the fine lines around her eyes more pronounced than usual.

"I know you think we're being unfair," her mother said quietly.

Jamie didn't respond. Anything she said right now would probably make things worse.

"When I was your age in Taiwan, my parents were much stricter than your father and I are with you. If they'd caught me with a boy in my room—" She shook her head. "I probably wouldn't have left the house until I was eighteen."

"This isn't Taiwan," Jamie muttered. "It's Illinois. In 2003."

"I know that. And that's why we're trying to find a balance. But Jamie—" Her mother's voice caught, and Jamie looked up, surprised to see emotion in her mother's carefully controlled expression. "You're my only daughter. My baby girl. And I know you think you're all grown up, but you're still so young. There are things that can happen—consequences that can change your whole life—"

"Mom, we were just kissing."

"I know. And maybe that's all it was going to be. But things can escalate quickly. Faster than you think. And I—" Her mother paused, seeming to choose her words carefully. "I don't want you to make choices you're not ready for. Choices you might regret."

Jamie felt a flash of anger. "You don't trust me to make my own choices."

"I trust you to make the choices you think are right in the moment. I don't trust teenage hormones and opportunity." Her mother reached out and tucked a strand of hair behind Jamie's ear, a gesture from childhood. "I was a teenager once too, you know. I remember what it feels like, how intense everything is. How much you think you know."

"So you understand why this is so unfair."

"No, I understand why you think it's unfair. There's a difference." Her mother stood up. "Two weeks, Jamie. That's all. And if you and Marcus really care about each other, two weeks won't change that."

After her mother left, Jamie lay back down and stared at the ceiling some more. Two weeks wouldn't change how she felt about Marcus, but what if it changed how he felt about her? What if he decided she was too much drama, that the whole sneaking around and lying and getting caught wasn't worth it? What if he met someone else, someone whose parents weren't so strict, someone who didn't come with so many complications?

The thoughts spiraled, each one worse than the last.

Downstairs, she heard Tyler come home, heard his surprised voice asking where she was, heard the low murmur of her parents explaining. Great. Now her little brother would know too. He'd probably give her endless grief about it, make kissing noises whenever Marcus's name came up, turn it into ammunition for sibling warfare.

She must have dozed off because the next thing she knew, her mother was calling her down for dinner. The grandfather clock said 6:45. She'd lost over an hour to anxious half-sleep.

Dinner was awkward. Tyler kept glancing at her with barely suppressed glee, clearly dying to make a comment but restrained by their parents' presence. Her father ate in silence, cutting his chicken into precise geometric pieces. Her mother made small talk about Aunt Linda's recovery from her mystery stomach ailment, about the sales at the outlet mall, about anything except the elephant in the room.

Jamie pushed food around her plate and said nothing.

After dinner, her father asked her to help him with the dishes. They stood side by side at the sink, him washing and her drying, the same routine they'd had since she was tall enough to reach the counter.

"Your mother was very upset," he said quietly, scrubbing at a plate. "More than she showed you."

Jamie dried a glass with more force than necessary. "I said I was sorry."

"I know you did. But sorry doesn't mean much if you don't understand why what you did was wrong."

"I do understand. I lied. It was wrong."

Her father handed her another glass. "It's not just the lying, Jamie. It's the trust. When you lie to us, even about something small, it makes us question everything. If you lied about this, what else might you lie about? Where else might you cut corners?"

Jamie felt the words like a physical blow. Her father, who'd always been her champion, who'd helped her with math homework and taught her how to change a tire and told her she could be anything she wanted—he was questioning her integrity. Her fundamental trustworthiness.

"I made one mistake," she said, her voice tight.

"One mistake we caught," her father corrected. "And that's what worries me. Not what happened today, but what it might mean for the future. You're going to college in less than two years. You'll have complete freedom. And if you can't be trusted now, with small freedoms—"

"I get it," Jamie interrupted. "You don't trust me. Message received."

Her father turned off the water and looked at her directly. "I want to trust you. But you have to earn it back. Do you understand?"

Jamie nodded, not trusting herself to speak without crying.

"Good. Then let's start fresh. Two weeks. Follow the rules. Show us that you can be responsible. And then we'll talk about Marcus again."

After the dishes were done, Jamie escaped back to her room. She desperately wanted her phone—wanted to text Kelly, wanted to know if Marcus had tried to call, wanted some connection to the outside world. But her phone was still downstairs, and asking for it felt like admitting defeat.

She tried to focus on homework instead. Summer reading for AP English—_The Great Gatsby_, which she'd read before but was supposed to reread for the class discussion in the fall. She opened to the first page but couldn't concentrate. The words swam in front of her eyes.

Around nine o'clock, her mother appeared in the doorway again, this time holding Jamie's Nokia. "You had three missed calls from Marcus. And several texts from Kelly asking if you're okay."

Jamie's heart leaped. "Can I—"

"You can call Marcus. Ten minutes. Then I want the phone back."

Jamie took the phone like it was made of gold. Her mother lingered in the doorway for a moment, as if wanting to say something else, then left.

Jamie dialed Marcus's number with shaking fingers.

He picked up on the first ring.
