Focus group analyses on the best messaging to downplay risks. Legal memos outlining strategies to shield executives from criminal prosecution. A spreadsheet calculating the acceptable number of deaths before the negative publicity would outweigh the profit margins.

The acceptable number was twenty thousand.

"Jesus," Maya whispered.

A shadow fell across her table. She looked up sharply, her hand instinctively moving to close her phone.

A woman stood there, maybe forty, with silver-streaked hair pulled back in a tight bun. She wore a faded denim jacket over a black t-shirt that read DATA IS THEFT in block letters. Her eyes were sharp, calculating, the kind of eyes that saw through bullshit.

"You're reading the Vitae docs," the woman said. It wasn't a question.

Maya said nothing.

"I'm Vera. I run the local chapter of the Digital Truth Coalition. I saw you click through from the feed." She gestured toward the empty seat across from Maya. "Mind if I sit?"

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Yes, you do. And if you're smart, you'll let me sit before the Vitae surveillance algorithm flags your device and sends someone considerably less friendly than me to find you."

Maya's jaw tightened. She wanted to tell this woman to leave her alone, to let her read in peace, to stop dragging her deeper into something she didn't fully understand. Instead, she nodded toward the empty seat.

Vera slid into the booth, her movements economical. She placed her own phone face-down on the table—a model at least five years old, its screen covered in a privacy film that made it impossible to read from any angle but directly above.

"First time looking at leaked corporate docs?" Vera asked.

"First time any of it mattered to me."

"What changed?"

Maya hesitated. The woman in white, her empty eyes, the dark trail down her chin. Marcus Chen and his tablet, his gift card, his questions. The way the story was being scrubbed from the feeds even as it happened.

"I saw someone die today," Maya said finally. "At NeoMart. From EternaFlow."

Vera nodded slowly. "You're the one they're looking for."

"What?"

"Vitae's put out feelers through their contacts in the LAPD. They're trying to identify witnesses from the NeoMart incident. Offering rewards for information. They want to control the narrative before it gets away from them."

"I didn't see anything. I heard screaming and came back, but by the time I got there—"

"Doesn't matter what you saw. Matters what they think you saw." Vera leaned forward, her voice dropping. "You need to understand something. What you're reading right now? Those documents are going to destroy Vitae Corp. Their stock is already crashing. The FDA can't ignore this. Criminal investigations are inevitable. But before that happens—before the full weight of justice, such as it is, comes down on them—they're going to try to contain the damage. That means finding the leakers. Intimidating the witnesses. Buying silence where they can, threatening it where they can't."

"I'm nobody," Maya said. "I sort data for twelve hours a day. I have forty-four dollars in my bank account. I'm not a threat to anyone."

"You were there. You refused their sample. You walked away when their crisis management team tried to buy you off. You clicked on leaked documents from a encrypted source. You're on their radar now, whether you want to be or not."

Maya's coffee had gone cold. She wrapped her hands around the cup anyway, needing something to anchor her. The documents were still loading on her phone—more memos, more test results, more evidence of a corporation that had knowingly released a product that would kill thousands of people in pursuit of profit.

"What do you want from me?" Maya asked.

"Nothing. I'm here to offer you something." Vera pulled a slim card from her jacket pocket and slid it across the table. It was black, unmarked except for a string of numbers printed in small white text. "That's a secure relay number. If Vitae comes after you—when they come after you—call it. We have lawyers. We have safe houses. We have people who know how to keep witnesses alive long enough to testify."

"I'm not testifying about anything."

"You don't have a choice anymore. The moment you clicked that link, you became part of this story." Vera stood, her expression softening slightly. "I know you're scared. I know you just want to go home and forget any of this happened. But people are dying, and the only way to stop it is to make sure the truth comes out. All of it. You don't have to be a hero. You just have to survive long enough to tell what you saw."

She walked away before Maya could respond, disappearing into the coffee shop's dim interior and then out onto the street.

Maya sat alone in the booth, staring at the black card. The numbers meant nothing to her, just random digits, but she found herself entering them into her contacts anyway. Not under a name—she wasn't that stupid—just a string of symbols that would mean nothing to anyone who might later scroll through her phone.

Her screen lit up with a notification.

Marcus Chen had sent her a friend request on three different social media platforms.

Maya's stomach dropped. She'd never given him her name. Had never given him anything except the sight of her face in a crowd. But apparently, that had been enough. Facial recognition software, cross-referenced against NeoMart's customer database, matched with the biometric data she'd given when she'd paid for her toothpaste with her thumbprint.

They knew exactly who she was.

Her phone buzzed again. A text from an unknown number.

*Ms. Reeves, this is Marcus Chen from Vitae Corp. I wanted to follow up on our brief conversation this evening. I realize I may have come across as insensitive during a traumatic time. I'd like to make it right. Would you be available for a call tomorrow? We're prepared to offer significant compensation for your time.*

Maya's finger hovered over the delete button. Instead, she screenshotted the message and sent it to the relay number Vera had given her. She didn't know why. Maybe because Vera had been right—she was part of this now, whether she wanted to be or not.

The response came within seconds.

*Don't respond. Don't engage. Go home but don't stay there. Pack what you need and wait for further instructions.*

Maya read the message three times. Each reading made it feel less real and more terrifying. This was insane. She wasn't some whistleblower or activist. She was a data sorter who'd come to NeoMart for toothpaste. This wasn't her fight.

But the woman's face wouldn't leave her mind. Those empty eyes. The dark trail down her chin. The way the body had been removed so quickly, so efficiently, as though Vitae Corp had been waiting for it.

The acceptable number was twenty thousand.

Maya finished her cold coffee, gathered her things, and walked out into the neon-soaked darkness of the Arts District. The maglev station glowed three blocks away. Her capsule apartment waited beyond that, familiar and safe and utterly insufficient as a hiding place.

She thought about her mother. About the experimental treatments, the false hope, the way the medical companies had extracted every dollar from a dying woman's bank account. About how corporations always won, how they bought justice and silence and survival for themselves while everyone else just tried to make it through another day.

Maya pulled out her phone and opened her banking app.

Forty-four dollars and thirty-one cents.

It would have to be enough.

She turned away from the maglev station and started walking deeper into the city, into neighborhoods where the surveillance cameras had blind spots and the corporate reach hadn't quite extended. Behind her, her phone buzzed again—Marcus Chen, persistent as a tumor—but she didn't look back.

The rain started just after midnight, acidic and cold, hissing against the cracked pavement of what had once been called Skid Row before the city rebranded it as the "Urban Renewal District." Maya ducked under the corrugated metal awning of a shuttered convenience store, watching water stream past her feet in oily rivulets that caught the reflected glow of distant holographic advertisements.

Her phone had died six blocks back. Just as well—they couldn't track a dead phone, and the silence felt like a small mercy. She'd stuffed it into a drainage grate, watching it tumble into darkness, taking with it Marcus Chen's insistent messages and whatever surveillance protocols Vitae Corp had embedded in her digital footprint.

The capsule apartment keys were still in her pocket, useless now. She couldn't go back there. Vera's instructions had been clear: *don't stay there*. Maya had grabbed what she could carry—a change of clothes, her grandmother's silver bracelet, the forty-four dollars she'd converted to physical cash at a 24-hour kiosk. Everything else was abandoned. Her entire life, such as it was, reduced to what fit in a canvas messenger bag.

She tried not to think about what she'd left behind. The photos on her tablet. The few books she'd managed to keep from her mother's collection. The cheap coffeemaker that took five minutes to brew a single cup but had been hers, familiar, a small anchor in a world designed to keep people like her unmoored.

A figure emerged from the rain, hunched under a transparent poncho that did nothing to hide the bulk of the person beneath. Maya tensed, her hand moving instinctively to the keys in her pocket—not much of a weapon, but better than nothing.

"You Maya Reeves?" The voice was rough, feminine, tinged with an accent Maya couldn't place.

"Who's asking?"

"Vera sent me. Said you'd be somewhere around here, looking lost and trying not to look lost." The woman pulled back her hood, revealing a face that had seen hard decades—scar tissue along one cheek, a cybernetic eye that gleamed faintly red, hair buzzed close to the scalp. "Name's Bosch. I'm supposed to take you somewhere safe."

"How do I know Vera actually sent you?"

"You don't." Bosch shrugged. "But you got two choices right now. Come with me, or wait here until Vitae's contractors find you. They got people combing the Arts District already. Face scanners at every transit hub. Your picture's being distributed to private security firms with a ten-thousand-dollar bounty for your location."

Maya's stomach twisted. "Ten thousand dollars for a data sorter who witnessed a death?"

"Ten thousand for the woman who accessed leaked documents and went dark immediately after. You're not just a witness anymore. You're a liability who knows too much." Bosch gestured down the street, where a battered electric van idled at the curb, its charging port sparking intermittently. "We need to move. Now."

The van's interior smelled of old coffee and synthetic leather. Maya climbed into the back, finding a bench seat that had been modified with restraining harnesses—the kind used for prisoner transport. Bosch caught her looking.

"Used to run an underground railroad for undocumented workers before the Citizenship Consolidation Act," she explained, sliding into the driver's seat. "These days I help people who pissed off the wrong corporations. Career change, same clientele—people who need to disappear."

The van lurched forward, its electric motor whining as they accelerated through streets Maya didn't recognize. The rain hammered against the roof, drowning out all but the closest sounds. Through the tinted windows, she caught glimpses of the city at night—homeless encampments huddled under highway overpasses, automated delivery drones buzzing through the downpour, billboards cycling through advertisements for products Maya would never afford.

One of the billboards showed the Vitae Corp logo, that double helix and sunrise, but the usual EternaFlow imagery had been replaced with a simple message in white text: *VITAE CORP: COMMITTED TO SAFETY. COMMITTED TO TRANSPARENCY. COMMITTED TO YOU.*

Maya laughed, the sound sharp and bitter in the enclosed space.

"They're already rewriting the story," she said.

"They've been rewriting it since the first body dropped." Bosch took a hard left, the van's tires squealing. "The FDA's review board is staffed with former Vitae executives. The mainstream media outlets are all owned by the same parent companies that own shares in Vitae. By tomorrow morning, the narrative will be 'isolated incidents' and 'pre-existing conditions' and 'unfortunate but statistically inevitable.' By next week, people will be buying EternaFlow again because they'll convince themselves they're in the safe sixty-seven percent."

"But the documents—"

"Will be labeled as deepfakes. As corporate espionage from competitors. As the work of radical anti-progress terrorists." Bosch glanced at Maya in the rearview mirror. "You read about the Panacea scandal in 2039?"

Maya's throat tightened. "My mother died from complications. The treatments destroyed her immune system."

"Then you know how this ends. Some settlements. Some regulatory reforms. Some executives retiring with their fortunes intact. And the people who died? They become statistics. Footnotes in medical journals. Cautionary tales that no one actually learns from."

The van turned again, descending into what looked like an old parking structure that predated the Vertical Expansion. The levels went down and down, past crumbling concrete pillars spray-painted with territorial markers from gangs that probably didn't exist anymore. At the lowest level, Bosch pulled into a space next to a reinforced steel door.

"We walk from here," she said.

The door opened onto a tunnel—narrow, dimly lit by emergency strips that ran along the ceiling. The air was cool and damp, tasting of mildew and old earth. Maya followed Bosch through passages that seemed to branch and reconnect randomly, a maze beneath the city that she suspected had been here long before the mega-corporations had paved over the surface.

"What is this place?" Maya asked, her voice echoing strangely.

"Old metro tunnels. Maintenance access for infrastructure that doesn't officially exist anymore. The city sealed most of them after the Expansion, but some of us kept a few routes open. Useful for moving people and information when the surface gets too hot."

They walked for what felt like twenty minutes, though Maya had lost all sense of time and direction. Finally, Bosch stopped at another door—this one newer, equipped with a biometric scanner that looked military-grade. She pressed her palm to it, and mechanisms clicked and whirred.

The space beyond was larger than Maya expected. It had once been some kind of control room, walls lined with ancient electrical panels and defunct monitoring equipment. Now it had been retrofitted into a makeshift operations center. Screens displayed news feeds, stock tickers, and what looked like surveillance footage from various locations around the city. A dozen people worked at terminals, their faces lit by the blue glow of their monitors. The air hummed with the sound of servers and cooling fans.

Vera sat at a central desk, three screens arranged in a semicircle around her. She looked up as Maya entered, her expression unreadable.

"You made it. Good."

"You could have warned me I'd have a bounty on my head," Maya said.

"Would you have believed me? You barely believed me in the coffee shop." Vera gestured to an empty chair. "Sit. We need to move quickly. Vitae's containment strategy is accelerating faster than we anticipated."

Maya sat, her legs weak from the walk and the accumulated stress. On one of Vera's screens, she could see the leaked documents—the ones she'd been reading at The Analog—but now they were annotated, highlighted, cross-referenced with other files.

"How many people have died?" Maya asked.

"Confirmed deaths? Eighty-seven in the last twelve hours. But that's just what's been reported. The real number is probably three or four times that. Hospitals are being pressured to code the deaths as unrelated incidents. Heart attacks. Strokes. Existing conditions." Vera pulled up a spreadsheet that made Maya's stomach turn—names, ages, locations, times of death. "We're compiling our own database. Trying to establish a pattern that can't be dismissed."

"And the whistleblower? TruthSerum?"

"Dead. They found her body this morning in her apartment. Ruled it a suicide." Vera's jaw tightened. "She'd set up dead-man switches, backups, encrypted caches. That's why the documents are still circulating despite Vitae's best efforts to scrub them. But the original source is gone, which means they'll try to discredit everything she released."

Maya stared at the faces on the screen—the dead and dying, their lives reduced to data points in a spreadsheet. She thought about the woman in white at NeoMart, about the acceptable number of twenty thousand, about the ten-thousand-dollar bounty on her own head for the crime of bearing witness.

"What do you want from me?" she asked again.

"We need you to testify," Vera said. "Not just about what you saw at NeoMart. About everything. The documents you accessed. The way Vitae tried to buy your silence. The speed of their response. We're building a case—not for the courts, because the courts are bought—but for public opinion. We need people to see a face, hear a story, understand that this isn't just statistics and spreadsheets. It's real people dying while corporations calculate profit margins."

"I'm not a hero."

"I'm not asking you to be. I'm asking you to survive long enough to tell the truth." Vera leaned back in her chair. "In three days, there's going to be a public hearing at City Hall. Emergency session called by a few council members who aren't completely in Vitae's pocket. It's our best chance to get testimony on record before they shut everything down. But we need witnesses. We need someone who was there, who saw it happen, who can look into cameras and make people understand what Vitae Corp knowingly released into the world."

Maya looked around the operations center, at the people working in the dim light, fighting a battle against enemies with infinite resources and no conscience. She thought about her forty-four dollars, about her capsule apartment she'd never see again, about her mother dying while corporations extracted profit from her desperation.

She thought about walking away. About finding some new city, some new identity, some way to disappear into the vast anonymity of a world that didn't care about people like her.

But the woman's empty eyes wouldn't leave her mind.

"Three days," Maya said. "Then what?"

"Then we see if the truth actually matters anymore."

The safe house was a converted shipping container welded to three others in a stack, hidden in what Bosch called "the Gaps"—the neglected spaces between the Vertical Expansion's massive support pillars where the city's infrastructure had grown too dense for even corporate surveillance to penetrate completely. Maya's quarters consisted of a cot, a chemical toilet behind a privacy screen, and a single overhead light that flickered every few minutes.

She sat on the cot at 3 AM, unable to sleep, watching the light flicker and counting the seconds between each spasm. Thirty-seven. Thirty-eight. Forty-two. The inconsistency bothered her more than it should have, but her mind needed something to focus on besides the reality that she was now a fugitive hiding in a shipping container because she'd witnessed a death and clicked the wrong link.

Her old life felt impossibly distant. The data sorting facility with its endless blue light. The capsule apartment with its coffeemaker. The simple transaction of selling hours for dollars, of existing in the margins without making waves. She'd been invisible for so long that visibility felt like a kind of violation.

A soft knock on the corrugated metal door. Maya tensed.

"It's Vera. I brought food."

Maya opened the door to find Vera holding two steaming containers of something that smelled like actual vegetables—a luxury Maya hadn't experienced in months. They sat on the cot together, eating in silence while the light flickered overhead.

"You don't have to do this," Vera said finally. "The hearing. We can find someone else. Another witness."

"I thought you needed me specifically."

"I need someone. But I can see what this is costing you." Vera set down her container, her expression softer than Maya had seen before. "Most people who end up here are activists, journalists, people who chose this fight. You just wanted toothpaste."

Maya laughed, the sound hollow. "Forty-four-dollar toothpaste."

"The most expensive tube in history, apparently." Vera smiled, but it faded quickly. "I mean it, though. If you want out, we can arrange transport. New identity, new city. You'd have to stay quiet, never talk about what you saw, but you could build something resembling a normal life again."

The offer hung in the air between them. Maya thought about her mother again—not the dying version, but the one from earlier memories. The woman who'd marched in protests when Maya was a child, who'd screamed at corporate lobbyists outside government buildings, who'd believed that people could make a difference if they just stood up and spoke truth to power.

That version of her mother had died long before the cancer, worn down by years of watching the powerful remain powerful while the righteous remained poor.

"What happens if I testify and nothing changes?" Maya asked.

"Then at least you tried. That counts for something."

"Does it? I've watched people try for my entire life. I've watched them fight and struggle and sacrifice, and the corporations just keep winning. They have more money, more lawyers, more politicians in their pockets. What's one testimony going to accomplish?"

Vera was quiet for a moment, her eyes distant. "Six years ago, I was a data analyst for a pharmaceutical company. Not Vitae—one of their competitors. I found discrepancies in their clinical trials. Not huge ones, just enough to suggest they were cherry-picking results. I went to my supervisor. He told me to forget about it. I went to HR. They offered me a promotion and a substantial raise to drop the issue."

"What did you do?"

"Took the money. Bought a house. Told myself that it was just business, that this was how the world worked, that one person couldn't change anything." Vera's jaw tightened. "Six months later, the drug they'd released caused kidney failure in children. Hundreds of them. I watched those lawsuits on the news while I sat in my new house, and I realized that silence has a price too. Maybe higher than the price of speaking up."

"So you became an activist."

"I became someone who can still look at herself in the mirror." Vera stood, gathering the empty food containers. "Sleep on it. You've got time to decide. The hearing's not for three days."

After Vera left, Maya lay on the cot and stared at the flickering light. She thought about prices and costs, about what she'd already paid and what she stood to lose. The calculus felt impossible—her safety versus the lives of strangers, her anonymity versus some abstract notion of justice that maybe didn't even exist anymore.

Her mother's voice echoed in her memory: *They only win if we let them.*

But they had won. They always won. That was the lesson Maya had learned from her mother's death, from the Panacea scandal, from every single battle between corporations and people that she'd witnessed in her thirty-four years. The system wasn't broken—it was working exactly as designed, extracting maximum profit while distributing minimum consequence.

So why did the woman's empty eyes keep haunting her?

Maya must have slept eventually, because she woke to sunlight filtering through gaps in the shipping container's walls, creating strange geometric patterns on the floor. Her back ached from the thin cot. Her mouth tasted like rust. She could hear activity outside—voices, footsteps, the hum of generators powering whatever operations the Digital Truth Coalition ran from this hidden warren of containers.

When she emerged, she found the Gaps transformed by daylight into something almost beautiful. The massive support pillars rose like metal trees, their surfaces covered in murals and graffiti that told stories of resistance and survival. Solar panels had been jury-rigged between containers, creating a patchwork canopy. Gardens grew in vertical planters, actual living plants producing actual food. Children played in a cleared space, their laughter echoing off the metal surfaces.

It was a community. An entire hidden ecosystem of people who'd fallen through the cracks or been pushed out, who'd decided to build something in the spaces the corporations didn't care about.

Bosch found Maya standing at the edge of the main clearing, watching the children play.

"They're why we fight," Bosch said. "Not for some abstract principle. For them. So maybe they grow up in a world where corporations can't just kill people and calculate it as a cost of doing business."

"How many people live here?"

"In the Gaps? Maybe three hundred. Across the city, in places like this? Thousands. Tens of thousands. People who couldn't afford the Vertical Expansion, or who didn't want to live in corporate-owned housing, or who pissed off the wrong algorithm and got blacklisted from employment." Bosch gestured at the solar panels. "We're off-grid. Off-books. Invisible to the systems that run the city above us."

"Until they decide you're not."

"Until then." Bosch smiled grimly. "Come on. Vera wants you to see something."

They walked through the container community to a larger unit that served as the operations center's secondary location. Inside, more screens displayed news feeds from around the country. Maya's stomach sank as she read the headlines:

*VITAE CORP CEO: "DEATHS LINKED TO SABOTAGED PRODUCT"*

*FDA PRELIMINARY FINDINGS: NO SYSTEMIC ISSUES WITH ETERNAFLOW*

*ARRESTS MADE IN ETERNAFLOW TAMPERING INVESTIGATION*

Vera stood in front of the screens, her expression carved from stone. "They're moving faster than we expected. Blaming everything on sabotage. They've arrested three people—two warehouse workers and a shipping contractor—claiming they introduced contaminants during distribution."

"Were they actually involved?" Maya asked.

"No. They're patsies. People with criminal records, financial problems, easy to frame. Vitae's legal team probably offered them deals—plead guilty, serve minimal time, get a payout when it's all over. Classic corporate playbook."

On one screen, footage showed one of the arrested men being led into a police station. He looked terrified, broken, defeated. The chyron below read: *SUSPECT CONFESSES TO TAMPERING WITH ETERNAFLOW SHIPMENTS.*

"Jesus," Maya whispered. "They're just going to blame some random workers and walk away?"

"Not if we can help it." Vera pulled up a new screen showing a live feed of City Hall. "The hearing's been moved up. Tomorrow, not three days from now. They're trying to rush through their narrative before we can organize effective opposition. We need to move tonight."

"Tonight? I thought—"

"Plans change. Vitae knows we have witnesses. They're going to try to discredit everyone before we can testify. We need to get you into City Hall before they lock down access." Vera turned to face Maya directly. "I need to know right now—are you in or out?"

Maya looked at the screens. The arrested workers. The sanitized news coverage. The CEO's practiced expression of concern. The stock price already recovering as the market bought the sabotage story. The machinery of power grinding truth into convenient fiction.

She thought about her mother. About the woman in white. About the acceptable number of twenty thousand deaths.

She thought about the price of silence and the cost of speaking.

"I'm in," Maya said.

The plan was simple in the way that terrible ideas often are: get Maya into City Hall before dawn, hidden among the janitorial staff who cleaned the building before the hearing. Vera had contacts—people who owed favors, people who believed in the cause, people who were simply willing to take money to look the other way. By midnight, Maya had been fitted with a maintenance uniform that smelled of industrial detergent and given a security badge with someone else's face on it.

"The scanners are old," Bosch explained, attaching the badge to Maya's collar. "They read the chip, not the photo. As long as you move with confidence and don't give anyone a reason to look twice, you'll be fine."

Maya didn't feel fine. She felt like her heart was trying to escape through her throat.

The van that took them into the city center was different from the one that had brought her to the Gaps—newer, corporate-branded with a cleaning company logo that Bosch assured her was "mostly legitimate." Maya sat in the back with five actual maintenance workers who'd been briefed on the situation and paid enough to keep quiet. They didn't speak to her during the drive, but one woman—older, with callused hands and tired eyes—squeezed Maya's shoulder as they pulled up to the service entrance.

"My daughter died three days ago," the woman said quietly. "They're calling it a heart condition. She was twenty-six and had been drinking EternaFlow for two months." Her grip tightened. "You tell them. You tell everyone what these fuckers did."

The City Hall service entrance was monitored by a single bored security guard who barely glanced at their badges as they filed past. Maya kept her eyes down, following the others through corridors that smelled of floor polish and recycled air. The building was massive, a monument to civic pride from an era when people still believed government served someone other than corporate interests. Now it was mostly empty, its vast chambers and ornate halls used for the theater of democracy while actual decisions got made in private conference rooms and encrypted message boards.

The maintenance crew split up to cover different sections. Maya followed her designated partner—a man named Luis who'd worked at City Hall for fifteen years and knew every closet, every locked door, every place where security cameras had blind spots. He led her to a storage room on the third floor, directly above the main hearing chamber.

"You'll wait here," Luis said, unlocking the door to reveal a space crammed with folding chairs and unused office equipment. "Hearing starts at nine. Vera's people will come get you when it's time. There's water in that cooler, and a bathroom two doors down. Don't leave this room unless the building's on fire."

"What if someone comes in?"

"They won't. This room hasn't been opened in six months." He handed her a small radio. "Channel seven if you need help. Otherwise, stay quiet and stay patient."

Then he was gone, and Maya was alone in the darkness with the folding chairs.

The hours crawled past with the particular agony of forced stillness. Maya sat on the floor, back against the wall, listening to the building wake up around her. Footsteps in the hallway. Voices discussing scheduling and procedure. The distant hum of the air conditioning system cycling on. She checked the radio every few minutes, but it remained silent except for occasional bursts of static.

At some point, she must have dozed off, because she woke to the sound of the radio crackling to life.

"Maya, it's Vera. You there?"

Maya fumbled for the radio. "I'm here."

"Good. Things are moving fast. The hearing's been restructured. Vitae's lawyers filed an emergency motion to limit testimony to 'qualified expert witnesses only.' They're trying to exclude anyone who isn't a doctor or scientist."

"Does that include me?"

"Technically, yes. But we've got a workaround. Council Member Torres is sympathetic—her nephew died from EternaFlow complications, though she can't say that publicly yet. She's going to invoke the public comment period, which allows anyone to speak for three minutes. It's not much, but it's something."

"Three minutes."

"Three minutes to tell your story. To put a human face on the statistics. To make them see what Vitae Corp actually did." Vera paused. "Can you do that?"

Maya thought about the woman's empty eyes. About her mother's medical bills. About the acceptable number of twenty thousand. About the price of silence.

"Yes."

"Okay. Stay ready. I'll signal when it's time."

The radio went silent again. Maya stood and paced the small room, her legs stiff from sitting. Three minutes. What could she possibly say in three minutes that would matter? How did you compress a woman's death, a corporation's calculated evil, the weight of systemic injustice into a statement short enough to fit between commercial breaks?

She practiced in her head. Started and stopped a dozen times. Every version sounded wrong—too angry, too clinical, too emotional, too detached. The words wouldn't organize themselves into anything coherent.

Through the floor, she could hear the hearing beginning. Muffled voices, the bang of a gavel, the scrape of chairs. She pressed her ear to the ground and caught fragments—something about procedure, about decorum, about the importance of evidence-based testimony. The voice sounded formal, bloodless, the kind of language designed to drain meaning from tragedy.

The radio crackled again. "Stand by. Torres is about to open public comment."

Maya moved to the door, her hand on the handle, waiting. Her pulse hammered in her ears. Everything she'd ever been—the invisible data sorter, the woman who kept her head down and survived—screamed at her to stay in this room, to let someone else speak, to disappear back into the Gaps and build a quiet life where corporations couldn't find her.

But the woman's eyes wouldn't leave her mind.

"Now," Vera's voice said through the radio. "Move now."

Maya opened the door and stepped into the hallway. A young man in a Digital Truth Coalition t-shirt stood waiting, gesturing urgently toward a stairwell. She followed him down two flights, her footsteps echoing off concrete walls, her breathing ragged. They emerged into a side corridor that led to the main hearing chamber. Through the double doors, she could see rows of seats filled with reporters, activists, corporate representatives. At the front, a panel of council members sat behind an elevated bench, looking down at the proceedings with expressions ranging from concern to barely concealed boredom.

"You go through those doors, walk straight to the podium, state your name, and speak," the young man said. "You'll have three minutes. Maybe less if they try to shut you down. Make them count."

He pushed open the doors.

The chamber was larger than Maya had expected, its ceiling soaring overhead, its walls lined with portraits of long-dead politicians who'd probably been just as corrupt as the current ones. Every seat was full. Cameras lined the back wall, their lenses reflecting the harsh overhead lights. At the center of it all, a wooden podium with a microphone stood waiting.

Maya walked toward it, her legs moving on autopilot while her mind screamed. Every eye in the room tracked her movement. She heard murmurs, questions, the scratch of pens on paper as reporters took notes. At the front of the chamber, a woman with silver hair and sharp eyes—Council Member Torres, Maya assumed—nodded almost imperceptibly.

Maya reached the podium. Gripped its edges to keep her hands from shaking. Looked up at the cameras, at the council members, at the rows of faces waiting to judge whether her pain was real enough to matter.

The microphone squealed with feedback, and then fell silent.

"State your name for the record," Torres said.

Maya's mouth was dry. Her voice came out smaller than she intended. "Maya Reeves."

"Ms. Reeves, you have three minutes to address the council."

Three minutes. The woman's empty eyes. Her mother's medical bills. The acceptable number. The price of silence.

Maya took a breath and began to speak.
